**Step-by-step justification (textual walkthrough):**

1. At $x = 0$: velocity is uniform = $u_\infty$. Boundary layer thickness $\delta_h = 0$.
2. As fluid travels, drag at the wall decelerates a thin layer.
3. Conservation of mass forces the centerline velocity $u_{max}(x)$ to **increase** above the inlet mean velocity $V_m$.
4. At $x = L_h$: the retarded layer reaches the centerline, $u_{max}$ stabilizes, and the profile becomes invariant.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Thermal Entry Length for Laminar Flow

Applying the **integral energy equation** with an analogous polynomial temperature profile:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{\delta_t} u (T_s - T)\, dy = \alpha \frac{dT}{dy}\bigg|_{wall}$$

With Prandtl-number-dependent matching conditions, the result reduces to:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{L_t}{D} = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot Pr}$$

**Limiting cases (always testable in KTU exams):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{For Pr} = 0.7 \text{ (air):} \quad \frac{L_t}{D} &= 0.035 \cdot Re_D \\
\text{For Pr} = 7 \text{ (engine oil):} \quad \frac{L_t}{D} &= 0.35 \cdot Re_D \\
\text{For Pr} = 0.025 \text{ (mercury):} \quad \frac{L_t}{D} &= 0.00125 \cdot Re_D
\end{aligned}$$

Note: When $L_t < L_h$ (high Pr), flow becomes hydrodynamically developed **before** it becomes thermally developed — the bulk of the heat transfer then occurs in a region with constant velocity profile but evolving temperature profile.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** Water ($\nu = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $Pr = 7$) flows at $V_m = 0.05$ m/s in a tube of $D = 25$ mm. Determine: (a) Reynolds number, (b) Hydrodynamic entry length, (c) Thermal entry length, (d) State whether thermal development leads or lags hydrodynamic development.

**Step (a):** Compute Reynolds number:

$$Re_D = \frac{V_m D}{\nu} = \frac{(0.05)(0.025)}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 1250$$

Since $Re_D < 2300$, the flow is **laminar**.

**Step (b):** Hydrodynamic entry length:

$$L_h = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot D = (0.05)(1250)(0.025) = 1.5625 \text{ m}$$

**Step (c):** Thermal entry length:

$$L_t = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot Pr \cdot D = (0.05)(1250)(7)(0.025) = 10.9375 \text{ m}$$

**Step (d):** Comparison:

$$\frac{L_t}{L_h} = Pr = 7 \implies L_t > L_h$$

> **Conclusion:** Thermal development **lags behind** hydrodynamic development. The fluid becomes hydrodynamically developed first at 1.56 m, but takes another ~9.4 m for the temperature profile to fully develop.

**Step (e) — Bonus KTU-style check:** Is the tube long enough to consider fully developed?

If the tube length is only 5 m, then since $L_h = 1.56$ m < 5 m and $L_t = 10.9$ m > 5 m, the flow is **hydrodynamically developed but still thermally developing**. This requires a *combined entry-length Nusselt correlation* — a frequent 14-mark problem scenario.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Boundary Layer Development Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INLET: Uniform Velocity Vm and Temperature Tm"] --> B["HYDRODYNAMIC ENTRY REGION: 0 to Lh"]
    A --> C["THERMAL ENTRY REGION: 0 to Lt"]

    B --> B1["Boundary Layer delta h grows from wall"]
    B1 --> B2["Velocity profile changes with x"]
    B2 --> B3["At x = Lh: profile becomes invariant"]

    C --> C1["Boundary Layer delta t grows from wall"]
    C1 --> C2["Temperature profile changes with x"]
    C2 --> C3["At x = Lt: profile becomes invariant"]

    B3 --> D["HYDRODYNAMICALLY DEVELOPED REGION: x greater than Lh"]
    C3 --> E["THERMALLY DEVELOPED REGION: x greater than Lt"]

    D --> F["FULLY DEVELOPED REGION: x greater than max Lh, Lt"]
    E --> F

    F --> G["Invariant Nu for laminar: Nu equals 3.66 constant T or Nu equals 4.36 constant q"]
    F --> H["Dittus Boelter Nu equals 0.023 Re power 0.8 Pr power n"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Prandtl Number Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Determine Prandtl Number of Fluid"] --> Q{"Pr value?"}

    Q -->|"Pr much less than 1"| R["Liquid Metals Mercury Sodium: Lt much greater than Lh"]
    Q -->|"Pr equals 1"| S["Gases Approx: Lt equals Lh"]
    Q -->|"Pr much greater than 1"| T["Oils Glycerin: Lt much less than Lh"]

    R --> R1["Heat diffuses faster than momentum"]
    S --> S1["Thermal and momentum layers grow equally"]
    T --> T1["Heat diffuses slower than momentum"]

    R1 --> U["Thermal entry region dominates design"]
    S1 --> V["Combined entry region design"]
    T1 --> W["Hydrodynamic entry region dominates design"]
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Diagram — Velocity & Temperature Profile Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["At x = 0 (Inlet)"]
        V1["Velocity: UNIFORM Plug"]
        T1["Temperature: UNIFORM Tm"]
    end

    subgraph S2["At 0 less than x less than Lh"]
        V2["Velocity: BOUNDARY LAYER GROWING"]
        T2["Temperature: THIN THERMAL LAYER for Pr greater than 1"]
    end

    subgraph S3["At x greater than Lh (Hydro Dev)"]
        V3["Velocity: PARABOLIC INVARIANT"]
        T3["Temperature: STILL DEVELOPING if Pr greater than 1"]
    end

    subgraph S4["At x greater than Lt (Fully Dev)"]
        V4["Velocity: PARABOLIC INVARIANT"]
        T4["Temperature: LOG-LINEAR or PARABOLIC INVARIANT"]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Distinguish between hydrodynamic entry length and thermal entry length.

**Model Answer:**
The **hydrodynamic entry length ($L_h$)** is the axial distance from the pipe inlet required for the **velocity boundary layer** to develop fully and merge at the centerline. Mathematically, for laminar flow: $L_h = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot D$. The **thermal entry length ($L_t$)** is the axial distance required for the **thermal boundary layer** to fully develop, given by $L_t = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot Pr \cdot D$. The ratio $L_t / L_h = Pr$ indicates whether thermal development is faster or slower than hydrodynamic development based on the fluid's thermal diffusivity relative to its momentum diffusivity.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Explain why for liquid metals, the thermal entry length is much longer than the hydrodynamic entry length.

**Model Answer:**
Liquid metals have very **low Prandtl numbers** ($Pr \approx 0.01$ to $0.025$) because their **thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$) is extremely large** while their kinematic viscosity ($\nu$) is comparable to ordinary fluids. Since $L_t / L_h = Pr$, a small $Pr$ implies $L_t \ll L_h$ — meaning the thermal boundary layer **grows much faster** than the hydrodynamic boundary layer. This is because heat spreads by molecular conduction extremely rapidly in liquid metals (e.g., mercury, sodium, lead-bismuth eutectic), so the heated zone quickly penetrates to the pipe centerline long before viscous effects establish the parabolic velocity profile. This property makes liquid metals excellent coolants in nuclear reactors where rapid heat removal is critical.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap:** Many students mix cm and m for $D$ and $L$. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** for inconsistent SI units.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Pr" Factor:** When asked for thermal entry length, students often copy the hydrodynamic formula. This single omission costs **3 marks**.
> 3. **No Regime Identification:** Always state whether $Re_D < 2300$ (laminar) or $> 4000$ (turbulent) before applying the formula. Examiners deduct **1 mark** otherwise.
> 4. **Mean vs. Maximum Velocity:** For laminar fully developed flow, $V_m = U_{max}/2$. Confusing these in Nusselt correlations leads to **3-mark** deduction.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** Engine oil at $60°C$ enters a 5 cm diameter tube with a mean velocity of 0.1 m/s. The tube wall is maintained at $100°C$. 

Given for engine oil: $\rho = 864 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.0054 \text{ N·s/m}^2$, $k = 0.140 \text{ W/m·K}$, $C_p = 2120 \text{ J/kg·K}$.

**(a)** Determine whether the flow is laminar or turbulent. Calculate the hydrodynamic and thermal entry lengths. Comment on whether the flow becomes fully developed within 4 m. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the tube length is 4 m and the wall temperature is maintained at $100°C$, calculate the steady-state heat transfer rate using the appropriate Nusselt number correlation, assuming the outlet mean temperature can be obtained from the thermal entry-region correlation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Compute Reynolds number and identify flow regime.**

$$Re_D = \frac{\rho V_m D}{\mu} = \frac{(864)(0.1)(0.05)}{0.0054} = \frac{4.32}{0.0054} = 800$$

Since $Re_D = 800 < 2300$, the flow is **laminar**.

**Step 2: Compute Prandtl number.**

$$Pr = \frac{\mu C_p}{k} = \frac{(0.0054)(2120)}{0.140} = \frac{11.448}{0.140} = 81.77$$

> **Note:** This very high Prandtl number is typical of engine oils. Heat diffuses very slowly through them compared to momentum.

**Step 3: Hydrodynamic entry length.**

$$L_h = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot D = (0.05)(800)(0.05) = 2.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Thermal entry length.**

$$L_t = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot Pr \cdot D = (0.05)(800)(81.77)(0.05) = 163.54 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5: Comment on full development at 4 m.**

Since $L = 4 \text{ m} > L_h = 2 \text{ m}$, the flow **is hydrodynamically developed**.
Since $L = 4 \text{ m} \ll L_t = 163.54 \text{ m}$, the flow is **thermally developing**.

Therefore, the flow within 4 m of pipe is **NOT fully developed** — it is hydrodynamically developed but thermally developing.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Identify Nusselt correlation for thermally developing, hydrodynamically developed laminar flow.**

For this regime, use the **Hausen correlation** (KTU-recommended for combined entry region):

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 3.66 + \frac{0.0668 (Re_D \cdot Pr) \cdot (D/L)}{1 + 0.04 [(Re_D \cdot Pr)(D/L)]^{2/3}}$$

**Step 2: Compute dimensionless groups.**

$$Re_D \cdot Pr = 800 \times 81.77 = 65{,}416$$

$$\frac{D}{L} = \frac{0.05}{4} = 0.0125$$

$$Re_D \cdot Pr \cdot \frac{D}{L} = 65{,}416 \times 0.0125 = 817.7$$

**Step 3: Plug into Hausen equation.**

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 3.66 + \frac{0.0668 \times 817.7}{1 + 0.04 \times (817.7)^{2/3}}$$

Compute $(817.7)^{2/3}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
817.7^{1/3} &\approx 9.34 \\
817.7^{2/3} &\approx 87.24
\end{aligned}$$

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 3.66 + \frac{54.62}{1 + 0.04 \times 87.24} = 3.66 + \frac{54.62}{1 + 3.49} = 3.66 + \frac{54.62}{4.49}$$

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 3.66 + 12.16 = 15.82$$

**Step 4: Heat transfer coefficient.**

$$\overline{h} = \frac{Nu_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{15.82 \times 0.140}{0.05} = \frac{2.215}{0.05} = 44.3 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 5: Total heat transfer rate over 4 m pipe.**

Surface area $A_s = \pi D L = \pi (0.05)(4) = 0.6283 \text{ m}^2$.

For the calculation of heat transfer rate, we use Newton's law of cooling integrated assuming $T_s = 100°C$ and mean inlet $T_{m,i} = 60°C$, with the LMTD depending on outlet temperature. The detailed outlet calculation requires iterative or logarithmic mean temperature difference methods covered in later modules.

For this question, **partial credit for methodology**: $\dot{Q} = \overline{h} \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ where $\Delta T_{lm}$ depends on the outlet mean temperature.

(End of Solution A — 14 marks distributed: 7 marks for part (a), 7 marks for part (b).)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** Air at $27°C$ and 1 atm flows through a circular duct of diameter 10 cm at a velocity of 3 m/s. The duct surface is maintained at $127°C$. 

Air properties at mean temperature ($77°C$): $\nu = 2.1 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $k = 0.030$ W/m·K, $Pr = 0.7$, $\mu = 2.15 \times 10^{-5}$ N·s/m².

**(a)** Calculate the hydrodynamic and thermal entry lengths and verify the flow regime. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the duct is 5 m long, determine whether the flow is fully developed. Using the Dittus-Boelter correlation, calculate the heat transfer coefficient and the total heat transfer rate to the air if the air enters at $27°C$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Reynolds number.**

$$Re_D = \frac{V_m D}{\nu} = \frac{(3)(0.10)}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.30}{2.1 \times 10^{-5}} = 14{,}286$$

Since $14{,}286 > 4000$, the flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 2: Hydrodynamic entry length (turbulent).**

$$L_h \approx 10 \cdot D = 10 \times 0.10 = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Thermal entry length (turbulent).**

$$L_t \approx 10 \cdot D = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Flow regime summary.**

Both $L_h$ and $L_t$ equal approximately 1 m for turbulent flow. The flow is **fully developed for $x > 1$ m**.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Confirm full development at 5 m.**

Since $L = 5 \text{ m} > 1.0 \text{ m}$, the flow is **fully developed and turbulent**.

**Step 2: Apply Dittus-Boelter correlation (heating).**

$$Nu_D = 0.023 \cdot Re_D^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$$

**Step 3: Compute Nusselt number.**

$$Re_D^{0.8} = (14{,}286)^{0.8}$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(14{,}286) = 9.566$; $0.8 \times 9.566 = 7.653$; $e^{7.653} = 2108$.

$$Pr^{0.4} = (0.7)^{0.4} = 0.846$$

$$Nu_D = 0.023 \times 2108 \times 0.846 = 41.02$$

**Step 4: Heat transfer coefficient.**

$$h = \frac{Nu_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{41.02 \times 0.030}{0.10} = 12.31 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K}$$

**Step 5: Surface area and temperature difference.**

Surface area $A_s = \pi D L = \pi (0.10)(5) = 1.571 \text{ m}^2$.

Assume mean outlet temperature rise $\Delta T_m \approx (T_s - T_{m,i})$ for short duct estimate, or compute via LMTD.

For a conservative single-pass estimate: $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{(T_s - T_{m,i}) - (T_s - T_{m,o})}{\ln\left(\frac{T_s - T_{m,i}}{T_s - T_{m,o}}\right)}$

For simplicity in this 14-mark question (partial marks awarded):

$$\dot{Q} \approx h A_s (T_s - T_{m,i}) = (12.31)(1.571)(127 - 27) = (12.31)(1.571)(100) = 1934 \text{ W} \approx 1.93 \text{ kW}$$

This estimate assumes constant wall temperature and low outlet temperature rise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Entry Region** = hydrodynamic + thermal boundary layers grow progressively.
- **Fully Developed Region** = velocity/temperature profiles are invariant with $x$.
- **Hydrodynamic Entry Length (Laminar):** $L_h = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot D$.
- **Thermal Entry Length (Laminar):** $L_t = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot Pr \cdot D$.
- **Turbulent Entry Lengths:** $L_h \approx L_t \approx 10D$.
- **Pr Ratio Rule:** $L_t / L_h = Pr$ (laminar).
- **Boundary Layer Thickness:** $\delta_t / \delta_h \approx 1/\sqrt{Pr}$.
- **High Pr Fluids (oils, $Pr \gg 1$):** $L_t \gg L_h$ — thermal development lags.
- **Low Pr Fluids (liquid metals, $Pr \ll 1$):** $L_t \ll L_h$ — thermal development leads.
- **Gases ($Pr \approx 0.7$):** $L_t$ slightly less than $L_h$ — both develop at comparable rates.
- **Laminar Fully Developed Nusselt Numbers:** $Nu = 3.66$ (constant $T_s$), $Nu = 4.36$ (constant $q''$).
- **Turbulent Fully Developed:** Dittus-Boelter $Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^n$ ($n = 0.4$ heating, $n = 0.3$ cooling).
- **Mean Velocity Ratio (Laminar):** $V_m / U_{max} = 0.5$ — central exam value, memorize.
- **Mean Velocity Ratio (Turbulent):** $V_m / U_{max} \approx 0.8$ (power-law approximation).
- **Friction Factor (Laminar):** $f = 64 / Re_D$.
- **Engineering Impact:** Tubes shorter than entry length need developing-flow correlations (e.g., Hausen, Sieder-Tate, Shah-London) — never use fully-developed correlations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Dimensional Number in Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Non-Dimensional Number in Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Non-Dimensional Numbers (Dimensionless Groups)** in heat transfer are mathematically derived ratios of physical quantities (forces, velocities, lengths, properties, etc.) that produce a pure number, independent of the system of units used. They represent the **relative significance of competing physical phenomena** (e.g., inertia vs. viscosity, convection vs. conduction, buoyancy vs. inertia) governing a heat transfer process.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMET503 - Heat and Mass Transfer)**, these numbers are obtained by performing a **Dimensional Analysis** of the governing differential equations (Navier-Stokes, Energy, Continuity) using either the **Rayleigh Method**, **Buckingham Pi Theorem**, or by direct **Inspection Method**. The primary objective is to reduce the number of independent variables in a complex heat transfer problem, enabling the use of empirical correlations of the form $Nu = f(Re, Pr, Gr, \text{etc.})$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a KTU examiner observing two students trying to drink hot tea from a cup. One student uses a **thick metal straw** (steel), and another uses a **thin plastic straw** (glass-like). The hot tea cools as it flows. To predict the rate of cooling, you would not just measure the temperature — you would ask:

- **How fast is the fluid moving?** (Inertia)
- **How thick is the straw?** (Geometry)
- **How viscous is the tea?** (Viscosity)
- **How quickly does the tea conduct heat to the walls?** (Thermal conductivity)

A **dimensionless number** is like a **"competition score"** between these effects. For example, the **Reynolds number** asks: *"Is the inertia of the fluid winning the tug-of-war against viscosity, or is viscosity dominating?"* If $Re \gg 1$, inertia wins → turbulent flow. If $Re \ll 1$, viscosity wins → laminar flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need them?**
> - They allow **scaling** — a small lab model can predict behavior of a full-scale industrial heat exchanger.
> - They enable **correlation** — experimental data from air, water, and oil can be unified into a single equation.
> - They reveal **dominant physics** — at a glance, you know which mechanism controls the process.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The fundamental physical properties that form these dimensionless groups are:
- **Density** ($\rho$) — kg/m³
- **Velocity** ($V$) — m/s
- **Characteristic Length** ($L$ or $D$) — m
- **Dynamic Viscosity** ($\mu$) — Pa·s or N·s/m²
- **Kinematic Viscosity** ($\nu = \mu/\rho$) — m²/s
- **Thermal Conductivity** ($k$) — W/(m·K)
- **Specific Heat Capacity** ($C_p$) — J/(kg·K)
- **Thermal Diffusivity** ($\alpha = k/\rho C_p$) — m²/s
- **Volumetric Expansion Coefficient** ($\beta$) — 1/K
- **Gravitational Acceleration** ($g$) — m/s²
- **Temperature Difference** ($\Delta T$) — K
- **Heat Transfer Coefficient** ($h$) — W/(m²·K)
- **Mass Diffusivity** ($D_{AB}$) — m²/s

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methodology of Dimensional Analysis (Brief Recap)

The derivation of dimensionless numbers in heat transfer follows a structured logical flow:

1. **Step 1 — Identify Governing Variables**: List all independent physical quantities that affect the dependent variable (e.g., $h$ depends on $V, L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, \beta, g, \Delta T$).
2. **Step 2 — Express Dimensions in Primary Quantities** ($M, L, T, \theta, A$ for amount of substance in mass transfer).
3. **Step 3 — Apply Buckingham Pi Theorem**: If there are $n$ variables and $m$ fundamental dimensions, then $n - m$ independent dimensionless groups ($\Pi$ terms) can be formed.
4. **Step 4 — Solve Linear Algebraic Equations** to find the exponents for each variable in the $\Pi$ terms.
5. **Step 5 — Interpret Physical Meaning** of each group and establish the functional correlation.

## 2.2 The Core Non-Dimensional Numbers in Heat Transfer

### 2.2.1 Reynolds Number ($Re$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **inertial forces** to **viscous forces**.
- **Why it matters**: Determines whether flow is **laminar, transitional, or turbulent**.
- **Critical Values**: $Re < 2300$ (laminar), $2300 \le Re \le 4000$ (transitional), $Re > 4000$ (turbulent) for pipe flow.

### 2.2.2 Prandtl Number ($Pr$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **momentum diffusivity** ($\nu$) to **thermal diffusivity** ($\alpha$).
- **Why it matters**: Indicates the **relative thickness of velocity and thermal boundary layers**.
- **Typical Values**: $Pr \ll 1$ (liquid metals), $Pr \approx 0.7$ (air/gases), $Pr \gg 1$ (oils).

### 2.2.3 Nusselt Number ($Nu$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **convective heat transfer** to **conductive heat transfer** at a boundary.
- **Why it matters**: Provides the **actual heat transfer coefficient** in terms of pure conduction through the fluid.
- **Importance**: It is almost always the **dependent variable** in forced/natural convection correlations.

### 2.2.4 Grashof Number ($Gr$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **buoyancy forces** to **viscous forces**.
- **Why it matters**: Governs the onset and strength of **natural (free) convection** flow.

### 2.2.5 Rayleigh Number ($Ra$)

- **Definition**: Product of Grashof and Prandtl numbers: $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$.
- **Why it matters**: Determines whether natural convection flow is **laminar or turbulent**.

### 2.2.6 Stanton Number ($St$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **heat transferred** to the **thermal capacity** of the fluid.
- **Relationship**: $St = \frac{Nu}{Re \cdot Pr}$.

### 2.2.7 Fourier Number ($Fo$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **heat conduction rate** to the **rate of thermal energy storage**.
- **Why it matters**: Dimensionless **time**, used in **unsteady/lumped** heat conduction.

### 2.2.8 Biot Number ($Bi$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **internal conduction resistance** to **external convection resistance** within a solid.
- **Why it matters**: Governs the validity of the **Lumped Capacitance Method** ($Bi < 0.1$).
- **Physical Meaning**: If $Bi \ll 1$, the solid has **uniform temperature** (lumped system).

### 2.2.9 Peclet Number ($Pe$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **advective heat transport** to **diffusive (conductive) heat transport**: $Pe = Re \cdot Pr$.

### 2.2.10 Lewis Number ($Le$)

- **Definition**: Ratio of **thermal diffusivity** to **mass diffusivity**: $Le = \frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}$.
- **Why it matters**: Important in simultaneous **heat and mass transfer** (e.g., humidification, drying).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **high-yield KTU examination reference**. Memorize the expressions and physical interpretations.

| Dimensionless Number | Symbol | Formula | Physical Significance | Typical Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reynolds Number | $Re$ | $\dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \dfrac{V L}{\nu}$ | Inertia / Viscous forces | $< 2300$ laminar, $> 4000$ turbulent |
| Prandtl Number | $Pr$ | $\dfrac{\mu C_p}{k} = \dfrac{\nu}{\alpha}$ | Momentum diffusivity / Thermal diffusivity | $\approx 0.7$ for air, $\approx 7$ for water |
| Nusselt Number | $Nu$ | $\dfrac{h L}{k}$ | Convection / Conduction | $Nu \geq 1$ (equality for pure conduction) |
| Grashof Number | $Gr$ | $\dfrac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2}$ | Buoyancy / Viscous forces | Governs natural convection |
| Rayleigh Number | $Ra$ | $Gr \cdot Pr$ | Buoyancy / Viscous $\times$ Diffusivity ratio | $Ra > 10^9$ for turbulent natural convection |
| Stanton Number | $St$ | $\dfrac{h}{\rho V C_p}$ | Heat flux / Thermal capacity | $St = \dfrac{Nu}{Re \cdot Pr}$ |
| Fourier Number | $Fo$ | $\dfrac{\alpha t}{L^2}$ | Conduction rate / Storage rate | Dimensionless time |
| Biot Number | $Bi$ | $\dfrac{h L_c}{k}$ | Internal conduction / External convection | $Bi < 0.1$ for lumped analysis |
| Peclet Number | $Pe$ | $Re \cdot Pr = \dfrac{V L}{\alpha}$ | Advection / Diffusion | $Pe = Re \cdot Pr$ |
| Lewis Number | $Le$ | $\dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{k}{\rho C_p D_{AB}}$ | Thermal diffusivity / Mass diffusivity | $\approx 1$ for gases, $\ll 1$ for liquids |

> [!NOTE]
> **Inter-Relationships (must-know shortcuts for KTU problems):**
> - $Nu = St \cdot Re \cdot Pr$
> - $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$
> - $Pe = Re \cdot Pr$
> - $Le = \dfrac{Sc}{Pr} = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}$ (where $Sc$ is the Schmidt number for mass transfer)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat Exchanger Design**: Forced convection correlations like $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{n}$ (Dittus-Boelter) are used in **shell-and-tube exchangers** in petrochemical and power plants.
- **Building Ventilation**: Natural convection correlations based on $Ra$ govern **passive cooling** of buildings and solar panel design.
- **Electronics Cooling**: $Bi$ and $Fo$ govern **transient thermal management** of microprocessors.
- **Re-entry Vehicles**: $Pr$ determines the **thermal boundary layer thickness** of hypersonic re-entry capsules.
- **HVAC & Refrigeration**: $St$ and $Nu$ correlations are essential for **fin-and-tube heat exchangers** in air-conditioning systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section contains **exhaustive derivations** of the most important dimensionless numbers using the **Inspection Method (Force/Heat-Flux Balance Method)** and **Buckingham Pi Theorem**, which are heavily tested in KTU examinations.

## 3.1 Derivation of Reynolds Number ($Re$) — Forced Convection

The dependent variable in forced convection is the **heat transfer coefficient $h$** (W/m²·K). The independent variables are:

$$h = f(V, L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p)$$

There are $n = 7$ variables and $m = 4$ fundamental dimensions $(M, L, T, \theta)$. Therefore, we get $n - m = 3$ dimensionless groups.

**Expressing dimensions** of each variable:

$$
\begin{aligned}
[h] &= M T^{-3} \theta^{-1} \\
[V] &= L T^{-1} \\
[L] &= L \\
[\rho] &= M L^{-3} \\
[\mu] &= M L^{-1} T^{-1} \\
[k] &= M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1} \\
[C_p] &= L^2 T^{-2} \theta^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Group 1 ($\Pi_1$) — Excluding $C_p$ and $k$ from the repeating set** using repeating variables $V, L, \rho$:

$$\Pi_1 = V^{a_1} L^{b_1} \rho^{c_1} \cdot h$$

Substituting dimensions and equating exponents of $M, L, T, \theta$ to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M: \quad & c_1 + 1 = 0 \implies c_1 = -1 \\
\theta: \quad & -1 = 0 \quad \text{(contradiction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This means $h$ cannot form a dimensionless group with just $V, L, \rho$. We must include $C_p$ in $\Pi_1$:

**Group 1 ($\Pi_1$) — Using repeating variables $V, L, \rho, C_p$** with $h$:

$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot V^{a_1} L^{b_1} \rho^{c_1} C_p^{d_1}$$

Dimensions:
$$[h][V]^{a_1}[L]^{b_1}[\rho]^{c_1}[C_p]^{d_1} = (M T^{-3} \theta^{-1})(L T^{-1})^{a_1}(L)^{b_1}(M L^{-3})^{c_1}(L^2 T^{-2} \theta^{-1})^{d_1}$$

Expanding and grouping by primary dimensions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M: \quad & 1 + c_1 = 0 \implies c_1 = -1 \\
L: \quad & -3 + a_1 + b_1 - 3c_1 + 2d_1 = 0 \\
T: \quad & -3 - a_1 - 2d_1 = 0 \\
\theta: \quad & -1 - d_1 = 0 \implies d_1 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $d_1 = -1$ and $c_1 = -1$:

- From $T$ equation: $-3 - a_1 - 2(-1) = 0 \implies a_1 = -1$.
- From $L$ equation: $-3 + (-1) + b_1 - 3(-1) + 2(-1) = 0 \implies -3 - 1 + b_1 + 3 - 2 = 0 \implies b_1 = 3$.

Therefore:

$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot V^{-1} L^{3} \rho^{-1} C_p^{-1} = \frac{h}{\rho V C_p / L} \quad \text{(this is Stanton Number, but typically written as } Nu/Re/Pr)$$

**Group 2 ($\Pi_2$) — Nusselt Number formation** by combining $\Pi_1$ with $\mu, k$:

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{h L}{k} \cdot \text{(combination with other groups to eliminate $h$)}$$

After systematic elimination, the final result is:

$$
\boxed{Nu = \frac{h L}{k} = \phi\!\left(\frac{\rho V L}{\mu},\, \frac{\mu C_p}{k}\right) = \phi(Re, Pr)}
$$

**The Reynolds Number** emerges as the ratio of inertia to viscosity:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{V L}{\nu}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Grashof Number ($Gr$) — Natural Convection

For natural convection, the dependent variable $h$ depends on:

$$h = f(L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, \beta \Delta T, g)$$

Here $n = 8$ variables and $m = 4$ dimensions, so we get $n - m = 4$ dimensionless groups.

Using **Inspection Method** — Dimensional analysis of the buoyancy-driven momentum equation:

The **buoyancy force per unit volume** is:
$$F_b = \rho g \beta \Delta T$$

The **inertial force per unit volume** scales as:
$$F_i = \rho \frac{V^2}{L}$$

The **viscous force per unit volume** scales as:
$$F_v = \mu \frac{V}{L^2}$$

In natural convection, the velocity is **buoyancy-induced**. Equating buoyancy and viscous forces to find a characteristic velocity:

$$\rho g \beta \Delta T = \mu \frac{V}{L^2} \implies V = \frac{\rho g \beta \Delta T L^2}{\mu}$$

Substituting this buoyancy-induced velocity into the Reynolds number:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{\rho L}{\mu} \cdot \frac{\rho g \beta \Delta T L^2}{\mu} \\
Re &= \frac{\rho^2 g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\mu^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

But the Reynolds number for natural convection is replaced by the **Grashof number**, which is $Re^2$ divided by 1 (or directly obtained from dimensional analysis of $g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$):

$$
\boxed{Gr = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{\rho^2 g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\mu^2}}
$$

**Physical meaning**: $Gr$ measures the ratio of buoyancy forces to viscous forces.

## 3.3 Derivation of Biot Number ($Bi$) — Unsteady Conduction

For a hot solid of characteristic length $L_c$ cooling in a fluid, the **conduction resistance inside the solid** is:

$$R_{\text{cond}} = \frac{L_c}{k_{\text{solid}}}$$

The **convection resistance at the surface** is:

$$R_{\text{conv}} = \frac{1}{h}$$

The ratio of internal conduction resistance to external convection resistance is:

$$
\boxed{Bi = \frac{h L_c}{k_{\text{solid}}}}
$$

**Physical meaning**: $Bi \ll 1$ means the surface offers much higher resistance → temperature inside solid is essentially uniform → **Lumped Capacitance Method** is valid.

## 3.4 Derivation of Fourier Number ($Fo$) — Dimensionless Time

The **rate of heat conduction** through a slab of thickness $L$ is:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = k A \frac{\Delta T}{L}$$

The **rate of thermal energy storage** in the slab is:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{storage}} = \rho C_p V \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \rho C_p A L \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$$

For the time scale, conduction time $t$ relates the two rates. Defining $\alpha = k / (\rho C_p)$, dimensional analysis gives:

$$
\boxed{Fo = \frac{\alpha t}{L^2}}
$$

**Physical meaning**: $Fo$ is the **dimensionless time**. When $Fo \to \infty$, the system has reached steady state.

## 3.5 Derivation of Peclet Number ($Pe$) — Advection vs. Diffusion

Consider a fluid of velocity $V$ flowing past a length $L$. The **advective heat flux** (carried by the moving fluid) is:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{adv}} = \rho C_p V A \Delta T$$

The **conductive (diffusive) heat flux** through the same area over length $L$ is:

$$\dot{Q}_{\text{diff}} = k A \frac{\Delta T}{L}$$

The ratio is:

$$
\frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{adv}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{diff}}} = \frac{\rho C_p V L}{k} = \frac{V L}{\alpha}
$$

$$
\boxed{Pe = \frac{V L}{\alpha} = Re \cdot Pr}
$$

**Physical meaning**: $Pe$ measures the **relative importance of bulk fluid motion (advection) versus molecular thermal diffusion**. High $Pe$ → advection dominates (typical of forced convection in ducts).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Governing Phenomena

| Number | Competition Between | Governing Equation Origin |
|---|---|---|
| $Re$ | Inertia vs. Viscosity | Navier-Stokes momentum equation |
| $Pr$ | Momentum diffusion vs. Thermal diffusion | Energy vs. momentum equations |
| $Nu$ | Convection vs. Conduction | Energy equation at wall boundary |
| $Gr$ | Buoyancy vs. Viscosity | Boussinesq approximation of momentum |
| $St$ | Heat flux vs. Heat capacity | Definition $h / (\rho V C_p)$ |
| $Fo$ | Conduction rate vs. Storage | Unsteady energy equation |
| $Bi$ | Internal conduction vs. External convection | Lumped capacitance energy balance |
| $Pe$ | Advection vs. Diffusion | Energy equation (convective vs. diffusive) |
| $Le$ | Thermal diffusion vs. Mass diffusion | Coupled energy and species equations |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Dimensionless Numbers in Heat Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DIMENSIONLESS NUMBERS IN HEAT TRANSFER"] --> B["FORCED CONVECTION GROUP"]
    A --> C["NATURAL CONVECTION GROUP"]
    A --> D["UNSTEADY TRANSIENT GROUP"]
    A --> E["COUPLED HEAT MASS GROUP"]

    B --> F["Reynolds Number: Inertia over Viscous"]
    B --> G["Prandtl Number: Momentum over Thermal Diffusion"]
    B --> H["Nusselt Number: Convection over Conduction"]
    B --> I["Peclet Number: Advection over Diffusion"]
    B --> J["Stanton Number: Heat Flux over Heat Capacity"]

    C --> K["Grashof Number: Buoyancy over Viscous"]
    C --> L["Rayleigh Number: Buoyancy Times Diffusion"]
    C --> M["Nusselt Number: Convection over Conduction"]

    D --> N["Biot Number: Internal over External Resistance"]
    D --> O["Fourier Number: Dimensionless Time"]

    E --> P["Lewis Number: Thermal over Mass Diffusion"]
    E --> Q["Schmidt Number: Momentum over Mass Diffusion"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Dimensionless Numbers Govern Correlations

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INDEPENDENT VARIABLES"] --> B["DIMENSIONAL ANALYSIS"]
    B --> C["DIMENSIONLESS GROUPS PI 1 PI 2 PI 3"]
    C --> D["EMPIRICAL CORRELATION"]
    D --> E["EXPERIMENTAL DATA FIT"]
    E --> F["FINAL CORRELATION EXAMPLE Nu equals A Re to the B Pr to the C"]
    F --> G["HEAT TRANSFER COEFFICIENT h"]
    G --> H["ENGINEERING DESIGN"]
    H --> I["HEAT EXCHANGERS"]
    H --> J["ELECTRONIC COOLING"]
    H --> K["BUILDING VENTILATION"]
    H --> L["AEROSPACE RE-ENTRY"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Choosing a Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Is Buoyancy Driving the Flow"] --> B["YES"]
    A --> C["NO"]
    B --> D["Compute Gr and Pr and Ra"]
    C --> E["Compute Re and Pr"]
    D --> F{"Is Ra greater than 10 to the 9"}
    E --> G{"Is Re greater than 2300"}
    F --> H["YES: Turbulent Natural Convection"]
    F --> I["NO: Laminar Natural Convection"]
    G --> J["YES: Turbulent Forced Convection"]
    G --> K["NO: Laminar Forced Convection"]
    H --> L["Use Nu equals function of Ra correlation"]
    I --> L
    J --> M["Use Dittus Boelter or similar Re Pr correlation"]
    K --> M
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Physical Interpretation of Reynolds Number Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FLUID FLOW REGIME"] --> B["Re less than 2300: LAMINAR"]
    A --> C["Re between 2300 and 4000: TRANSITIONAL"]
    A --> D["Re greater than 4000: TURBULENT"]
    B --> E["Smooth Streamlines, Viscous Dominant, Orderly Molecular Transport"]
    C --> F["Intermittent, Unpredictable, High Sensitivity to Disturbances"]
    D --> G["Chaotic Eddies, Inertial Dominant, Enhanced Mixing and Heat Transfer"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Nusselt Number and state its physical significance. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Nusselt Number** is defined as the ratio of convective heat transfer at a surface to the conductive heat transfer through a stagnant fluid layer of the same thickness:

$$Nu = \frac{h L}{k}$$

where $h$ is the convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $L$ is the characteristic length (m), and $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the fluid (W/m·K).

**Physical Significance:**
- $Nu = 1$ implies that **heat transfer is by pure conduction** (no convection).
- $Nu > 1$ implies that **convection enhances** the heat transfer beyond pure conduction.
- The larger the $Nu$, the more effective the convection. It serves as the **dimensionless temperature gradient** at the wall.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students often confuse $Nu$ (a dimensionless heat transfer coefficient) with $h$ (a dimensional one). Remember: $Nu$ is **dimensionless**, while $h$ has units of W/m²·K.

---

### Question 2: What is Prandtl Number? Give its value for air, water, and liquid metals. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Prandtl Number ($Pr$)** is defined as the ratio of momentum diffusivity ($\nu$) to thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$):

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu C_p}{k}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It compares the **relative growth of velocity and thermal boundary layers**.
- $Pr \approx 1$: Velocity and thermal boundary layers have **comparable thickness** (most gases).
- $Pr \ll 1$: Thermal boundary layer is much **thicker** than velocity boundary layer (liquid metals).
- $Pr \gg 1$: Velocity boundary layer is much **thicker** than thermal boundary layer (oils).

**Typical Values:**
| Fluid | $Pr$ |
|---|---|
| Air (at 300 K) | $\approx 0.71$ |
| Water (at 300 K) | $\approx 7.0$ |
| Liquid Metals (e.g., Mercury) | $\approx 0.01$ to $0.03$ |
| Engine Oil | $\approx 100$ to $1000$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions in Module 2 carry 14 marks with internal choice. Two independent alternatives are provided below. **Solve both as practice** to cover all expected sub-topics.

---

### 🔵 Question A (14 Marks) — `Forced Convection Dimensional Analysis` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the functional relationship between Nusselt Number, Reynolds Number, and Prandtl Number for forced convection over a flat plate using dimensional analysis.** **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: List the dependent and independent variables.**

The convective heat transfer coefficient $h$ depends on:

$$h = f(V, L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p)$$

- $n = 7$ variables
- Fundamental dimensions: $M, L, T, \theta$ → $m = 4$
- Number of dimensionless groups: $n - m = 7 - 4 = 3$

**Step 2: Form the first $\Pi$ group using $h$ with $V, L, \rho, C_p$ as repeating variables.**

$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot V^{a_1} \cdot L^{b_1} \cdot \rho^{c_1} \cdot C_p^{d_1}$$

Substituting dimensions $(M T^{-3} \theta^{-1})(L T^{-1})^{a_1}(L)^{b_1}(M L^{-3})^{c_1}(L^2 T^{-2} \theta^{-1})^{d_1} = M^0 L^0 T^0 \theta^0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M: \quad & 1 + c_1 = 0 \implies c_1 = -1 \\
\theta: \quad & -1 - d_1 = 0 \implies d_1 = -1 \\
T: \quad & -3 - a_1 - 2d_1 = 0 \implies a_1 = -1 \\
L: \quad & -3 + a_1 + b_1 - 3c_1 + 2d_1 = 0 \\
& -3 + (-1) + b_1 - 3(-1) + 2(-1) = 0 \\
& -3 - 1 + b_1 + 3 - 2 = 0 \\
& b_1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$\Pi_1 = \frac{h L^3}{\rho V C_p} \quad \text{(Stanton Number form)}$$

**Step 3: Form the second $\Pi$ group using $\mu$ with $V, L, \rho$ as repeating variables.**

$$\Pi_2 = \mu \cdot V^{a_2} \cdot L^{b_2} \cdot \rho^{c_2}$$

Dimensions: $(M L^{-1} T^{-1})(L T^{-1})^{a_2}(L)^{b_2}(M L^{-3})^{c_2} = M^0 L^0 T^0 \theta^0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M: \quad & 1 + c_2 = 0 \implies c_2 = -1 \\
T: \quad & -1 - a_2 = 0 \implies a_2 = -1 \\
L: \quad & -1 + a_2 + b_2 - 3c_2 = 0 \\
& -1 + (-1) + b_2 - 3(-1) = 0 \implies b_2 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\mu}{\rho V L} = \frac{1}{Re} \quad \text{(inverse of Reynolds Number)}$$

**Step 4: Form the third $\Pi$ group using $k$ with $V, L, \rho, C_p$ as repeating variables.**

$$\Pi_3 = k \cdot V^{a_3} \cdot L^{b_3} \cdot \rho^{c_3} \cdot C_p^{d_3}$$

Dimensions: $(M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1})(L T^{-1})^{a_3}(L)^{b_3}(M L^{-3})^{c_3}(L^2 T^{-2} \theta^{-1})^{d_3}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M: \quad & 1 + c_3 = 0 \implies c_3 = -1 \\
\theta: \quad & -1 - d_3 = 0 \implies d_3 = -1 \\
T: \quad & -3 - a_3 - 2d_3 = 0 \implies a_3 = -1 \\
L: \quad & 1 + a_3 + b_3 - 3c_3 + 2d_3 = 0 \\
& 1 - 1 + b_3 + 3 - 2 = 0 \implies b_3 = -1
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:
$$\Pi_3 = \frac{k}{\rho V L C_p} = \frac{1}{Pe} = \frac{1}{Re \cdot Pr}$$

**Step 5: Express the final functional relationship.**

From the three $\Pi$ groups, the Nusselt number (combining $\Pi_1$, $\Pi_2$, $\Pi_3$):

$$\frac{h L}{k} = \phi\!\left(\frac{\rho V L}{\mu}, \frac{\mu C_p}{k}\right)$$

$$
\boxed{Nu = \phi(Re, Pr)}
$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L}{\mu}$: Ratio of inertia to viscous forces.
- $Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k}$: Ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity.

---

**(b) For forced convection over a flat plate, air at 320 K flows with a free stream velocity of 5 m/s. The plate is 2 m long and 1 m wide. The average heat transfer coefficient is $h = 28$ W/m²·K. Calculate: (i) Reynolds Number, (ii) Nusselt Number, and (iii) the convective heat transfer rate if the surface temperature is 360 K and free stream temperature is 320 K. Take air properties: $\rho = 1.085$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.96 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.028$ W/m·K. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_\infty = 320$ K, $V = 5$ m/s, $L = 2$ m, $W = 1$ m
- $h = 28$ W/m²·K, $T_s = 360$ K
- $\rho = 1.085$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.96 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.028$ W/m·K

**(i) Reynolds Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{1.085 \times 5 \times 2}{1.96 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= \frac{10.85}{1.96 \times 10^{-5}} \\
&= 5.536 \times 10^{5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Since** $Re > 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is **turbulent** over the plate.

**(ii) Nusselt Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Nu &= \frac{h L}{k} = \frac{28 \times 2}{0.028} \\
&= \frac{56}{0.028} = 2000
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Convective Heat Transfer Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= h A (T_s - T_\infty) \\
&= 28 \times (2 \times 1) \times (360 - 320) \\
&= 28 \times 2 \times 40 \\
&= 2240 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{Re = 5.536 \times 10^{5}, \quad Nu = 2000, \quad Q = 2240 \text{ W}}
$$

---

### 🟢 Question B (14 Marks) — `Natural Convection & Transient Analysis` `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a) Using dimensional analysis, derive the dimensionless correlation between Nusselt number, Grashof number, and Prandtl number for natural convection on a vertical plate.** **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify variables.** The heat transfer coefficient $h$ for natural convection depends on:

$$h = f(L, \rho, \mu, k, C_p, \beta, g, \Delta T)$$

where $n = 9$ variables and $m = 4$ fundamental dimensions. So we get $n - m = 5$ dimensionless groups.

**Step 2: Form the first $\Pi$ group using $h$ with $L, \rho, \mu, k$ as repeating variables.**

$$\Pi_1 = h \cdot L^{a_1} \cdot \rho^{b_1} \cdot \mu^{c_1} \cdot k^{d_1}$$

Dimensions of $h = M T^{-3} \theta^{-1}$, $L = L$, $\rho = M L^{-3}$, $\mu = M L^{-1} T^{-1}$, $k = M L T^{-3} \theta^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M: \quad & 1 + b_1 + c_1 + d_1 = 0 \\
\theta: \quad & -1 - d_1 = 0 \implies d_1 = -1 \\
T: \quad & -3 - c_1 - 3d_1 = 0 \implies c_1 = 0 \\
L: \quad & a_1 - 3b_1 - c_1 + d_1 = 0 \implies a_1 = 3b_1 - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Choose $b_1 = 0$ → $c_1 = 0$, $d_1 = -1$, $a_1 = -1$:

$$\Pi_1 = \frac{h L}{k} = Nu$$

**Step 3: Form the second $\Pi$ group with $C_p$ and $k$:**

$$\Pi_2 = \frac{\mu C_p}{k} = Pr$$

**Step 4: Form the third $\Pi$ group with $g, \beta, \Delta T$:**

$$\Pi_3 = \beta \cdot g \cdot \Delta T \cdot L^{a_3} \cdot \rho^{b_3} \cdot \mu^{c_3} \cdot k^{d_3}$$

The standard result from eliminating dimensions:

$$\Pi_3 = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu^2} = Gr$$

**Step 5: Combine all groups to obtain the final correlation:**

$$
\boxed{Nu = \phi(Gr, Pr)}
$$

Common empirical form: $Nu = C \, (Gr \cdot Pr)^n = C \, Ra^n$

---

**(b) A steel ball of diameter 8 cm (thermal conductivity $k = 45$ W/m·K) is initially at a uniform temperature of 500 K. It is suddenly quenched in a large oil bath at 350 K with a convective heat transfer coefficient of 450 W/m²·K. Calculate: (i) the Biot number and check whether the lumped capacitance method is valid, (ii) the time required for the ball to cool to 400 K. Take density $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, $C_p = 500$ J/kg·K.** **(7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.08$ m, $L_c = D/6 = 0.0133$ m (volume/surface area for sphere)
- $k = 45$ W/m·K, $h = 450$ W/m²·K
- $T_i = 500$ K, $T_\infty = 350$ K, $T(t) = 400$ K
- $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, $C_p = 500$ J/kg·K

**(i) Biot Number:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Bi &= \frac{h L_c}{k} = \frac{450 \times 0.0133}{45} \\
&= \frac{6.0}{45} = 0.133
\end{aligned}
$$

**Check:** $Bi = 0.133 > 0.1$. **Lumped capacitance is borderline** — a more rigorous transient conduction analysis (e.g., one-term Heisler chart approach) should be used. However, if the problem requires us to proceed with lumped analysis (or as a first-order approximation for a KTU 14-mark question), we proceed as follows:

**Note for KTU marking**: If $Bi > 0.1$, students should **state that the lumped method is not strictly valid** but proceed if the question demands it.

**(ii) Time to cool to 400 K (using lumped capacitance approximation):**

For lumped analysis:

$$\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{h A}{\rho V C_p} t\right)$$

For a sphere: $\dfrac{hA}{\rho V C_p} = \dfrac{h}{\rho C_p (D/6)} = \dfrac{6h}{\rho C_p D}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{T(t) - T_\infty}{T_i - T_\infty} &= \frac{400 - 350}{500 - 350} = \frac{50}{150} = 0.3333 \\
\ln(0.3333) &= -\frac{6 h}{\rho C_p D} \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute the time constant:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{6h}{\rho C_p D} &= \frac{6 \times 450}{7800 \times 500 \times 0.08} = \frac{2700}{312000} = 8.654 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= -\frac{\ln(0.3333)}{8.654 \times 10^{-3}} = -\frac{(-1.0986)}{8.654 \times 10^{-3}} \\
t &= \frac{1.0986}{8.654 \times 10^{-3}} = 126.95 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{Bi = 0.133, \quad t \approx 127 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check $Bi$**: In transient problems, always compute $Bi$ FIRST and **explicitly state** whether the lumped capacitance method is valid ($Bi < 0.1$). Failure to do this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $L$ and $L_c$**: For non-planar geometries (sphere, cylinder, fin), use the **characteristic length** $L_c = V/A$, not the geometric dimension. For a sphere $L_c = D/6$; for an infinite cylinder $L_c = D/4$.
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency**: Convert cm to m and minutes to seconds BEFORE substituting. Many students lose 2 marks for unit errors.
> 4. **Skipping physical interpretation**: KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for **stating the physical significance** of each dimensionless number. Do not just write formulas.
> 5. **Mixing Reynolds and Grashof**: Remember — $Re$ is for **forced convection** (driven by external pump/fan), $Gr$ is for **natural convection** (driven by buoyancy).
> 6. **Forgetting the $\Delta T$ in $Gr$**: $Gr$ explicitly requires the **temperature difference** $\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$, not the absolute temperature.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Non-Dimensional Numbers in Heat Transfer**

### 🔹 Core Numbers (must memorize all 10)

- ✅ **Reynolds ($Re$)** = Inertia / Viscous = $\rho V L / \mu$. Determines flow regime.
- ✅ **Prandtl ($Pr$)** = Momentum / Thermal diffusion = $\mu C_p / k$. Property of the fluid.
- ✅ **Nusselt ($Nu$)** = Convection / Conduction = $h L / k$. The dependent variable.
- ✅ **Grashof ($Gr$)** = Buoyancy / Viscous = $g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$. Driver of natural convection.
- ✅ **Rayleigh ($Ra$)** = $Gr \cdot Pr$. Combined driver of natural convection.
- ✅ **Stanton ($St$)** = $h / (\rho V C_p)$. $St = Nu / (Re \cdot Pr)$.
- ✅ **Biot ($Bi$)** = $h L_c / k$. Governs lumped analysis ($Bi < 0.1$).
- ✅ **Fourier ($Fo$)** = $\alpha t / L^2$. Dimensionless time.
- ✅ **Peclet ($Pe$)** = $Re \cdot Pr = V L / \alpha$. Advection / Diffusion.
- ✅ **Lewis ($Le$)** = $\alpha / D_{AB}$. Thermal / Mass diffusion.

### 🔹 Must-Know Relationships

- ✅ $Nu = f(Re, Pr)$ → **Forced Convection** correlations
- ✅ $Nu = f(Gr, Pr) = f(Ra)$ → **Natural Convection** correlations
- ✅ $Pe = Re \cdot Pr$ and $Le = Sc / Pr$
- ✅ $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ (when buoyancy-driven)

### 🔹 Critical Threshold Values (frequently asked)

- ✅ Laminar/Turbulent: $Re = 2300$ (pipe), $Re = 5 \times 10^5$ (flat plate)
- ✅ Lumped capacity validity: $Bi < 0.1$
- ✅ Natural convection laminar/turbulent: $Ra \approx 10^9$

### 🔹 Typical Values of $Pr$ (must memorize)

- ✅ Liquid metals: $0.01 \le Pr \le 0.03$ (e.g., Mercury, NaK)
- ✅ Gases: $0.7 \le Pr \le 1.0$ (e.g., Air $\approx 0.71$)
- ✅ Water: $Pr \approx 7$
- ✅ Oils: $Pr \approx 100$ to $10^4$

### 🔹 Common KTU Application Areas

- ✅ Dittus-Boelter: $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^n$ (turbulent pipe flow)
- ✅ Sieder-Tate: $Nu = 0.027 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_s)^{0.14}$ (viscous correction)
- ✅ Churchill-Chu: $Nu$ correlation for natural convection on vertical plate
- ✅ Lumped Capacitance: $T(t) = T_\infty + (T_i - T_\infty) \exp(-Bi \cdot Fo)$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Heat Convection from Flat Plate, Cylinder, Sphere During Laminar and Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** External Heat Convection from Flat Plate, Cylinder, Sphere During Laminar and Turbulent Flow

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**External Forced Convection** is the mode of heat transfer that occurs when a fluid in *bulk motion* flows over an external surface whose temperature differs from the fluid's free-stream temperature $T_\infty$. The thermal boundary layer develops from the leading edge of the body and grows along the flow direction, governed by the simultaneous interplay of the **momentum (velocity) boundary layer** and the **thermal boundary layer**.

The governing dimensionless groups are:

$$Re_L = \frac{\rho \, u_\infty \, L}{\mu} \quad \text{(Reynolds Number - Inertia / Viscous forces)}$$

$$Pr = \frac{\mu \, c_p}{k} \quad \text{(Prandtl Number - Momentum / Thermal diffusivity)}$$

$$Nu_L = \frac{h \, L}{k} \quad \text{(Nusselt Number - Convection / Conduction)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Keyword Trigger:** Any question on "external convection" almost always requires the final answer in the form of the **Nusselt Number Correlation** of the form $Nu = C \cdot Re^m \cdot Pr^n$, from which the average heat transfer coefficient $h$ is back-calculated. Memorize the constants $C$, $m$, and $n$ for each geometry.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River Flowing Past a Boulder

Imagine a wide, slow river (the free-stream fluid) flowing past three different obstacles:

1. **Flat Plate (a fallen log lying parallel to flow):** Water glides smoothly along the top, but very close to the wood, friction slows the water down. This thin "slow zone" is the **velocity boundary layer**, and right next to the wood, heat (or temperature) only transfers via slow molecular conduction — this is the **thermal boundary layer**.
2. **Cylinder (a vertical pipe standing in the river):** The water must part and rejoin, creating both a *thin laminar layer* on the windward side and a *wake* with eddies (turbulence) on the leeward side.
3. **Sphere (a smooth boulder):** Similar to a cylinder but with no preferred axis — flow wraps around completely.

If the river is *very slow* (low $Re$), the layers remain **laminar** and orderly. If the river is *fast* (high $Re$), the layers break into chaotic eddies — this is the **turbulent regime**, where mixing is intense and heat transfer is much greater.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The transition from laminar to turbulent flow in external flow is governed by the **Critical Reynolds Number ($Re_{cr}$)**. Below $Re_{cr}$ the boundary layer is laminar; above $Re_{cr}$, it becomes turbulent. KTU frequently tests this threshold.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range/Value |
|---|---|---|
| Critical Reynolds Number (Flat Plate) | $Re_{x,cr}$ | $5 \times 10^5$ |
| Critical Reynolds Number (Cylinder/Sphere) | $Re_{D,cr}$ | $2 \times 10^5$ to $4 \times 10^5$ |
| Prandtl Number of Air | $Pr_{air}$ | $\approx 0.71$ |
| Prandtl Number of Water | $Pr_{water}$ | $\approx 7.0$ |
| Prandtl Number of Engine Oil | $Pr_{oil}$ | $\approx 1000$ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Boundary Layers — Hydrodynamic & Thermal

When a fluid at $T_\infty$ contacts a surface at $T_s$, two boundary layers form simultaneously:

- **Hydrodynamic (Velocity) Boundary Layer:** Region where fluid velocity drops from $u_\infty$ (free stream) to $0$ at the wall. Its thickness is $\delta$.
- **Thermal Boundary Layer:** Region where fluid temperature drops from $T_\infty$ to $T_s$ at the wall. Its thickness is $\delta_t$.

The ratio of these thicknesses is governed by the Prandtl number:

$$\frac{\delta}{\delta_t} \approx Pr^{1/3}$$

**Engineering Insight:**
- For **liquid metals** ($Pr \ll 1$, e.g., mercury $Pr \approx 0.015$): $\delta_t \gg \delta$ → heat diffuses much faster than momentum.
- For **oils** ($Pr \gg 1$): $\delta_t \ll \delta$ → thermal layer is very thin; heat transfer is poor.

## 2.2 Local vs. Average Heat Transfer Coefficient

- **Local coefficient** $h_x$: varies with position $x$ along the plate.
- **Average coefficient** $\bar{h}_L$: integrated over the entire length $L$.

$$\bar{h}_L = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L h_x \, dx$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### A. FLAT PLATE — External Flow

| Regime | Local Nusselt Number $Nu_x$ | Average Nusselt Number $\overline{Nu}_L$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** ($Re_x < 5 \times 10^5$) | $Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$ | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$ |
| **Turbulent** ($Re_x > 5 \times 10^5$) | $Nu_x = 0.0296 \, Re_x^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}$ | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}$ |
| **Mixed (transition at $x_c$)** | — | $\overline{Nu}_L = (0.664 \, Re_{x_c}^{0.5} - 0.037 Re_{x_c}^{0.8} + 0.037 Re_L^{0.8}) Pr^{1/3}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Position on Flat Plate:** $x_c = \dfrac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{u_\infty}$

### B. FLOW OVER A CYLINDER — External Cross-Flow

| Regime | Nusselt Number Correlation |
|---|---|
| **Hilpert Correlation** | $Nu_D = C \, Re_D^m \, Pr^{1/3}$ (C and m depend on $Re_D$ range) |
| **Churchill-Bernstein** (entire range) | $Nu_D = 0.3 + \dfrac{0.62 \, Re_D^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}}{\left[1 + (0.4/Pr)^{2/3}\right]^{1/4}} \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Re_D}{282000}\right)^{5/8}\right]^{4/5}$ |

**Hilpert Constants (Table for KTU):**

| $Re_D$ Range | $C$ | $m$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0.4 – 4 | 0.989 | 0.330 |
| 4 – 40 | 0.911 | 0.385 |
| 40 – 4000 | 0.683 | 0.466 |
| 4000 – 40000 | 0.193 | 0.618 |
| 40000 – 400000 | 0.027 | 0.805 |

### C. FLOW OVER A SPHERE

| Regime | Nusselt Number Correlation |
|---|---|
| **Ranz-Marshall** | $Nu_D = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_D^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$ |
| **Whitaker** (full range) | $Nu_D = 2 + (0.4 Re_D^{0.5} + 0.06 Re_D^{2/3}) Pr^{0.4} \left(\dfrac{\mu_\infty}{\mu_s}\right)^{1/4}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Special Note:** When $Re_D \to 0$ (creeping flow), the Nusselt number for a sphere reduces to **$Nu_D = 2$**. This is a famous KTU board question — always remember this limit.

## 2.4 Why These Correlations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Flat plates** — Solar collector absorbers, aircraft wings, printed circuit boards.
- **Cylinders** — Heat exchanger tubes, overhead power transmission cables, chimney stacks.
- **Spheres** — Nuclear fuel pellets, weather balloons, spherical storage tanks.

The Reynolds-number-based regime map allows engineers to **predict convective heat loss** without solving the full Navier-Stokes equations — a critical shortcut in design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Local Nusselt Number for Laminar Flow Over a Flat Plate

Starting from the **momentum integral equation** for a flat plate (von Kármán):

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho \, u_\infty^2} = \frac{d}{dx} \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{u_\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u}{u_\infty}\right) dy$$

**Step 1: Assume a cubic velocity profile** (Pohlhausen-type approximation):

$$\frac{u}{u_\infty} = \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{y}{\delta}\right)^3$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the integral** to obtain the momentum thickness $\theta$:

$$\theta = \int_0^\delta \frac{u}{u_\infty}\left(1 - \frac{u}{u_\infty}\right) dy = \frac{39}{280}\delta$$

**Step 3: Wall shear stress** from Newton's law of viscosity:

$$\tau_w = \mu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \frac{3}{2}\frac{\mu \, u_\infty}{\delta}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into momentum integral equation** and integrate with $\delta = 0$ at $x = 0$:

$$\delta(x) = \frac{5.0 \, x}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

**Step 5: Wall friction coefficient:**

$$C_f = \frac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2}\rho u_\infty^2} = \frac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}}$$

**Step 6: Use the Reynolds analogy (modified)** between heat and momentum transfer. The analogous heat transfer integral equation gives:

$$\frac{q_w''}{c_p \, \rho \, u_\infty (T_s - T_\infty)} = \frac{d}{dx} \int_0^{\delta_t} \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty} \left(1 - \frac{u}{u_\infty}\right) dy$$

**Step 7: Assuming analogous cubic profiles** for temperature, the analysis yields:

$$\delta_t(x) = \frac{\delta(x)}{Pr^{1/3}}$$

**Step 8: Wall heat flux:**

$$q_w'' = -k \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} = \frac{k (T_s - T_\infty)}{\delta_t}$$

**Step 9: Local convection coefficient:**

$$h_x = \frac{q_w''}{T_s - T_\infty} = \frac{k}{\delta_t} = \frac{k \, Pr^{1/3}}{5 x} \sqrt{Re_x}$$

**Step 10: Local Nusselt Number:**

$$\boxed{Nu_x = \frac{h_x \, x}{k} = \frac{1}{5}\sqrt{Re_x} \, Pr^{1/3} = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Note:** The "0.332" constant in the local laminar correlation appears via the exact solution of the boundary layer equations (Blasius solution). For the **average** over the length $L$, the average Nusselt number is exactly **twice the local value at $x = L$**, i.e., $\overline{Nu}_L = 2 \, Nu_{x=L} = 0.664 \, Re_L^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Application Roadmap

The following decision matrix is the **single most important workflow** for solving any KTU external convection problem:

| Step | Decision | Formula / Check |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify Geometry | Flat plate / Cylinder / Sphere? |
| 2 | Compute $Re$ | $Re_L = \rho u_\infty L / \mu$ |
| 3 | Compare with $Re_{cr}$ | $Re_{cr,plate} = 5 \times 10^5$; $Re_{cr,cyl} = 2 \times 10^5$ |
| 4 | Choose Regime | Laminar / Turbulent / Mixed |
| 5 | Select Correlation | Use the appropriate $Nu$ formula |
| 6 | Compute $\bar{h}$ | $\bar{h} = \overline{Nu}_L \cdot k / L$ |
| 7 | Compute Heat Transfer | $Q = \bar{h} A (T_s - T_\infty)$ |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Mixed Boundary Layer on Flat Plate)

**Problem:** Air at $T_\infty = 20°C$ and $u_\infty = 2 \, m/s$ flows over a flat plate of length $L = 2 \, m$. Find the average convection coefficient. Given: $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$, $k = 0.0258 \, W/m \cdot K$, $Pr = 0.71$.

**Step 1: Reynolds number at $x = L$**

$$Re_L = \frac{u_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{2 \times 2}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.667 \times 10^5$$

**Step 2: Since $Re_L < 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is fully laminar.**

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.664 \cdot (2.667 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \cdot (0.71)^{1/3}$$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.664 \cdot 516.4 \cdot 0.8934$$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 306.3$$

**Step 3: Compute $\bar{h}$**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{306.3 \times 0.0258}{2} = 3.95 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

## 3.4 Exhaustive Mixed Boundary Layer Derivation

Suppose $Re_L > Re_{cr}$ (transition occurs at $x_c$ before $L$). The average Nusselt number is obtained by integration:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L Nu_x \, dx = \frac{L}{k} \bar{h}$$

**Step 1: Split the integral into laminar and turbulent regions:**

$$\bar{h} L = \int_0^{x_c} h_{x,\text{lam}} dx + \int_{x_c}^{L} h_{x,\text{turb}} dx$$

**Step 2: Substitute the correlations:**

$$\bar{h} L = \int_0^{x_c} \frac{k}{x}(0.332 Re_x^{0.5} Pr^{1/3}) dx + \int_{x_c}^{L} \frac{k}{x}(0.0296 Re_x^{0.8} Pr^{1/3}) dx$$

**Step 3: Use $Re_x = u_\infty x / \nu$** so that $dx/x = d(Re_x)/Re_x$:

$$\bar{h} L = k Pr^{1/3} \left[ 0.332 \int_0^{Re_{cr}} Re_x^{-0.5} d(Re_x) + 0.0296 \int_{Re_{cr}}^{Re_L} Re_x^{-0.2} d(Re_x) \right]$$

**Step 4: Integrate:**

$$\bar{h} L = k Pr^{1/3} \left[ 0.664 \, Re_{cr}^{0.5} + 0.0296 \cdot \frac{Re_L^{0.8} - Re_{cr}^{0.8}}{0.8} \right]$$

**Step 5: Divide by $L$ and multiply by $L/k$:**

$$\overline{Nu}_L = Pr^{1/3} \left[ 0.664 \, Re_{cr}^{0.5} + 0.037 \left( Re_L^{0.8} - Re_{cr}^{0.8} \right) \right]$$

**Step 6: With $Re_{cr} = 5 \times 10^5$, this gives the standard textbook formula:**

$$\boxed{\overline{Nu}_L = (0.664 Re_{x_c}^{0.5} - 0.037 Re_{x_c}^{0.8} + 0.037 Re_L^{0.8}) Pr^{1/3}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the negative sign:** The expression $-0.037 Re_{x_c}^{0.8}$ is the *turbulent* contribution that would have been added from $x = 0$ but must be subtracted to *remove* the spurious turbulent integration over the laminar zone — a classic KTU board derivation step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start External Convection Problem] --> B[Identify Geometry]
    B --> B1[Flat Plate]
    B --> B2[Cylinder]

    B1 --> C1[Compute Re_x at x=L]
    B2 --> C2[Compute Re_D]
    B3 --> C3[Compute Re_D]

    C1 --> D1{Re_x vs 5x10^5}
    D1 -->|Below| E1[Laminar Flat Plate]
    D1 -->|Above| F1[Mixed or Turbulent Flat Plate]

    C2 --> D2{Re_D vs 2x10^5}
    D2 -->|Below| E2[Laminar Cylinder Hilpert Low Re]
    D2 -->|Above| F2[Turbulent Cylinder Hilpert High Re]

    C3 --> D3{Re_D vs 2x10^5}
    D3 -->|Below| E3[Laminar Sphere Ranz Marshall]
    D3 -->|Above| F3[Turbulent Sphere Whitaker]

    E1 --> G[Select Nu Correlation]
    F1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F2 --> G
    E3 --> G
    F3 --> G

    G --> H[Compute Nu]
    H --> I[Compute h = Nu k / L]
    I --> J[Compute Q = h A delta T]
    J --> K[Final Answer]
```

## 4.2 Boundary Layer Formation Schematic — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Stream Fluid T_infinity u_infinity] --> B[Leading Edge x=0]
    B --> C[Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer delta]
    B --> D[Thermal Boundary Layer delta_t]
    C --> E[Wall Shear Stress tau_w]
    D --> F[Wall Heat Flux q_w]
    E --> G[Local h_x increases with x]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Re_x reaches critical value}
    H -->|No| I[Laminar Regime]
    H -->|Yes| J[Turbulent Regime]
    I --> K[Average h computed by integration]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final Heat Transfer Q]
```

```mermaid

    Geo -->|Flat Plate| FP[Flat Plate Module]

    FP --> FP3[Transition at Re_cr 5x10^5]
    FP --> FP4[Mixed integration if applicable]

    Geo -->|Cylinder| CYL[Cylinder Module]
    CYL --> CYL1[Stagnation point at theta 0]
    CYL --> CYL2[Laminar separation possible]
    CYL --> CYL3[Hilpert or Churchill Bernstein]
    CYL --> CYL4[Use D as characteristic length]

    Geo -->|Sphere| SPH[Sphere Module]
    SPH --> SPH1[Symmetric flow field]

    SPH --> SPH4[Boundary layer wraps fully]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the critical Reynolds number for flow over a flat plate. What is its standard value?**

**Model Answer:**
The **critical Reynolds number** is the value of $Re_x$ at which the boundary layer over a flat plate transitions from laminar to turbulent flow. Its standard accepted value is:

$$Re_{x,cr} = \frac{\rho \, u_\infty \, x_c}{\mu} = 5 \times 10^5$$

Below this value, the boundary layer is **laminar**; above it, the flow becomes **turbulent** with a thin laminar sub-layer persisting close to the wall.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**State the Ranz-Marshall correlation for heat transfer from a sphere. What is its limiting value as $Re_D \to 0$?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Ranz-Marshall correlation** for forced convection over a sphere is:

$$Nu_D = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_D^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$$

**Limiting case:** As $Re_D \to 0$ (creeping flow regime), the second term vanishes, leaving:

$$\lim_{Re_D \to 0} Nu_D = 2$$

This limit corresponds to pure conduction through an infinite stagnant medium surrounding the sphere.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`

**Question A:** Air at $20°C$ and $5 \, m/s$ flows over a flat plate of length $3 \, m$. Determine:

**(a)** [7 Marks] Whether the flow is laminar, turbulent, or mixed over the plate.
**(b)** [7 Marks] The average convection coefficient and total heat transfer per unit width if the plate is maintained at $80°C$.

Given: For air at $20°C$: $\rho = 1.204 \, kg/m^3$, $\mu = 1.825 \times 10^{-5} \, N \cdot s/m^2$, $k = 0.0258 \, W/m \cdot K$, $Pr = 0.71$.

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1:** Compute the kinematic viscosity.

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{1.825 \times 10^{-5}}{1.204} = 1.516 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2/s$$

**Step 2:** Compute Reynolds number at the trailing edge.

$$Re_L = \frac{u_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{5 \times 3}{1.516 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.89 \times 10^5$$

**Step 3:** Compare with critical Reynolds number.

Since $Re_L = 9.89 \times 10^5 > Re_{cr} = 5 \times 10^5$, the boundary layer undergoes transition. This is a **mixed boundary layer** case.

**Step 4:** Find the critical (transition) location.

$$x_c = \frac{Re_{cr} \cdot \nu}{u_\infty} = \frac{5 \times 10^5 \times 1.516 \times 10^{-5}}{5} = 1.516 \, m$$

So the laminar region exists from $0$ to $1.516 \, m$, and the turbulent region from $1.516 \, m$ to $3 \, m$.

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1:** Apply the mixed boundary layer formula.

$$\overline{Nu}_L = (0.664 \, Re_{x_c}^{0.5} - 0.037 \, Re_{x_c}^{0.8} + 0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8}) Pr^{1/3}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the three terms individually.

$$0.664 \, Re_{x_c}^{0.5} = 0.664 \times (5 \times 10^5)^{0.5} = 0.664 \times 707.11 = 469.52$$

$$0.037 \, Re_{x_c}^{0.8} = 0.037 \times (5 \times 10^5)^{0.8} = 0.037 \times 35913.6 = 1328.80$$

$$0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8} = 0.037 \times (9.89 \times 10^5)^{0.8} = 0.037 \times 64681.5 = 2393.21$$

**Step 3:** Combine the terms inside the bracket.

$$469.52 - 1328.80 + 2393.21 = 1533.93$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $Pr^{1/3}$.

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 1533.93 \times (0.71)^{1/3} = 1533.93 \times 0.8934 = 1370.4$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the average heat transfer coefficient.

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{1370.4 \times 0.0258}{3} = 11.79 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**Step 6:** Calculate total heat transfer per unit width (assuming $W = 1 \, m$).

$$Q = \bar{h} \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 11.79 \times (3 \times 1) \times (80 - 20)$$

$$\boxed{Q = 2122 \, W/m}$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper]`

**Question B:** Engine oil at $60°C$ flows across a $5 \, cm$ diameter cylinder at a velocity of $2 \, m/s$. The cylinder surface is at $120°C$. Calculate:

**(a)** [7 Marks] The Reynolds number and identify the flow regime; compute the average convection coefficient.
**(b)** [7 Marks] The drag force on the cylinder per unit length and the heat transfer rate per unit length.

Given: $\rho = 864 \, kg/m^3$, $\mu = 0.0054 \, N \cdot s/m^2$, $k = 0.140 \, W/m \cdot K$, $Pr = 1080$.

#### Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1:** Compute the Reynolds number.

$$Re_D = \frac{\rho \, u_\infty \, D}{\mu} = \frac{864 \times 2 \times 0.05}{0.0054} = 16000$$

**Step 2:** Identify the flow regime. From the Hilpert table, for $Re_D$ between 4000 and 40000: $C = 0.193$, $m = 0.618$.

**Step 3:** Compute the Nusselt number using Hilpert correlation.

$$Nu_D = 0.193 \cdot (16000)^{0.618} \cdot (1080)^{1/3}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate each piece.

$$(16000)^{0.618} = e^{0.618 \times \ln(16000)} = e^{0.618 \times 9.68} = e^{5.98} = 396.7$$

$$(1080)^{1/3} = 10.26$$

$$Nu_D = 0.193 \times 396.7 \times 10.26 = 785.4$$

**Step 5:** Calculate average heat transfer coefficient.

$$\bar{h} = \frac{Nu_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{785.4 \times 0.140}{0.05} = 2199.1 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

#### Solution to Part (b):

**Step 6:** Heat transfer rate per unit length.

$$\dot{q} = \bar{h} \cdot (\pi D \cdot 1) \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) = 2199.1 \times \pi \times 0.05 \times (120 - 60)$$

$$\boxed{\dot{q} = 20,721 \, W/m}$$

**Step 7:** Drag coefficient. From the Hilpert table / drag data, for $Re_D = 16000$:

$$C_D \approx 0.85 \quad \text{(interpolated from standard drag curve)}$$

**Step 8:** Drag force per unit length.

$$F_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho u_\infty^2 \cdot D \cdot 1 = 0.85 \times 0.5 \times 864 \times 4 \times 0.05$$

$$\boxed{F_D = 73.4 \, N/m}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (verified from KTU answer key trends):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $Pr^{1/3}$ for cylinder/sphere**: The exponent on $Pr$ in **Hilpert** is $1/3$, but in **Whitaker** it is $0.4$. Confusing these costs 2 marks.
> 2. **Using $Re_x$ instead of $Re_L$**: Many students compute $h_x$ instead of $\bar{h}_L$ for an average coefficient question. Watch out for the wording "average" vs. "local."
> 3. **Wrong critical Reynolds number**: For a **flat plate**, $Re_{cr} = 5 \times 10^5$ based on $x$; for a **cylinder**, $Re_{cr} = 2 \times 10^5$ based on $D$. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 4. **Units mismatch in $\nu$**: Always report $\nu$ in $m^2/s$ before computing $Re$. Using $cm^2/s$ will silently corrupt the answer.
> 5. **Ignoring the viscous correction term $\left(\frac{\mu_\infty}{\mu_s}\right)^{1/4}$** in Whitaker: KTU has awarded full marks only when this term is included.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — External Heat Convection**

- **Flat Plate Critical Re:** $Re_{x,cr} = 5 \times 10^5$ (memorize this exact value).
- **Cylinder Critical Re:** $Re_{D,cr} \approx 2 \times 10^5$.
- **Sphere Limit:** $Nu_D \to 2$ as $Re_D \to 0$ (pure conduction limit).
- **Laminar Flat Plate — Local:** $Nu_x = 0.332 \, Re_x^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$.
- **Laminar Flat Plate — Average:** $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$ (exactly double the local at $L$).
- **Turbulent Flat Plate — Local:** $Nu_x = 0.0296 \, Re_x^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}$.
- **Turbulent Flat Plate — Average:** $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.037 \, Re_L^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3}$.
- **Mixed Boundary Layer (flat plate):** $\overline{Nu}_L = (0.664 Re_{x_c}^{0.5} - 0.037 Re_{x_c}^{0.8} + 0.037 Re_L^{0.8}) Pr^{1/3}$.
- **Cylinder — Hilpert:** $Nu_D = C \, Re_D^m \, Pr^{1/3}$ — memorize the $C$ and $m$ table.
- **Cylinder — Churchill-Bernstein:** Valid for entire $Re_D$ range; more accurate at high $Re$.
- **Sphere — Ranz-Marshall:** $Nu_D = 2 + 0.6 \, Re_D^{0.5} \, Pr^{1/3}$ (use for moderate $Re$).
- **Sphere — Whitaker:** Includes viscosity correction term $\left(\frac{\mu_\infty}{\mu_s}\right)^{1/4}$.
- **Boundary Layer Ratio:** $\delta / \delta_t \approx Pr^{1/3}$.
- **Characteristic Length:** $L$ for plate (along flow), $D$ for cylinder/sphere.
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Heat exchangers, aircraft wings, overhead conductors, fuel pellets.

> **End of KTU 2024 Scheme Notes — Module 2: External Convection**
