topics.title: Internal Heat Transfer in a Circular Pipe During Laminar and Turbulent Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internal Heat Transfer in a Circular Pipe During Laminar and Turbulent Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Internal heat transfer in a circular pipe** refers to the convective heat exchange that occurs when a fluid flows inside a closed conduit of circular cross-section, exchanging thermal energy with the pipe wall. This phenomenon is governed by the coupled action of **forced convection** (driven by an external pressure gradient or pump) and **conduction** through the fluid, and is classified into two principal regimes based on the **Reynolds number (Re)**: **Laminar flow** (Re < **2300**) and **Turbulent flow** (Re > **4000**).

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the analysis is broken down into two distinct physical regions along the pipe length:

- **Entry Region (Developing Region):** The zone near the pipe inlet where both the **velocity boundary layer** and the **thermal boundary layer** are progressively growing from the wall toward the centerline.
- **Fully Developed Region:** The zone downstream where the boundary layers have merged at the pipe centerline. Here, the dimensionless velocity and temperature profiles become invariant in the streamwise direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A critical distinction must be made between **Hydrodynamic Entry Length ($L_h$)** and **Thermal Entry Length ($L_t$)**. They are NOT equal — their ratio depends entirely on the **Prandtl number (Pr)** of the fluid.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Intuition

Imagine you turn on a **cold garden hose** that has been lying in the hot afternoon sun. The water entering the hose is cold, but as it travels down the pipe, it gradually warms up because heat conducts inward from the hot pipe wall.

- **Near the inlet:** Only a thin outer layer of water is warm; the core is still cold. This thin warm layer is the **thermal boundary layer** growing inward.
- **Farther down the pipe:** The warm layer thickens until it reaches the center — the entire water cross-section is now warm. This is the **fully developed thermal region**.
- **Laminar vs. Turbulent:** If the water flows slowly (smooth, layered), the warm layer grows slowly, and mixing is weak. If the water flows fast (chaotic, swirling eddies), the warm layer grows rapidly, and the entire cross-section becomes warm almost immediately.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Reynolds Number as a "Chaos Meter":**
> - **Re < 2300** → Laminar (smooth, orderly, predictable)
> - **2300 < Re < 4000** → Transitional (unstable, avoid in design)
> - **Re > 4000** → Turbulent (chaotic, excellent mixing)

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value/Range | SI Unit |
|--------|----------|--------------------|---------|
| **Re** | Reynolds Number | < 2300 (Laminar), > 4000 (Turbulent) | Dimensionless |
| **Pr** | Prandtl Number | Water ≈ **7**, Air ≈ **0.7**, Oils ≈ **100–10000** | Dimensionless |
| **Nu** | Nusselt Number | Laminar fully dev: **3.66 – 4.36**; Turbulent: **10 – 10⁵** | Dimensionless |
| **f** | Darcy Friction Factor | Laminar: 64/Re; Turbulent: from Moody chart | Dimensionless |
| **Gz** | Graetz Number | $Gz = Re \cdot Pr \cdot (D/L)$ | Dimensionless |
| **D** | Pipe Inner Diameter | Typically **0.01 – 0.5 m** | m |
| **L** | Pipe Length | Typically **1 – 100 m** | m |

---

## 1.4 Two Critical Boundary Conditions

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the ability to distinguish between:

1. **Constant Wall Heat Flux ($q_w''$ = constant):** Typical of electric resistance heaters, nuclear reactor fuel rods, or condenser tubes. The heat flux is uniform along the wall.
2. **Constant Wall Temperature ($T_w$ = constant):** Typical of heat exchangers where one fluid is phase-changing (boiling/condensing) or where temperature-controlled heating is used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the distinction matters:** The fully developed Nusselt numbers differ significantly:
> - $Nu_D = 3.66$ for constant $T_w$ (laminar)
> - $Nu_D = 4.36$ for constant $q_w''$ (laminar)
> These are benchmark values you MUST memorize for the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reynolds Number — Foundation of Flow Classification

The **Reynolds number** quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces within a fluid:

$$Re_D = \frac{\rho \cdot u_m \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{u_m \cdot D}{\nu}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density $[\text{kg/m}^3]$
- $u_m$ = mean (bulk) velocity $[\text{m/s}]$
- $D$ = pipe inner diameter $[\text{m}]$
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity $[\text{kg/(m·s)}]$
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$

**Physical interpretation:**
- High Re → Inertia dominates → Fluid particles travel in irregular paths → **Turbulent**
- Low Re → Viscous forces dominate → Fluid particles travel in parallel layers → **Laminar**

---

## 2.2 The Prandtl Number — The "Fluid Personality" Parameter

$$\Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{c_p \cdot \mu}{k}$$

where:
- $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ = thermal diffusivity $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$
- $c_p$ = specific heat at constant pressure $[\text{J/(kg·K)}]$
- $k$ = thermal conductivity $[\text{W/(m·K)}]$

**Physical interpretation:**
- **Pr << 1** (e.g., liquid metals): Heat diffuses much faster than momentum → Thermal boundary layer is **much thicker** than velocity boundary layer.
- **Pr >> 1** (e.g., oils): Momentum diffuses faster than heat → Velocity boundary layer is **thicker** than thermal boundary layer.
- **Pr ≈ 1** (e.g., gases): Both boundary layers grow at similar rates.

---

## 2.3 Entry Length Correlations

### 2.3.1 Hydrodynamic Entry Length (Laminar)

The hydrodynamic entry length is where the velocity boundary layer transitions from its inlet profile to the fully developed parabolic profile:

$$\frac{L_h}{D} = 0.05 \cdot Re_D$$

**Validity:** Laminar flow only.

### 2.3.2 Thermal Entry Length (Laminar)

The thermal entry length depends strongly on Pr:

$$\frac{L_t}{D} = \begin{cases} 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot \Pr & \text{for laminar flow} \\[4pt] 10 & \text{for turbulent flow (approximate)} \end{cases}$$

### 2.3.3 Graetz Number — The Convective Heat Transfer Master Variable

The **Graetz number** combines Re, Pr, and the length-to-diameter ratio into a single dimensionless parameter that characterizes the relative importance of convection to conduction in the streamwise direction:

$$Gz_D = Re_D \cdot \Pr \cdot \frac{D}{L} = \frac{u_m \cdot D^2}{\alpha \cdot L}$$

- **Large Gz** → Short pipe or high flow → Heat transfer dominated by convection.
- **Small Gz** → Long pipe or low flow → Heat transfer approaches pure conduction.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Flow Regime | Boundary Condition | Governing Equation | Nusselt Number |
|---|------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Laminar, Fully Developed** | Constant $T_w$ | $\frac{1}{u_m} \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} = \alpha \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right)$ | $Nu_D = 3.66$ |
| 2 | **Laminar, Fully Developed** | Constant $q_w''$ | Same as above with $\partial T_w/\partial x$ = const | $Nu_D = 4.36$ |
| 3 | **Laminar, Entry Region** | Both | Hausen / Shah correlations | $Nu_D = 1.86 \left(\frac{Re \cdot Pr \cdot D}{L}\right)^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}$ |
| 4 | **Turbulent, Fully Developed** | Both | Dittus-Boelter | $Nu_D = 0.023 \cdot Re_D^{0.8} \cdot Pr^n$ |
| 5 | **Turbulent, Fully Developed** | Both | Sieder-Tate | $Nu_D = 0.027 \cdot Re_D^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu_b}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}$ |
| 6 | **Turbulent, Fully Developed** | Both | Gnielinski (more accurate) | $Nu_D = \frac{(f/8)(Re - 1000) Pr}{1 + 12.7(f/8)^{0.5}(Pr^{2/3} - 1)}$ |

**Where:**
- $n = 0.4$ when fluid is being **heated** ($T_w > T_b$)
- $n = 0.3$ when fluid is being **cooled** ($T_w < T_b$)
- $f$ = Darcy friction factor
- $\mu_b$ = viscosity at bulk mean temperature
- $\mu_w$ = viscosity at wall temperature

### 2.4.1 Friction Factor Equations

| Flow Regime | Friction Factor Equation |
|-------------|--------------------------|
| **Laminar** | $f = \frac{64}{Re_D}$ |
| **Turbulent (smooth pipe, Petukhov)** | $f = (0.790 \ln Re_D - 1.64)^{-2}$ |
| **Turbulent (Colebrook, rough)** | $\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re_D \sqrt{f}}\right)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The Sieder-Tate and Gnielinski correlations are the **gold standard** for turbulent internal flow in the KTU 2024 Scheme. Dittus-Boelter is acceptable for quick estimates but is less accurate over wide Pr ranges.

---

## 2.5 The Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) — Driving Force for Heat Transfer

For a pipe with fluid entering at $T_{m,i}$ and leaving at $T_{m,o}$, with wall temperature varying from $T_{w,i}$ to $T_{w,o}$:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{(T_{w,o} - T_{m,o}) - (T_{w,i} - T_{m,i})}{\ln\left(\frac{T_{w,o} - T_{m,o}}{T_{w,i} - T_{m,i}}\right)}$$

The total heat transfer rate is then:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{m,o} - T_{m,i}) = U \cdot A_s \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

where $U$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient and $A_s = \pi D L$ is the pipe's inner surface area.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Flow Regime | Why Internal Heat Transfer Matters |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Power plant condensers** | Turbulent water | Steam condenses on outer surface; turbulent water inside efficiently removes heat |
| **Nuclear reactor fuel channels** | Turbulent (high Pr) | Constant heat flux from fuel rods requires accurate $Nu$ prediction |
| **Chemical reactor cooling coils** | Laminar (oils) | High-Pr fluids in small tubes are routinely laminar |
| **HVAC chilled water piping** | Turbulent | Energy-efficient design requires precise pressure drop AND heat loss calculations |
| **Solar thermal collectors** | Laminar to transitional | Low flow rates + small diameters often yield laminar regime |
| **Oil pipelines in cold regions** | Turbulent (high Pr) | Heat loss to environment affects viscosity and pumping cost |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Design Rule of Thumb:** Designers always aim for **turbulent flow** (Re > 10,000) when heat transfer is the primary goal, because $h$ increases roughly as $Re^{0.8}$ — a 10× increase in Re yields nearly a 6× increase in $h$.

# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Fully Developed Laminar Nusselt Number (Constant Wall Heat Flux)

This is the **most-tested derivation** in KTU exams for this module. We derive $Nu_D = 4.36$.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — The Energy Equation

For a Newtonian fluid in steady, incompressible, fully developed laminar flow with constant properties and axial conduction neglected:

$$u(r) \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right)$$

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Substitute the Velocity Profile

For fully developed laminar flow in a circular pipe, the velocity profile is **Hagen-Poiseuille** parabolic:

$$u(r) = 2 u_m \left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]$$

Substituting into the energy equation:

$$2 u_m \left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right] \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right)$$

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Apply Fully Developed Thermal Condition

For **constant wall heat flux** in the fully developed region, the temperature profile shape is **invariant** with x. This means we can write:

$$T(r,x) = T_w(x) - \theta(r)$$

where $\theta(r)$ is a function of r only. Differentiating with respect to x:

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} = \frac{dT_w}{dx} = \text{constant}$$

The wall temperature must increase linearly along the pipe to maintain constant $q_w''$.

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Substitute the Simplified Forms

Substituting the simplified form of $T$ into the energy equation:

$$2 u_m \left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right] \frac{dT_w}{dx} = \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{d\theta}{dr}\right)$$

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Define a Non-Dimensional Radius

Let $\eta = r/R$, so $dr = R \, d\eta$ and $r = R\eta$. Then:

$$2 u_m (1 - \eta^2) \frac{dT_w}{dx} = \frac{\alpha}{R^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\eta} \frac{d}{d\eta}\left(\eta \frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{1}{\eta} \frac{d}{d\eta}\left(\eta \frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\right) = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} (1 - \eta^2) \frac{dT_w}{dx}$$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Integrate Twice

**First integration:**

$$\eta \frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \int \eta(1 - \eta^2) \, d\eta = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \left(\frac{\eta^2}{2} - \frac{\eta^4}{4}\right) + C_1$$

By symmetry at the centerline ($r=0$, i.e., $\eta = 0$), $\frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = 0$, so $C_1 = 0$.

$$\frac{d\theta}{d\eta} = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \left(\frac{\eta}{2} - \frac{\eta^3}{4}\right)$$

**Second integration:**

$$\theta(\eta) = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \int \left(\frac{\eta}{2} - \frac{\eta^3}{4}\right) d\eta = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \left(\frac{\eta^2}{4} - \frac{\eta^4}{16}\right) + C_2$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Apply Wall Boundary Condition

At the wall, $r = R$ (i.e., $\eta = 1$), $\theta = 0$ (since $T = T_w$ at the wall):

$$0 = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \left(\frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{16}\right) + C_2 = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \cdot \frac{3}{16} + C_2$$

Therefore:

$$C_2 = -\frac{3 u_m R^2}{8 \alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx}$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Final Temperature Profile

$$\theta(\eta) = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \left(\frac{\eta^2}{4} - \frac{\eta^4}{16} - \frac{3}{16}\right)$$

Or equivalently:

$$\theta(\eta) = \frac{u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \left(\frac{\eta^2}{2} - \frac{\eta^4}{4} - \frac{3}{8}\right)$$

### 3.1.9 Step 8 — Heat Flux at the Wall

By Fourier's law at the wall:

$$q_w'' = -k \left.\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right|_{r=R} = -k \left.\frac{d\theta}{dr}\right|_{r=R}$$

Computing $\frac{d\theta}{dr}$ at $r = R$ from the $\eta$-derivative:

$$\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\bigg|_{\eta=1} = \frac{2 u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx} \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{4}\right) = \frac{u_m R^2}{2\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx}$$

Converting back to r-derivative using $\frac{d\theta}{dr} = \frac{1}{R}\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}$:

$$q_w'' = -k \cdot \frac{1}{R} \cdot \frac{u_m R^2}{2\alpha} \cdot \frac{dT_w}{dx} = -\frac{k u_m R}{2\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx}$$

### 3.1.10 Step 9 — Bulk Mean Temperature Derivative

Energy balance over a control volume gives:

$$\dot{m} c_p \frac{dT_m}{dx} = q_w'' \cdot \pi D = q_w'' \cdot 2\pi R$$

For constant $q_w''$, integrating: $T_m(x) = T_{m,i} + \frac{q_w'' \pi D x}{\dot{m} c_p}$. Note that for fully developed flow with constant $q_w''$, $T_w$ and $T_m$ both increase linearly at the **same rate**:

$$\frac{d(T_w - T_m)}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{dT_w}{dx} = \frac{dT_m}{dx}$$

### 3.1.11 Step 10 — Define the Convective Coefficient

$$h = \frac{q_w''}{T_w - T_m}$$

$$h = \frac{-\frac{k u_m R}{2\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx}}{T_w - T_m}$$

### 3.1.12 Step 11 — Compute the Nusselt Number

$$Nu_D = \frac{h D}{k} = \frac{h (2R)}{k}$$

Substituting $h$:

$$Nu_D = \frac{2R}{k} \cdot \frac{-\frac{k u_m R}{2\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx}}{T_w - T_m} = \frac{-2 u_m R^2}{\alpha (T_w - T_m)} \frac{dT_w}{dx}$$

But since $\frac{dT_w}{dx} = \frac{dT_m}{dx}$ and the difference $(T_w - T_m)$ is constant in the fully developed region, we can use the mean temperature difference. The wall-to-mean difference can be computed from the profile:

$$T_w - T_m = \frac{2}{u_m R^2} \int_0^R u(r) \cdot \theta(r) \cdot r \, dr$$

Performing this integration (a long but standard calculation):

$$T_w - T_m = \frac{11}{48} \frac{u_m R^2}{\alpha} \frac{dT_w}{dx}$$

### 3.1.13 Step 12 — Final Result

$$Nu_D = \frac{-2 u_m R^2 / \alpha}{\frac{11}{48} u_m R^2 / \alpha} = \frac{2}{\frac{11}{48}} = \frac{96}{11} \approx 4.36$$

$$\boxed{Nu_D = 4.36 \quad \text{(Constant Wall Heat Flux, Fully Developed Laminar)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Similar Derivation Outline: Constant Wall Temperature (Result Nu = 3.66)

For constant $T_w$, the wall temperature is fixed, so $dT_w/dx = 0$. However, the bulk temperature $T_m$ changes along x, and the fully developed condition becomes:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left[\frac{T_w - T(r,x)}{T_w - T_m(x)}\right] = 0$$

Following the same integration procedure, the result is:

$$\boxed{Nu_D = 3.66 \quad \text{(Constant Wall Temperature, Fully Developed Laminar)}}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Laminar (Re < 2300) | Turbulent (Re > 4000) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| **Velocity profile** | Parabolic (Hagen-Poiseuille) | Nearly flat (power-law approx) |
| **Nusselt Number (FD, $T_w$ const)** | **3.66** | **Gnielinski/Sieder-Tate** |
| **Friction factor** | $f = 64/Re$ | Petukhov / Colebrook |
| **Entry length** | $L_h = 0.05 Re D$; $L_t = 0.05 Re Pr D$ | $L_h \approx 10D$; $L_t \approx 10D$ |
| **Mixing** | Molecular only | Molecular + turbulent eddies |
| **Wall shear stress** | $\tau_w = 8 \mu u_m / D$ | $\tau_w = f \rho u_m^2 / 8$ |
| **Pressure drop** | Linear in velocity | Nearly linear in velocity squared |
| **Pr effect** | Strong | Mild |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Determining Nusselt Number in a Heated Pipe

**Problem:** Water flows through a pipe of diameter $D = 0.05$ m at $u_m = 1.5$ m/s. The water is at $T_m = 50°C$ and the wall is at $T_w = 70°C$ (constant). Compute $h$ and $Nu_D$.

**Properties of water at 50°C:**
- $\rho = 988$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 0.547 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/(m·s)
- $k = 0.645$ W/(m·K)
- $c_p = 4181$ J/(kg·K)
- $Pr = 3.55$

**Step 1 — Reynolds number:**

$$Re_D = \frac{988 \times 1.5 \times 0.05}{0.547 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{74.1}{0.000547} \approx 1.355 \times 10^5$$

Since $Re_D = 135,500 \gg 4000$, flow is **turbulent**.

**Step 2 — Friction factor (Petukhov):**

$$f = (0.790 \ln(135500) - 1.64)^{-2}$$

$$= (0.790 \times 11.818 - 1.64)^{-2} = (9.336 - 1.64)^{-2} = (7.696)^{-2} = 0.01688$$

**Step 3 — Gnielinski correlation:**

$$Nu_D = \frac{(f/8)(Re - 1000) Pr}{1 + 12.7 (f/8)^{0.5}(Pr^{2/3} - 1)}$$

Computing intermediate values:
- $f/8 = 0.01688/8 = 0.00211$
- $Re - 1000 = 134500$
- $Pr^{2/3} = 3.55^{0.667} = 2.314$
- $Pr^{2/3} - 1 = 1.314$
- $(f/8)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.00211} = 0.04594$

**Numerator:** $0.00211 \times 134500 \times 3.55 = 1007.6$

**Denominator:** $1 + 12.7 \times 0.04594 \times 1.314 = 1 + 0.7667 = 1.7667$

$$Nu_D = \frac{1007.6}{1.7667} = 570.4$$

**Step 4 — Convective coefficient:**

$$h = \frac{Nu_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{570.4 \times 0.645}{0.05} = \frac{367.9}{0.05} = 7358 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

$$\boxed{Nu_D = 570.4, \quad h = 7358 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary of All Key Nusselt Correlations

| Correlation | Application Range | Accuracy |
|-------------|------------------|----------|
| $Nu = 3.66$ | Laminar FD, $T_w$ = const | Exact |
| $Nu = 4.36$ | Laminar FD, $q_w''$ = const | Exact |
| Hausen: $Nu = 3.66 + \frac{0.0668 (D/L) Re Pr}{1 + 0.04[(D/L) Re Pr]^{2/3}}$ | Laminar entry region, $T_w$ = const | ±5% |
| Shah: $Nu = 1.302 (Gz)^{-1/3} - 1$ (for $Gz < 33.3$) | Laminar entry, $q_w''$ = const | ±5% |
| Dittus-Boelter: $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^n$ | Turbulent, $0.7 < Pr < 160$, $Re > 10,000$ | ±25% |
| Sieder-Tate: $Nu = 0.027 Re^{0.8} Pr^{1/3} (\mu_b/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | Turbulent, viscosity correction | ±25% |
| Gnielinski | Turbulent, $0.5 < Pr < 2000$, $2300 < Re < 5 \times 10^6$ | ±20% |

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between hydrodynamic entry length and thermal entry length for laminar flow in a circular pipe. State the correlations used to compute them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hydrodynamic Entry Length ($L_h$) | Thermal Entry Length ($L_t$) |
|--------|-----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Length required for velocity boundary layer to become fully developed | Length required for thermal boundary layer to become fully developed |
| **Laminar correlation** | $L_h/D = 0.05 \cdot Re_D$ | $L_t/D = 0.05 \cdot Re_D \cdot Pr$ |
| **Dependence** | On Re only | On Re and Pr |
| **Physical meaning** | Region where viscous effects propagate from walls to centerline | Region where thermal effects propagate from walls to centerline |
| **Typical value (water, Re=1000)** | $L_h = 50D$ | $L_t = 50D \times 7 = 350D$ (much longer) |

**Key Insight:** For Pr >> 1 (oils), $L_t \gg L_h$. For Pr << 1 (liquid metals), $L_t \ll L_h$. For gases (Pr ≈ 1), they are approximately equal.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain why the fully developed Nusselt number for constant heat flux ($Nu = 4.36$) is greater than for constant wall temperature ($Nu = 3.66$) in laminar pipe flow.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The difference arises from the **shape of the dimensionless temperature profile** $\theta^* = (T_w - T)/(T_w - T_m)$ in the two cases:

- **Constant $T_w$:** The wall temperature is fixed, but the bulk temperature rises along the pipe. The driving temperature difference $(T_w - T_m)$ is **largest at the inlet** and decreases. To maintain energy balance, the **temperature gradient at the wall must be larger near the inlet**, but the fully developed profile has a moderate gradient. This yields a **lower average $h$** and thus $Nu = 3.66$.

- **Constant $q_w''$:** The wall heat flux is fixed, so the wall temperature gradient is **fixed by Fourier's law**. The temperature profile is more "uniform" with stronger gradients in the core, yielding a **higher average $h$** and thus $Nu = 4.36$.

**Quantitative reasoning:** The wall-to-bulk temperature difference for constant $q_w''$ is $\Delta T = 11q_w''D/(48 k)$, while for constant $T_w$ it is approximately $\Delta T = 4.36 q_w''D/k$ (effectively a smaller gradient integrated across the cross-section). The ratio $4.36/3.66 = 1.19$ reflects the inherent physics of the two boundary conditions.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### 📌 **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**Question A:** 
**(a)** Derive the expression for the fully developed Nusselt number in laminar flow through a circular pipe with **constant wall heat flux**. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Engine oil at $T_{m,i} = 60°C$ enters a 5-m long circular tube of inner diameter $D = 25$ mm with a mean velocity of $u_m = 0.05$ m/s. The tube wall is maintained at a constant temperature of $T_w = 100°C$. Determine:
  - (i) The Reynolds and Prandtl numbers
  - (ii) The Nusselt number using a suitable correlation
  - (iii) The outlet bulk mean temperature of the oil
  - (iv) The total heat transfer rate

**Engine oil properties at 60°C:** $\rho = 870$ kg/m³, $c_p = 2130$ J/(kg·K), $\mu = 0.0874$ kg/(m·s), $k = 0.143$ W/(m·K), $Pr = 1300$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Assumptions:** (Write these down first for full marks)
1. Steady, incompressible flow
2. Constant fluid properties
3. Negligible viscous dissipation
4. Negligible axial conduction
5. Fully developed flow (Hagen-Poiseuille profile)
6. Constant wall heat flux
7. Newtonian fluid

**Derivation steps (as shown in Section 3.1):**

1. Start with energy equation:

$$u(r) \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} = \alpha \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r \frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right)$$

2. Apply velocity profile: $u(r) = 2u_m[1 - (r/R)^2]$

3. For constant $q_w''$ in FD region: $\frac{\partial T}{\partial x} = \frac{dT_w}{dx}$ = constant

4. Let $\eta = r/R$, integrate twice, apply BCs: $\frac{d\theta}{d\eta}\vert_{\eta=0}=0$ and $\theta(\eta=1)=0$

5. Derive wall gradient: $\frac{d\theta}{dr}\vert_{r=R} = -\frac{u_m R}{2\alpha}\frac{dT_w}{dx}$

6. Compute bulk mean temperature: $T_w - T_m = \frac{11 u_m R^2}{48 \alpha}\frac{dT_w}{dx}$

7. Combine: $h = \frac{q_w''}{T_w - T_m} = \frac{24k}{11D}$

8. Therefore:

$$\boxed{Nu_D = \frac{hD}{k} = \frac{24}{11} = 4.36}$$

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $D = 0.025$ m, $L = 5$ m, $u_m = 0.05$ m/s, $T_{m,i} = 60°C$, $T_w = 100°C$ (constant), oil properties as listed.

**Step (i) — Re and Pr:**

$$Re_D = \frac{\rho u_m D}{\mu} = \frac{870 \times 0.05 \times 0.025}{0.0874} = \frac{1.0875}{0.0874} = 12.44$$

Since $Re_D = 12.44 \ll 2300$, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

$$Pr = 1300 \text{ (given)}$$

**Step (ii) — Nusselt Number:**

Check entry length:

$$L_t = 0.05 \cdot Re \cdot Pr \cdot D = 0.05 \times 12.44 \times 1300 \times 0.025 = 20.22 \text{ m}$$

Since $L = 5$ m $< L_t = 20.22$ m, the flow is in the **thermal entry region**!

We use the **Hausen correlation** for laminar entry region, constant $T_w$:

$$Nu_D = 3.66 + \frac{0.0668 \cdot (D/L) \cdot Re \cdot Pr}{1 + 0.04 \cdot [(D/L) \cdot Re \cdot Pr]^{2/3}}$$

Compute the Graetz number:

$$Gz_D = Re \cdot Pr \cdot \frac{D}{L} = 12.44 \times 1300 \times \frac{0.025}{5} = 12.44 \times 1300 \times 0.005 = 80.86$$

Compute numerator and denominator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.0668 \times 80.86 = 5.401$$

$$[(D/L) \cdot Re \cdot Pr]^{2/3} = 80.86^{2/3} = 18.74$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 1 + 0.04 \times 18.74 = 1 + 0.7496 = 1.7496$$

$$Nu_D = 3.66 + \frac{5.401}{1.7496} = 3.66 + 3.087 = 6.75$$

$$\boxed{Nu_D \approx 6.75}$$

**Step (iii) — Outlet Bulk Mean Temperature:**

First compute $h$:

$$h = \frac{Nu_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{6.75 \times 0.143}{0.025} = \frac{0.965}{0.025} = 38.6 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

For constant wall temperature, use the exponential temperature profile:

$$\frac{T_w - T_{m,o}}{T_w - T_{m,i}} = \exp\left(-\frac{\pi D L h}{\dot{m} c_p}\right)$$

Compute mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho u_m A_c = 870 \times 0.05 \times \frac{\pi (0.025)^2}{4} = 870 \times 0.05 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} = 0.02136 \text{ kg/s}$$

Compute exponent:

$$\frac{\pi D L h}{\dot{m} c_p} = \frac{\pi \times 0.025 \times 5 \times 38.6}{0.02136 \times 2130} = \frac{15.156}{45.50} = 0.3331$$

$$\frac{100 - T_{m,o}}{100 - 60} = e^{-0.3331} = 0.7166$$

$$100 - T_{m,o} = 0.7166 \times 40 = 28.66$$

$$\boxed{T_{m,o} = 71.34°C}$$

**Step (iv) — Total Heat Transfer Rate:**

$$Q = \dot{m} c_p (T_{m,o} - T_{m,i}) = 0.02136 \times 2130 \times (71.34 - 60)$$

$$Q = 0.02136 \times 2130 \times 11.34 = 516.0 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 516 \text{ W}}$$

---

### 📌 **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question B:** 
**(a)** Explain the physical significance of the Prandtl number. How does it affect the relative growth of velocity and thermal boundary layers? Provide examples for liquid metals, gases, and oils. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Water at $T_{m,i} = 20°C$ flows through a smooth circular tube of diameter $D = 50$ mm at a mass flow rate of $\dot{m} = 0.5$ kg/s. The tube wall is subjected to a **constant heat flux** of $q_w'' = 5000$ W/m². Determine:
  - (i) The Reynolds number
  - (ii) The Nusselt number using Dittus-Boelter and Gnielinski correlations
  - (iii) The length of tube required to heat the water to $T_{m,o} = 50°C$
  - (iv) The wall temperature at the outlet

**Water properties at 35°C (mean):** $\rho = 994$ kg/m³, $c_p = 4178$ J/(kg·K), $\mu = 0.725 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/(m·s), $k = 0.625$ W/(m·K), $Pr = 4.83$ **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### **Part (a) — Prandtl Number Explanation (7 Marks)**

**Definition:**

$$\Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{c_p \mu}{k} = \frac{\text{momentum diffusivity}}{\text{thermal diffusivity}}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- It measures the **relative effectiveness of momentum and heat transport** by diffusion in the fluid.
- When $\Pr = 1$, momentum and heat diffuse at the same rate.
- When $\Pr > 1$, heat diffuses **more slowly** than momentum (momentum BL is thicker).
- When $\Pr < 1$, heat diffuses **more rapidly** than momentum (thermal BL is thicker).

**Effects on Boundary Layers:**

| Fluid Type | Pr Value | $\delta_T / \delta$ | Physical Explanation |
|------------|----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Liquid metals** (Mercury, Sodium) | $0.001 < \Pr < 0.1$ | $\delta_T \gg \delta$ | Heat spreads much faster than momentum; thermal BL is very thick |
| **Gases** (Air, N₂, CO₂) | $0.7 < \Pr < 1.0$ | $\delta_T \approx \delta$ | Heat and momentum diffuse at similar rates |
| **Oils** (Engine oil, transformer oil) | $100 < \Pr < 10,000$ | $\delta_T \ll \delta$ | Heat diffuses very slowly; thermal BL is very thin |

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Liquid metal cooled nuclear reactor:** Pr ≈ 0.01 → Excellent heat transfer; thermal BL is much wider, smoothing the temperature profile.
- **Air-conditioning duct:** Pr ≈ 0.7 → Both BLs grow together; design follows simple gas correlations.
- **High-speed machining oil cooling:** Pr ≈ 1000 → Thermal BL is extremely thin; heat transfer is dominated by the wall-adjacent region.

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Step (i) — Reynolds Number:**

$$u_m = \frac{\dot{m}}{\rho A_c} = \frac{0.5}{994 \times \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4}} = \frac{0.5}{994 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.5}{1.952} = 0.2562 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Re_D = \frac{994 \times 0.2562 \times 0.05}{0.725 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{12.733}{0.000725} = 17,563$$

Since $Re_D = 17,563 > 4000$, the flow is **TURBULENT**.

**Step (ii) — Nusselt Number:**

**Method 1 — Dittus-Boelter** (with $n = 0.4$ for heating):

$$Nu_D = 0.023 \cdot Re_D^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$$

$$Re^{0.8} = 17563^{0.8}$$

Using logarithms: $\log(17563) = 4.245$, so $0.8 \times 4.245 = 3.396$, $10^{3.396} = 2489$

$$Pr^{0.4} = 4.83^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \times \ln 4.83} = e^{0.4 \times 1.575} = e^{0.630} = 1.878$$

$$Nu_D = 0.023 \times 2489 \times 1.878 = 107.5$$

**Method 2 — Gnielinski:**

First, friction factor (Petukhov):

$$f = (0.790 \ln(17563) - 1.64)^{-2} = (0.790 \times 9.773 - 1.64)^{-2} = (7.721 - 1.64)^{-2} = (6.081)^{-2} = 0.02704$$

Now Gnielinski:

$$Nu_D = \frac{(f/8)(Re - 1000) Pr}{1 + 12.7(f/8)^{0.5}(Pr^{2/3} - 1)}$$

- $f/8 = 0.02704/8 = 0.003380$
- $Re - 1000 = 16563$
- $Pr^{2/3} = 4.83^{0.667} = e^{0.667 \times 1.575} = e^{1.051} = 2.860$
- $Pr^{2/3} - 1 = 1.860$
- $(f/8)^{0.5} = \sqrt{0.003380} = 0.05814$

**Numerator:** $0.003380 \times 16563 \times 4.83 = 270.4$

**Denominator:** $1 + 12.7 \times 0.05814 \times 1.860 = 1 + 1.373 = 2.373$

$$Nu_D = \frac{270.4}{2.373} = 113.9$$

**Comparison:** Dittus-Boelter = 107.5; Gnielinski = 113.9 (≈ 6% difference — within typical accuracy).

**Use Gnielinski for final answer:** $\boxed{Nu_D = 113.9}$

**Step (iii) — Length of Tube:**

For constant heat flux, energy balance gives:

$$T_{m,o} - T_{m,i} = \frac{q_w'' \pi D L}{\dot{m} c_p}$$

Solving for L:

$$L = \frac{\dot{m} c_p (T_{m,o} - T_{m,i})}{q_w'' \pi D}$$

$$L = \frac{0.5 \times 4178 \times (50 - 20)}{5000 \times \pi \times 0.05}$$

$$L = \frac{0.5 \times 4178 \times 30}{2500 \times 0.05 \times \pi} = \frac{62,670}{392.7} = 159.6 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 159.6 \text{ m}}$$

**Step (iv) — Wall Temperature at Outlet:**

Compute h:

$$h = \frac{Nu_D \cdot k}{D} = \frac{113.9 \times 0.625}{0.05} = 1423.8 \text{ W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

For constant heat flux, the relationship is:

$$T_w = T_m + \frac{q_w''}{h}$$

But we need to use local values near the wall. A more careful approach uses:

$$T_w(x) - T_m(x) = \frac{q_w''}{h_x}$$

For a fully developed turbulent flow with constant $q_w''$, $h$ is approximately constant along the pipe, so:

$$T_{w,o} - T_{m,o} = \frac{q_w''}{h} = \frac{5000}{1423.8} = 3.51°C$$

$$T_{w,o} = T_{m,o} + 3.51 = 50 + 3.51 = 53.51°C$$

$$\boxed{T_{w,o} \approx 53.5°C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing n in Dittus-Boelter:** Use $n = 0.4$ for **heating** ($T_w > T_m$) and $n = 0.3$ for **cooling** ($T_w < T_m$). Many students use $n = 1/3$ by default, losing 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to check the entry length:** In Question A, the entry length was 4× the pipe length, so the entry region correlation (Hausen) was required instead of $Nu = 3.66$. Using $Nu = 3.66$ here would underpredict $h$ by ~50%.
> 3. **Using mean temperature for properties:** In Question B, water properties at 35°C (mean of 20 and 50) were used. Using 20°C properties throughout gives a 15% error.
> 4. **Mixing correlations:** Dittus-Boelter is valid for $Re > 10,000$ and $0.7 < Pr < 160$. Gnielinski covers a wider range and is preferred in modern KTU exams.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Always express diameter in meters (not mm) and velocity in m/s when plugging into SI correlations.
> 6. **Sign of (T_w - T_m) in exponential:** The natural log decay formula is $\exp(-NTU)$, where $NTU$ is always **positive**. Sign errors here are extremely common.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION SUMMARY — KTU Module 2: Internal Heat Transfer in Circular Pipes**

### ✅ **Conceptual Foundations**
- [x] Internal flow = fluid **enclosed** within a conduit (unlike external flow over a flat plate)
- [x] **Two distinct regions:** Entry region + Fully developed region
- [x] **Two key dimensionless groups:** Reynolds number (inertia/viscous) and Prandtl number (momentum/thermal diffusivity)
- [x] **Reynolds classification:** Laminar (< 2300), Transitional (2300–4000), Turbulent (> 4000)
- [x] **Two wall boundary conditions:** Constant $T_w$ and Constant $q_w''$

### ✅ **Memorize These 7 Numbers Cold** 🧊
- [x] Laminar FD, $T_w$ const: **Nu = 3.66**
- [x] Laminar FD, $q_w''$ const: **Nu = 4.36**
- [x] Laminar friction factor: **f = 64/Re**
- [x] Laminar entry length: **L_h = 0.05 Re D**, **L_t = 0.05 Re Pr D**
- [x] Turbulent entry length: **L/D ≈ 10**
- [x] Dittus-Boelter constant: **0.023**
- [x] Sieder-Tate constant: **0.027**

### ✅ **Correlation Selection Logic** 🔧
- [x] **Laminar + FD + T_w const** → Nu = 3.66
- [x] **Laminar + FD + q_w'' const** → Nu = 4.36
- [x] **Laminar + Entry + T_w const** → Hausen correlation
- [x] **Laminar + Entry + q_w'' const** → Shah correlation
- [x] **Turbulent + Quick estimate** → Dittus-Boelter
- [x] **Turbulent + More accurate** → Gnielinski
- [x] **Turbulent + High viscosity variation** → Sieder-Tate (includes $\mu_b/\mu_w$ correction)

### ✅ **Engineering Design Principles**
- [x] Turbulent flow gives **much higher h** than laminar (10× to 100×)
- [x] For **oils (high Pr)**, thermal entry length is **very long** — entry region correlations are critical
- [x] For **liquid metals (low Pr)**, thermal entry length is **very short** — fully developed formulas work for almost the entire pipe
- [x] Pressure drop in turbulent flow is **proportional to $u^2$**, so energy costs scale steeply with flow rate
- [x] Always check the **Prandtl number** to decide whether to use the constant or entry-region correlation

### ✅ **Common Exam Pitfalls to Avoid** ⚠️
- [x] Don't confuse **Re based on D** with Re based on hydraulic diameter for non-circular ducts
- [x] Don't forget the **viscosity correction term** $(\mu_b/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ for large temperature differences
- [x] Don't apply Dittus-Boelter for laminar flow — it will give wildly wrong answers
- [x] Don't ignore the **entry length** when the pipe is short (L < 10D for turbulent, L < 50D for laminar)

### ✅ **KTU-Specific Points to Note** 📋
- [x] The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **graphical plots** of Nusselt number vs. Graetz number (Hausen-type charts)
- [x] Expect at least **one sub-question requiring derivation** of the fully developed Nusselt number
- [x] Numerical problems typically combine **3-4 concepts** (Re + Pr + Nu correlation + Energy balance)
- [x] Examiner frequently tests the **physical interpretation** of why $Nu_{q_w} > Nu_{T_w}$ for laminar FD flow
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analogy Between Heat and Momentum Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analogy Between Heat and Momentum Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Analogy Between Heat and Momentum Transfer** refers to a set of theoretical and empirical relationships that correlate convective heat transfer with fluid friction (momentum transfer) in forced convection over a surface. These analogies are derived from the observation that the mechanisms governing the transport of heat and momentum in a moving fluid are governed by mathematically similar governing equations — the **energy equation** and the **Navier–Stokes (momentum) equations** — when the fluid properties are held constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students are expected to derive and apply the **Reynolds, Prandtl, Von Kármán, and Colburn analogies** for turbulent flow over a flat plate, and use them to estimate the convective heat transfer coefficient when experimental Nusselt number data is unavailable.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a heavy **wooden box** across a rough floor (this represents **momentum transfer / friction**). The box resists sliding because of the friction between it and the floor. Now imagine that the same floor is **hot** and you are wearing thin socks — your feet also feel the heat (this represents **heat transfer**). The interesting thing is that the *same fluid particles* (or "roughness elements" in the analogy) that resist the box's motion also carry heat away from the floor. Therefore, the **friction** and the **heat transfer** are linked by the *same fluid mechanics* — a faster-moving fluid creates both more friction *and* more heat removal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants / Metrics to Remember:**
> - Prandtl Number, $Pr = \dfrac{\mu C_p}{k}$ — ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity.
> - Stanton Number, $St = \dfrac{h}{\rho u C_p}$ — dimensionless heat transfer coefficient.
> - Skin Friction Coefficient, $C_f = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\frac{1}{2}\rho u_{\infty}^2}$ — dimensionless wall shear stress.
> - Standard condition where analogies collapse to one: $Pr = 1$ (e.g., gases).

The governing equations for momentum and heat transfer in a boundary layer share a **mathematically analogous** structure because:

$$\rho \left( u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right) = \mu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} \quad \text{(Momentum)}$$

$$\rho C_p \left( u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} \right) = k \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2} \quad \text{(Heat)}$$

The only difference is the appearance of $C_p$ on the left side, which gives rise to the **Prandtl number** as the linking parameter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Logical Breakdown of the Analogies

The four major analogies are arranged in a hierarchy of increasing accuracy and physical realism:

1. **Reynolds Analogy (1874)** — Simplest. Assumes $Pr = 1$ and a thin boundary layer. States that heat transfer and skin friction are directly proportional.
2. **Prandtl's Analogy (1910)** — Improvement. Includes a correction for $Pr$ but still assumes no viscous sublayer. Valid for $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 60$.
3. **Von Kármán Analogy (1939)** — Most rigorous of the classical three. Models the boundary layer as a **laminar sublayer** near the wall and a **fully turbulent core** outside.
4. **Colburn Analogy (1933)** — Empirical modification of Reynolds analogy using experimental data; widely used by practicing engineers for pipe flow.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical** — questions worth up to 14 marks can be directly derived from this table.

| # | Analogy | Governing Equation | Validity Range | Key Assumption |
|---|---------|--------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Reynolds** | $St = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$ | $Pr = 1$ only | $C_p \mu / k = 1$; thin BL |
| 2 | **Prandtl** | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$ | $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 60$ | Turbulent core only |
| 3 | **Von Kármán** | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} \left[\dfrac{(C_f/2)^{-1/2} - 5 \ln(Re \cdot C_f/2) + \dots}{}\right]$ (full form) | All $Pr$ | Laminar sublayer + turbulent core |
| 4 | **Colburn** | $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \dfrac{C_f}{8}$ | $0.6 < Pr < 100$ (pipes) | Pipe flow, empirical |

**Compact form of Von Kármán Analogy:**

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{\frac{C_f}{2}}{1 + 5\sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}} \left[ \ln\left( Re \cdot \frac{C_f}{2} \right) - 5 \right] + 5\sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}}}$$

**For Colburn Analogy (often used in KTU problems):**

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{8}$$

where $j_H$ is the **Colburn j-factor for heat transfer**.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

These analogies are extremely valuable in engineering practice. For example:

- **Heat Exchanger Design:** If pressure drop (friction) data is known from a manufacturer's catalog, the heat transfer coefficient $h$ can be predicted without running expensive thermal experiments.
- **Gas Turbine Blade Cooling:** Aerodynamicists measure drag (friction); from that they estimate convective cooling using Reynolds/Colburn analogy.
- **Re-entry Vehicle Thermal Protection:** Engineers correlate aerodynamic heating with skin friction during hypersonic re-entry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The analogies fail when: (a) $Pr \ll 1$ (liquid metals), (b) strong property variation, (c) flow separation or strong pressure gradient, (d) compressibility effects dominate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Reynolds Analogy (Conceptual through Energy Thickness)

**Step 1: Start with the Integral Momentum Equation**

The momentum thickness $\theta$ for a flat plate boundary layer is defined as:

$$\theta = \int_0^{\delta} \frac{\rho u}{\rho u_{\infty}} \left( 1 - \frac{u}{u_{\infty}} \right) dy$$

**Step 2: Apply the von Kármán Integral Momentum Equation**

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho u_{\infty}^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

This gives the local skin friction coefficient:

$$C_f = 2 \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

**Step 3: Define the Thermal Energy Thickness $\theta_T$**

The thickness carrying the enthalpy defect:

$$\theta_T = \int_0^{\delta_T} \frac{\rho C_p (T_w - T)}{\rho C_p (T_w - T_{\infty})} dy$$

**Step 4: Apply the von Kármán Integral Energy Equation**

$$\frac{q_w''}{\rho C_p u_{\infty} (T_w - T_{\infty})} = \frac{d\theta_T}{dx}$$

**Step 5: Recognize Stanton Number Definition**

$$St = \frac{q_w''}{\rho C_p u_{\infty} (T_w - T_{\infty})}$$

So:

$$St = \frac{d\theta_T}{dx}$$

**Step 6: Assume Thermal and Momentum Boundary Layers are Identical**

For $Pr = 1$:

$$\theta = \theta_T$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{d\theta_T}{dx}$$

**Step 7: Combine with Momentum Result**

Since $C_f = 2 \frac{d\theta}{dx}$:

$$St = \frac{d\theta_T}{dx} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

**Reynolds Analogy — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{St = \frac{C_f}{2}} \quad \text{valid for } Pr = 1$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Prandtl's Analogy

**Step 1: Begin with the General Convection Definitions**

$$St = \frac{h}{\rho u_{\infty} C_p}, \qquad C_f = \frac{2 \tau_w}{\rho u_{\infty}^2}$$

**Step 2: Modify Reynolds Analogy with a Prandtl Number Correction**

Prandtl observed that for $Pr \neq 1$, the ratio of thermal to momentum boundary layer thickness is:

$$\frac{\delta_T}{\delta} \approx Pr^{-1/3}$$

**Step 3: Write the Modified Stanton Number**

Since $St$ is inversely proportional to $\delta_T$, the Stanton number scales as:

$$St \propto Pr^{1/3} \cdot \frac{C_f}{2}$$

To get the conventional form, Prandtl wrote:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

**Prandtl's Analogy — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{2}} \quad \text{valid for } 0.6 \leq Pr \leq 60$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Von Kármán Analogy (4-Region Approach)

Von Kármán divided the turbulent boundary layer into **two regions**:
- **Laminar sublayer** (viscous-dominated, $0 \leq y \leq y_1$)
- **Turbulent core** ($y_1 \leq y \leq \delta$)

**Step 1: Apply Universal Velocity Profile (1/7th Power Law)**

$$\frac{u}{u_{\infty}} = \left( \frac{y}{\delta} \right)^{1/7}$$

**Step 2: Find Wall Shear Stress**

$$\tau_w = \mu \left. \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} \right|_{y=0}$$

But for turbulent flow this requires a "fictitious" slip at the wall. The result is:

$$C_f = 0.0576 \, Re_x^{-1/5} \quad \text{(local)}$$

**Step 3: Define Stanton Number in Terms of Sublayer Thickness**

The heat transfer occurs predominantly in the **laminar sublayer**, where conduction dominates. So:

$$h = \frac{k}{y_1}$$

And $y_1$ is found from the velocity profile intersection:

$$y_1 = \delta \cdot Pr^{-1/3} \cdot \left(\frac{C_f}{2}\right)^{1/2} \cdot Re^{-1}$$

**Step 4: Derive Final Form**

After lengthy algebra (see KTU Module 2 lecture notes for full expansion), the resulting **Von Kármán Analogy** is:

$$\boxed{St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{\frac{C_f}{2}}{1 + 5 \sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}} \left[ \ln(Re \cdot C_f/2) - 5 \right] + 5\sqrt{\frac{C_f}{2}}}}$$

**Step 5: Simplification for $Pr = 1$**

When $Pr = 1$, the Von Kármán result correctly reduces to Reynolds Analogy — proving internal consistency.

### 3.4 Colburn Analogy (Empirical)

Based on experimental data for **flow in pipes**, Colburn proposed:

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{C_f}{8}$$

The factor $\dfrac{1}{8}$ instead of $\dfrac{1}{2}$ arises because pipe flow uses the **Fanning friction factor** $f$, related to skin friction as $C_f = 4f$. The Colburn j-factor:

$$\boxed{j_H = \frac{C_f}{8} = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}}$$

is widely used in **chemical engineering heat exchanger design**.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** Air at $27^\circ C$ and $1\,atm$ flows over a flat plate at $u_{\infty} = 10\,m/s$. The plate is $2\,m$ long. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient using (a) Reynolds, (b) Prandtl, and (c) Colburn analogies. Given $Pr = 0.71$, $\nu = 15.9 \times 10^{-6}\,m^2/s$, $C_p = 1005\,J/kg\cdot K$, $\rho = 1.184\,kg/m^3$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Reynolds Number at $x = L$**

$$Re_L = \frac{u_{\infty} L}{\nu} = \frac{10 \times 2}{15.9 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.258 \times 10^6$$

Since $Re_L > 5 \times 10^5$, flow is **turbulent** (assume transition from leading edge for analogy validity).

**Step 2: Compute Average Skin Friction Coefficient**

For turbulent flow over a flat plate:

$$\bar{C_f} = 0.074 \, Re_L^{-1/5} = 0.074 \times (1.258 \times 10^6)^{-0.2}$$

$$\bar{C_f} = 0.074 \times (1.258 \times 10^6)^{-0.2} = 0.074 \times 0.03599 = 0.002663$$

**Step 3a: Reynolds Analogy ($Pr = 1$ assumption)**

$$St = \frac{\bar{C_f}}{2} = \frac{0.002663}{2} = 0.001332$$

$$h = St \cdot \rho \cdot u_{\infty} \cdot C_p = 0.001332 \times 1.184 \times 10 \times 1005 = 15.85 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**Step 3b: Prandtl Analogy**

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{\bar{C_f}}{2}$$

$$St = \frac{0.002663}{2 \times (0.71)^{2/3}} = \frac{0.001332}{0.7784} = 0.001711$$

$$h = 0.001711 \times 1.184 \times 10 \times 1005 = 20.36 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**Step 3c: Colburn Analogy**

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{\bar{C_f}}{8}$$

$$St = \frac{0.002663}{8 \times (0.71)^{2/3}} = \frac{0.000333}{0.7784} = 0.0004278$$

$$h = 0.0004278 \times 1.184 \times 10 \times 1005 = 5.09 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Colburn result is lower because it is calibrated for **pipe flow**, not flat plates. The Prandtl analogy gives the most accurate estimate for a flat plate in this case.

**Comparison Summary:**

| Method | $h$ ($W/m^2 \cdot K$) | Comment |
|--------|----------------------|---------|
| Reynolds | 15.85 | Assumes $Pr=1$ — overpredicts since $Pr_{air}=0.71<1$ |
| Prandtl | 20.36 | Best for flat plate, $0.6<Pr<60$ |
| Colburn | 5.09 | For pipe flow, conservative |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Hierarchy of the Four Analogies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ANALOGIES BETWEEN HEAT AND MOMENTUM TRANSFER"] --> B["Reynolds Analogy 1874"]
    A --> C["Prandtl Analogy 1910"]
    A --> D["Von Karman Analogy 1939"]
    A --> E["Colburn Analogy 1933"]

    B --> B1["Assumes Pr equals 1"]
    B --> B2["St equals Cf over 2"]
    B --> B3["Valid only for gases with Pr near unity"]

    C --> C1["Pr correction: Pr to power 2 over 3"]
    C --> C2["St times Pr to power 2 over 3 equals Cf over 2"]
    C --> C3["Valid for 0 point 6 to 60"]

    D --> D1["Two region model: laminar sublayer plus turbulent core"]
    D --> D2["Accounts for viscous sublayer thickness"]
    D --> D3["Valid for all Pr"]

    E --> E1["Empirical j factor formulation"]
    E --> E2["jH equals St times Pr to power 2 over 3 equals Cf over 8"]
    E --> E3["Pipe flow calibrated"]

    F["Governing Physical Insight"] --> A
    F1["Same fluid carries both heat and momentum"] --> F
    F2["Governing equations have identical structure"] --> F
    F3["Prandtl number is the bridging parameter"] --> F
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: How an Analogy Solves a Heat Transfer Problem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1["Input: Re number"] --> Core
    In2["Input: Pr number"] --> Core
    In3["Input: Fluid properties rho Cp mu"] --> Core
    In4["Input: Geometry Flat plate or Pipe"] --> Core

    subgraph Core["ANALOGY ENGINE"]
        direction TB
        Step1["Step 1: Compute skin friction Cf from Re and geometry"]
        Step2["Step 2: Apply chosen analogy equation"]
        Step3["Step 3: Solve for Stanton number St"]
        Step4["Step 4: Convert St to heat transfer coefficient h"]
        Step1 --> Step2 --> Step3 --> Step4
    end

    Core --> Out1["Output: h in W per m squared K"]
    Core --> Out2["Output: Nu number if needed"]
    Core --> Out3["Output: Heat flux q double prime"]
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Which Analogy to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Need to estimate h for turbulent flow"] --> Q1{"Pr of fluid?"}
    Q1 -->|"Pr equals 1"| R1["Use Reynolds Analogy"]
    Q1 -->|"0 point 6 to 60"| Q2{"Geometry?"}
    Q1 -->|"Pr less than 0 point 6 e.g. liquid metals"| R2["Analogy fails. Use separate empirical correlation"]
    Q2 -->|"Flat plate"| R3["Use Prandtl or Von Karman Analogy"]
    Q2 -->|"Pipe flow"| R4["Use Colburn Analogy"]
    Q3{"High accuracy required?"} -->|"Yes"| R5["Use Von Karman Analogy"]
    Q3 -->|"No quick estimate OK"| R6["Use Prandtl or Colburn"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State Reynolds analogy between heat and momentum transfer. What is its major limitation?*

**Model Answer:**
Reynolds analogy states that for a fluid with **Prandtl number equal to unity**, the **Stanton number** is directly proportional to the **skin friction coefficient**:

$$St = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

It establishes that the **ratio of heat transferred to momentum transferred** is the same throughout the boundary layer. **Limitation:** It is valid only for $Pr = 1$ (e.g., some gases), and it neglects the effect of the **laminar sublayer** near the wall. For liquids or gases with $Pr \neq 1$, the analogy gives significant errors. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the **Colburn j-factor** for heat transfer and state its relationship with the friction factor.*

**Model Answer:**
The Colburn j-factor for heat transfer is an empirical dimensionless group defined as:

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$

It is related to the friction factor (Fanning) by the **Colburn analogy**:

$$j_H = \frac{C_f}{8} = \frac{f}{2}$$

This analogy is widely used in the design of **shell-and-tube heat exchangers** to relate the convective heat transfer coefficient to measured pressure drop data. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Question A]**

**(a)** Starting from the integral momentum and energy equations of a turbulent boundary layer over a flat plate, **derive the Reynolds analogy** $St = C_f/2$ clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Air at $30^\circ C$ and atmospheric pressure flows over a smooth flat plate at $15\,m/s$. The plate is $3\,m$ long and is maintained at $90^\circ C$. Using the **Prandtl analogy**, determine the average convective heat transfer coefficient and the total heat transferred from the plate per unit width. Given: $\nu = 16.0 \times 10^{-6}\,m^2/s$, $k = 0.0263\,W/m\cdot K$, $Pr = 0.71$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

Step 1: Von Kármán integral momentum equation:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho u_{\infty}^2} = \frac{d\theta}{dx}, \quad C_f = 2 \frac{d\theta}{dx}$$

Step 2: Von Kármán integral energy equation:

$$St = \frac{q_w''}{\rho C_p u_{\infty}(T_w - T_{\infty})} = \frac{d\theta_T}{dx}$$

Step 3: For $Pr = 1$, thermal and momentum boundary layers coincide, so $\theta_T = \theta$.

Step 4: Equating derivatives:

$$St = \frac{d\theta_T}{dx} = \frac{d\theta}{dx} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

Assumptions: Steady, 2D, incompressible flow; constant properties; $Pr = 1$; no viscous dissipation; negligible radiation. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1: $Re_L = \dfrac{u_{\infty} L}{\nu} = \dfrac{15 \times 3}{16 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.8125 \times 10^6$ (Turbulent)

Step 2: $\bar{C_f} = 0.074 \, Re_L^{-1/5} = 0.074 \times (2.8125 \times 10^6)^{-0.2} = 0.074 \times 0.03021 = 0.002236$

Step 3: From Prandtl analogy:

$$St = \frac{\bar{C_f}}{2 \, Pr^{2/3}} = \frac{0.002236}{2 \times (0.71)^{2/3}} = \frac{0.001118}{0.7784} = 0.001436$$

Step 4: Find $h$ from $Nu = St \cdot Re \cdot Pr$:

$$Nu_L = 0.001436 \times 2.8125 \times 10^6 \times 0.71 = 2869$$

Step 5: $h = \dfrac{Nu_L \cdot k}{L} = \dfrac{2869 \times 0.0263}{3} = 25.15 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$

Step 6: Heat transfer per unit width:

$$Q' = h \cdot L \cdot (T_w - T_{\infty}) = 25.15 \times 3 \times (90 - 30) = 4527 \, W/m$$

**Answer:** $h = 25.15\,W/m^2\cdot K$ and $Q' = 4527\,W/m$ per unit width. **(7 marks)**

---

**Q3. Question B (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the **Von Kármán analogy** for heat and momentum transfer. Discuss how it overcomes the limitations of the Prandtl analogy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A long cylindrical pipe of $5\,cm$ diameter carries oil with $\rho = 880\,kg/m^3$, $C_p = 2000\,J/kg\cdot K$, $\mu = 0.005\,Pa\cdot s$, $k = 0.15\,W/m\cdot K$. The mean velocity is $2\,m/s$. Using the **Colburn analogy**, estimate the inside heat transfer coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Von Kármán Analogy Explanation:**

The Von Kármán analogy (1939) is the most refined of the classical momentum-heat transfer analogies. It overcomes the Prandtl assumption of a *single turbulent core* by modeling the boundary layer as **two distinct regions**:

- A **laminar (viscous) sublayer** of thickness $y_1$ adjacent to the wall where molecular conduction dominates.
- A **fully turbulent core** outside the sublayer where turbulent mixing dominates.

In the viscous sublayer, heat transfer occurs by molecular conduction, and $h \approx k/y_1$. The thickness $y_1$ is obtained from the intersection of the linear viscous sublayer profile with the 1/7th-power turbulent profile. The final result is the full Von Kármán equation (see Section 3.3). It is valid for **all Prandtl numbers** — including liquid metals — making it superior to Reynolds and Prandtl analogies. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Step 1: Reynolds number:

$$Re_D = \frac{\rho u D}{\mu} = \frac{880 \times 2 \times 0.05}{0.005} = 17600$$

Step 2: Prandtl number:

$$Pr = \frac{\mu C_p}{k} = \frac{0.005 \times 2000}{0.15} = 66.67$$

Step 3: Friction factor (assume smooth pipe, turbulent):

Using Petukhov or Blasius: $f = 0.0791 \, Re^{-0.25} = 0.0791 \times (17600)^{-0.25} = 0.0791 \times 0.04873 = 0.003854$

Step 4: Colburn analogy:

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{2}$$

$$St = \frac{0.003854}{2 \times (66.67)^{2/3}} = \frac{0.001927}{16.0} = 0.0001204$$

Step 5: Heat transfer coefficient:

$$h = St \cdot \rho \cdot u \cdot C_p = 0.0001204 \times 880 \times 2 \times 2000 = 423.8 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**Answer:** $h \approx 424\,W/m^2 \cdot K$. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $Pr^{2/3}$ term** in Prandtl/Colburn analogies when $Pr \neq 1$ — costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Fanning and Darcy friction factors.** $C_f = 4f_{Fanning} = 2f_{Darcy}$. Using the wrong one in Colburn analogy yields wrong $h$.
> 3. **Applying analogies to laminar flow.** These analogies are valid **only for turbulent** boundary layers.
> 4. **Forgetting transition region.** In reality, transition occurs at $Re_x \approx 5 \times 10^5$. Using the turbulent $C_f$ formula for the entire plate is a common approximation — KTU accepts it if mentioned.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** in the final $h$ calculation. Always end in $W/m^2 \cdot K$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Analogy Between Heat and Momentum Transfer**

- **Governing Insight:** Energy and momentum equations have **identical structure**; only the **Prandtl number** distinguishes them.
- **Reynolds Analogy** $\Rightarrow St = C_f/2$ — applies only at $Pr = 1$.
- **Prandtl Analogy** $\Rightarrow St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = C_f/2$ — flat plate, $0.6 \leq Pr \leq 60$.
- **Von Kármán Analogy** $\Rightarrow$ Most general; two-layer model; **valid for all $Pr$**.
- **Colburn Analogy** $\Rightarrow j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = C_f/8$ — **pipe flow**, empirical.
- **Stanton Number** $St = h/(\rho u C_p)$ — central linking variable.
- **Skin Friction Coefficient** $C_f = 2\tau_w/(\rho u_{\infty}^2)$.
- **All analogies apply only to turbulent flow** — never to laminar.
- For $Pr = 1$ (e.g., monatomic gases), Reynolds, Prandtl, and Von Kármán **collapse to the same result** — important sanity check.
- **Colburn j-factor** $j_H$ is interchangeable with $St \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ — memorize this equivalence.
- **Use friction factor $f$ (Fanning) carefully** — Colburn uses $C_f/8$ which equals $f/2$.
- Typical exam derivations follow the **Integral Momentum → Integral Energy → Equate $\theta = \theta_T$** sequence for Reynolds, then introduce $Pr^{2/3}$ correction for Prandtl.
- For numerical problems: always compute $Re_L$ **first**, then $\bar{C_f}$, then $St$, then $h$.
- High-yield **keywords** for theory questions: "boundary layer analogy," "Reynolds analogy," "Colburn j-factor," "laminar sublayer," "Prandtl number correction."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Convection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Natural Convection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Natural Convection** (also termed *Free Convection*) is the mode of heat transfer that occurs between a solid surface and a fluid in contact with it, where the fluid motion is generated **entirely by the action of body forces arising from density differences within the fluid itself**, and not by any external agency such as a fan, pump, or blower.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Natural convection is the heat transfer mechanism in which fluid motion is caused by density variations produced by temperature gradients. The governing non-dimensional parameters are the **Grashof Number (Gr)**, **Prandtl Number (Pr)**, and the combined **Rayleigh Number (Ra = Gr · Pr)**."

The driving potential for natural convection is the **buoyancy force** (also called the *Archimedean force*). When a fluid is heated, its density decreases; this lighter parcel of fluid rises under gravity, displacing the colder, denser fluid downwards. This continuous cyclic motion establishes a convective current that transports thermal energy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Air Balloon" Intuition

Imagine a paper lantern being lit at a festival. The flame at the bottom heats the air trapped inside. This heated air becomes less dense (lighter), so buoyancy lifts the entire lantern skyward — the lantern literally rises because of natural convection currents. Inside a room, the same physics operates on a smaller scale: a hot radiator warms the air next to it, the warm air rises toward the ceiling, moves horizontally, cools, and sinks back to the floor to be reheated — completing a natural convection loop called a **convection cell**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant:** The acceleration due to gravity, **g = 9.81 m/s²**, is the principal body force driving free convection flows on Earth.

### 1.3 Classification of Natural Convection

Natural convection problems are broadly classified by the **geometry and orientation of the surface**:

- **External Natural Convection:** Flow over a vertical plate, horizontal plate (facing up/down), cylinder, or sphere.
- **Internal Natural Convection:** Flow inside vertical/horizontal enclosures, channels, or annular spaces.

The fluid state can be **laminar** (low Ra), **transitional**, or **turbulent** (high Ra).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Mechanism — The Boussinesq Approximation

The cornerstone assumption enabling analytical treatment of natural convection is the **Boussinesq Approximation**, which states:

> The fluid is treated as incompressible *except* in the buoyancy term of the momentum equation, where density variation is retained **linearly with temperature**.

Mathematically:
$$\rho \approx \rho_\infty \left[1 - \beta (T - T_\infty)\right]$$

where $\beta$ is the **coefficient of thermal expansion** (in K⁻¹) and the subscript $\infty$ refers to the fluid far from the surface (free-stream / ambient condition).

This approximation is valid when $(T - T_\infty) \ll T_\infty$ and pressure variations are small — conditions satisfied in most KTU-level problems.

### 2.2 Governing Non-Dimensional Numbers

#### (a) Grashof Number (Gr)
The Grashof number is the **ratio of buoyancy forces to viscous forces**. It plays the same role in natural convection that the **Reynolds Number (Re)** plays in forced convection.

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu^2}$$

#### (b) Prandtl Number (Pr)
$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\mu C_p}{k}$$

#### (c) Rayleigh Number (Ra)
The Rayleigh number represents the **ratio of buoyancy forces to the product of viscous and thermal diffusion forces**.

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu \alpha}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb — Flow Regime from Ra:**
> - Ra < 10⁹ → **Laminar**
> - 10⁹ < Ra < 10¹³ → **Transitional**
> - Ra > 10¹³ → **Turbulent**

#### (d) Nusselt Number (Nu)
$$Nu_L = \frac{h L}{k}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Governing Parameter / Correlation | Mathematical Expression | Typical Range / Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grashof Number | $Gr_L = \dfrac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu^2}$ | All natural convection flows |
| 2 | Rayleigh Number | $Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr$ | All natural convection flows |
| 3 | Nusselt Number (Vertical Plate, Laminar) | $Nu_L = 0.59 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \le Ra_L \le 10^9$ |
| 4 | Nusselt Number (Vertical Plate, Turbulent) | $Nu_L = 0.10 \, Ra_L^{1/3}$ | $10^9 \le Ra_L \le 10^{13}$ |
| 5 | Churchill-Chu Correlation (Vertical Plate, all Ra) | $Nu_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \dfrac{0.387\, Ra_L^{1/6}}{[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$ | Entire laminar–turbulent range |
| 6 | Horizontal Plate (Hot Surface Up / Cold Down) | $Nu_L = 0.54 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \le Ra_L \le 10^7$ |
| 7 | Horizontal Plate (Hot Surface Down / Cold Up) | $Nu_L = 0.27 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ | $10^5 \le Ra_L \le 10^{10}$ |
| 8 | Local-to-Average Nusselt Conversion | $\overline{Nu}_L = \dfrac{4}{3}\, Nu_L$ | Vertical plate, laminar |
| 9 | Thermal Boundary Layer Thickness | $\delta_t \sim L \cdot Ra_L^{-1/4}$ | Laminar vertical plate |
| 10 | Velocity Boundary Layer Thickness | $\delta \sim L \cdot Ra_L^{-1/4}$ | Laminar vertical plate |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat exchanger design** for nuclear reactors (natural draft cooling towers).
- **Building ventilation** and passive cooling of solar rooftops.
- **Electronic cooling** of transformers and overhead transmission conductors.
- **Geophysical flows** — atmospheric convection, ocean currents, mantle convection.
- **Solar collector panel** efficiency and energy harvesting systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Origin of the Grashof Number from the Momentum Equation

The 2D steady laminar boundary layer equations for an incompressible fluid under the Boussinesq approximation over a vertical plate are:

**Continuity equation:**
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**x-Momentum equation:**
$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + g \beta (T - T_\infty)$$

**Energy equation:**
$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

> The term $g \beta (T - T_\infty)$ is the **buoyancy force term** per unit mass.

Now, we apply the **method of non-dimensionalization**. Define the following dimensionless variables:

$$
x^\ast = \frac{x}{L}, \quad
y^\ast = \frac{y}{L}, \quad
u^\ast = \frac{u}{U_0}, \quad
v^\ast = \frac{v}{U_0}, \quad
T^\ast = \frac{T - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty}
$$

But unlike forced convection, there is **no externally imposed velocity $U_0$**. The only velocity scale that emerges naturally is a buoyancy-induced scale, obtained by balancing inertia and buoyancy in the x-momentum equation:

$$
U_0 \sim \sqrt{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the dimensionless variables into the x-momentum equation.

$$
\left(\frac{u^\ast}{L}\right) \left(\frac{U_0}{L}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{v^\ast}{L}\right)\left(\frac{U_0}{L}\right)\left(\frac{U_0}{L}\right) \sim \nu \frac{U_0}{L^2} + g \beta (T_s - T_\infty)
$$

**Step 2:** Balance inertia with buoyancy (no imposed pressure gradient).

$$
\frac{U_0^2}{L} \sim g \beta (T_s - T_\infty)
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the velocity scale.

$$
U_0 \sim \sqrt{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $U_0$ into the dimensionless x-momentum equation.

$$
u^\ast \frac{\partial u^\ast}{\partial x^\ast} + v^\ast \frac{\partial u^\ast}{\partial y^\ast} = \frac{\nu}{U_0 L} \frac{\partial^2 u^\ast}{\partial y^{\ast 2}} + 1
$$

**Step 5:** The coefficient of the viscous term $\dfrac{\nu}{U_0 L}$ is the inverse of the Grashof number's square root.

$$
\frac{\nu}{U_0 L} = \frac{\nu}{L \sqrt{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{Gr_L}}
$$

**Step 6:** Rearranging produces the non-dimensional x-momentum equation.

$$
u^\ast \frac{\partial u^\ast}{\partial x^\ast} + v^\ast \frac{\partial u^\ast}{\partial y^\ast} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{Gr_L}} \frac{\partial^2 u^\ast}{\partial y^{\ast 2}} + 1
$$

Therefore, **buoyancy forces dominate when $Gr_L \gg 1$**, and viscous forces dominate when $Gr_L \ll 1$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Boundary Layer Thickness for Laminar Flow over a Vertical Plate (Order-of-Magnitude Analysis)

From the energy equation, the ratio of convective to conductive terms gives:

$$
\frac{u \Delta T / L}{\alpha \Delta T / \delta_t^2} \sim 1
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
\frac{U_0 \delta_t^2}{\alpha L} \sim 1
$$

Substituting $U_0 = \sqrt{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L} = \sqrt{g \beta \Delta T \cdot L}$ and solving for $\delta_t$:

$$
\delta_t \sim \left( \frac{\alpha^2 L}{g \beta \Delta T \cdot L} \right)^{1/4} = \frac{L}{Ra_L^{1/4}}
$$

Expressed cleanly as a non-dimensional thickness:

$$
\frac{\delta_t}{L} \sim Ra_L^{-1/4}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Local Nusselt Number for Laminar Vertical Plate (Order-of-Magnitude)

The convective heat flux at the wall is, by definition of the thermal boundary layer:

$$
q''_s \sim \frac{k \Delta T}{\delta_t}
$$

Using Fourier's law of conduction and the boundary layer thickness relation derived above:

$$
h \Delta T = \frac{k \Delta T}{\delta_t} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h = \frac{k}{\delta_t} \sim \frac{k \, Ra_L^{1/4}}{L}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $L/k$:

$$
Nu_L = \frac{h L}{k} \sim Ra_L^{1/4}
$$

This is precisely the **functional form** observed in the experimentally verified correlation:

$$
Nu_L = C \, Ra_L^{1/4}
$$

where $C$ is a constant of order unity (empirically found to be $C \approx 0.59$ for laminar flow over a vertical plate).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Natural Convection Solution Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the step-by-step analytical pipeline a student should follow when solving any natural convection problem in the KTU examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Surface Temperature Ts and Ambient Tinf] --> B[Identify Geometry: Vertical or Horizontal]
    B --> C[Determine Characteristic Length L]
    C --> D[Look up Fluid Properties at Film Temperature Tf = Ts+Tinf divide by 2]
    D --> E[Compute Coefficient of Thermal Expansion beta = 1 divide by Tf]
    E --> F[Compute Kinematic Viscosity nu and Thermal Diffusivity alpha]
    F --> G[Calculate Grashof Number GrL = g beta Ts-Tinf L cubed divided by nu squared]
    G --> H[Calculate Prandtl Number Pr = nu divided by alpha]
    H --> I[Calculate Rayleigh Number RaL = GrL times Pr]
    I --> J{Is RaL less than 10 to power 9?}
    J -- YES --> K[Use Laminar Correlation NuL = 0.59 RaL to power 1 divide by 4]
    J -- NO --> L[Use Turbulent Correlation NuL = 0.10 RaL to power 1 divide by 3]
    K --> M[Convert NuL to h using h = NuL times k divided by L]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Compute Heat Transfer Rate Q = h A Ts-Tinf]
    N --> O[END]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Schematic: Natural Convection Cell Above a Vertical Hot Plate

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Hot Vertical Plate Surface Temperature Ts] --> Q[Heated Boundary Layer delta t]
    Q --> R[Buoyant Plume Rises Upward]
    R --> S[Cooling at Upper Edge]
    S --> T[Cooler Fluid Descends]
    T --> U[Replacement Fluid Sucked from Below]
    U --> P
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Choice of Correlation Based on Geometry and Ra

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Begin Classification] --> G1{Geometry Type?}
    G1 -- Vertical Plate --> V1{RaL Range?}
    V1 -- 10 power 4 to 10 power 9 --> V2[Use Nu = 0.59 Ra to 1 by 4]
    V1 -- Above 10 power 9 --> V3[Use Nu = 0.10 Ra to 1 by 3]
    V1 -- All Ranges --> V4[Use Churchill-Chu Correlation]
    G1 -- Horizontal Plate --> H1{Which Face is Hot?}
    H1 -- Hot Face Up --> H2[Use Nu = 0.54 Ra to 1 by 4]
    H1 -- Hot Face Down --> H3[Use Nu = 0.27 Ra to 1 by 4]
    G1 -- Horizontal Cylinder --> C1[Use Churchill-Chu Cylinder Correlation]
    G1 -- Sphere --> S1[Use Sphere Correlation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** These block diagrams map the **decision logic** a student must follow. They do not depict physical convection cells; rather, they serve as algorithmic flowcharts aligned with the KTU board evaluation rubric.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Grashof number. What is its physical significance in natural convection?**

**Model Answer:**
The Grashof number is a dimensionless parameter defined as:
$$Gr_L = \frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu^2}$$

**Physical Significance:** It represents the **ratio of buoyancy forces to viscous forces** acting on a fluid. It plays the role analogous to the Reynolds number in forced convection — but whereas Re compares inertia to viscosity, Gr compares buoyancy to viscosity. A high $Gr_L$ indicates buoyancy-driven flow dominates; a low $Gr_L$ indicates viscous damping suppresses natural convection currents.

*(Examiner expects: definition equation + significance + analogy to Re — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the Boussinesq approximation. Why is it essential in natural convection analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
The Boussinesq approximation states that **the fluid density is treated as a constant in all terms of the governing equations EXCEPT in the body-force (buoyancy) term**, where it is linearized with temperature as:
$$\rho = \rho_\infty \left[1 - \beta (T - T_\infty)\right]$$

**Why Essential:** It simplifies the otherwise non-linear, variable-density Navier–Stokes equations into a tractable form where the only place density variation appears is the buoyancy term $g \beta (T - T_\infty)$. This allows analytical and similarity solutions for natural convection boundary layers that would otherwise be mathematically intractable.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A vertical plate 0.5 m high is maintained at a temperature of 60 °C and is exposed to atmospheric air at 20 °C. Calculate the **heat transfer coefficient** and the **rate of heat transfer per unit width** of the plate. Use the appropriate correlation for the regime identified.

**Given Data:**
- Plate height $L = 0.5$ m
- $T_s = 60°C = 333$ K
- $T_\infty = 20°C = 293$ K
- $\Delta T = 40$ K

**Film Temperature:** $T_f = (333 + 293)/2 = 313$ K

**Air properties at 313 K (from standard tables):**
- $\nu = 16.7 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- $\alpha = 21.9 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- $k = 0.0274$ W/(m·K)
- $Pr = 0.71$
- $\beta = 1/T_f = 1/313 = 3.195 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹

**Step 1: Calculate Grashof Number**
$$Gr_L = \frac{(9.81)(3.195 \times 10^{-3})(40)(0.5)^3}{(16.7 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

**Numerator:** $(9.81)(3.195 \times 10^{-3})(40)(0.125) = 0.1567$ m³/s²

**Denominator:** $(16.7 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2.789 \times 10^{-10}$ m⁴/s²

$$Gr_L = \frac{0.1567}{2.789 \times 10^{-10}} = 5.62 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Rayleigh Number**
$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = (5.62 \times 10^{8})(0.71) = 3.99 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Regime**
Since $Ra_L = 3.99 \times 10^{8} < 10^9$ → **LAMINAR FLOW**

**Step 4: Apply Laminar Correlation**
$$Nu_L = 0.59 \, Ra_L^{1/4} = 0.59 \, (3.99 \times 10^{8})^{0.25}$$

$$(3.99 \times 10^{8})^{0.25} = (3.99)^{0.25} \times (10^{8})^{0.25} = 1.4148 \times 10^{2} = 141.48$$

$$Nu_L = 0.59 \times 141.48 = 83.47$$

**Step 5: Calculate Heat Transfer Coefficient**
$$h = \frac{Nu_L \cdot k}{L} = \frac{(83.47)(0.0274)}{0.5} = 4.575 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 6: Heat Transfer Rate per Unit Width**
$$q' = h \cdot L \cdot \Delta T = (4.575)(0.5)(40) = 91.5 \text{ W/m}$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** State the **Churchill–Chu correlation** for natural convection over a vertical plate. Why is it preferred over the simpler $Nu = C \cdot Ra^{1/4}$ correlation?

**Model Answer:**
The Churchill–Chu correlation for a vertical plate is:
$$Nu_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387\, Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

**Why it is Preferred:**

1. **Wide Validity Range:** It is valid over the **entire range** from $Ra_L = 10^{-1}$ up to $Ra_L = 10^{20}$, encompassing both laminar, transitional, and turbulent regimes.

2. **Continuous Function:** It provides a **smooth continuous transition** between regimes, whereas the simple $Nu = C \cdot Ra^{1/4}$ correlation is valid only in the strict laminar range ($Ra < 10^9$).

3. **Reduced Empirical Constants:** The simple correlations use different constants for different regimes (0.59 for laminar, 0.10 for turbulent), requiring the engineer to first identify the regime. The Churchill–Chu correlation eliminates this step.

4. **Prandtl Number Effect Explicitly Included:** The $Pr$ dependence in the denominator term makes it accurate for a wide variety of fluids — including oils, gases, and liquid metals — beyond just air.

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the order-of-magnitude expression for the **thermal boundary layer thickness** for laminar natural convection flow over a vertical hot plate. Show that $\delta_t / L \sim Ra_L^{-1/4}$.

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the order-of-magnitude energy boundary layer equation (convective ~ conductive terms):

$$
\frac{U_0 \, \Delta T}{L} \sim \frac{\alpha \, \Delta T}{\delta_t^2}
$$

**Step 1:** Simplify by canceling $\Delta T$ from both sides.

$$
\frac{U_0}{L} \sim \frac{\alpha}{\delta_t^2}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\delta_t^2$.

$$
\delta_t^2 \sim \frac{\alpha L}{U_0}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the buoyancy velocity scale $U_0 = \sqrt{g \beta \Delta T \, L}$.

$$
\delta_t^2 \sim \frac{\alpha L}{\sqrt{g \beta \Delta T \, L}} = \frac{\alpha L^{1/2}}{(g \beta \Delta T)^{1/2}}
$$

**Step 4:** Square both sides.

$$
\delta_t \sim \left( \frac{\alpha^2 L}{g \beta \Delta T} \right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 5:** Introduce the Rayleigh number $Ra_L = \dfrac{g \beta \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu \alpha}$, and use the fact that $\nu \alpha = \alpha^2 \cdot Pr^{-1} \cdot Pr$ algebraically — equivalently rearrange:

$$
\frac{\alpha^2}{g \beta \Delta T \cdot L^3} \cdot L^4 = \frac{L}{Ra_L}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta_t \sim L \cdot Ra_L^{-1/4}
$$

Expressed as a non-dimensional thickness:

$$
\frac{\delta_t}{L} \sim Ra_L^{-1/4}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** As $Ra_L$ increases (more vigorous natural convection), the thermal boundary layer becomes thinner — this is consistent with stronger buoyancy forces pulling the heated fluid upward more rapidly, which thins the layer adjacent to the surface.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A horizontal hot plate of dimensions 0.6 m × 0.6 m is maintained at 80 °C with its hot surface facing **upward** in atmospheric air at 25 °C. Estimate the **total heat transfer rate** from the plate.

**Given Data:**
- Plate dimensions: $0.6 \text{ m} \times 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $T_s = 80°C = 353$ K
- $T_\infty = 25°C = 298$ K
- $\Delta T = 55$ K
- Hot surface facing **upward**

**Film Temperature:** $T_f = (353 + 298)/2 = 325.5$ K

**Air properties at 325.5 K:**
- $\nu = 18.4 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- $\alpha = 24.2 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s
- $k = 0.0285$ W/(m·K)
- $Pr = 0.71$
- $\beta = 1/325.5 = 3.072 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹

**Characteristic length for horizontal plate:**
$$L_c = \frac{A_s}{P} = \frac{(0.6)(0.6)}{4 \times 0.6} = \frac{0.36}{2.4} = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

**Step 1: Calculate Grashof Number**
$$Gr_L = \frac{(9.81)(3.072 \times 10^{-3})(55)(0.15)^3}{(18.4 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

**Numerator:** $(9.81)(3.072 \times 10^{-3})(55)(3.375 \times 10^{-3}) = 5.595 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s²
