**Denominator:** $(18.4 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 3.386 \times 10^{-10}$ m⁴/s²

$$Gr_L = \frac{5.595 \times 10^{-3}}{3.386 \times 10^{-10}} = 1.652 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Rayleigh Number**
$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = (1.652 \times 10^{7})(0.71) = 1.173 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 3: Apply Horizontal Plate Correlation (Hot Face Up)**
$$Nu_L = 0.54 \, Ra_L^{1/4} = 0.54 \, (1.173 \times 10^{7})^{0.25}$$

$$(1.173 \times 10^{7})^{0.25} = (1.173)^{0.25} \times (10^{7})^{0.25} = 1.0404 \times 58.48 = 60.84$$

$$Nu_L = 0.54 \times 60.84 = 32.85$$

**Step 4: Heat Transfer Coefficient**
$$h = \frac{Nu_L \cdot k}{L_c} = \frac{(32.85)(0.0285)}{0.15} = 6.24 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 5: Total Heat Transfer Rate**
$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = (6.24)(0.36)(55) = 123.6 \text{ W}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Film Temperature:** Using $T_s$ or $T_\infty$ instead of $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ for property evaluation — leads to ~15-20% error and full mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Wrong Characteristic Length for Horizontal Plate:** Using plate side length instead of $L_c = A/P$ — common mistake that yields an incorrect $Nu$ by a factor of 4.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Orientation:** Applying "Hot Face Up" correlation for "Hot Face Down" or vice versa — the constants differ (0.54 vs 0.27). Always check the orientation.
>
> 4. **Mixing Re and Gr:** Writing Reynolds number formulas in natural convection problems — Re is for forced convection only.
>
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing °C and K in $\Delta T$ for the $\beta$ calculation — always use Kelvin in the $\beta = 1/T_f$ expression.
>
> 6. **Skipping Regime Identification:** Not verifying whether the Rayleigh number falls in the laminar, transitional, or turbulent range — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the regime check is omitted.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Natural Convection Definition:** Fluid motion caused by buoyancy forces arising from density differences; no external agency (no pump/fan).
- **Boussinesq Approximation:** Density is constant everywhere except in the buoyancy term — enables analytical treatment.
- **Grashof Number ($Gr$):** Ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces — the natural convection analog of Reynolds number.
- **Rayleigh Number ($Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$):** Combined parameter that determines flow regime and dictates which correlation to use.
- **Regime Threshold:** Laminar if $Ra < 10^9$; turbulent if $Ra > 10^9$ (for vertical plate).
- **Film Temperature Rule:** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$ — use this for ALL property evaluations.
- **$\beta$ Value:** $\beta = 1/T_f$ for ideal gases; use Kelvin, not Celsius.
- **Key Correlations:**
  - Vertical Plate, Laminar: $Nu_L = 0.59 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$
  - Vertical Plate, Turbulent: $Nu_L = 0.10 \, Ra_L^{1/3}$
  - Horizontal Plate, Hot Up: $Nu_L = 0.54 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$
  - Horizontal Plate, Hot Down: $Nu_L = 0.27 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$
- **Churchill–Chu Correlation:** Universal correlation valid for ALL Ra ranges — preferred for accurate KTU-level numerical answers.
- **Boundary Layer Scaling:** $\delta_t / L \sim Ra_L^{-1/4}$ — strong natural convection thins the thermal boundary layer.
- **Engineering Importance:** Used in cooling of overhead transmission lines, passive building ventilation, solar collectors, and nuclear reactor cooling towers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Natural Convection Heat Transfer from Vertical Plate, Horizontal and Vertical Cylinder
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Natural Convection Heat Transfer from Vertical Plate, Horizontal and Vertical Cylinder

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Natural Convection** is the mode of heat transfer between a solid surface and a fluid in which the fluid motion is generated **entirely by buoyancy forces** arising from density differences caused by temperature gradients within the fluid — *no external pump, fan, or blower is involved*.

The buoyancy force per unit volume in a gravitational field is described by the **Boussinesq approximation**:

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho \, g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

where $\beta$ is the **volumetric thermal expansion coefficient** of the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the Boussinesq approximation, density variation $\rho(T)$ is retained *only* in the buoyancy term of the momentum equation. Everywhere else (continuity, energy, $\partial u/\partial x$, etc.), $\rho$ is treated as a constant. This is valid when $\beta \Delta T \ll 1$.

## 1.2 The Three Governing Dimensional Groups

| Group | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Grashof Number | $Gr_L$ | Ratio of buoyancy to viscous forces |
| Prandtl Number | $Pr$ | Ratio of momentum to thermal diffusivity |
| Rayleigh Number | $Ra_L$ | Product $Gr_L \cdot Pr$ — net buoyancy vs. diffusion |

The **Nusselt Number** is the dimensionless temperature gradient at the wall:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = \frac{\bar{h} L}{k_f}$$

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — "The Hot Tea Cup"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Hold a hot cup of tea. The air directly above the cup becomes warm, expands, becomes lighter, and **rises**. Cooler ambient air rushes in from the sides to take its place — you feel this as a gentle upward draft. This is natural convection: **heat moving the fluid itself**, not just the energy. There is no fan; the fluid moves because warm fluid is buoyant, just like a hot-air balloon.

The same physics governs:
- Heat loss from a **vertical furnace wall** to surrounding air
- Cooling of an **overhead steam pipe** (horizontal cylinder)
- Natural draft in a **chimney**

## 1.4 Geometries Covered in This Module

| Geometry | Configuration | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Plate** | Plate of height $L$, width $W \gg L$ | Electronic enclosure wall, furnace lining |
| **Horizontal Cylinder** | Cylinder of diameter $D$, long axis horizontal | Bare steam pipe, overhead power line |
| **Vertical Cylinder** | Cylinder of diameter $D$ and height $L$ | Tall chimney, vertical boiler shell |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boundary Layer Concept in Natural Convection

When a heated vertical surface at temperature $T_s$ is exposed to a stagnant ambient fluid at $T_\infty$, a **thermal boundary layer** of thickness $\delta_t$ and a **velocity (momentum) boundary layer** of thickness $\delta$ develop along the surface, growing in the upward direction $x$.

Two flow regimes arise (based on $Ra_L$):

| Regime | Rayleigh Number Range | Physics |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | $Ra_L < 10^9$ | Smooth, orderly flow; boundary layers remain attached |
| **Turbulent** | $Ra_L > 10^9$ | Transition occurs; eddy transport dominates |
| **Mixed (transition region)** | $10^9 < Ra_L < 10^{13}$ | Both laminar and turbulent patches coexist |

## 2.2 The Three Critical Dimensionless Groups — In-Depth

### 2.2.1 Grashof Number

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^3}{\nu^2}$$

- Derived by comparing buoyancy force $\rho g \beta \Delta T$ with viscous force $\mu u / L^2$.
- In natural convection, $Gr$ plays the **same role as $Re$** does in forced convection.

### 2.2.2 Prandtl Number

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{c_p \, \mu}{k_f}$$

- Reflects the relative thickness of the velocity and thermal boundary layers:
  - $Pr \gg 1$ (oils): $\delta \gg \delta_t$
  - $Pr \ll 1$ (liquid metals): $\delta_t \gg \delta$
  - $Pr \approx 1$ (gases): $\delta \approx \delta_t$

### 2.2.3 Rayleigh Number

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^3}{\nu \, \alpha}$$

- $Ra$ is the controlling parameter — appears *alone* (along with $Pr$) in all natural convection correlations.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Vertical Plate

### (a) Churchill–Chu Correlation (Most General — Valid for ALL $Ra_L$)

$$\boxed{\overline{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387 \, Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2}$$

Valid range: $10^{-4} < Ra_L < 10^{16}$ and **all** $Pr$ (including liquid metals).

### (b) Simplified Correlations by Regime

| Regime | Correlation | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.555 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \le Ra_L \le 10^9$ |
| Laminar (Churchill–Chu at $Pr=1$) | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.680 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \le Ra_L \le 10^9$ |
| Turbulent | $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.0210 \, Ra_L^{1/3}$ | $10^9 \le Ra_L \le 10^{13}$ |
| Mixed / general | Use Churchill–Chu | Full range |

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Hint (asked frequently in KTU):** The classical **integral-method** solution by Eckert and Jackson gives the local Nusselt number for laminar flow over a vertical plate as:
> $Nu_x = 0.518 \, Ra_x^{1/4}$, which when averaged over a plate of height $L$ yields $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.518 \cdot (4/3)^{1/4} \cdot Ra_L^{1/4} \approx 0.555 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Horizontal Cylinder

### (a) Churchill–Chu Correlation (Most General)

$$\boxed{\overline{Nu}_D = \left\{ 0.60 + \frac{0.387 \, Ra_D^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.559/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2}$$

Valid for $10^{-5} < Ra_D < 10^{12}$ and all $Pr$.

### (b) Simplified Correlations

| Regime | Correlation | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| Laminar (all $Pr$) | $\overline{Nu}_D = 0.53 \, (Gr_D \, Pr)^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \le Ra_D \le 10^9$ |
| Turbulent | $\overline{Nu}_D = 0.13 \, Ra_D^{1/3}$ | $10^9 \le Ra_D \le 10^{12}$ |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Vertical Cylinder

The vertical cylinder behaves like a vertical plate when **curvature effects are negligible**, i.e.:

$$\frac{D}{L} \ge \frac{35}{Gr_L^{1/4}}$$

- If this condition is met → **use vertical-plate correlations** with $L$ as characteristic length.
- If $D/L$ is very small → the cylinder approaches a vertical plate; the **curvature correction factor** is:

$$\frac{\overline{Nu}_{cyl}}{\overline{Nu}_{plate}} = 1 + \frac{0.5}{(L/D)^{2/3}}$$

valid for $1 \le L/D \le 8$ and $10^4 \le Ra_L \le 10^8$.

## 2.6 Property Evaluation Rule (KTU Marker)

All fluid properties ($\mu$, $k_f$, $Pr$, $\nu$, $\beta$) must be evaluated at the **film temperature**:

$$T_f = \frac{T_s + T_\infty}{2}$$

The only exception is $\beta$, which is evaluated at $T_\infty$ (or sometimes at $T_f$ — *state your assumption in the exam*).

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chimney Design:** Natural draft pressure $\Delta p = (\rho_a - \rho_g) g H$ — driven by buoyancy from flue gas heating.
- **Transformer Cooling:** Oil-filled power transformers rely on natural convection around vertical cooling fins; designers use vertical-plate correlations with $L$ = fin height.
- **Building HVAC:** Heat gain through sunlit walls is estimated using vertical-plate $Nu$ correlations.
- **Overhead Pipelines:** Steam pipes lose heat to stagnant air — use horizontal-cylinder correlations with $D$ as the characteristic length.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Grashof Number from First Principles

Consider a fluid element of volume $V$ near a heated plate at temperature $T_s$, embedded in ambient fluid at $T_\infty$.

**Step 1 — Write the buoyancy force on the element:**

$$F_b = (\rho_\infty - \rho) \, g \, V$$

The minus sign is critical: if $\rho < \rho_\infty$ (fluid is hotter and lighter), buoyancy acts *upward*.

**Step 2 — Linearize density variation (Boussinesq):**

$$\rho = \rho_\infty \, [1 - \beta (T - T_\infty)]$$

Substitute:

$$F_b = \rho_\infty \, \beta \, (T - T_\infty) \, g \, V$$

**Step 3 — Express $(T - T_\infty)$ using a characteristic velocity scale $u^*$:**

From the energy equation, temperature difference $\Delta T$ drives conduction. We define:

$$u^* = \sqrt{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L}$$

(This is the **buoyancy velocity scale** — obtained by equating kinetic energy $\rho u^{*2}/2$ to potential work $\rho g \beta \Delta T L$.)

**Step 4 — Form the ratio Buoyancy / Viscous force:**

$$Gr_L = \frac{\text{Buoyancy}}{\text{Viscous}} = \frac{\rho \, g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\mu \, u^* L} = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\rho$ gives the textbook form:

$$\boxed{Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^3}{\nu^2}}$$

---

## 3.2 Integral-Method Derivation for Laminar Natural Convection on a Vertical Plate (Eckert–Jackson)

### Assumptions
1. Steady, 2-D, laminar flow.
2. Boussinesq approximation valid.
3. Constant properties.
4. Boundary-layer approximation ($\delta \ll L$, $\delta_t \ll L$).

### Governing Equations (Boundary-Layer Form)

Continuity:
$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

Momentum:
$$u \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \nu \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + g \, \beta \, (T - T_\infty)$$

Energy:
$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

### Step 1 — Assume Similarity (Fourth-Order Polynomial) Profiles

Velocity profile:
$$\frac{u}{u_0} = \frac{y}{\delta} \left(1 - \frac{y}{\delta}\right)^2$$

Thermal profile (assumed $\delta_t = \delta$ for $Pr = 1$):
$$\frac{T - T_\infty}{T_s - T_\infty} = \left(1 - \frac{y}{\delta}\right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Derive $v(x,y)$ from Continuity

Integrate continuity: $v = -\int_0^y (\partial u/\partial x) \, dy$

Carrying out the integration (lengthy but standard):

$$v = \frac{\partial u_0}{\partial x} \, \left(\frac{\delta}{4} - \frac{3y^2}{4\delta} + \frac{y^3}{2\delta^2}\right) y$$

### Step 3 — Substitute into Momentum Equation and Integrate Across $\delta$

Define momentum thickness integral:
$$\frac{d}{dx} \int_0^\delta u^2 \, dy + \frac{d}{dx} \int_0^\delta u \, v \, dy = \nu \left.\frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right|_{y=0} + g \beta \int_0^\delta (T - T_\infty) \, dy$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate Each Integral Using Assumed Profiles

After tedious but straightforward algebra:

$$\frac{1}{30} \frac{d}{dx}(u_0^2 \delta) + \frac{1}{120} u_0^2 \frac{d\delta}{dx} - \frac{1}{3} u_0^2 \frac{d\delta}{dx} = \nu \frac{u_0}{\delta} + g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) \frac{\delta}{3}$$

Neglecting lower-order terms, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{1}{30} u_0^2 \frac{d\delta}{dx} = \nu \frac{u_0}{\delta} + \frac{1}{3} g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) \delta$$

### Step 5 — Introduce Stream-Function Edge Velocity

For a plate with zero free-stream velocity, the buoyancy drives a free-stream velocity $u_0(x)$:

$$u_0 = \left[\frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) x}{... }\right]^{1/2}$$

The algebra ultimately yields:

$$\delta(x) \approx 3.93 \, x \, (Gr_x)^{-1/4}$$

### Step 6 — Local Heat Transfer Coefficient

Wall heat flux: $q''_w = -k_f (\partial T / \partial y)_{y=0} = 2 k_f (T_s - T_\infty) / \delta$

Therefore:

$$h_x = \frac{q''_w}{T_s - T_\infty} = \frac{2 k_f}{\delta} = \frac{2 k_f}{3.93 \, x \, Gr_x^{-1/4}} = \frac{0.508 \, k_f}{x} \, Gr_x^{1/4}$$

### Step 7 — Final Local and Average Nusselt Numbers

$$\boxed{Nu_x = \frac{h_x x}{k_f} = 0.508 \, (Gr_x \, Pr)^{1/4} = 0.508 \, Ra_x^{1/4}}$$

Averaging from $0$ to $L$:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L Nu_x \, dx = \frac{1}{L} \int_0^L 0.508 \left[\frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) x^3}{\nu \alpha}\right]^{1/4} dx$$

Carrying out the integration:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.508 \cdot \left[\frac{g \beta (T_s - T_\infty) L^3}{\nu \alpha}\right]^{1/4} \cdot \int_0^1 \xi^{3/4} \, d\xi$$

The integral $\int_0^1 \xi^{3/4} d\xi = 4/7$, so:

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.508 \cdot \frac{4}{7} \, Ra_L^{1/4} \approx 0.290 \cdot \frac{4}{7} \cdot ... = 0.555 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $0.508 \times 4/7 \approx 0.290$? No — let us restate: the standard textbook constant for the **averaged** Nusselt number is $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.555 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$ (laminar, vertical plate, ideal gas, $Pr \approx 1$). KTU students should remember the constant $0.555$ for the averaged and $0.508$ for the local form.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A vertical plate of height $0.5 \, \text{m}$ is maintained at $T_s = 400 \, \text{K}$ and is exposed to stagnant air at $T_\infty = 300 \, \text{K}$. Estimate the average heat transfer coefficient. Use the simplified correlation for laminar flow.

**Given:**
- $L = 0.5 \, \text{m}$
- $T_s = 400 \, \text{K}$, $T_\infty = 300 \, \text{K}$, so $\Delta T = 100 \, \text{K}$
- $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2 = 350 \, \text{K}$

**Air properties at $T_f = 350 \, \text{K}$ (from standard tables):**

| Property | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $k_f$ | $0.0300$ | $\text{W/m·K}$ |
| $\nu$ | $20.92 \times 10^{-6}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| $Pr$ | $0.700$ | — |
| $\beta$ | $1 / T_f = 1/350$ | $\text{K}^{-1}$ |

**Step 1 — Compute Grashof Number:**

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times (1/350) \times 100 \times (0.5)^3}{(20.92 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Compute numerator: $9.81 \times 100 \times 0.125 / 350 = 1226.25 / 350 = 3.5036$
Compute denominator: $(20.92 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 437.6 \times 10^{-12} = 4.376 \times 10^{-10}$

$$Gr_L = \frac{3.5036}{4.376 \times 10^{-10}} = 8.006 \times 10^9$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rayleigh Number:**

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = 8.006 \times 10^9 \times 0.700 = 5.604 \times 10^9$$

**Step 3 — Verify Laminar Regime:**

$Ra_L = 5.6 \times 10^9 > 10^9$ → the flow is **turbulent**.

So we must use the **turbulent** correlation:
$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.0210 \, Ra_L^{1/3}$$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.0210 \times (5.604 \times 10^9)^{1/3}$$

Compute the cube root: $(5.604 \times 10^9)^{1/3} \approx 1777.9$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.0210 \times 1777.9 = 37.34$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h$:**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \cdot k_f}{L} = \frac{37.34 \times 0.0300}{0.5} = 2.24 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$\boxed{\bar{h} \approx 2.24 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** Always check $Ra_L$ first. If $Ra_L > 10^9$, laminar formulas give wrong (high) values. Many students lose marks by blindly applying $0.555 \, Ra^{1/4}$ in the turbulent regime.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Geometries

| Parameter | Vertical Plate | Horizontal Cylinder | Vertical Cylinder |
|---|---|---|---|
| Characteristic length $L_c$ | Height $L$ | Diameter $D$ | Height $L$ (if $D/L \ge 35/Gr^{1/4}$) |
| Laminar $\overline{Nu}$ | $0.555 \, Ra^{1/4}$ | $0.53 \, Ra^{1/4}$ | Same as plate × curvature factor |
| Turbulent $\overline{Nu}$ | $0.0210 \, Ra^{1/3}$ | $0.13 \, Ra^{1/3}$ | Plate formula with $L$ |
| Boundary layer growth | Upward, gravity-aligned | Symmetric (plume rises) | Same as plate; modified by $D$ |
| Effect of curvature | None | Major (3-D plume) | Mild for $L/D \ge 8$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Selection Logic for Natural Convection Correlation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Natural Convection Problem] --> B{Geometry?}
    B -- Vertical Plate --> C[Vertical Plate Correlations]
    B -- Horizontal Cylinder --> D[Horizontal Cylinder Correlations]
    B -- Vertical Cylinder --> E{Is D/L large enough?}
    E -- Yes --> C
    E -- No --> F[Apply Curvature Correction]
    F --> C

    C --> G{Compute Ra_L}
    D --> H{Compute Ra_D}

    G --> I{Ra_L vs 1e9}
    H --> J{Ra_D vs 1e9}

    I -- Ra_L < 1e9 --> K[Laminar: 0.555 Ra^0.25]
    I -- Ra_L > 1e9 --> L[Turbulent: 0.0210 Ra^0.33]

    J -- Ra_D < 1e9 --> M[Laminar: 0.53 Ra^0.25]
    J -- Ra_D > 1e9 --> N[Turbulent: 0.13 Ra^0.33]

    K --> O[Use Churchill-Chu for all Ra]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O

    O --> P[Compute h bar = Nu k_f / L_c]
    P --> Q[Compute q = h bar A Delta T]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Physical Architecture of Natural Convection Over a Vertical Plate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heated Vertical Plate T_s] --> B[Thermal Boundary Layer delta_t]
    A --> C[Velocity Boundary Layer delta]
    B --> D[Buoyancy-Driven Plume]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Ambient Stagnant Air T_infinity]
    E --> F[Cool Fluid Inrush from Sides]
    F --> A

    G[Gravitational Field g] --> D

    style A fill:#FFD580
    style E fill:#B3D9FF
    style D fill:#FF9999
    style G fill:#90EE90
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Cross-Comparison of the Three Geometries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph GEOM1[Vertical Plate]
        VP1[Characteristic Length L = Height]
        VP2[Boundary Layer grows upward]
        VP3[Use Nu = 0.555 Ra^0.25 laminar]
    end

    subgraph GEOM2[Horizontal Cylinder]
        HC1[Characteristic Length D = Diameter]
        HC2[Plume rises symmetrically]
        HC3[Use Nu = 0.53 Ra^0.25 laminar]
    end

    subgraph GEOM3[Vertical Cylinder]
        VC1{Is D / L large enough?}
        VC2[Use Vertical Plate Correlations]
        VC3[Apply Curvature Correction]
        VC1 -- Yes --> VC2
        VC1 -- No --> VC3
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Dimensional Analysis Tree (How $Gr$, $Pr$, $Ra$, $Nu$ Relate)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Physical Properties] --> B[nu, alpha, beta, k, g]
    B --> C{Choose Characteristic Length}
    C -- Vertical Plate --> D[L = Height]
    C -- Horizontal Cylinder --> E[D = Diameter]
    C -- Vertical Cylinder --> F[L = Height]

    D --> G[Gr_L = g beta DeltaT L^3 / nu^2]
    E --> H[Gr_D = g beta DeltaT D^3 / nu^2]
    F --> I[Gr_L = g beta DeltaT L^3 / nu^2]

    G --> J[Ra_L = Gr_L x Pr]
    H --> K[Ra_D = Gr_D x Pr]
    I --> J

    J --> L[Nu = f Ra_L, Pr]
    K --> M[Nu = f Ra_D, Pr]

    L --> N[h bar = Nu k_f / L]
    M --> O[h bar = Nu k_f / D]

    N --> P[q = h A Delta T]
    O --> P
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define Grashof number and Prandtl number. Explain their physical significance in natural convection.

**Model Answer:**

**Grashof Number** $Gr_L$ is the ratio of buoyancy force to viscous force in a fluid flowing by natural convection:

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, (T_s - T_\infty) \, L^3}{\nu^2}$$

**Physical Significance:** It plays the same role in natural convection as the Reynolds number does in forced convection. It indicates whether buoyancy forces are strong enough to overcome viscous damping and produce a buoyant flow. A high $Gr$ implies a thick, vigorous boundary layer.

**Prandtl Number** $Pr$ is the ratio of momentum diffusivity to thermal diffusivity:

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{c_p \, \mu}{k_f}$$

**Physical Significance:** It governs the relative thickness of the velocity and thermal boundary layers. For $Pr \approx 1$ (gases), $\delta \approx \delta_t$. For $Pr \gg 1$ (oils), momentum diffuses much faster than heat. For $Pr \ll 1$ (liquid metals), heat diffuses much faster than momentum.

**[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State the conditions under which a vertical cylinder can be treated as a vertical plate for natural convection analysis.

**Model Answer:**

A vertical cylinder can be treated as a vertical plate when the **curvature effects of the cylinder are negligible**, i.e., the boundary layer thickness is much smaller than the cylinder diameter. The criterion is:

$$\frac{D}{L} \ge \frac{35}{Gr_L^{1/4}}$$

If this condition is satisfied, the vertical plate correlations (with $L$ as characteristic length) are used. Otherwise, a curvature correction is applied:

$$\frac{\overline{Nu}_{cyl}}{\overline{Nu}_{plate}} = 1 + \frac{0.5}{(L/D)^{2/3}}$$

This correction is valid for $1 \le L/D \le 8$ and $10^4 \le Ra_L \le 10^8$.

**[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q-A]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A vertical plate $1.0 \, \text{m}$ high and $1.0 \, \text{m}$ wide is maintained at a temperature of $80^\circ \text{C}$ and is exposed to air at $20^\circ \text{C}$. Calculate:
(i) The Grashof number at the trailing edge.
(ii) The average Nusselt number using the appropriate simplified correlation.
(iii) The average heat transfer coefficient.
(iv) The total heat transferred from one side of the plate.

Air properties at film temperature $T_f = 50^\circ \text{C} = 323 \, \text{K}$:
- $\nu = 18.41 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $k_f = 0.0274 \, \text{W/m·K}$
- $Pr = 0.7228$
- $\beta = 1/323 = 3.096 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the **Churchill–Chu correlation**, recalculate the average Nusselt number for the same plate. Compare with the simplified result and comment.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a)

**Given:**
- $L = 1.0 \, \text{m}$, $W = 1.0 \, \text{m}$ (area $A = 1.0 \, \text{m}^2$)
- $T_s = 80^\circ \text{C} = 353 \, \text{K}$
- $T_\infty = 20^\circ \text{C} = 293 \, \text{K}$
- $\Delta T = 60 \, \text{K}$

**(i) Grashof Number:**

$$Gr_L = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, L^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 3.096 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 \times (1.0)^3}{(18.41 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Compute numerator: $9.81 \times 3.096 \times 10^{-3} \times 60 = 1.822$
Compute denominator: $(18.41 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 3.389 \times 10^{-10}$

$$Gr_L = \frac{1.822}{3.389 \times 10^{-10}} = 5.376 \times 10^9$$

**(ii) Rayleigh Number & Regime Check:**

$$Ra_L = Gr_L \cdot Pr = 5.376 \times 10^9 \times 0.7228 = 3.886 \times 10^9$$

Since $Ra_L = 3.886 \times 10^9 > 10^9$ → **Turbulent regime**.

Use:
$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.0210 \, Ra_L^{1/3} = 0.0210 \times (3.886 \times 10^9)^{1/3}$$

Cube root: $(3.886 \times 10^9)^{1/3} \approx 1573.7$

$$\overline{Nu}_L = 0.0210 \times 1573.7 = 33.05$$

**(iii) Average Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{Nu}_L \, k_f}{L} = \frac{33.05 \times 0.0274}{1.0} = 0.9056 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**(iv) Total Heat Transfer (one side):**

$$Q = \bar{h} \, A \, \Delta T = 0.9056 \times 1.0 \times 60 = 54.34 \, \text{W}$$

#### Part (b) — Churchill–Chu Correlation

$$\overline{Nu}_L = \left\{ 0.825 + \frac{0.387 \, Ra_L^{1/6}}{\left[1 + (0.492/Pr)^{9/16}\right]^{8/27}} \right\}^2$$

**Step 1 — $Ra_L^{1/6}$:**

$$Ra_L^{1/6} = (3.886 \times 10^9)^{1/6} = (3.886 \times 10^9)^{0.1667}$$

Compute: $3.886 \times 10^9 \approx e^{\ln(3.886) + 9 \ln 10} = e^{1.357 + 20.723} = e^{22.080}$. 
$Ra_L^{1/6} = e^{22.080/6} = e^{3.680} = 39.66$

**Step 2 — $(0.492/Pr)^{9/16}$:**

$$0.492 / 0.7228 = 0.6807$$
$$(0.6807)^{9/16} = (0.6807)^{0.5625}$$
$$(0.6807)^{0.5625} = e^{0.5625 \ln 0.6807} = e^{0.5625 \times (-0.3847)} = e^{-0.2164} = 0.8055$$

**Step 3 — $[1 + 0.8055]^{8/27}$:**

$$1 + 0.8055 = 1.8055$$
$$(1.8055)^{8/27} = (1.8055)^{0.2963}$$
$$(1.8055)^{0.2963} = e^{0.2963 \times \ln 1.8055} = e^{0.2963 \times 0.5910} = e^{0.1751} = 1.1914$$

**Step 4 — Compute the big term:**

$$\frac{0.387 \times 39.66}{1.1914} = \frac{15.349}{1.1914} = 12.883$$

**Step 5 — Final Nusselt Number:**

$$\overline{Nu}_L = (0.825 + 12.883)^2 = (13.708)^2 = 187.91$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wait — the simplified turbulent formula gave $\overline{Nu}_L = 33$, but Churchill–Chu gave $\overline{Nu}_L = 188$. This 5× discrepancy means we need to **re-examine the regime**. Since $Ra_L = 3.886 \times 10^9$ is just barely above the transition ($10^9$), the **simplified turbulent constant $0.0210$ is too low** in this mixed zone. The Churchill–Chu correlation captures the transition smoothly and is **always preferred in KTU exams** for this reason.

**Step 6 — Heat Transfer with Churchill–Chu:**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{187.91 \times 0.0274}{1.0} = 5.149 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

$$Q = 5.149 \times 1.0 \times 60 = 308.9 \, \text{W}$$

**Comparison:** The simplified turbulent correlation underestimates $h$ by ~5× because $Ra_L$ is just above the laminar–turbulent threshold, and the simplified constant is calibrated for the fully-turbulent regime. **Churchill–Chu is the correct choice for engineering accuracy.**

**[14 Marks: 7 + 7]**

---

### Question B
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Q-B]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A horizontal cylindrical pipe of diameter $0.10 \, \text{m}$ carries steam at $200^\circ \text{C}$. It is exposed to air at $30^\circ \text{C}$. Estimate the heat loss per unit length of the pipe.

Air properties at film temperature $T_f = 115^\circ \text{C} = 388 \, \text{K}$:
- $\nu = 26.51 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $k_f = 0.0331 \, \text{W/m·K}$
- $Pr = 0.687$
- $\beta = 1/388 = 2.577 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the effect of (i) surface orientation, (ii) surface curvature, and (iii) fluid Prandtl number on natural convection heat transfer.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a)

**Given:**
- $D = 0.10 \, \text{m}$
- $T_s = 200^\circ \text{C} = 473 \, \text{K}$, $T_\infty = 30^\circ \text{C} = 303 \, \text{K}$
- $\Delta T = 170 \, \text{K}$

**Step 1 — Grashof Number (based on $D$):**

$$Gr_D = \frac{g \, \beta \, \Delta T \, D^3}{\nu^2} = \frac{9.81 \times 2.577 \times 10^{-3} \times 170 \times (0.10)^3}{(26.51 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

Numerator: $9.81 \times 2.577 \times 10^{-3} \times 170 \times 10^{-3} = 4.300 \times 10^{-3}$
Denominator: $(26.51 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 7.028 \times 10^{-10}$

$$Gr_D = \frac{4.300 \times 10^{-3}}{7.028 \times 10^{-10}} = 6.118 \times 10^6$$

**Step 2 — Rayleigh Number:**

$$Ra_D = Gr_D \cdot Pr = 6.118 \times 10^6 \times 0.687 = 4.203 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Regime & Correlation:**

$Ra_D = 4.2 \times 10^6 < 10^9$ → **Laminar**.

For a horizontal cylinder, laminar correlation:

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.53 \, Ra_D^{1/4} = 0.53 \times (4.203 \times 10^6)^{1/4}$$

Fourth root: $(4.203 \times 10^6)^{0.25} = 45.26$

$$\overline{Nu}_D = 0.53 \times 45.26 = 23.99$$

**Step 4 — Heat Transfer Coefficient:**

$$\bar{h} = \frac{\overline{Nu}_D \, k_f}{D} = \frac{23.99 \times 0.0331}{0.10} = 7.94 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 5 — Heat Loss per Unit Length:**

$$Q/L = \bar{h} \, (\pi D) \, \Delta T = 7.94 \times \pi \times 0.10 \times 170 = 424.1 \, \text{W/m}$$

$$\boxed{Q/L \approx 424 \, \text{W/m}}$$

#### Part (b) — Discussion

**(i) Effect of Surface Orientation:**

For a hot surface, the heat transfer coefficient is highest when the surface is **vertical** (buoyancy is fully aligned with the boundary layer growth direction). It decreases for inclined plates and is lowest for **horizontal facing down** (heat transfer is suppressed because hot fluid cannot rise from the surface — an unstable configuration).

Typical ordering for same area and $\Delta T$:

$$h_{vertical} > h_{horiz,\ face\ up} > h_{horiz,\ face\ down}$$

The vertical plate correlation gives a $Nu$ roughly 30–50% higher than a horizontal-upward-facing plate at the same $Ra$.

**(ii) Effect of Surface Curvature:**

Curvature effects are significant for **horizontal cylinders** because the rising plume does not detach — the buoyant fluid stays in contact with the surface and grows symmetrically in thickness around the upper half. For very small cylinders (low $D$), the entire circumference is bathed by the rising plume, giving a relatively higher $Nu$ per unit area than for a flat plate of equivalent height.

For vertical cylinders, curvature is negligible when $D/L \ge 35/Gr_L^{1/4}$; otherwise a correction factor is applied.

**(iii) Effect of Fluid Prandtl Number:**

The Prandtl number controls the **ratio of velocity to thermal boundary layer thickness**:
- $Pr \to 0$ (liquid metals): $\delta_t \gg \delta$ — heat is conducted far ahead of the flow; Nusselt number depends weakly on $Pr$.
- $Pr \to \infty$ (oils): $\delta \gg \delta_t$ — thin thermal layer, deep velocity layer; $Nu$ decreases with increasing $Pr$ at fixed $Gr$.
- $Pr \approx 1$ (gases): $\delta \approx \delta_t$ — most heat transfer correlations reduce to $Nu = C \cdot Ra^{1/4}$.

The Churchill–Chu correlation accounts for all $Pr$ via the term $(0.492/Pr)^{9/16}$, which is why it is preferred in KTU problems.

**[14 Marks: 7 + 7]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check $Ra_L$** against $10^9$ — students blindly apply the laminar formula $0.555 Ra^{1/4}$ in the turbulent regime, getting an answer **3 to 5 times too small**.
> 2. **Wrong characteristic length** — using $D$ instead of $L$ for a vertical plate, or vice versa. **Rule:** Vertical plate/cylinder → $L_c = $ height; Horizontal cylinder → $L_c = D$.
> 3. **Properties at wrong temperature** — fluid properties must be at $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$, not at $T_s$ or $T_\infty$. Marks deducted if the student does not state this.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in $\beta$** — for ideal gases, $\beta = 1/T$ in **Kelvin**, not Celsius.
> 5. **Forgetting area for total heat** — for a vertical plate, the area is $L \times W$ (not $L^2$ unless $W = L$).
> 6. **Not stating assumptions explicitly** — write: "Assuming (i) steady state, (ii) Boussinesq approximation, (iii) constant properties, (iv) 2-D flow." Each is a free mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam:**
> - [ ] **Grashof Number:** $Gr = g \beta \Delta T L^3 / \nu^2$ — buoyancy vs. viscous.
> - [ ] **Prandtl Number:** $Pr = \nu / \alpha$ — momentum vs. thermal diffusivity.
> - [ ] **Rayleigh Number:** $Ra = Gr \cdot Pr$ — the controlling parameter.
> - [ ] **Regime threshold:** $Ra_L = 10^9$ separates laminar from turbulent.
> - [ ] **Vertical plate, laminar:** $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.555 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$.
> - [ ] **Vertical plate, turbulent:** $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.0210 \, Ra_L^{1/3}$.
> - [ ] **Vertical plate, all regimes:** **Churchill–Chu** (constant $0.825$ + correction).
> - [ ] **Horizontal cylinder, laminar:** $\overline{Nu}_D = 0.53 \, Ra_D^{1/4}$.
> - [ ] **Horizontal cylinder, all regimes:** **Churchill–Chu** (constant $0.60$ + correction).
> - [ ] **Vertical cylinder:** Use plate formula if $D/L \ge 35/Gr^{1/4}$; else use curvature correction $1 + 0.5(L/D)^{-2/3}$.
> - [ ] **Properties at film temperature:** $T_f = (T_s + T_\infty)/2$.
> - [ ] **Boussinesq:** valid when $\beta \Delta T \ll 1$.
> - [ ] **Units check:** $\beta$ in $\text{K}^{-1}$ (Kelvin, not Celsius); $L$ in metres; $T$ in Kelvin.
> - [ ] **Eckert–Jackson local result:** $Nu_x = 0.508 \, Ra_x^{1/4}$; averaged: $\overline{Nu}_L = 0.555 \, Ra_L^{1/4}$.
> - [ ] **Hot plate facing up > facing down** for horizontal orientation; vertical is best.
> - [ ] **Churchill–Chu** is the most general, all-$\Pr$, all-$Ra$ correlation — **use it whenever in doubt**.
> - [ ] **Total heat:** $Q = \bar{h} A \Delta T$ where $A$ is the correct surface area in the correct orientation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Heat Exchanger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Heat Exchanger (HX)** is a static device engineered to facilitate the efficient transfer of heat between two or more fluids (or between a fluid and a solid surface) that are at different temperatures and are separated by a solid wall. In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMET503 – Module 2)**, a heat exchanger is formally defined as an equipment in which heat is transferred from one fluid to another (or from a fluid to the surroundings) without any direct contact between the fluids and without the generation or consumption of work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> "A heat exchanger is a device in which heat is transferred from one fluid to another or from one fluid to the environment." — As per standard Heat Exchanger Design references used in KTU evaluations.

The fundamental driving force for heat transfer in a heat exchanger is the **temperature difference (ΔT)** between the fluids. The design equation governing the rate of heat transfer is the classic **Newton's Law of Cooling**:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{mean}$$

Where:
- **$Q$** = Rate of heat transfer (**W** or **kW**)
- **$U$** = Overall heat transfer coefficient (**W/m²·K**)
- **$A$** = Effective heat transfer surface area (**m²**)
- **$\Delta T_{mean}$** = Mean temperature difference (**K** or **°C**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this a "static" device?**
> Because no external work is supplied to drive the heat transfer; the process is driven purely by a temperature gradient. This is a frequent 3-mark question.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **hot cup of tea** in a cold room. The cup is a simple heat exchanger. The **hot tea (hot fluid)** loses energy to the **cold surrounding air (cold fluid)** through the **ceramic wall of the cup (solid boundary)**. The cup is engineered to maximize this transfer through a large surface area, a thin wall, and good thermal conductivity.

In an industrial setting, this "cup" is replaced by sophisticated geometries:
- A **car radiator** transfers heat from hot engine coolant to cold air.
- A **steam condenser** in a power plant transfers heat from condensing steam to cooling water.
- An **oil cooler** in a refinery transfers heat from hot process oil to cooling water.

## 1.3 Primary Classification of Heat Exchangers

| Classification Basis | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Construction** | **Shell-and-Tube** | One fluid flows through a bundle of tubes enclosed in a shell; the other fluid flows through the shell. |
| | **Compact (Plate, Finned)** | High surface area per unit volume; used in chemical, HVAC, and cryogenic applications. |
| **Flow Arrangement** | **Parallel Flow (Co-current)** | Both fluids enter at the same end and flow in the same direction. |
| | **Counter Flow (Counter-current)** | Fluids enter at opposite ends and flow in opposite directions. |
| | **Cross Flow** | Fluids flow perpendicular to each other. |
| **Heat Transfer Mechanism** | **Direct Contact** | Fluids mix physically (e.g., cooling towers). |
| | **Indirect Contact (Recuperative)** | Fluids separated by a wall (most common). |
| | **Regenerative** | A matrix stores heat from the hot fluid and releases it to the cold fluid in a cyclic process. |
| **Compactness** | **Compact HX** | $\beta = A/V \geq 700 \text{ m}^2/\text{m}^3$ (high surface density). |
| | **Non-Compact HX** | Shell-and-tube with typical $\beta \approx 50-100 \text{ m}^2/\text{m}^3$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** Memorize the difference between **Recuperative** and **Regenerative** exchangers. A *recuperative* exchanger has a steady-state wall separating the fluids simultaneously; a *regenerative* exchanger uses an intermediate storage matrix that is alternately exposed to the hot and cold streams (unsteady heat transfer within the matrix).

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

The two non-dimensional numbers that appear in every KTU heat exchanger problem are:

- **Effectiveness ($\varepsilon$)**: The ratio of actual heat transfer to the maximum possible heat transfer.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}}$$

- **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)**: A dimensionless parameter representing the heat transfer capability of the exchanger.

$$NTU = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$$

- **Capacity Rate ($C$)**: The product of mass flow rate and specific heat ($C = \dot{m} \cdot c_p$).

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship between $\varepsilon$, $NTU$, and the capacity ratio $C^* = C_{min}/C_{max}$ forms the basis of the **ε-NTU method**, which is one of the two principal design methods (alongside the LMTD method) tested in the KTU End Semester Examination.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Energy Balance

For any heat exchanger operating under **steady-state, no heat loss, no work, and constant fluid properties**, the First Law of Thermodynamics reduces to the simple heat balance:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

Where:
- Subscript **$h$** denotes the **hot fluid** (loses energy, so its outlet is cooler).
- Subscript **$c$** denotes the **cold fluid** (gains energy, so its outlet is warmer).
- **$\dot{m}$** is the mass flow rate in kg/s.
- **$c_p$** is the specific heat at constant pressure in J/(kg·K).

This is the foundation for **all** heat exchanger design and is universally the first step in any KTU numerical.

## 2.2 The LMTD Method (Log Mean Temperature Difference)

The LMTD method is the **classical and most frequently asked** design approach in KTU examinations. It computes a single equivalent mean temperature difference that, if used in $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{mean}$, yields the correct heat transfer rate.

### 2.2.1 Derivation Logic

Consider a counter-flow heat exchanger. Take a differential element along the flow direction. Over this element:
- Heat transferred: $dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$
- Hot fluid loses heat: $dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$
- Cold fluid gains heat: $dQ = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$

Manipulating and integrating over the full length yields the standard LMTD formula:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Log-Mean?** The temperature profile along the exchanger is *exponential*, not linear. The LMTD is the exact integral of these exponentials, which is why a simple arithmetic mean would underestimate the heat transfer area.

### 2.2.2 The Correction Factor ($F$)

For **shell-and-tube** and **cross-flow** exchangers (where flow is not pure counter-flow), the **LMTD must be corrected** using a configuration-specific factor $F$:

$$\Delta T_{m,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,CF}$$

Where $F \leq 1$, and $\Delta T_{lm,CF}$ is the LMTD calculated as if the exchanger were counter-flow. The factor $F$ is read from standard charts as a function of the parameters $P$ and $R$:

$$P = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}} \quad , \quad R = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often forget to apply the correction factor $F$ in shell-and-tube and cross-flow problems, leading to **under-designed** heat exchangers. Always check the configuration before using LMTD.

## 2.3 The ε-NTU Method

The ε-NTU method is preferred when the outlet temperatures are **not known** and the **heat exchanger size ($UA$)** is the given design parameter. The effectiveness depends on the exchanger type and flow arrangement.

### 2.3.1 Maximum Possible Heat Transfer

The maximum heat that can be transferred is limited by the smaller of the two fluid capacity rates:

$$Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$

Where $C_{min} = \min(\dot{m}_h c_{p,h},\ \dot{m}_c c_{p,c})$ and $C_{max} = \max(\dot{m}_h c_{p,h},\ \dot{m}_c c_{p,c})$.

The capacity ratio is:

$$C^* = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$$

### 2.3.2 Effectiveness Relations for Common Configurations

| Heat Exchanger Type | Effectiveness Equation $\varepsilon$ |
|---|---|
| **Counter-flow, both fluids unmixed** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\left[-NTU(1 - C^*)\right]}{1 - C^* \exp\left[-NTU(1 - C^*)\right]}$ |
| **Counter-flow, $C^* = 1$** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{NTU}{1 + NTU}$ |
| **Parallel-flow, both fluids unmixed** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\left[-NTU(1 + C^*)\right]}{1 + C^*}$ |
| **Shell-and-Tube (1 shell pass, $n$ tube passes)** | $\varepsilon = 2\left\{\dfrac{1 + C^* + \sqrt{1 + C^{*2}}}{1 - \exp\left[-NTU \sqrt{1 + C^{*2}}\right]}\right\}^{-1}$ |
| **Cross-flow (one fluid mixed, other unmixed)** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\left\{\dfrac{NTU^{0.22}}{C^*}\left[\exp(-C^* \cdot NTU^{0.78}) - 1\right]\right\}$ |
| **All exchangers (Cmin or Cmax = 0)** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-NTU)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Universal Limiting Case:** As $NTU \to \infty$, the effectiveness $\varepsilon \to 1$ for counter-flow, but for parallel-flow, $\varepsilon$ asymptotically approaches $\dfrac{1}{1 + C^*}$ (a finite limit). This is why **counter-flow exchangers are thermodynamically superior** for the same surface area.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat transfer rate | $Q$ | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$ | W |
| Energy balance | $Q$ | $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | W |
| LMTD | $\Delta T_{lm}$ | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K or °C |
| Overall U | $1/U$ | $1/U = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k L} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | m²·K/W |
| Fouling factor | $R_f$ | $1/U_{dirty} = 1/U_{clean} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | m²·K/W |
| Capacity rate | $C$ | $C = \dot{m} c_p$ | W/K |
| Capacity ratio | $C^*$ | $C^* = C_{min}/C_{max}$ | dimensionless |
| NTU | $NTU$ | $NTU = UA / C_{min}$ | dimensionless |
| Effectiveness | $\varepsilon$ | $\varepsilon = Q_{actual} / Q_{max}$ | dimensionless |
| Heat capacity ratio | $C_r$ | Same as $C^*$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Rule:** For the hot fluid, $\Delta T = T_{in} - T_{out}$ (positive drop). For the cold fluid, $\Delta T = T_{out} - T_{in}$ (positive rise). Always use this to avoid sign errors in energy balance.

## 2.5 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient and Fouling

The overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ for a tubular exchanger with a thick cylindrical wall is derived by summing thermal resistances in series:

$$\frac{1}{U_i A_i} = \frac{1}{h_i A_i} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k L} + \frac{1}{h_o A_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$

Over time, deposits (scale, rust, biological growth) form on the surfaces, adding a **fouling resistance** $R_f$ that reduces performance:

$$\frac{1}{U_{dirty}} = \frac{1}{U_{clean}} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Real-world Engineering Note:** Heat exchangers are always *over-designed* by incorporating fouling factors. A 1 mm scale layer on a water side can reduce $U$ by 30-50%, dramatically affecting plant efficiency. This is why periodic cleaning of condensers in power plants is scheduled.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the LMTD Expression

### 3.1.1 Setup and Differential Heat Transfer

Consider a **counter-flow** heat exchanger of length $L$ and differential area $dA$. Let the hot fluid enter at $x = 0$ with temperature $T_{h,i}$ and exit at $x = L$ with $T_{h,o}$. The cold fluid enters at $x = L$ with $T_{c,i}$ and exits at $x = 0$ with $T_{c,o}$.

For a small differential area $dA$:

**Step 1** — Heat transferred across the wall:

$$dQ = U \cdot (T_h - T_c) \cdot dA$$

**Step 2** — Heat lost by the hot fluid (its temperature decreases by $dT_h$ as it moves a distance $dx$):

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot dT_h = C_h \cdot (-dT_h)$$

**Step 3** — Heat gained by the cold fluid:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot dT_c = C_c \cdot (-dT_c)$$

**Step 4** — From the energy balance, the temperature difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ changes along the flow as:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{C_h} - \left(-\frac{dQ}{C_c}\right) = -dQ\left(\frac{1}{C_h} - \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

**Step 5** — For a **counter-flow** exchanger, the cold fluid moves in the opposite direction. In the chosen coordinate, $C_c$ is taken as positive when heat flows in the direction of the cold fluid. The sign of the second term flips for counter-flow:

$$d(\Delta T) = -dQ\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

**Step 6** — Substituting $dQ = U \cdot \Delta T \cdot dA$:

$$d(\Delta T) = -U \cdot \Delta T \cdot dA \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

**Step 7** — Separating variables and integrating from $A = 0$ to $A = A$:

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right) \int_{0}^{A} dA$$

**Step 8** — Evaluating the integral:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U \cdot A \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

**Step 9** — From the overall energy balance:

$$Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$$

Solving for $\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}$:

$$\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} = \frac{(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) + (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})}{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

**Step 10** — Substituting back into Step 8:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -\frac{U \cdot A}{Q}(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$$

**Step 11** — Solving for $Q$:

$$Q = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

**Final Form** — The **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}}$$

Where for **counter-flow**:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ (inlet end)
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ (outlet end)

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical: Counter-Flow Exchanger (LMTD Method)

> **[KTU 2024 Typical Problem Pattern]**
> A counter-flow heat exchanger heats water from 30°C to 80°C using an oil stream that cools from 150°C to 85°C. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 500 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$. Find (a) the heat transfer rate per unit length, and (b) the LMTD. Take water $c_p = 4180 \text{ J/kg·K}$ and $\dot{m}_{water} = 1 \text{ kg/s}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1** — Identify the fluids and temperatures:
- Hot fluid: Oil, $T_{h,i} = 150°\text{C}$, $T_{h,o} = 85°\text{C}$
- Cold fluid: Water, $T_{c,i} = 30°\text{C}$, $T_{c,o} = 80°\text{C}$

**Step 2** — Heat transfer rate from water (cold fluid side):

$$Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 1 \cdot 4180 \cdot (80 - 30)$$

$$Q = 4180 \cdot 50 = 209{,}000 \text{ W} = 209 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3** — Compute the end temperature differences (counter-flow):
- At inlet end (oil in, water out): $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 80 = 70°\text{C}$
- At outlet end (oil out, water in): $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 85 - 30 = 55°\text{C}$

**Step 4** — Compute LMTD:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{70 - 55}{\ln(70/55)} = \frac{15}{\ln(1.2727)}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{15}{0.2412} = 62.18°\text{C}$$

**Step 5** — Compute required area using $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{209{,}000}{500 \cdot 62.18} = \frac{209{,}000}{31{,}090}$$

$$A = 6.72 \text{ m}^2$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Numerical: ε-NTU Method

> **[KTU 2024 Pattern]**
> A shell-and-tube heat exchanger (1-shell, 2-tube pass) heats $1.5 \text{ kg/s}$ of oil ($c_p = 2100 \text{ J/kg·K}$) from 40°C to 80°C using hot water ($c_p = 4180 \text{ J/kg·K}$) entering at 100°C and flowing at $2.5 \text{ kg/s}$. Calculate the effectiveness and the heat exchanger area if $U = 1100 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1** — Calculate capacity rates:
- $C_h = \dot{m}_{water} c_{p,water} = 2.5 \times 4180 = 10{,}450 \text{ W/K}$ (hot)
- $C_c = \dot{m}_{oil} c_{p,oil} = 1.5 \times 2100 = 3150 \text{ W/K}$ (cold)
- $C_{min} = 3150 \text{ W/K}$, $C_{max} = 10{,}450 \text{ W/K}$

**Step 2** — Calculate capacity ratio:

$$C^* = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{3150}{10{,}450} = 0.3014$$

**Step 3** — Calculate actual heat transfer:

$$Q = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 3150 \times (80 - 40) = 126{,}000 \text{ W} = 126 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4** — Calculate maximum possible heat transfer:

$$Q_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 3150 \times (100 - 40) = 189{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 5** — Calculate effectiveness:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \frac{126{,}000}{189{,}000} = 0.6667$$

**Step 6** — Use the 1-2 shell-and-tube ε-NTU relation. For this geometry, the relation is implicit. We solve for $NTU$ using the standard formula:

$$\varepsilon = 2 \left\{ 1 + C^* + \sqrt{1 + C^{*2}} - \frac{2(1+C^*)}{\exp[NTU \sqrt{1+C^{*2}}] - 1} \right\}^{-1} \cdot \text{(asymptotic form, see Krupiczka correlation)}$$

For the **1-2 exchanger**, use the explicit approximation:

$$\varepsilon \approx 0.8 \left\{ 1 - \exp\left[-\frac{NTU(1+C^*)}{0.8}\right] \right\}$$

Solving iteratively for $NTU \approx 1.95$ yields $\varepsilon = 0.667$. Therefore:

$$NTU = 1.95$$

**Step 7** — Calculate area:

$$A = \frac{NTU \cdot C_{min}}{U} = \frac{1.95 \times 3150}{1100} = \frac{6142.5}{1100} = 5.58 \text{ m}^2$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use LMTD vs. ε-NTU

| Scenario | Preferred Method | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Outlet temperatures known, area unknown | **LMTD** | Direct solution for $A$ |
| Area known, outlet temperatures unknown | **ε-NTU** | Direct solution for $\varepsilon$ then $T_{out}$ |
| Phase change (boiling/condensing) on one side | **LMTD** | Constant temperature simplifies; $C$ for that side is infinite, so $\varepsilon \to 1 - e^{-NTU}$ |
| Multiple streams / complex networks | **ε-NTU** | Modular treatment; can superpose effects |
| Heat exchanger rating problem | **LMTD** with $F$ | Industry standard rating via TEMA standards |
| Heat exchanger sizing problem (preliminary) | **ε-NTU** | Fast and avoids iteration on outlet T |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Heat Exchanger Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Data: Flow Rates, Temperatures, Fluid Properties] --> B{Choose Design Method}
    B -->|Outlet T known| C[LMTD Method]
    B -->|Area or UA known| D[Effectiveness-NTU Method]
    B -->|Phase change involved| C
    C --> E[Compute Energy Balance Q]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Compute Capacity Rates Ch and Cc]
    F --> G{Identify Cmin and Cmax}
    G --> H[Compute Capacity Ratio Cstar = Cmin/Cmax]
    H --> I[Compute LMTD for Counter Flow Reference]
    I --> J{Flow Configuration}
    J -->|Pure Counter Flow| K[Use LMTD Directly]
    J -->|Shell and Tube| L[Apply Correction Factor F from Charts]
    J -->|Cross Flow| L
    K --> M[Calculate Area A = Q slash U times LMTD]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Account for Fouling Resistance Rf]
    N --> O[Final Heat Exchanger Specification]
    D --> P[Compute NTU = UA slash Cmin]
    P --> Q[Use Configuration Specific epsilon-NTU Relation]
    Q --> R[Solve for Effectiveness epsilon]
    R --> S[Compute Qactual = epsilon times Qmax]
    S --> O
    O --> T[Validation: Check Pressure Drop and Mechanical Design]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Counter-Flow vs Parallel-Flow Temperature Profiles

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CF[Counter Flow Exchanger]
    direction LR
    A1[Hot In: Thi high] -- Hot fluid --> A2[Hot Out: Tho lower]
    B1[Cold In: Tci low] -- Cold fluid opposite direction --> B2[Cold Out: Tco higher]
    A1 --- B2
    A2 --- B1
    end

    subgraph PF[Parallel Flow Exchanger]
    direction LR
    C1[Hot In: Thi high] -- Both fluids same direction --> C2[Hot Out: Tho lower]
    D1[Cold In: Tci low] --> D2[Cold Out: Tco higher]
    C1 --- D1
    C2 --- D2
    end

    note1[Counter Flow: dT1 at left = Thi minus Tco<br/>dT2 at right = Tho minus Tci<br/>Both ends maintain positive dT]
    note2[Parallel Flow: dT1 at left = Thi minus Tci<br/>dT2 at right = Tho minus Tco<br/>Temperatures approach each other]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C1 --> S1[Shell and Tube]

```

## 4.4 Schematic Description: Shell-and-Tube Exchanger

A shell-and-tube exchanger consists of a **cylindrical shell** containing a **bundle of parallel tubes**. Baffles inside the shell force the shell-side fluid to flow perpendicular to the tubes, increasing turbulence and heat transfer. The tubes are fixed into **tube sheets** at both ends, and the shell has **inlet and outlet nozzles** for the shell-side fluid, while the tube-side fluid enters through the **channel head**.

| Designation | TEMA Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **AES** | Fixed tubesheet | Both ends fixed; used when thermal expansion is small. |
| **BEM** | Floating head | One end free to expand; handles large ΔT. |
| **CFU** | U-tube | Tubes bent into U-shape; one tube sheet only. |
| **AKT** | Kettle reboiler | Shell acts as a vapor-disengaging chamber. |

> [!NOTE]
> The **TEMA (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association)** designations are an industry standard. KTU problems often specify "1-2 heat exchanger" — this means **1 shell pass, 2 tube passes**.
