2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R_f = \dfrac{\delta_f}{k_f}$ | Fouling resistance = thickness / conductivity of deposit | m²·K/W | | 2 | $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,h} + R_{f,c}$ | Additive resistance in fouled condition | m²·K/W | | 3 | $A_{\text{design}} = \beta \cdot A_{\text{clean}}$ | Safety factor applied to clean area | m² | | 4 | $F_T = \dfrac{\text{LMTD}_{\text{actual}}}{\text{LMTD}_{\text{CF}}}$ | LMTD correction factor (combined with $R_f$ for rating) | dimensionless | | 5 | $Q = U_d \cdot A \cdot \text{LMTD} \cdot F_T$ | Realistic heat duty with fouling & CF | W | | 6 | $R_{f,\text{total}} = \dfrac{1}{U_d} - \dfrac{1}{U_c}$ | Net fouling resistance added by deposits | m²·K/W | | 7 | $U_d = \dfrac{U_c}{1 + U_c R_{f,\text{total}}}$ | Inverted form for direct calculation | W/m²·K | | 8 | $\eta_{\text{fouling}} = \dfrac{U_d}{U_c} = \dfrac{1}{1 + U_c R_{f,\text{total}}}$ | Fouling efficiency ratio (always $<1$) | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitfall Avoidance:** The vertical pipe `|` symbol is **forbidden** in markdown tables. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if you need to indicate absolute value. Example: $R_f = \delta_f \big/ k_f$ written as $\dfrac{\delta_f}{k_f}$. ## 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering - **Power Plants:** Condenser tubes in thermal stations lose 20–30% of their heat transfer capacity annually due to biofouling. Scheduled cleaning restores performance. - **HVAC & Refrigeration:** Chilled water systems use $R_f = 0.0002$ m²·K/W as a TEMA design norm. - **Petrochemical:** Coke formation in cracking furnaces requires $R_f$ up to 0.004 m²·K/W. - **Dairy/Food Industry:** Pasteurizers must include cleaning-in-place (CIP) cycles precisely because of milk-protein fouling. > [!TIP] > **KTU Real-world Linkage:** A 14-mark KTU design problem often asks: *"Design a shell-and-tube heat exchanger to cool oil from 150 °C to 80 °C using water at 30 °C, including a fouling factor of 0.0003 m²·K/W."* — Master the formula chain above to secure full marks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient with Fouling We consider a cylindrical tube carrying hot fluid inside, with cold fluid flowing on the shell side. Let: - $r_i, r_o$ = inner and outer radii of the tube - $D_i, D_o$ = inner and outer diameters - $L$ = length of tube - $k_w$ = thermal conductivity of tube wall - $h_i, h_o$ = inside and outside convective coefficients - $R_{f,i}, R_{f,o}$ = fouling resistances (inner and outer surfaces) ### Step 1: Heat transfer rate through each resistance layer For steady-state, the heat flow $Q$ passes through five resistors in series: $$Q = \frac{\Delta T_i}{R_{conv,i}} = \frac{\Delta T_w}{R_{wall}} = \frac{\Delta T_o}{R_{conv,o}} = \frac{\Delta T_{f,i}}{R_{f,i}} = \frac{\Delta T_{f,o}}{R_{f,o}}$$ ### Step 2: Express each resistance in terms of $Q$ **Convective resistance inside tube:** $$\Delta T_{conv,i} = Q \cdot \frac{1}{h_i \cdot \pi D_i L}$$ **Fouling resistance (inside):** $$\Delta T_{f,i} = Q \cdot \frac{R_{f,i}}{\pi D_i L}$$ **Conductive resistance (cylindrical wall):** $$\Delta T_{wall} = Q \cdot \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k_w L}$$ **Fouling resistance (outside):** $$\Delta T_{f,o} = Q \cdot \frac{R_{f,o}}{\pi D_o L}$$ **Convective resistance outside tube:** $$\Delta T_{conv,o} = Q \cdot \frac{1}{h_o \cdot \pi D_o L}$$ ### Step 3: Sum all temperature drops (Energy conservation) $$\Delta T_{\text{total}} = T_{h} - T_{c} = \Delta T_{conv,i} + \Delta T_{f,i} + \Delta T_{wall} + \Delta T_{f,o} + \Delta T_{conv,o}$$ Substituting each expression and factoring out $Q$: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_{\text{total}} = Q \cdot \Bigg[ & \frac{1}{h_i \pi D_i L} + \frac{R_{f,i}}{\pi D_i L} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k_w L} \\[6pt] & + \frac{R_{f,o}}{\pi D_o L} + \frac{1}{h_o \pi D_o L} \Bigg] \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 4: Group as overall resistance $$\Delta T_{\text{total}} = Q \cdot \frac{1}{UA}$$ Where the **overall resistance per unit length** is: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{UA} = \frac{1}{\pi D_i L} \left(\frac{1}{h_i} + R_{f,i}\right) &+ \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2\pi k_w L} \\[6pt] &+ \frac{1}{\pi D_o L}\left(\frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,o}\right) \end{aligned}$$ ### Step 5: Recast in terms of $U_o$ (outer-area basis) Multiplying both sides by $\pi D_o L$: $$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{D_o}{D_i h_i} + \frac{D_o R_{f,i}}{D_i} + \frac{D_o \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,o}$$ ### Step 6: Identify the clean vs. fouled decomposition The clean overall resistance is: $$\frac{1}{U_{o,\text{clean}}} = \frac{D_o}{D_i h_i} + \frac{D_o \ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}$$ Therefore, the **fouled** overall coefficient obeys: $$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{U_{o,\text{fouled}}} = \frac{1}{U_{o,\text{clean}}} + R_{f,o} + \frac{D_o}{D_i}R_{f,i}\;}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The inner fouling resistance is **amplified by the diameter ratio** $D_o/D_i$ when expressed on the outer area basis. This is a frequently tested KTU derivation — write it on your answer sheet in **exactly this boxed form** to score full marks. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Model Solution) **Problem:** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger has a clean overall coefficient $U_c = 850$ W/m²·K. Hot-side fouling factor is 0.0004 m²·K/W, and cold-side is 0.0002 m²·K/W. Compute: (a) The fouled overall coefficient $U_d$ (b) The percentage drop in heat transfer (c) The required percentage increase in area to maintain the same duty ### Solution (a) — Fouled $U$: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{U_d} &= \frac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,h} + R_{f,c} \\[4pt] &= \frac{1}{850} + 0.0004 + 0.0002 \\[4pt] &= 0.001176 + 0.0004 + 0.0002 \\[4pt] &= 0.001776 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \end{aligned}$$ $$U_d = \frac{1}{0.001776} = 563.06 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$ ### Solution (b) — Percentage drop: $$\begin{aligned} \%\text{Drop} &= \frac{U_c - U_d}{U_c} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= \frac{850 - 563.06}{850} \times 100 \\[4pt] &= 33.76\% \end{aligned}$$ ### Solution (c) — Area compensation: Since $Q = U A \, \Delta T_{lm} = \text{const}$: $$\frac{A_d}{A_c} = \frac{U_c}{U_d} = \frac{850}{563.06} = 1.510$$ $$\Rightarrow A \text{ must increase by } 51.0\%$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fouling Resistance Network — Mermaid Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["HOT FLUID
T_h"]:::hot B["INSIDE CONVECTION
1 divided by h_i"]:::resistor C["FOULING LAYER HOT
R_fh"]:::fouling D["TUBE WALL
ln D_o over D_i divided by 2 pi k L"]:::resistor E["FOULING LAYER COLD
R_fc"]:::fouling F["OUTSIDE CONVECTION
1 divided by h_o"]:::resistor G["COLD FLUID
T_c"]:::cold A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G classDef hot fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef cold fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:2px classDef resistor fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:1px classDef fouling fill:#c0c0c0,stroke:#333,color:#000,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **dashed grey nodes** represent the **additional fouling resistances** that did not exist in a clean heat exchanger. The flow shows heat traversing the resistance network from hot to cold fluid. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Fouling Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart TB start["CLEAN HEAT EXCHANGER
U equals 850 W per m2 K"]:::clean operate["CONTINUOUS OPERATION
Time in months"]:::process deposit["DEPOSITION BEGINS
R_f increases with time"]:::fouling monitor["U FALLS TO U_d
Heat duty drops below rated Q"]:::warning decision{"PERFORMANCE
BELOW LIMIT"}:::decision clean["SCHEDULED CLEANING
CIP or mechanical brushing"]:::action restore["RESTORED TO NEAR U_c
Cycle repeats"]:::clean abandon["EARLY RETIREMENT
Unacceptable Q drop"]:::critical start --> operate --> deposit --> monitor --> decision decision -- "Yes" --> clean --> restore --> operate decision -- "No, critical" --> abandon classDef clean fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef fouling fill:#c0c0c0,stroke:#333,color:#000 classDef process fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef warning fill:#ff9f1c,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#a8dadc,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef action fill:#95e1d3,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef critical fill:#e63946,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Thermal Resistance vs. Time — Asymptotic Fouling Build-up ```mermaid flowchart LR axis["TIME t in days"]:::axis rf["FOULING RESISTANCE R_f
m2 K per W"]:::curve rfclean["CLEAN STATE
R_f equals 0"]:::start rfinf["ASYMPTOTE
R_f infinity"]:::end cft["CRITICAL CLEANING
TRIGGER POINT"]:::threshold rfclean --> rf rf --> rfinf rf --> cft classDef axis fill:#fff,stroke:#000 classDef curve fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef start fill:#4ecdc4,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef end fill:#e63946,stroke:#000,color:#fff classDef threshold fill:#ffe66d,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!TIP] > The fouling curve is **asymptotic** — it rises quickly initially (nucleation phase) and then levels off as the deposit reaches a self-limiting thickness. This is why **predictive maintenance scheduling** is critical in KTU-board case-study questions. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** **Q1. Define the term "fouling factor" in heat exchangers. State its units and physical significance.** **Model Answer:** The fouling factor ($R_f$) is the additional thermal resistance offered by unwanted deposits (scales, corrosion products, biological growth, or chemical reaction products) that accumulate on the heat transfer surfaces during operation. It is defined as the ratio of the deposit thickness $\delta_f$ to its thermal conductivity $k_f$: $$R_f = \frac{\delta_f}{k_f} \quad [\text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W}]$$ **Significance:** It is used as a **design safety margin** to ensure the heat exchanger delivers rated heat duty even after months of operation, and to size the heat transfer area realistically. --- > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q2. List any four types of fouling encountered in industrial heat exchangers with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | # | Type | Example | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Crystallization/Scaling** | CaCO₃ deposition in cooling water systems | | 2 | **Sedimentation** | Silt settling in low-velocity condenser tubes | | 3 | **Chemical Reaction** | Coking in crude oil preheaters | | 4 | **Biological** | Algal biofilm in seawater-cooled exchangers | | 5 | **Corrosion** | Fe₂O₃ layer on carbon steel tubes | | 6 | **Freezing** | Wax deposition in cold crude oil lines | --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module 2 Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]** ### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the overall heat transfer coefficient of a fouled cylindrical tube heat exchanger. Clearly show how the fouling resistances appear in the final expression. **(b) [7 Marks]** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger handles $50{,}000$ kg/h of oil (specific heat $2.1$ kJ/kg·K) being cooled from 110 °C to 75 °C using water entering at 30 °C. The clean overall heat transfer coefficient is 1200 W/m²·K and the LMTD is 35 °C. If the fouling factors on the hot and cold sides are 0.0003 m²·K/W and 0.0002 m²·K/W respectively, determine: - (i) The clean heat transfer area - (ii) The fouled overall heat transfer coefficient - (iii) The required area after fouling to maintain the same duty (use LMTD correction factor $F_T = 0.96$) #### Model Solution (a): (See Section 3.1 derivation above — present the **complete 6-step boxed derivation** starting from the basic heat flow equation.) #### Model Solution (b): **(i) Clean area:** $$Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T = \frac{50{,}000}{3600} \times 2100 \times (110 - 75)$$ $$Q = 13.889 \times 2100 \times 35 = 1{,}020{,}833 \;\; \text{W}$$ $$A_{\text{clean}} = \frac{Q}{U_c \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{1{,}020{,}833}{1200 \times 35} = 24.31 \;\; \text{m}^2$$ **(ii) Fouled $U_d$:** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{U_d} &= \frac{1}{1200} + 0.0003 + 0.0002 \\ &= 0.000833 + 0.0005 = 0.001333 \;\; \text{m}^2\cdot\text{K/W} \end{aligned}$$ $$U_d = 750 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$ **(iii) Fouled area with $F_T$:** $$A_{\text{fouled}} = \frac{Q}{U_d \cdot F_T \cdot \text{LMTD}} = \frac{1{,}020{,}833}{750 \times 0.96 \times 35}$$ $$A_{\text{fouled}} = 40.47 \;\; \text{m}^2$$ **Percentage area increase:** $\dfrac{40.47 - 24.31}{24.31} \times 100 = 66.5\%$ --- ### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **additive thermal resistance model** for a heat exchanger. Show the role of fouling resistance in modifying the overall $U$. Use a suitable block diagram. **(b) [7 Marks]** Two heat exchangers operate in series. The first has $U_1 = 800$ W/m²·K with $R_{f1} = 0.0003$ m²·K/W. The second has $U_2 = 600$ W/m²·K with $R_{f2} = 0.0004$ m²·K/W. Calculate the percentage drop in effective heat transfer for the combined system and identify which exchanger is contributing more to the degradation. #### Model Solution (a): Present the additive resistance model in a clear 5-resistor diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block). State: $$\frac{1}{U_d} = \frac{1}{U_c} + \sum R_f$$ Explain how the clean coefficient $U_c$ is **de-rated** by the sum of fouling resistances, and that the design always uses $U_d \leq U_c$. #### Model Solution (b): **Exchanger 1 fouled $U$:** $$U_{d1} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{800} + 0.0003} = \frac{1}{0.00125 + 0.0003} = \frac{1}{0.00155} = 645.16 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$ % drop in Exchanger 1: $\frac{800 - 645.16}{800} \times 100 = 19.35\%$ **Exchanger 2 fouled $U$:** $$U_{d2} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{600} + 0.0004} = \frac{1}{0.001667 + 0.0004} = \frac{1}{0.002067} = 483.87 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$ % drop in Exchanger 2: $\frac{600 - 483.87}{600} \times 100 = 19.36\%$ **Combined fouled $U$ (series thermal resistances):** $$\frac{1}{U_{\text{combined}}} = \frac{1}{645.16} + \frac{1}{483.87} = 0.00155 + 0.002067 = 0.003617$$ $$U_{\text{combined, fouled}} = 276.50 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$ **Combined clean $U$:** $$\frac{1}{U_{\text{clean, combined}}} = \frac{1}{800} + \frac{1}{600} = 0.00125 + 0.001667 = 0.002917$$ $$U_{\text{clean, combined}} = 342.86 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$$ **Overall percentage drop:** $$\frac{342.86 - 276.50}{342.86} \times 100 = 19.36\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **add percentages** of individual drops (19.35% + 19.36% = 38.7%) instead of computing the **combined resistance** in series. The correct answer is **19.36%**, because in series thermal resistance, the percentage drop is the same for the combined system as for each unit operating alone. **Lose 2 marks if this mistake is made.** **Identification of major contributor:** Exchanger 2 contributes more degradation in **absolute terms** because its individual drop $(600 - 483.87 = 116.13$ W/m²·K) is larger than Exchanger 1's $(800 - 645.16 = 154.84$ W/m²·K). *Wait — correction:* Exchanger 1 has the larger absolute drop. The more degraded exchanger in relative terms has a smaller $U_c$ for a given $R_f$, meaning low-$U$ units are **disproportionately penalized** by the same $R_f$ value. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024** - ✅ **Fouling** = unwanted deposit on heat transfer surface → adds thermal resistance. - ✅ **Fouling Factor** $R_f = \delta_f / k_f$ in **m²·K/W**. - ✅ **Additive model:** $\dfrac{1}{U_d} = \dfrac{1}{U_c} + R_{f,h} + R_{f,c}$. - ✅ Fouling always **reduces** $U$ (i.e., $U_d < U_c$). - ✅ To maintain same $Q$, you must **increase area** by factor $U_c / U_d$. - ✅ Inner fouling on the tube side is amplified by **$D_o / D_i$** when expressed on outer area basis. - ✅ **TEMA standard values** must be cited (typical: 0.0001 to 0.0004 m²·K/W for water, oil). - ✅ Fouling curves are **asymptotic** with time — plan periodic cleaning. - ✅ Six mechanisms: crystallization, sedimentation, chemical, corrosion, biological, freezing. - ✅ **Combined with LMTD correction factor $F_T$** for cross-flow / multi-pass exchangers. - ✅ Units must match: $R_f$ in m²·K/W, $U$ in W/m²·K, $A$ in m². - ✅ Watch the **board pitfall**: do not add percentage drops; recompute combined $U$ from resistances. - ✅ Cite **TEMA or HEDH** as source of $R_f$ values in design problems for full marks. - ✅ Real-world linkage: Kerala process industries (coir retting, dairy, marine) suffer severe biofouling → always include $R_f$ in design.

topics.title: LMTD and NTU Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LMTD and NTU Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> The **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is defined as the *equivalent constant temperature difference* that would yield the same rate of heat transfer between two fluids as the actual variable temperature difference existing in a heat exchanger, assuming a **constant overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** along the length of the exchanger.

In simpler words: in a real heat exchanger, the temperature difference between the hot and cold fluids keeps changing as the fluids flow along the length. To make calculations simple, engineers replace this *varying* ΔT with a *single equivalent* ΔT — and that equivalent is the **LMTD**.

**Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
Imagine you are walking down a slope whose steepness keeps changing. To estimate the *average effort* you would feel, you don't take the steepest point or the gentlest point — you take a weighted average. The LMTD works exactly like this: it is the "logarithmic average" of the two-end temperature differences of the heat exchanger, weighted so that the end with the smaller difference gets more weight (because the heat transfer is dominated by the region with the smaller ΔT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Logarithmic" and not "Arithmetic"?**
> The heat transfer equation is **exponential in nature** (it comes from a differential equation where heat flux is proportional to local ΔT). Therefore, the *correct* average is the **logarithmic mean**, not the arithmetic mean. The LMTD always lies **closer to the smaller temperature difference** than the arithmetic mean.

---

### 1.2 Number of Transfer Units (NTU) Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> The **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** is a dimensionless parameter that represents the *heat transfer capability* of a heat exchanger, expressed as the ratio of the *overall conductance* (UA) to the *minimum heat capacity rate* (C_min) of the fluid streams.

The NTU method is used in **rating problems** where the **outlet temperatures are unknown** and the heat exchanger's physical design (U, A, flow arrangement) is given. Instead of computing LMTD, we use a parameter called **Effectiveness (ε)**, which represents how close the heat exchanger comes to the *theoretical maximum possible heat transfer*.

**Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
Think of NTU like the **size of a sponge** used to absorb water, and Effectiveness like **how much water it actually absorbed** versus the maximum it *could* have absorbed.
- A **big sponge (high NTU)** absorbs more water (higher effectiveness).
- A **small sponge (low NTU)** absorbs less.
- The *shape* of the sponge (parallel flow, counter flow, cross flow) determines the *pattern* of absorption.

The NTU method is essentially: *"Given the size (NTU) and the shape (flow arrangement), how effective is the heat exchanger?"*

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $U$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ |
| $A$ | Heat transfer area | $\text{m}^2$ |
| $\dot{m}$ | Mass flow rate | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| $C_p$ | Specific heat at constant pressure | $\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$ |
| $C = \dot{m}C_p$ | Heat capacity rate | $\text{W/K}$ |
| $Q$ | Heat transfer rate | $\text{W}$ |
| $T$ | Temperature | $\text{K}$ or $^\circ\text{C}$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Effectiveness | Dimensionless |
| $F$ | LMTD correction factor | Dimensionless |
| $C_r$ | Heat capacity ratio | Dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LMTD Method — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Identify the two end temperature differences:**
   - $\Delta T_1$ = Temperature difference at the **inlet** end of the heat exchanger.
   - $\Delta T_2$ = Temperature difference at the **outlet** end of the heat exchanger.

2. **Ensure proper sign convention:**
   - $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ must both be **positive**. Always assign the *larger* value to $\Delta T_1$ to keep the logarithm positive.

3. **Apply the LMTD formula:**
   $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

4. **Apply a correction factor (if required):**
   - For **counter-flow** and **parallel-flow**: $F = 1$ (no correction needed).
   - For **multi-pass shell-and-tube** and **cross-flow** exchangers: $F < 1$, and the actual $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$.

5. **Compute heat transfer rate:**
   $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$$

---

### 2.2 NTU Method — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Define the heat capacity rates:**
   - $C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h}$ (hot fluid)
   - $C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c}$ (cold fluid)
   - $C_{\min} = \min(C_h, C_c)$, $C_{\max} = \max(C_h, C_c)$
   - $C_r = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$ (heat capacity ratio, $0 \le C_r \le 1$)

2. **Define the maximum possible heat transfer:**
   $$Q_{\max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})$$
   This is the absolute upper limit — what the exchanger *would* transfer if the minimum-capacity fluid reached the inlet temperature of the other fluid.

3. **Define Effectiveness:**
   $$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{C_h(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out})}{C_{\min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})} = \frac{C_c(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})}{C_{\min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})}$$

4. **Compute NTU:**
   $$\text{NTU} = \frac{U A}{C_{\min}}$$

5. **Use the appropriate $\varepsilon$-NTU relation** for the given flow arrangement (see formula sheet below).

6. **Solve for the unknown** (usually the outlet temperature or the heat transfer rate).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This formula sheet covers the *exact equations* expected in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) for Module 2 of PCMET503.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Validity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | LMTD | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2 > 0$ |
| 2 | Heat Transfer (LMTD) | $Q = U A \, F \, \Delta T_{lm}$ | $F = 1$ for co-current and counter-current |
| 3 | Correction Factor (Shell-and-Tube 2-4) | $F = \dfrac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{1-R}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\ln\!\left[\frac{2-P(R+1)-\sqrt{R^2+1}}{2-P(R+1)+\sqrt{R^2+1}}\right]}$ | Where $R = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$, $P = (T_{h,out}-T_{h,in})/(T_{c,in}-T_{h,in})$ |
| 4 | NTU Definition | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{\min}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Heat Capacity Ratio | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}}$ | $0 \le C_r \le 1$ |
| 6 | Effectiveness | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = \dfrac{C_h(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out})}{C_{\min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})}$ | $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$ |
| 7 | Maximum Heat Transfer | $Q_{\max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$ | Theoretical upper bound |
| 8 | $\varepsilon$-NTU: Counter-Flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]}$ | Best performance |
| 9 | $\varepsilon$-NTU: Parallel-Flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1+C_r)\big]}{1 + C_r}$ | Lowest performance |
| 10 | $\varepsilon$-NTU: $C_r = 0$ (any flow) | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-\text{NTU})$ | When one fluid is a phase-change fluid |
| 11 | Outlet Temperature — Hot Fluid | $T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \varepsilon \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_h}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$ | If $C_h = C_{\min}$: $T_{h,out}=T_{h,in}-\varepsilon(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$ |
| 12 | Outlet Temperature — Cold Fluid | $T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \varepsilon \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_c}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$ | If $C_c = C_{\min}$: $T_{c,out}=T_{c,in}+\varepsilon(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$ |
| 13 | Special: $C_r = 1$ Counter-Flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{NTU}}{1 + \text{NTU}}$ | Useful simplification |
| 14 | Special: $C_r = 1$ Parallel-Flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp(-2\,\text{NTU})}{2}$ | Useful simplification |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of LMTD/NTU |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Power Plants** | Sizing condensers, economizers, and feedwater heaters in Rankine cycles |
| **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Design of evaporators, condensers, and chilled-water coils |
| **Chemical Process Industry** | Rating and design of shell-and-tube reactors with heating/cooling jackets |
| **Petroleum Refining** | Crude oil preheaters, distillation column reboilers/condensers |
| **Renewable Energy** | Solar thermal collectors, geothermal heat exchangers, PCM-based thermal storage |
| **Automotive** | Radiators, oil coolers, EGR coolers, battery thermal management in EVs |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When solving numericals, always **state which fluid is $C_{\min}$** explicitly. Examiners award marks for clarity in identifying $C_{\min}$, $C_r$, and the flow arrangement *before* applying any formula.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD Formula

**Starting Assumption:** Consider a counter-flow heat exchanger with the hot fluid entering at $T_{h,i}$ and leaving at $T_{h,o}$, and the cold fluid entering at $T_{c,i}$ and leaving at $T_{c,o}$, where $T_{h,i} > T_{c,i}$ and $T_{h,o} > T_{c,o}$.

**Step 1:** Consider a differential element of area $dA$ at a distance $x$ from the inlet. The temperature difference across this element is $\Delta T(x)$.

**Step 2:** The heat transferred across this differential element is:
$$dQ = U \, \Delta T(x) \, dA$$

**Step 3:** From energy balance on the hot fluid (losing heat):
$$dQ = -C_h \, dT_h = C_h \, d(\Delta T)$$
(since $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$ in counter-flow, and $dT_c = -\frac{C_h}{C_c}dT_h$)

**Step 4:** Substituting into the heat transfer equation:
$$C_h \, d(\Delta T) = U \, \Delta T \, dA$$

**Step 5:** Separating variables:
$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = \frac{U}{C_h} \, dA$$

**Step 6:** Integrating from inlet ($\Delta T_1$) to outlet ($\Delta T_2$) and from area $0$ to $A$:

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = \frac{U}{C_h} \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = \frac{U A}{C_h}$$

**Step 7:** The total heat transfer is also given by:
$$Q = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_h (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)$$

**Step 8:** Now, the rate of heat transfer can be expressed using a *mean* temperature difference:
$$Q = U A \, \Delta T_m$$

**Step 9:** Equating the two expressions for $Q$:
$$C_h (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) = U A \, \Delta T_m$$

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{C_h (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{U A}$$

**Step 10:** From Step 6, we have $\dfrac{U A}{C_h} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)$, so $\dfrac{C_h}{U A} = \dfrac{1}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$.

**Step 11:** Substituting:
$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

$$\boxed{\,\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}\,} \quad \text{(LMTD Formula)}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Effectiveness-NTU Relation (Counter-Flow)

**Starting Setup:** Let $T_{h,i}$, $T_{h,o}$ be the hot fluid inlet/outlet temperatures, and $T_{c,i}$, $T_{c,o}$ be the cold fluid inlet/outlet temperatures for a counter-flow heat exchanger.

**Step 1:** Heat transferred:
$$Q = C_h(T_{h,i}-T_{h,o}) = C_c(T_{c,o}-T_{c,i})$$

**Step 2:** Express the actual heat transfer in terms of effectiveness:
$$Q = \varepsilon \, C_{\min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$$

**Step 3:** Let $C_{\min} = C_h$ (hot fluid has the minimum capacity rate — Case A). Then:
$$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \varepsilon(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$$

For the cold fluid:
$$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{C_h}{C_c}\varepsilon(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i}) = T_{c,i} + C_r \varepsilon (T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$$
where $C_r = C_h/C_c = C_{\min}/C_{\max}$.

**Step 4:** Apply the LMTD heat transfer equation for counter-flow (no correction factor needed):
$$Q = U A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$

**Step 5:** Express the end temperature differences:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$

**Step 6:** Substituting the outlet temperatures from Step 3:
$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - \left[T_{c,i} + C_r \varepsilon (T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})\right] = (1 - C_r \varepsilon)(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$$

$$\Delta T_2 = \left[T_{h,i} - \varepsilon(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})\right] - T_{c,i} = (1-\varepsilon)(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$$

**Step 7:** Substituting into the LMTD equation:
$$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

$$Q = U A (T_{h,i}-T_{c,i}) \cdot \frac{(1 - C_r \varepsilon) - (1-\varepsilon)}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 - C_r \varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}\right]}$$

**Step 8:** Simplifying the numerator:
$$(1 - C_r \varepsilon) - (1 - \varepsilon) = \varepsilon(1 - C_r)$$

**Step 9:** Substituting $Q = \varepsilon C_{\min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})$ and $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{\min}$:

$$\varepsilon C_{\min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i}) = \text{NTU} \cdot C_{\min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i}) \cdot \frac{\varepsilon(1-C_r)}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-C_r\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}\right]}$$

**Step 10:** Cancel $C_{\min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})\varepsilon$ (non-zero for $\varepsilon \neq 0$):

$$1 = \text{NTU} \cdot \frac{1 - C_r}{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 - C_r \varepsilon}{1 - \varepsilon}\right]}$$

**Step 11:** Solve for the logarithm:
$$\ln\!\left[\frac{1-C_r\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}\right] = \text{NTU}(1-C_r)$$

$$\frac{1-C_r\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon} = \exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]$$

**Step 12:** Solve for $\varepsilon$:

$$1 - C_r \varepsilon = (1-\varepsilon)\exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]$$

$$1 - C_r \varepsilon = \exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big] - \varepsilon \exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]$$

$$\varepsilon \left[\exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big] - C_r\right] = \exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big] - 1$$

$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon = \frac{\exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big] - 1}{\exp\!\big[\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big] - C_r} = \frac{1 - \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The second form is obtained by multiplying numerator and denominator by $\exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]$.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Problem:** A counter-flow heat exchanger is used to condense steam at atmospheric pressure. Saturated steam condenses at $100^\circ\text{C}$, and cooling water enters at $20^\circ\text{C}$ with a flow rate of $0.5\,\text{kg/s}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 2000\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$, and the heat exchanger area is $A = 0.5\,\text{m}^2$. Take $C_{p,w} = 4180\,\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$.

**Find:** (i) The effectiveness, (ii) The water outlet temperature, (iii) The LMTD and verify $Q$.

**Step 1: Identify fluid properties.**
The condensing steam has $C_h \to \infty$ (temperature is constant during condensation), so $C_{\min} = C_{\text{water}}$.
$$C_{\min} = \dot{m}_w C_{p,w} = 0.5 \times 4180 = 2090\,\text{W/K}$$
$$C_r = \frac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}} \to 0$$

**Step 2: Compute NTU.**
$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U A}{C_{\min}} = \frac{2000 \times 0.5}{2090} = \frac{1000}{2090} \approx 0.4785$$

**Step 3: Compute effectiveness (for $C_r = 0$):**
$$\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-\text{NTU}) = 1 - \exp(-0.4785) = 1 - 0.6196 = 0.3804$$

**Step 4: Compute water outlet temperature:**
$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \varepsilon(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}) = 20 + 0.3804 \times (100 - 20) = 20 + 30.43 = 50.43^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 5: Compute LMTD.**
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 100 - 50.43 = 49.57^\circ\text{C}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 100 - 20 = 80^\circ\text{C}$ (steam is isothermal)

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{80 - 49.57}{\ln(80/49.57)} = \frac{30.43}{\ln(1.6138)} = \frac{30.43}{0.4785} \approx 63.6^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 6: Verify heat transfer.**
$$Q = U A \, \Delta T_{lm} = 2000 \times 0.5 \times 63.6 = 63{,}600\,\text{W} \approx 63.6\,\text{kW}$$

**Verification using effectiveness:**
$$Q = \varepsilon \, C_{\min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}) = 0.3804 \times 2090 \times 80 = 63{,}602\,\text{W} \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Heat Exchanger Analysis — Decision Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given Heat Exchanger Problem"] --> B["Are Inlet AND Outlet Temperatures Known?"]
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Use LMTD Method"]
    B -- "No" --> D["Use NTU Effectiveness Method"]
    
    C --> C1["Compute Delta T1 and Delta T2 at Both Ends"]
    C1 --> C2["Is Flow Arrangement Counter or Parallel?"]
    C2 -- "Yes" --> C3["F equals 1"]
    C2 -- "No Cross or Multi-Pass" --> C4["Compute Correction Factor F"]
    C3 --> C5["Q equals U A F LMTD"]
    C4 --> C5
    
    D --> D1["Compute Ch and Cc for Both Fluids"]
    D1 --> D2["Identify Cmin and Cmax"]
    D2 --> D3["Compute Cr equals Cmin over Cmax"]
    D3 --> D4["Compute NTU equals UA over Cmin"]
    D4 --> D5["Apply Epsilon NTU Relation for Flow Type"]
    D5 --> D6["Compute Epsilon"]
    D6 --> D7["Compute Qactual equals Epsilon Qmax"]
    D7 --> D8["Solve for Unknown Outlet Temperatures"]
    
    C5 --> Z["End: Heat Transfer Rate Q Obtained"]
    D8 --> Z
```

### 4.2 Counter-Flow vs Parallel-Flow Temperature Profile (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Counter["Counter Flow Arrangement"]
        C1["Hot In: 200 C"] --> C2["Heat Transfer Along Length"]
        C2 --> C3["Hot Out: 120 C"]
        C4["Cold In: 30 C"] --> C5["Heat Transfer Along Length"]
        C5 --> C6["Cold Out: 110 C"]
    end
    
    subgraph Parallel["Parallel Flow Arrangement"]
        P1["Hot In: 200 C"] --> P2["Heat Transfer Along Length"]
        P2 --> P3["Hot Out: 100 C"]
        P4["Cold In: 30 C"] --> P5["Heat Transfer Along Length"]
        P5 --> P6["Cold Out: 100 C"]
    end
```

### 4.3 $\varepsilon$-NTU Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Input: NTU and Cr"] --> Flow["Identify Flow Arrangement"]
    Flow --> F1["Counter Flow"]
    Flow --> F2["Parallel Flow"]
    Flow --> F3["Shell and Tube 2-4"]
    Flow --> F4["Cross Flow Both Unmixed"]
    
    F1 --> E1["epsilon equals 1 minus exp minus NTU 1 minus Cr over 1 minus Cr exp minus NTU 1 minus Cr"]
    F2 --> E2["epsilon equals 1 minus exp minus NTU 1 plus Cr over 1 plus Cr"]
    F3 --> E3["Use Standard Charts from Data Book"]
    F4 --> E4["epsilon equals 1 minus exp NTU power 0.22 Cr minus 1 over exp NTU power 0.78 Cr minus 1"]
```

### 4.4 Physical Setup Schematic — Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Shell["Shell Side Hot Fluid"]
        S1["Hot Inlet T_h_in"] --> S2["Shell Baffle Zone"]
        S2 --> S3["Shell Baffle Zone"]
        S3 --> S4["Hot Outlet T_h_out"]
    end
    
    subgraph Tubes["Tube Side Cold Fluid"]
        T1["Cold Inlet T_c_in"] --> T2["Tube Pass 1"]
        T2 --> T3["Return Header"]
        T3 --> T4["Tube Pass 2"]
        T4 --> T5["Cold Outlet T_c_out"]
    end
    
    S2 -.Heat Transfer U A.-> T2
    S2 -.Heat Transfer U A.-> T4
    S3 -.Heat Transfer U A.-> T4
    S3 -.Heat Transfer U A.-> T2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**. Why is the **logarithmic mean** preferred over the **arithmetic mean** for heat exchanger analysis?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
The Log Mean Temperature Difference is defined as the *equivalent constant temperature difference* between the hot and cold fluids that would give the same rate of heat transfer as the actual varying temperature difference in a heat exchanger, assuming a **constant overall heat transfer coefficient (U)**.

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

**Why Logarithmic Mean? (1 Mark):**
The temperature profile along the length of a heat exchanger is **exponential in nature** (derived from the differential heat transfer equation $dQ = U\,\Delta T\,dA$). Therefore, the *correct statistical average* of this exponential distribution is the **logarithmic mean**, not the arithmetic mean. Using the arithmetic mean would lead to significant errors, especially when $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2 > 2$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Effectiveness** and **NTU** of a heat exchanger. Under what conditions is the NTU method preferred over the LMTD method?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Effectiveness (1 Mark):**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{C_h(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out})}{C_{\min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})}$$
It represents the ratio of actual heat transfer to the maximum possible heat transfer.

**NTU (1 Mark):**
$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}}$$
It is a dimensionless measure of the heat transfer capability (size) of the heat exchanger.

**When is NTU preferred? (1 Mark):**
The NTU method is preferred when the **outlet temperatures are not known** but the heat exchanger's *design parameters* (U, A) and *inlet conditions* are given — typical of **rating problems** or **design verification**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 2 carries internal choice. Students must attempt **EITHER Question A OR Question B** in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** A counter-flow heat exchanger is used to cool $1.2\,\text{kg/s}$ of oil ($C_p = 2100\,\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$) from $150^\circ\text{C}$ to $80^\circ\text{C}$ by water entering at $25^\circ\text{C}$ with a flow rate of $0.8\,\text{kg/s}$ ($C_p = 4180\,\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$). The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 850\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. Calculate: **(7 Marks)**
- (i) The LMTD
- (ii) The heat transfer area required

**(b)** What is the **LMTD correction factor (F)**? Explain its significance. For a 2-4 shell-and-tube heat exchanger, derive the expression for the correction factor and state the condition under which $F \to 0$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Heat capacity rates.**
$$C_h = \dot{m}_{\text{oil}} \times C_{p,\text{oil}} = 1.2 \times 2100 = 2520\,\text{W/K}$$
$$C_c = \dot{m}_w \times C_{p,w} = 0.8 \times 4180 = 3344\,\text{W/K}$$

**Step 2: Heat transferred from oil.**
$$Q = C_h(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}) = 2520 \times (150-80) = 2520 \times 70 = 176{,}400\,\text{W}$$

**Step 3: Water outlet temperature (energy balance).**
$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 25 + \frac{176{,}400}{3344} = 25 + 52.75 = 77.75^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4: LMTD (counter-flow).**
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 77.75 = 72.25^\circ\text{C}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 25 = 55^\circ\text{C}$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{72.25 - 55}{\ln(72.25/55)} = \frac{17.25}{\ln(1.3136)} = \frac{17.25}{0.2727} = 63.26^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 5: Area (counter-flow, $F = 1$).**
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{176{,}400}{850 \times 63.26} = \frac{176{,}400}{53{,}771} \approx 3.28\,\text{m}^2$$

---

**Part (b) — Conceptual & Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Correction Factor — Definition (2 Marks):**
The LMTD correction factor **F** accounts for the deviation of the actual temperature profile from the ideal counter-flow profile in **multi-pass** or **cross-flow** heat exchangers. It is defined as:

$$F = \frac{\Delta T_m}{\Delta T_{lm}}$$

where $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ is the true mean temperature difference to be used in $Q = UA\,\Delta T_m$.

**Significance (1 Mark):**
$F$ is always $\leq 1$. It is a measure of *thermodynamic performance degradation* of a multi-pass exchanger compared to an ideal counter-flow exchanger. A value close to 1 means near-ideal performance; a value close to 0 means the exchanger is operating near its **temperature cross** condition.

**Derivation of F for 2-4 Shell-and-Tube (3 Marks):**
The standard expression is:

$$F = \frac{\sqrt{R^2+1}\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1-R}{1-PR}\right]}{(R-1)\ln\!\left[\dfrac{2-P(R+1)-\sqrt{R^2+1}}{2-P(R+1)+\sqrt{R^2+1}}\right]}$$

where:
- $R = \dfrac{T_{h,out}-T_{h,in}}{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}} = \dfrac{C_c}{C_h} = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}}$ (heat capacity ratio)
- $P = \dfrac{T_{c,out}-T_{c,in}}{T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}}$ (temperature effectiveness)

**Condition for $F \to 0$ (1 Mark):**
$F \to 0$ when **temperature cross** occurs — i.e., when $P = \dfrac{2}{R+1-\sqrt{R^2+1}}$. At this point, the cold fluid outlet temperature exceeds the hot fluid outlet temperature in one section, and the effective driving temperature difference becomes zero, rendering the exchanger thermally ineffective.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern] — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** A shell-and-tube heat exchanger with two shell passes and four tube passes is used to heat water from $30^\circ\text{C}$ to $80^\circ\text{C}$ using a hot oil stream that cools from $150^\circ\text{C}$ to $100^\circ\text{C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 500\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$. Use the LMTD method to determine the **heat transfer area**. Take $F$ from the standard chart as $0.96$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the **NTU method**, determine the **outlet temperature of both fluids** for a counter-flow heat exchanger with $U = 1000\,\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$, $A = 2.0\,\text{m}^2$, hot fluid: $\dot{m}_h = 1.0\,\text{kg/s}$, $C_{p,h} = 2200\,\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $T_{h,in} = 200^\circ\text{C}$, cold fluid: $\dot{m}_c = 1.5\,\text{kg/s}$, $C_{p,c} = 4000\,\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$, $T_{c,in} = 30^\circ\text{C}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — LMTD Method (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Heat transfer rate.**
Assuming mass flow rates are not given, use the energy balance on water (as a reference):
$$Q = \dot{m}_w C_{p,w}(T_{w,out}-T_{w,in}) = \dot{m}_w \times 4180 \times (80-30) = 209{,}000\,\dot{m}_w$$

We use the LMTD method directly to find *area* in terms of unknown flow rates. The standard approach when only temperatures and $U$ are given:

**Step 2: LMTD calculation.**
For a 2-4 shell-and-tube exchanger, we treat it like counter-flow first to compute $\Delta T_{lm}$:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 80 = 70^\circ\text{C}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 100 - 30 = 70^\circ\text{C}$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{70-70}{\ln(70/70)} \quad \text{(indeterminate 0/0 form)}$$

Apply L'Hôpital's rule (or note that when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$):
$$\Delta T_{lm} = 70^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3: Heat transfer rate from energy balance on oil side:**
Without mass flow rates, we can only express $Q$ in terms of the unknown $\dot{m}_w$ or $\dot{m}_h$. 

For the **KTU exam**, when only temperatures are given (as here), we either need an additional mass flow rate or we determine the area by using an assumed energy balance.

Assuming the heat transferred is determined from the oil side (oil flow rate must be given). In this problem, **we use the LMTD method as follows**:

Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 70^\circ\text{C}$, the **corrected** mean temperature difference is:
$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm} = 0.96 \times 70 = 67.2^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 4: Express area in terms of $Q$:**
$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_m} = \frac{Q}{500 \times 67.2} = \frac{Q}{33{,}600}\,\text{m}^2$$

To find $Q$ numerically, we need at least one mass flow rate. If we are given $\dot{m}_{\text{oil}} = 1.0\,\text{kg/s}$, $C_{p,\text{oil}} = 2200\,\text{J/kg}\cdot\text{K}$:
$$Q = 1.0 \times 2200 \times (150-100) = 110{,}000\,\text{W}$$

$$A = \frac{110{,}000}{33{,}600} \approx 3.27\,\text{m}^2$$

---

**Part (b) — NTU Method (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Heat capacity rates.**
$$C_h = \dot{m}_h C_{p,h} = 1.0 \times 2200 = 2200\,\text{W/K}$$
$$C_c = \dot{m}_c C_{p,c} = 1.5 \times 4000 = 6000\,\text{W/K}$$
$$C_{\min} = C_h = 2200\,\text{W/K}, \quad C_{\max} = C_c = 6000\,\text{W/K}$$
$$C_r = \frac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}} = \frac{2200}{6000} = 0.3667$$

**Step 2: NTU.**
$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{C_{\min}} = \frac{1000 \times 2.0}{2200} = \frac{2000}{2200} = 0.9091$$

**Step 3: Effectiveness (counter-flow).**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\big[-\text{NTU}(1-C_r)\big]}$$

Compute $1 - C_r = 1 - 0.3667 = 0.6333$:
$$\text{NTU}(1-C_r) = 0.9091 \times 0.6333 = 0.5757$$
$$\exp(-0.5757) = 0.5623$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.5623}{1 - 0.3667 \times 0.5623} = \frac{0.4377}{1 - 0.2062} = \frac{0.4377}{0.7938} = 0.5514$$

**Step 4: Maximum heat transfer.**
$$Q_{\max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}) = 2200 \times (200-30) = 2200 \times 170 = 374{,}000\,\text{W}$$

**Step 5: Actual heat transfer.**
$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot Q_{\max} = 0.5514 \times 374{,}000 = 206{,}224\,\text{W} \approx 206.2\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 6: Outlet temperatures.**
Since $C_h = C_{\min}$:
$$T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \varepsilon(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}) = 200 - 0.5514 \times 170 = 200 - 93.74 = 106.26^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \frac{C_{\min}}{C_c}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in}) \cdot \varepsilon = 30 + \frac{2200}{6000}\times 0.5514 \times 170$$

Wait, more directly: $Q = C_c(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})$:
$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 30 + \frac{206{,}224}{6000} = 30 + 34.37 = 64.37^\circ\text{C}$$

**Verification:** $C_h(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}) = 2200 \times (200-106.26) = 2200 \times 93.74 = 206{,}228\,\text{W} \approx 206.2\,\text{kW}$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Mark-Loss Zones):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error (–2 Marks):** Students often compute $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$ and end up with a *negative* logarithm. Always define $\Delta T_1$ as the **larger** end difference.
>
> 2. **Forgetting $F$ in Multi-Pass Exchangers (–2 to –3 Marks):** Using raw LMTD (without $F$) in a 2-4 shell-and-tube or cross-flow exchanger is the **single most common error** in KTU exams. Always identify the flow arrangement first.
>
> 3. **Wrong $C_{\min}$ Identification (–1 to –2 Marks):** Confusing $C_{\min}$ and $C_{\max}$ in the NTU formula leads to a wrong $C_r$ and incorrect effectiveness. Always state: *"Since $C_h < C_c$, $C_{\min} = C_h$."*
>
> 4. **Using LMTD when Outlets are Unknown (–1 Mark):** If the problem gives you $U$, $A$, and inlet temperatures (but not outlet), you **must** use the NTU method, not LMTD.
>
> 5. **Units Inconsistency (–0.5 Mark):** Ensure all flow rates are in kg/s, $C_p$ in J/kg·K, $U$ in W/m²·K, and $A$ in m² to get $Q$ in Watts.
>
> 6. **Counter-flow Formula Misapplied to Parallel-flow (–2 Marks):** Using the counter-flow $\varepsilon$-NTU relation for a parallel-flow problem (or vice versa) gives the wrong answer and is immediately penalized.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save This for Last-Minute Review):**

- ☐ **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$; always ensure $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2 > 0$.
- ☐ **LMTD use case:** *Sizing problem* — when inlet and outlet temperatures are known; used to find $A$ or $U$.
- ☐ **Correction factor $F$:** $F = 1$ for counter-flow and parallel-flow; $F < 1$ for cross-flow and multi-pass shell-and-tube.
- ☐ **NTU formula:** $\text{NTU} = UA/C_{\min}$ — dimensionless; measures heat exchanger "size."
- ☐ **Effectiveness formula:** $\varepsilon = Q_{\text{actual}}/Q_{\max}$ — bounded between 0 and 1.
- ☐ **$Q_{\max}$ formula:** $Q_{\max} = C_{\min}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$ — happens when $C_{\min}$ fluid reaches the inlet temperature of the other fluid.
- ☐ **NTU use case:** *Rating problem* — when outlet temperatures are not known and $U$, $A$ are given.
- ☐ **Counter-flow always gives the highest $\varepsilon$** for a given NTU and $C_r$.
- ☐ **For $C_r = 0$ (condensation/evaporation):** $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp(-\text{NTU})$ — simple and elegant.
- ☐ **Outlet temperatures from $\varepsilon$:** $T_{h,out} = T_{h,in} - \varepsilon\dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_h}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$; $T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \varepsilon\dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_c}(T_{h,in}-T_{c,in})$.
- ☐ **Special case $C_r = 1$ counter-flow:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\text{NTU}}{1+\text{NTU}}$ — memorize this.
- ☐ **Special case $C_r = 1$ parallel-flow:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - e^{-2\,\text{NTU}}}{2}$ — memorize this.
- ☐ **Temperature cross** condition causes $F \to 0$ in LMTD method — the exchanger becomes ineffective beyond this point.
- ☐ **Energy balance always holds:** $C_h(T_{h,in}-T_{h,out}) = C_c(T_{c,out}-T_{c,in})$ — use this to verify your answers.
- ☐ **KTU exam weightage:** Module 2 (LMTD & NTU) typically carries **14–20 marks** in the ESE, so practice at least 3–4 full numericals before the exam.
- ☐ **Always state assumptions:** Constant $U$, no heat loss to surroundings, no phase change (unless stated), steady-state operation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Heat Pipes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Heat Pipes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Pipe:** A heat pipe is a passive, sealed, two-phase heat transfer device that transports large quantities of heat over considerable distances with an extremely small temperature difference between the heat source and heat sink. It achieves this by utilizing the latent heat of vaporization of an internal working fluid, in conjunction with a capillary wick structure that returns the condensate to the evaporator without any external pumping power.

The KTU 2024 Scheme PCMET503 syllabus categorizes the heat pipe as a **high-conductance thermal transport device** belonging to the family of **Phase-Change Heat Transfer** systems. It is a key topic in Module 2 under advanced conduction and heat transport mechanisms.

A heat pipe typically consists of:
- **Outer Casing (Vessel):** A sealed metallic or non-metallic tube (copper, aluminum, stainless steel).
- **Working Fluid:** A substance chosen to operate within a specific temperature range.
- **Wick Structure:** A porous medium that generates capillary pressure for fluid return.
- **Vapor Space:** The non-condensable-gas-free central core through which vapor flows.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Closed Water Cycle in a Thermosiphon Tea Kettle":**
> Imagine a tea kettle on a stove. Water at the bottom boils (evaporates) and rises as vapor to the cooler top section, where it condenses and drips back down. Now imagine sealing this entire system and inserting a sponge-like wick along the walls. The sponge pulls the cold liquid back to the hot end automatically — no pump required! This self-circulating, two-phase "boil-and-condense" cycle inside a sealed tube is exactly how a heat pipe works. The stove = **Evaporator**, the cool air around the top = **Condenser**, and the sponge = **Wick**.

The effective thermal conductivity of a heat pipe can reach values as high as **100,000 W/m·K** — which is **several thousand times** higher than that of solid copper (≈ **401 W/m·K**). This makes it one of the most efficient passive heat transporters ever engineered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to clearly distinguish between **Conventional (Cylindrical) Heat Pipes** and **Thermosyphons** (gravity-assisted, no wick) — both fall under the broader heat pipe classification.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are central to heat pipe design:

- **Latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$):** Typically $10^3$ to $10^4$ kJ/kg for common working fluids.
- **Surface tension of water at 100°C:** $\sigma = 0.0589$ N/m.
- **Standard operating pressure:** Near-saturation pressure of the working fluid at the operating temperature.
- **Effective thermal conductivity ($k_{eff}$):** $10^4$ to $10^5$ W/m·K.
- **Thermal resistance of a typical copper-water heat pipe:** $0.01$ to $0.1$ °C/W.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A heat pipe operates through a **closed thermodynamic cycle** that involves **two-phase flow** and **capillary-driven liquid return**. The six operational stages are:

1. **Heat Absorption (Evaporator Section):** External heat $Q_{in}$ is applied to one end of the sealed tube. The working fluid absorbs this energy and reaches saturation temperature.

2. **Phase Change (Liquid → Vapor):** The liquid vaporizes at constant saturation temperature, absorbing a large amount of energy in the form of latent heat $h_{fg}$.

3. **Vapor Transport (Adiabatic Section):** The high-pressure vapor flows along the central vapor core toward the cooler section, transferring minimal heat (this section is nearly isothermal).

4. **Heat Rejection (Condenser Section):** At the cold end, vapor condenses back to liquid, releasing its latent heat $Q_{out}$ to the external sink.

5. **Liquid Return via Wick (Capillary Action):** The condensed liquid is drawn back to the evaporator by capillary pressure generated in the porous wick structure.

6. **Cycle Closure:** The returned liquid is reheated, and the cycle repeats indefinitely (steady-state operation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it works so efficiently:**
> The "Why" lies in the energy density of the **latent heat** — vaporizing 1 kg of water carries ≈ **2257 kJ**, whereas heating the same 1 kg of water through 1 °C requires only **4.18 kJ**. Hence, very small mass flow rates are sufficient to transport huge heat loads.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat transport capacity | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$ | W | $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s) |
| Capillary pressure (cylindrical pore) | $\Delta P_{cap} = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}}$ | Pa | $\sigma$ = surface tension; $\theta$ = contact angle; $r_{eff}$ = effective pore radius |
| Effective thermal conductivity | $k_{eff} = \dfrac{Q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta T}$ | W/m·K | Defines the equivalent heat-conduction ability of the heat pipe |
| Thermal resistance (total) | $R_{th} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{Q} = R_{eva} + R_{adi} + R_{cond}$ | K/W | Sum of evaporator, adiabatic, and condenser resistances |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}}$ | kg/s | Heat carried per unit mass of fluid |
| Pressure balance (operating condition) | $\Delta P_{cap} \geq \Delta P_l + \Delta P_v + \Delta P_g$ | Pa | $l$ = liquid; $v$ = vapor; $g$ = gravity |
| Viscous pressure drop (laminar vapor) | $\Delta P_v = \dfrac{8 \mu_v \dot{m} L_{eff,v}}{\pi \rho_v r_v^{4}}$ | Pa | Hagen-Poiseuille flow assumption |
| Liquid pressure drop (Darcy's law) | $\Delta P_l = \dfrac{\mu_l \dot{m} L_{eff,l}}{K \cdot A_w \cdot \rho_l}$ | Pa | $K$ = wick permeability; $A_w$ = wick cross-section |
| Capillary limit (max heat) | $Q_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{cap, \max} - \Delta P_g}{\left(\dfrac{1}{Q}\right)}$ | W | Determined by wick and fluid properties |
| Boiling limit | $q_{boil} = \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \dfrac{k_{eff,l} \cdot h_{fg} \rho_v \sigma}{(\mu_l \cdot \Delta T_{sub})^2}$ | W/m² | Governed by nucleate boiling suppression |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember the **4 critical working limits** of a heat pipe: **Capillary limit, Boiling limit, Entrainment limit, and Sonic (Vapor) limit.** Mastering these is essential for securing full marks in KTU ESE Module 2.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heat pipes are deployed in:
- **Aerospace:** Satellite thermal control, spacecraft radiator panels.
- **Electronics:** CPU/GPU cooling in high-performance computing.
- **Renewable Energy:** Solar collector heat extraction.
- **Nuclear Engineering:** Spent-fuel rod cooling.
- **HVAC & Building Services:** Energy recovery ventilation.
- **Medical Devices:** Cryosurgery probes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capillary Pressure in a Cylindrical Pore

A heat pipe returns condensate to the evaporator through a wick whose pores act as miniature capillaries. The Young-Laplace equation governs the pressure jump across a curved liquid-vapor interface.

**Starting equation (Laplace pressure):**
$$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \sigma}{r}$$

**For a wetted cylindrical pore of radius $r_{eff}$ with contact angle $\theta$ (between liquid and wick):**
The effective radius of the meniscus is reduced by a factor of $\cos\theta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{cap} &= \frac{2 \sigma}{r_{eff} / \cos\theta} \\
&= \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logical explanation:**
- The surface tension $\sigma$ (N/m) acts along the perimeter of the meniscus.
- The contact angle $\theta$ (measured through the liquid) determines how the meniscus wets the wick.
- For a perfectly wetting fluid ($\theta = 0°$), $\cos\theta = 1$, and $\Delta P_{cap}$ is maximum.
- For a non-wetting fluid ($\theta > 90°$), $\cos\theta < 0$, and the capillary action is reversed (this is undesirable).

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}}}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Smaller pore radius $r_{eff}$ → larger capillary pressure → stronger liquid pumping. However, smaller pores also increase viscous drag, so optimization is necessary.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Thermal Conductivity of a Heat Pipe

The "effective thermal conductivity" of a heat pipe is a *hypothetical* conduction coefficient that, if applied to a solid rod of identical geometry, would deliver the same heat $Q$ under the same temperature difference $\Delta T$.

**Fourier's Law for an equivalent solid conductor:**
$$Q = k_{eff} \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T}{L_{eff}}$$

**Rearranging for $k_{eff}$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{eff} &= \frac{Q \cdot L_{eff}}{A \cdot \Delta T} \\
&= \frac{\dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot L_{eff}}{A \cdot \Delta T}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}}$ and noting $Q$ cancels:**
The effective conductivity is:
$$\boxed{k_{eff} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot L_{eff}}{A \cdot \Delta T}}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**
Given: $\dot{m} = 0.001$ kg/s, $h_{fg} = 2.257 \times 10^6$ J/kg (water), $L_{eff} = 0.5$ m, $A = 7.85 \times 10^{-5}$ m² (10 mm OD), $\Delta T = 5$ °C.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= 0.001 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 = 2257 \text{ W} \\
k_{eff} &= \frac{0.001 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 \times 0.5}{7.85 \times 10^{-5} \times 5} \\
&= \frac{1128.5}{3.925 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 2.875 \times 10^{6} \text{ W/m·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is approximately **7,000 times** the thermal conductivity of solid copper, illustrating the extraordinary transport capability of heat pipes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Pressure Balance Equation

For the heat pipe to function continuously, the **capillary pressure must overcome all parasitic pressure drops** in the liquid and vapor flow paths.

**General pressure balance:**
$$\Delta P_{cap} \geq \Delta P_l + \Delta P_v + \Delta P_g$$

**Expanding each term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_l &= \frac{\mu_l \dot{m} L_{eff,l}}{K \cdot A_w \cdot \rho_l} \quad \text{(Darcy flow through wick)} \\
\Delta P_v &= \frac{8 \mu_v \dot{m} L_{eff,v}}{\pi \rho_v r_v^{4}} \quad \text{(Hagen-Poiseuille vapor flow)} \\
\Delta P_g &= \rho_l g L \cdot \sin\psi \quad \text{(gravity head for tilted heat pipe)} \\
\Delta P_{cap} &= \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}} \quad \text{(Laplace capillary pumping)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final steady-state operating condition:**
$$\boxed{\frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}} \geq \Delta P_l + \Delta P_v + \Delta P_g}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** If $\Delta P_{cap} < \Delta P_l + \Delta P_v + \Delta P_g$, the wick will "dry out" at the evaporator and the heat pipe fails — this is the **capillary (or wicking) limit**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Heat Pipe vs. Solid Conductor

| **Property** | **Solid Copper Rod** | **Heat Pipe (Water-Copper)** |
|---|---|---|
| Effective $k_{eff}$ | 401 W/m·K | $10^4$ – $10^5$ W/m·K |
| Weight for same $Q$ | High | Very low |
| Passive operation | Yes | Yes |
| Isothermal capability | Poor | Excellent |
| Cost | Low | Moderate–High |
| Failure mode | Melting | Dry-out, gas contamination |
| Temperature range | -270 to 1083 °C | Limited by working fluid |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Heat Source Q_in] --> B[Evaporator Section]
    B --> C[Phase Change Liquid to Vapor]
    C --> D[Adiabatic Transport Section]
    D --> E[Condenser Section]
    E --> F[Phase Change Vapor to Liquid]
    F --> G[Wick Capillary Return]
    G --> B
    E --> H[Heat Rejection Q_out]
    B --> I[Wick Pumping Pressure]
    G --> I
    I --> J[Pressure Balance Equation]
    J --> K{Operating Limit Check}
    K --> L[Capillary Limit]
    K --> M[Boiling Limit]
    K --> N[Entrainment Limit]
    K --> O[Sonic Limit]
```

## 4.2 Heat Pipe Sectional Schematic (Mermaid Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOTEND["EVAPORATOR REGION"]
        H1[External Heat Input] --> H2[Liquid Film in Wick]
        H2 --> H3[Bubble Formation Nucleation]
        H3 --> H4[Liquid to Vapor Phase Change]
    end
    subgraph MIDDLE["ADIABATIC REGION"]
        M1[Vapor Flow Toward Condenser] --> M2[Pressure Drop in Vapor Core]
        M2 --> M3[Negligible Heat Loss to Surroundings]
    end
    subgraph COLDEND["CONDENSER REGION"]
        C1[Vapor Contacts Cold Wall] --> C2[Condensation Releases Latent Heat]
        C2 --> C3[Liquid Film Formation on Wick]
        C3 --> C4[External Heat Rejection to Sink]
    end
    H4 --> M1
    M3 --> C1
    C3 --> H2
```

## 4.3 Working Fluid Selection Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Application Temperature Range] --> B{Operating Temperature}
    B --> C[200 to 500 °C]
    B --> D[500 to 900 °C]
    B --> E[Negative 50 to 200 °C]
    C --> F[Mercury NaK Alloy]
    D --> G[Sodium Lithium]
    E --> H[Ammonia Acetone Methanol]
    F --> I[Stainless Steel Casing]
    G --> J[Stainless Steel Inconel Casing]
    H --> K[Aluminum Copper Casing]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide for KTU 2024:** These flow diagrams represent the **functional block architecture** of a heat pipe, emphasizing the cyclic nature of mass and energy transport. In the KTU ESE, students are expected to sketch a **labeled sectional view** of a heat pipe showing the wick, vapor space, and the direction of vapor/liquid flow — practice this drawing during revision.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a heat pipe. Mention any two advantages of heat pipes over conventional heat transfer devices.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> A heat pipe is a passive, sealed, two-phase heat transfer device that transports heat efficiently using the latent heat of vaporization of an internal working fluid, with a capillary wick returning the condensate.
>
> **Advantages (any two):**
> 1. Very high effective thermal conductivity (up to $10^5$ W/m·K) compared to solid metals like copper (401 W/m·K).
> 2. Passive operation with no moving parts — high reliability and zero external power consumption.
> 3. Nearly isothermal operation with small temperature difference between source and sink.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the four working limits of a conventional heat pipe and state the most commonly encountered one in practice.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four working limits of a conventional heat pipe are:
1. **Capillary (Wicking) Limit** — occurs when the wick cannot supply enough liquid to the evaporator.
2. **Boiling Limit** — when nucleate boiling in the evaporator suppresses further liquid vaporization.
3. **Entrainment Limit** — when high-velocity vapor shears liquid off the wick surface.
4. **Sonic (Vapor) Limit** — when vapor velocity reaches Mach 1, choking the flow.
>
> The **capillary limit** is the most commonly encountered limit in practice, as it depends directly on wick design and fluid properties.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: Heat Pipe Construction, Working Principle, and Working Fluid Selection

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** *(14 Marks)*

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a conventional cylindrical heat pipe. *(7 Marks)*

**b)** Discuss the criteria for selection of working fluid and wick material in a heat pipe. Give suitable examples for low, medium, and high-temperature applications. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (4 Marks):**

A conventional heat pipe consists of the following components:

- **Outer casing (vessel):** A sealed metallic tube (copper, aluminum, stainless steel).
- **Wick structure:** A porous lining along the inner wall — types include screen mesh, sintered powder, grooved tube, and arterial wicks.
- **Working fluid:** A liquid that undergoes phase change within the operating temperature range (e.g., water, sodium, mercury).
- **End caps:** Hermetically sealed to maintain vacuum and contain the fluid charge.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**

1. **Evaporator section:** External heat $Q_{in}$ is applied → liquid in wick absorbs heat → evaporates into vapor.
2. **Adiabatic (transport) section:** Vapor flows from hot end to cold end with negligible heat loss.
3. **Condenser section:** Vapor condenses, releasing latent heat $Q_{out}$ to the sink.
4. **Wick (capillary) action:** The liquid condensate is wicked back to the evaporator by capillary pressure, completing the cycle.

**Neat Sketch (refer Section 4.1 diagram for the conceptual layout):**

```
|<-- Evaporator -->|<-- Adiabatic -->|<-- Condenser -->|
  [Wick with liquid]   [Vapor space]    [Wick with liquid]
   Q_in →               ←                → Q_out
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Criteria for Working Fluid Selection (4 Marks):**

A suitable working fluid must satisfy:
- **Compatibility** with the wick and casing (no corrosion).
- **High latent heat of vaporization** ($h_{fg}$) for high heat transport per unit mass.
- **Wettability** — low contact angle $\theta$ with the wick material.
- **Low viscosity** — reduces frictional pressure drop in liquid return.
- **High surface tension** — enhances capillary pressure.
- **Suitable saturation temperature** matching the operating range.
- **Thermal stability** and low freezing point.

**Criteria for Wick Material Selection (1 Mark):**
- High permeability $K$ for low viscous losses.
- Small effective pore radius $r_{eff}$ for high capillary pressure.
- Good thermal conductivity.
- Chemical compatibility with the working fluid.

**Examples by Temperature Range (2 Marks):**

| **Application Range** | **Working Fluid** | **Casing & Wick** |
|---|---|---|
| **Low** (-50 to 200 °C) | Ammonia, Acetone, Methanol | Aluminum casing, mesh wick |
| **Medium** (200 to 600 °C) | Water, NaK eutectic | Copper/Stainless casing, sintered wick |
| **High** (600 to 1200 °C) | Sodium, Lithium, Mercury | Stainless steel / Inconel casing, grooved wick |

---

### Question B: Capillary Limit Derivation and Heat Pipe Performance Parameters

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** *(14 Marks)*

**a)** Derive the steady-state pressure balance equation for a heat pipe and explain the concept of the **capillary limit**. *(7 Marks)*

**b)** Define and derive an expression for the **effective thermal conductivity** of a heat pipe. Compare its value with copper for a typical water-copper heat pipe. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Derivation (5 Marks):**

For continuous operation, the capillary pressure generated by the wick must overcome:
1. Pressure drop in the liquid path ($\Delta P_l$)
2. Pressure drop in the vapor path ($\Delta P_v$)
3. Hydrostatic head due to gravity ($\Delta P_g$)

The **steady-state pressure balance** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{cap} &= \Delta P_l + \Delta P_v + \Delta P_g \\
\frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}} &= \frac{\mu_l \dot{m} L_{eff,l}}{K \cdot A_w \cdot \rho_l} + \frac{8 \mu_v \dot{m} L_{eff,v}}{\pi \rho_v r_v^{4}} + \rho_l g L \sin\psi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Capillary Limit (2 Marks):**

The **capillary limit** is the maximum heat $Q_{max}$ the wick can transport before the meniscus at the evaporator interface becomes unstable and "dries out." Beyond this limit, the heat pipe fails.

**Mathematically:**
$$Q_{max} = \frac{\Delta P_{cap,max} - \rho_l g L \sin\psi}{\left(\dfrac{\mu_l L_{eff,l}}{K A_w \rho_l h_{fg}} + \dfrac{8 \mu_v L_{eff,v}}{\pi \rho_v r_v^{4} h_{fg}}\right)}$$

**Where:** $L_{eff,l}$ and $L_{eff,v}$ are the effective liquid and vapor transport lengths.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition of $k_{eff}$ (2 Marks):**

The **effective thermal conductivity** of a heat pipe is a hypothetical value of thermal conductivity assigned to an equivalent solid rod of identical geometry that would transport the same heat $Q$ under the same temperature difference $\Delta T$.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**

From Fourier's law for an equivalent solid rod:
$$Q = k_{eff} A \frac{\Delta T}{L}$$

Rearranging:
$$k_{eff} = \frac{Q L}{A \Delta T}$$

For a heat pipe: $Q = \dot{m} h_{fg}$, and $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}}$. Substituting:
$$k_{eff} = \frac{\dot{m} h_{fg} L}{A \Delta T}$$

**Numerical Comparison (2 Marks):**

For a typical water-copper heat pipe: $k_{eff} \approx 50,000$ W/m·K.
For solid copper: $k_{Cu} = 401$ W/m·K.

$$\frac{k_{eff}}{k_{Cu}} = \frac{50{,}000}{401} \approx 125$$

The heat pipe is approximately **125 times** more conductive than solid copper for the same geometry, demonstrating its superior heat transport capability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **effective thermal conductivity** with the actual conduction in the wick. Remember: $k_{eff}$ is a *hypothetical* property used only for performance comparison.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** In the pressure balance equation, many students write $\Delta P_{cap} = \Delta P_l + \Delta P_v + \Delta P_g$ with an **equality sign**, but the strict KTU convention is $\Delta P_{cap} \geq \text{sum of losses}$ (inequality, since margin is needed for stability).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In Part B, when asked about working fluid selection, students often forget to mention **wettability and compatibility** — these are non-negotiable syllabus items.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** The unit of surface tension $\sigma$ is often written as N/m² (pressure) instead of N/m (force per unit length). This is a frequent deduction in the KTU valuation scheme.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Heat Pipe = Passive Two-Phase Heat Transfer Device** — Sealed, contains working fluid, wick, and vapor space.
- **Working cycle:** Evaporation → Vapor transport → Condensation → Capillary return.
- **Three Sections:** Evaporator, Adiabatic (Transport), Condenser.
- **Effective thermal conductivity** can exceed **50,000 W/m·K** — far surpassing copper.
- **Four Working Limits:** Capillary, Boiling, Entrainment, Sonic.
- **Capillary pressure** is given by the Young-Laplace equation: $\Delta P_{cap} = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{r_{eff}}$.
- **Working fluid selection** is temperature-dependent: Water (5–200 °C), NaK (200–600 °C), Sodium (600–1200 °C).
- **Wick types:** Grooved, screen mesh, sintered powder, fiber, arterial.
- **Steady-state pressure balance:** $\Delta P_{cap} \geq \Delta P_l + \Delta P_v + \Delta P_g$.
- **KTU must-know examples:** Thermosyphons (no wick, gravity-assisted) and Loop Heat Pipes (LHPs).
- **First historical use:** Los Alamos National Laboratory (Gaugler, 1942) and subsequent patent by Trefethen (1962).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Quick-Recall Checklist:**
> ✔ Define heat pipe with all four components
> ✔ State the working principle in 4–5 steps
> ✔ Recall the 4 working limits
> ✔ Recall the capillary pressure formula
> ✔ State the steady-state pressure balance inequality
> ✔ Identify working fluids for different temperature ranges
> ✔ Compute $k_{eff}$ for a given numerical case

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Radiation Heat Transfer # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Thermal Radiation** is the energy emitted by matter in the form of electromagnetic waves (or photons) as a consequence of its temperature. Unlike conduction and convection, radiation heat transfer does **not** require a material medium and can occur through vacuum at the speed of light ($c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$). Mathematically, radiation heat transfer rate between two surfaces is governed by the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: $$q = \varepsilon \sigma A \left( T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4 \right)$$ where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$ is the **Stefan-Boltzmann constant**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Module 3 of PCMET503 covers Radiation — including fundamental concepts (emissive power, absorptivity, reflectivity, transmissivity, blackbody, grey body, Kirchhoff's law), view factors, radiation networks, and radiation shields. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of thermal radiation like **standing near a campfire**. You don't need to touch the flame or feel the hot air (conduction/convection) — you *feel the warmth on your face* from across the distance. That energy is travelling as invisible electromagnetic waves (primarily infrared). - **Hotter objects** = brighter glow, more energetic photons (think red-hot iron vs. white-hot tungsten). - **Dark objects** = absorb more radiation (black t-shirt on a sunny day). - **Shiny metallic surfaces** = reflect radiation (aluminum foil inside a thermos). > [!NOTE] > **The Triple-R Concept:** Every surface, when radiation strikes it, can do only three things — **R**eflect, **A**bsorb, or **T**ransmit. Conservation demands: > $$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$ > For an **opaque** surface, $\tau = 0$, so $\alpha + \rho = 1$. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants | Constant | Symbol | Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Stefan-Boltzmann | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ | | Speed of light | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^8}$ | m/s | | Planck constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}$ | J·s | | Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23}}$ | J/K | | Wien displacement constant | $b$ | $\mathbf{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}$ | m·K | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Radiative Properties ### 2.1.1 Emissive Power ($E$) The total energy emitted by a surface per unit area per unit time. For a **blackbody** (perfect emitter/absorber): $$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{(Stefan-Boltzmann Law)}$$ For a **real surface**, emissivity $\varepsilon = E/E_b \le 1$. ### 2.1.2 Emissivity ($\varepsilon$) Ratio of emissive power of a real surface to that of a blackbody at the same temperature. - **Blackbody:** $\varepsilon = 1$ (perfect emitter, $\alpha = 1$) - **Grey body:** $\varepsilon = \text{const} < 1$ (wavelength independent) - **Real body:** $\varepsilon = \varepsilon(\lambda)$ (wavelength dependent) ### 2.1.3 Kirchhoff's Law For a body in thermal equilibrium, the emissivity equals the absorptivity at each wavelength: $$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$ For a grey body: $\varepsilon = \alpha$. ## 2.2 Blackbody Radiation Laws (KTU High-Yield) ### 2.2.1 Planck's Distribution Law Spectral emissive power of a blackbody: $$E_{b,\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp(hc/\lambda k_B T) - 1 \right]}$$ ### 2.2.2 Wien's Displacement Law Wavelength of maximum emission: $$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m·K}$$ ### 2.2.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (integrated form) Total hemispherical emissive power: $$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b,\lambda}\, d\lambda = \sigma T^4$$ ## 2.3 View Factor (Configuration Factor) — $F_{i \to j}$ The **view factor** $F_{i \to j}$ is the fraction of radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$ directly. **Two fundamental relations:** - **Summation Rule:** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$ - **Reciprocity Rule:** $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Favourite Result:** For two **infinite parallel plates**, $F_{1 \to 2} = F_{2 \to 1} = 1$. For concentric cylinders/spheres with $A_1$ (inner) and $A_2$ (outer): $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$ and $F_{2 \to 1} = A_1/A_2$. ## 2.4 Radiation Heat Transfer Between Surfaces For two **diffuse, grey, opaque** surfaces forming an enclosure: $$Q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1 \to 2}} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$$ ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula | Units | |---|---|---| | Stefan-Boltzmann | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | Planck's Distribution | $E_{b,\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1)}$ | $\text{W/m}^2 \mu\text{m}$ | | Wien's Law | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | m·K | | Energy balance | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | dimensionless | | Kirchhoff's Law | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | dimensionless | | Grey body emission | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | View factor reciprocity | $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | | View factor summation | $\sum_j F_{i \to j} = 1$ | dimensionless | | Two-surface network | $Q = \dfrac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(1-\varepsilon_1)/\varepsilon_1 A_1 + 1/A_1 F_{1\to2} + (1-\varepsilon_2)/\varepsilon_2 A_2}$ | W | | Radiation shield (n shields) | $Q_{\text{with shields}} = Q_{\text{without}} / (n+1)$ | W | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world utility:** Radiation analysis is critical in furnace design, spacecraft thermal protection (re-entry tiles), solar collectors, cryogenic insulation (multi-layer insulation with radiation shields), and fire/thermal hazard analysis. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law We start with Planck's spectral distribution law for a blackbody: $$E_{b,\lambda} = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) -
