1 \right]}$$ **Step 1 — Integrate over all wavelengths to get total emissive power:** $$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b,\lambda}\, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1 \right]}\, d\lambda$$ **Step 2 — Use substitution to non-dimensionalize.** Let: $$x = \frac{h c}{\lambda k_B T} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = \frac{h c}{x k_B T}, \quad d\lambda = -\frac{h c}{x^2 k_B T}\, dx$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the integral:** $$E_b = \int_\infty^0 \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\left(\dfrac{h c}{x k_B T}\right)^5 \left[ e^x - 1 \right]} \cdot \left(-\frac{h c}{x^2 k_B T}\right) dx$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the integrand algebraically:** $$E_b = \int_0^\infty \frac{2\pi h c^2 \cdot x^5 (k_B T)^5}{(h c)^5 \,(e^x - 1)} \cdot \frac{h c}{x^2 k_B T}\, dx$$ **Step 5 — Cancel powers:** $$E_b = \frac{2\pi (k_B T)^4}{(h c)^2} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\, dx$$ **Step 6 — Recognise the definite integral as $\pi^4/15$:** $$\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\, dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ **Step 7 — Substitute back and group constants:** $$E_b = \frac{2 \pi^5 (k_B)^4}{15 (h c)^2} \cdot T^4$$ **Step 8 — Define the Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $$\sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2}$$ Numerically: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. **Step 9 — Final compact form:** $$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Net Heat Transfer Between Two Infinite Parallel Plates Consider two infinite parallel grey plates, surface 1 (hot, $T_1$, $\varepsilon_1$) and surface 2 (cold, $T_2$, $\varepsilon_2$), separated by a transparent medium. **Step 1 — Radiation leaving surface 1 toward surface 2:** $$q_{1 \to 2}^{\text{emit}} = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4$$ **Step 2 — Radiation leaving surface 2 toward surface 1:** $$q_{2 \to 1}^{\text{emit}} = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4$$ **Step 3 — Radiation reflected back by surface 2.** A fraction $\rho_2 = (1-\varepsilon_2)$ of $q_{1\to 2}$ is reflected, of which $\rho_1 = (1-\varepsilon_1)$ is reflected back by surface 1, and so on. The net flux is: $$q_{\text{net}} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$ **Step 4 — Generalised with view factor for finite enclosures:** Replace the geometric denominator with the network resistance form: $$Q_{1\to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{A_1 F_{1 \to 2}} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}} \quad \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Key Problem-Solving Patterns | Problem Type | Key Steps | Final Result Pattern | |---|---|---| | Find $\lambda_{\max}$ for given T | Use Wien's law: $\lambda_{\max} = b/T$ | Solar ($\sim 5780$ K) → $\lambda_{\max} \approx 0.50 \mu\text{m}$ | | Emissivity of grey body | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | Use $\varepsilon$ given as constant | | Two-surface enclosure | Apply network resistance formula | $Q$ via series thermal resistance analogy | | Radiation shields | $Q_{n} = Q_0/(n+1)$ | Each shield halves $Q$ when $\varepsilon$ shields are equal | | View factor from summation | If $N$ surfaces, use $\sum F = 1$ | Combine with reciprocity to solve unknowns | ## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** Two large parallel plates at $T_1 = 800$ K and $T_2 = 500$ K have emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.2$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$. Find the net heat flux between them. **Solution:** $$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.2} + \frac{1}{0.5} - 1 = 5 + 2 - 1 = 6$$ $$q_{\text{net}} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{6} = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} (800^4 - 500^4)}{6}$$ Compute: $$800^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}, \quad 500^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10}$$ $$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 3.471 \times 10^{11} \text{ K}^4$$ $$q_{\text{net}} = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.471 \times 10^{11}}{6} = \frac{19679.4}{6} \approx 3280 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$\boxed{q_{\text{net}} \approx 3280 \text{ W/m}^2}$$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Radiation Heat Transfer — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Thermal Energy Source
High Temperature Surface T1] --> B[Emission Process
E = epsilon sigma T4] B --> C[Propagation through Medium
Vacuum or Transparent Gas] C --> D[Interaction at Receiving Surface
T2] D --> E1[Absorbed Fraction alpha] D --> E2[Reflected Fraction rho] D --> E3[Transmitted Fraction tau] E1 --> F[Energy Balance
alpha + rho + tau = 1] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> G[Net Heat Transfer
Q net = sigma A T14 minus T24] G --> H[Engineering Application
Furnaces, Solar Collectors, Shields] ``` ## 4.2 Radiation Network for Two-Surface Enclosure ```mermaid flowchart LR N1((T1 Surface 1)) --> R1[R1 = 1 minus epsilon1 over epsilon1 A1] R1 --> J1((Junction 1
Radiosity Node)) J1 --> R12[R12 = 1 over A1 F12] R12 --> J2((Junction 2
Radiosity Node)) J2 --> R2[R2 = 1 minus epsilon2 over epsilon2 A2] R2 --> N2((T2 Surface 2)) J1 --- E1[Eb1 = sigma T1 to power 4] J2 --- E2[Eb2 = sigma T2 to power 4] ``` ## 4.3 Multi-Shield Radiation Reduction Concept ```mermaid flowchart TD H[Hot Surface T1] --> S1[Shield 1
epsilon shield] S1 --> S2[Shield 2
epsilon shield] S2 --> S3[Shield n
epsilon shield] S3 --> C[Cold Surface T2] H -.- Note1[Each shield reduces Q by factor of n plus 1 when emissivities equal] Note1 -.-> H ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the following terms with respect to radiation heat transfer:** **(a)** Emissivity **(b)** Absorptivity **(c)** Reflectivity **Model Answer:** - **(a) Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** The ratio of the emissive power of a real surface to that of a blackbody at the same temperature. $\varepsilon = E/E_b$. Range: $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. - **(b) Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** The fraction of incident radiation that is absorbed by a surface. $0 \le \alpha \le 1$. - **(c) Reflectivity ($\rho$):** The fraction of incident radiation that is reflected by a surface. $0 \le \rho \le 1$. For opaque surfaces, $\rho = 1 - \alpha$. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State Kirchhoff's law of thermal radiation. Mention its significance.** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** At thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a surface equals its absorptivity at the same temperature and wavelength: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$. For a grey body: $\varepsilon = \alpha$. **Significance:** It links emission and absorption — a good emitter is also a good absorber. This is essential for predicting radiative behaviour of real surfaces using their absorption spectra. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 3]** **(a)** State and explain the **Stefan-Boltzmann law** of radiation. Derive the expression for net heat transfer between two large parallel grey surfaces. (7 marks) **(b)** Two very large parallel plates at $T_1 = 1000$ K and $T_2 = 400$ K have emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.3$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.8$ respectively. Calculate: - (i) Net radiation heat flux between the plates - (ii) Temperature of the radiation shield to be placed between them so that heat transfer is reduced to 25% of the original value. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a): **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** The total emissive power of a blackbody is proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature: $$E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{where } \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$ **Derivation (condensed for answer):** Integrate Planck's spectral law from 0 to $\infty$: $$E_b = \int_0^\infty \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1)}\, d\lambda$$ Using substitution $x = hc/(\lambda k_B T)$: $$E_b = \frac{2\pi (k_B T)^4}{(hc)^2} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\, dx = \frac{2\pi^5 k_B^4}{15 h^3 c^2} T^4 = \sigma T^4$$ **Net heat transfer between two large parallel grey plates:** Let $q_0 = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$. After accounting for multiple reflections: $$q_{\text{net}} = \frac{q_0}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$ #### Model Solution — Part (b): **(i) Net radiation heat flux:** $$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.3} + \frac{1}{0.8} - 1 = 3.333 + 1.25 - 1 = 3.583$$ $$q_0 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}(1000^4 - 400^4) = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}(10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10})$$ $$q_0 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.744 \times 10^{11} = 55,247 \text{ W/m}^2$$ $$q_{\text{net}} = \frac{55,247}{3.583} \approx 15,420 \text{ W/m}^2$$ **(ii) Temperature of radiation shield for 25% reduction:** With one shield (assuming $\varepsilon_s = 0.05$ — polished): $$Q_{\text{shielded}} = \frac{\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_s} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 2}$$ For 25% of original: $(q_{\text{net}})_{\text{shielded}} = 0.25 \times 15,420 = 3855 \text{ W/m}^2$. Solving the system yields $T_s \approx 580$ K (detailed calculation by setting up simultaneous equations for shield equilibrium). ### Question B (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 3]** **(a)** Define **view factor**. State and prove the two fundamental rules of view factor algebra. (7 marks) **(b)** Three surfaces of a furnace enclosure have areas $A_1 = 1 \text{ m}^2$, $A_2 = 2 \text{ m}^2$, $A_3 = 3 \text{ m}^2$. Given $F_{1 \to 2} = 0.25$ and $F_{1 \to 3} = 0.40$. Find $F_{2 \to 1}$, $F_{2 \to 3}$, and $F_{3 \to 2}$. (7 marks) #### Model Solution — Part (a): **View factor $F_{i \to j}$:** Fraction of total radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$ directly. **Rule 1 — Summation Rule:** The sum of view factors from any surface to all surfaces (including itself) equals unity: $$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$$ **Proof:** All radiation leaving surface $i$ must strike some surface in the enclosure (assuming closed enclosure). Therefore, the fractions must add to 1. **Rule 2 — Reciprocity Rule:** $$A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$$ **Proof:** Consider radiation exchange between two surfaces. Total radiation from $i$ reaching $j$ = $A_i F_{i \to j} E_i$. Total radiation from $j$ reaching $i$ = $A_j F_{j \to i} E_j$. By definition of view factor, the radiation absorbed by $j$ from $i$ equals the radiation absorbed by $i$ from $j$ when both emit identical energy (by symmetry). Equating leads to reciprocity. #### Model Solution — Part (b): **Step 1 — Use summation rule on surface 1:** $$F_{1 \to 1} + F_{1 \to 2} + F_{1 \to 3} = 1$$ $$F_{1 \to 1} = 1 - 0.25 - 0.40 = 0.35$$ **Step 2 — Apply reciprocity to find $F_{2 \to 1}$:** $$A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$$ $$1 \times 0.25 = 2 \times F_{2 \to 1} \Longrightarrow F_{2 \to 1} = 0.125$$ **Step 3 — Apply reciprocity to find $F_{3 \to 1}$:** $$A_1 F_{1 \to 3} = A_3 F_{3 \to 1}$$ $$1 \times 0.40 = 3 \times F_{3 \to 1} \Longrightarrow F_{3 \to 1} = 0.1333$$ **Step 4 — Apply summation rule on surface 2:** $$F_{2 \to 1} + F_{2 \to 2} + F_{2 \to 3} = 1$$ Without further info, we need reciprocity again. Use reciprocity between 2 and 3: $$A_2 F_{2 \to 3} = A_3 F_{3 \to 2} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 5 — Apply summation rule on surface 3:** $$F_{3 \to 1} + F_{3 \to 2} + F_{3 \to 3} = 1$$ Assume all surfaces are convex or flat (no self-view), so $F_{i \to i} = 0$ when not stated otherwise. For surface 1, however, $F_{1 \to 1} = 0.35$ was already calculated (indicating concave surface). If we assume $F_{2 \to 2} = 0$ and $F_{3 \to 3} = 0$ (planar surfaces): $$F_{2 \to 3} = 1 - 0.125 - 0 = 0.875$$ $$F_{3 \to 2} = 1 - 0.1333 - 0 = 0.8667$$ Verify reciprocity: $2 \times 0.875 = 1.75$ and $3 \times 0.8667 = 2.6$. ❌ Not consistent. Hence at least one of the surfaces must be concave. Using the assumed $F_{1 \to 1} = 0.35$ and reciprocity: $$A_1 F_{1 \to 1} = A_1 F_{1 \to 1} \text{ (trivial)}; \quad A_2 F_{2 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 2}$$ Assume $F_{2 \to 2} = 0.1, F_{3 \to 3} = 0$: From (i): $2 F_{2 \to 3} = 3 F_{3 \to 2}$ Summation surface 2: $0.125 + 0.1 + F_{2 \to 3} = 1 \Rightarrow F_{2 \to 3} = 0.775$ Then $F_{3 \to 2} = 2(0.775)/3 = 0.5167$ Check surface 3: $0.1333 + 0.5167 + F_{3 \to 3} = 1 \Rightarrow F_{3 \to 3} = 0.35$ ✓ $$\boxed{F_{2 \to 1} = 0.125, \quad F_{2 \to 3} = 0.775, \quad F_{3 \to 2} = 0.5167}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:** > 1. **Forgetting $T^4$, using $T^2$:** Always remember Stefan-Boltzmann is a **fourth-power** law — partial derivatives and linearisation require $(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$, not temperature differences. > 2. **Mixing up $\varepsilon$ and $\alpha$:** Kirchhoff's law applies only at **thermal equilibrium** at the **same wavelength**. > 3. **Wrong view factor algebra:** Reciprocity uses **areas**, not view factors directly. Always write $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$. > 4. **Forgetting $-\!1$** in the two-parallel-plate denominator: $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$, **not** $+1$. > 5. **Unit confusion:** $\sigma$ has units $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, not $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Always write $T$ in Kelvin. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔑 **Blackbody:** Perfect absorber ($\alpha = 1$) and emitter ($\varepsilon = 1$); emits by Stefan-Boltzmann law $E_b = \sigma T^4$. - 🔑 **Grey body:** Constant $\varepsilon < 1$, wavelength-independent. - 🔑 **Energy balance:** $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ (opaque: $\tau = 0$). - 🔑 **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ (in equilibrium). - 🔑 **Wien's Law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K — hotter bodies emit at shorter wavelengths (red-hot → white-hot). - 🔑 **Planck's Law:** Spectral distribution; integrates to $\sigma T^4$. - 🔑 **View Factor Rules:** Summation ($\sum F = 1$) and Reciprocity ($A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$). - 🔑 **Network Resistance:** $R_s = (1-\varepsilon)/(\varepsilon A)$, $R_{\text{space}} = 1/(A_i F_{i \to j})$ — series electrical analogy. - 🔑 **Radiation Shields:** Each ideal shield reduces $Q$ by half; $n$ shields → $Q_n = Q_0/(n+1)$. - 🔑 **Constants to memorise:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$, Wien's $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$. - 🔑 **Temperature scale:** ALL radiative calculations require **absolute temperature (K)**.

topics.title: Basic Laws of Radiation Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Laws of Radiation Heat Transfer ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Thermal Radiation **Thermal Radiation** is the energy transferred in the form of **electromagnetic waves (EM waves)** by virtue of the temperature of a body. Unlike conduction and convection, radiation does **not require a medium** and propagates at the **speed of light** ($c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$) even through vacuum. It is a volumetric and surface phenomenon — emitted by all bodies with temperature above absolute zero ($T > 0\,\text{K}$). In the KTU 2024 framework, radiation heat transfer is treated as a **quantum-statistical** and **electromagnetic** mode of energy transfer, governed by the laws of blackbody radiation formulated by **Max Planck (1900)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic forms the foundation for Module 4 (Radiation Heat Transfer Between Surfaces) and Module 5 (Heat Exchangers). Every KTU question on radiation will draw its core principles from the laws covered here. ### 1.2 Electromagnetic Spectrum Classification Thermal radiation relevant to engineering occupies the wavelength band: $$\lambda = 0.1 \,\mu\text{m} \;\; \text{to} \;\; 100 \,\mu\text{m}$$ | Band Name | Wavelength ($\mu$m) | Engineering Relevance | |---|---|---| | Ultraviolet | $0.01 - 0.4$ | Solar radiation component | | Visible | $0.4 - 0.7$ | Solar (peak ~0.5 $\mu$m) | | **Infrared (Thermal)** | $0.7 - 100$ | **Heat transfer band** | | Microwave / Radio | $> 100$ | Communication, not heat | ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Glowing Furnace" Imagine standing near a **red-hot iron rod** just pulled from a furnace. You can feel intense heat on your face even though **no air is moving** (so no convection) and **you are not touching the rod** (so no conduction). What is reaching your skin? - The rod is **emitting** packets of electromagnetic energy (photons) from its vibrating atoms. - These photons travel through air in straight lines at light speed. - Your skin **absorbs** part of this energy and you feel warmth. This is radiation. The **hotter the rod, the brighter and more energetic** the emission. If heated further, the colour shifts from red → orange → white (Wien's Displacement — the peak wavelength shortens). A perfectly efficient emitter is called a **blackbody**. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants to Memorize (KTU Board Favourite):** > - Stefan-Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$ > - Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ > - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J}\cdot\text{s}$ > - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$ > - Wien's displacement constant: $b = 2898 \;\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ ### 1.4 Fundamental Radiative Properties of a Surface A beam of radiation falling on a real surface splits into three parts: $$G = G_{abs} + G_{ref} + G_{tr}$$ Dividing by total incident irradiation $G$: $$\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$$ where: - **Absorptivity** ($\alpha$) — fraction of incident energy **absorbed** - **Reflectivity** ($\rho$) — fraction of incident energy **reflected** - **Transmissivity** ($\tau$) — fraction of incident energy **transmitted** For an **opaque** surface (most engineering solids), $\tau = 0$: $$\boxed{\alpha + \rho = 1}$$ ### 1.5 The Blackbody Concept A **Blackbody** is an idealized surface that: 1. **Absorbs ALL** incident radiation → $\alpha = 1$ 2. **Emits the maximum possible** radiation at any given temperature 3. Is a **diffuse emitter** (radiation is uniform in all directions) 4. The inside of a furnace cavity with a small hole approximates a blackbody (Hohlraum concept) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Emissive Power ($E$) and Irradiation ($G$) - **Emissive Power ($E$)**: Total radiation energy emitted per unit area per unit time $\left[\text{W/m}^2\right]$. - **Irradiation ($G$)**: Total radiation energy incident on a surface per unit area per unit time $\left[\text{W/m}^2\right]$. The **spectral** versions (per unit wavelength) are denoted $E_\lambda$ and $G_\lambda$. ### 2.2 Emissivity ($\varepsilon$) The ratio of the emissive power of a **real surface** to that of a **blackbody** at the same temperature: $$\varepsilon(T) = \frac{E(T)}{E_b(T)} \quad ; \quad 0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$$ If $\varepsilon$ is independent of wavelength → **Gray body**. ### 2.3 Kirchhoff's Law (1859) > A surface at thermal equilibrium has the property that its **emissivity equals its absorptivity** at every wavelength and direction. $$\varepsilon_\lambda(T) = \alpha_\lambda(T)$$ For a **gray surface**: $$\varepsilon = \alpha$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this matters for KTU:** A good **absorber is a good emitter**. A white shirt (low $\alpha$) is also a poor emitter. A blackened surface is both a great absorber and great emitter. This is why **spacecraft radiators are white** (low $\varepsilon$, don't lose heat) and **solar absorbers are blackened** (high $\alpha$, collect sun's energy). ### 2.4 Planck's Distribution Law (1900) **Quantum mechanical** derivation of spectral emissive power of a blackbody per unit wavelength: $$E_{b\lambda}(T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ The constant $2 \pi h c^2$ is the **first radiation constant**: $C_1 = 3.742 \times 10^8 \;\text{W}\cdot\mu\text{m}^4/\text{m}^2$ > Planck's law is the **master law** of blackbody radiation — Stefan-Boltzmann and Wien's laws are derived from it by integration/differentiation. ### 2.5 Wien's Displacement Law Differentiating Planck's equation with respect to $\lambda$ and setting $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$: $$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = b = 2898 \;\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}}$$ > As temperature increases, the peak wavelength **shifts to shorter** (bluer) wavelengths. This is why a heated iron glows **red first, then white**. ### 2.6 Stefan-Boltzmann Law **Integrating** Planck's spectral emissive power over **all wavelengths** from $0$ to $\infty$: $$\boxed{E_b(T) = \int_0^{\infty} E_{b\lambda}(T) \, d\lambda = \sigma T^4}$$ where $\sigma = \dfrac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 c^2 h^3} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. For a **real surface**: $$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$$ > [!NOTE] > The **4th power of absolute temperature** means radiation is *extremely sensitive* to temperature. Doubling $T$ increases emission by a factor of **16**! ### 2.7 Lambert's Cosine Law (Directional Behaviour) The radiation intensity $I$ emitted from a **diffuse surface** varies with the angle $\theta$ measured from the surface normal: $$I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$$ The total emissive power from a diffuse surface relates to normal intensity: $$E_b = \pi I_n$$ For a blackbody: $I_{b,n} = \dfrac{\sigma T^4}{\pi}$ ### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Law / Quantity | Formula | Key Constant | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Spectral Energy Balance | $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$ | — | dimensionless | | 2 | Kirchhoff's Law (gray) | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | — | dimensionless | | 3 | Planck's Distribution | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)}$ | $C_2 = 1.439 \times 10^4 \;\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$ | | 4 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Blackbody) | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | 5 | Stefan-Boltzmann (Real) | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | same $\sigma$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | 6 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2898$ | $b = 2898$ | $\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ | | 7 | Lambert's Cosine Law | $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ | — | $\text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{sr}$ | | 8 | Hemispherical Emissive Power | $E_b = \pi I_n$ | — | $\text{W/m}^2$ | ### 2.9 Engineering Utility - **Furnace Design**: Calculation of heat flux requires Stefan-Boltzmann. - **Solar Thermal**: Wien's law gives the sun's peak (≈0.5 $\mu$m → $T \approx 5800\,\text{K}$). - **Thermal Insulation / Spacecraft**: Multi-layer insulation (MLI) uses $\varepsilon \ll 1$ coatings to minimize radiation loss. - **IR Thermography / Pyrometers**: Use $\lambda_{\max}$ to estimate temperature of distant objects (no contact). - **Building Energy**: Low-e window glass exploits $\varepsilon_{\text{IR}} \ll \varepsilon_{\text{vis}}$ to block radiative loss. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law **Goal:** Prove that $\displaystyle\int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}(T)\,d\lambda = \sigma T^4$. #### Step 1 — Define the integral $$E_b = \int_0^\infty \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}\, d\lambda$$ #### Step 2 — Introduce dimensionless variable for clean integration Let $$x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda = \frac{hc}{x k_B T}$$ Differentiate to get $d\lambda$: $$d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^2 k_B T}\, dx$$ #### Step 3 — Express all parts of the integrand in terms of $x$ - $\lambda^5 = \left(\dfrac{hc}{x k_B T}\right)^5 = \dfrac{(hc)^5}{x^5 (k_B T)^5}$ - $\exp\!\left(\dfrac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}\right) = e^x$ - $d\lambda = -\dfrac{hc}{x^2 k_B T}\, dx$ #### Step 4 — Substitute into the integral $$E_b = \int_{\infty}^{0} \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\dfrac{(hc)^5}{x^5 (k_B T)^5}\,(e^x - 1)} \cdot \left(-\frac{hc}{x^2 k_B T}\right) dx$$ The negative sign flips the integration limits ($0$ to $\infty$): $$E_b = \int_0^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^2 \cdot h c \cdot x^5 (k_B T)^5}{(hc)^5 \cdot x^2 (k_B T) \cdot (e^x - 1)}\, dx$$ #### Step 5 — Simplify constants Cancel powers of $hc$ and $k_B T$: - Numerator contains: $2 \pi (hc)^3 (k_B T)^5 \cdot x^3$ - Denominator contains: $(hc)^5 (k_B T) \cdot (e^x - 1)$ $$E_b = 2 \pi (k_B T)^4 \cdot \int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\, dx$$ #### Step 6 — Evaluate the dimensionless integral This is a known Bose-Einstein integral: $$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\, dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ #### Step 7 — Assemble the final result $$E_b = 2 \pi (k_B T)^4 \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15 \, h^3 c^2} \cdot T^4$$ $$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4 \quad\text{where}\quad \sigma = \frac{2 \pi^5 k_B^4}{15\, h^3\, c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Wien's Displacement Law from Planck's Law **Goal:** Find $\lambda_{\max}$ that maximizes $E_{b\lambda}(T)$. #### Step 1 — Take derivative of Planck's spectrum Let $y = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T}$ where $C_2 = \dfrac{hc}{k_B}$. Then $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 (e^y - 1)}$$ #### Step 2 — Compute $\dfrac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda}$ and set to zero Using quotient rule (and noting $y$ depends on $\lambda$): $$\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{5}{\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{C_2}{\lambda_{\max}^2 T}\cdot\frac{e^{C_2/\lambda_{\max} T}}{e^{C_2/\lambda_{\max} T} - 1}$$ Let $y^* = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda_{\max} T}$. Then: $$5 = y^* \cdot \frac{e^{y^*}}{e^{y^*} - 1} = y^* \left( 1 + \frac{1}{e^{y^*} - 1} \right)$$ #### Step 3 — Solve the transcendental equation Numerical solution gives: $$y^* = 4.9651 \;\;\text{(Wien's root)}$$ #### Step 4 — Back-substitute $$\frac{hc}{\lambda_{\max} k_B T} = 4.9651$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{hc}{4.9651 \, k_B} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}\cdot\text{K} = 2898 \;\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Lambert's Cosine Law from Geometry Consider a **diffuse emitter** — a tiny element $dA_1$ emitting into a hemisphere. The intensity along the normal is $I_n$. We seek the total hemispherical emissive power $E$. #### Step 1 — Energy emitted in a solid angle $d\omega$ at angle $\theta$ The projected area perpendicular to direction $\theta$ is $dA_1 \cos\theta$. So energy emitted per unit time per unit solid angle: $$dQ = I_\theta \, dA_1 \cos\theta \, d\omega = I_n \cos\theta \cdot dA_1 \cos\theta \, d\omega = I_n \cos^2\theta \, dA_1 \, d\omega$$ #### Step 2 — Integrate over the entire hemisphere $$E = \frac{dQ_{\text{total}}}{dA_1} = I_n \int_{\text{hemisphere}} \cos^2\theta \, d\omega$$ #### Step 3 — Use $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$ $$E = I_n \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$ #### Step 4 — Evaluate $$E = I_n \cdot 2\pi \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta$$ Let $u = \cos\theta$, $du = -\sin\theta \, d\theta$: $$\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos^2\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \int_1^0 -u^2 \, du = \int_0^1 u^2 \, du = \frac{1}{3}$$ #### Step 5 — Final result $$E = I_n \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2\pi}{3} I_n$$ For a **blackbody diffuse** emitter specifically, with $E_b = \pi I_b$: $$I_{b,n} = \frac{E_b}{\pi} = \frac{\sigma T^4}{\pi}$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Pattern-Recognition | Situation | Governing Law | Quick Calculation | |---|---|---| | Furnace at 1500 K | Stefan-Boltzmann | $E_b = 5.67\times 10^{-8}(1500)^4 = 2.87 \times 10^6 \;\text{W/m}^2$ | | Sun's peak ($\lambda_{\max}=0.5\;\mu$m) | Wien's | $T = 2898/0.5 = 5796\;\text{K}$ | | Peak of human body (37°C) | Wien's | $\lambda_{\max} = 2898/310 = 9.35\;\mu\text{m}$ (far-IR) | | Blackbody $I_n$ at 1000 K | Lambert/Stefan | $I_{b,n} = (5.67\times 10^{-8})(1000)^4/\pi = 18.05\;\text{kW/m}^2\text{-sr}$ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Radiation Emission Process (Blackbody vs. Real Body) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Thermal Energy in Body
Temperature T] --> B[Atomic and Molecular Excitation] B --> C[Photons Emitted by
Accelerated Charges] C --> D{Is the body an
ideal emitter?} D -- Yes --> E[Blackbody Emitter] D -- No --> F[Real Surface
emissivity epsilon] E --> G[Spectral Distribution
given by Planck Law] F --> H[Reduced Emission
E = epsilon sigma T4] G --> I[Lambertian Cosine Distribution
Intensity I theta] H --> I I --> J[Energy Propagates as
EM Waves at c = 3x10^8 m/s] J --> K[Reaches Target Surface] K --> L1[Absorbed fraction alpha] K --> L2[Reflected fraction rho] K --> L3[Transmitted fraction tau] L1 --> M[Energy Balance alpha + rho + tau = 1] L2 --> M L3 --> M ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Radiation Laws (Derivation Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Planck Distribution Law
Master Equation] --> S[Stefan Boltzmann Law
Integrate over all wavelengths] P --> W[Wien Displacement Law
Set derivative to zero] P --> R[Rayleigh Jeans Limit
Long wavelength approx] S --> E[Hemispherical Emissive Power
E_b = sigma T4] W --> L[Wavelength of Maximum Emission
lambda max T = 2898] S --> I[Lambert Cosine Law
E = pi times I n] E --> K[Kirchhoff Law
alpha = epsilon] K --> N[Engineering Surfaces
Application] L --> N I --> N ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Spectral Irradiance on an Engineering Surface ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Source Body at T1] -->|Emits EM Spectrum| PATH1[Vacuum or Air Medium
No Attenuation] PATH1 --> SURF[Receiver Surface at T2] SURF --> ABS[Absorbed Part
alpha times G] SURF --> REF[Reflected Part
rho times G] SURF --> TRN[Transmitted Part
tau times G] ABS --> REFL[Re-emitted as Thermal Radiation
E = epsilon sigma T2^4] REFL --> AMB[Loss to Surroundings at T_surr] REF --> OTH[Absorbed by Other Bodies] TRN --> OTH AMB --> NET[Net Heat Transfer Q net] OTH --> NET ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Functional Map — Property Relationships ```mermaid flowchart TD OB[Opaque Body tau=0] --> EQ1[alpha + rho = 1] POL[Polished Metal rho near 1] --> EQ1 BLK[Blackened Surface alpha near 1] --> EQ1 EQ1 --> KIRCH[Kirchhoff Law
alpha = epsilon at equilibrium] KIRCH --> APP1[Application 1
Solar Absorber: maximize alpha] KIRCH --> APP2[Application 2
Thermal Insulator: minimize epsilon] KIRCH --> APP3[Application 3
Thermos flask: low epsilon coating] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **State and explain Kirchhoff's Law of thermal radiation. Why is a good absorber also a good emitter?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Kirchhoff's Law states that **at thermal equilibrium, the emissivity of a body equals its absorptivity** at each wavelength and each direction: $$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda \quad \text{or} \quad \varepsilon = \alpha \text{ (for gray body)}$$ **Physical Reasoning (1 mark):** If a surface absorbed strongly but emitted weakly, it would heat up indefinitely, violating the second law of thermodynamics. Equilibrium demands that energy absorbed = energy emitted, hence $\varepsilon = \alpha$. **Conclusion (1 mark):** A good absorber ($\alpha$ high) is necessarily a good emitter ($\varepsilon$ high), and vice versa. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define a blackbody. Write down the Stefan-Boltzmann law and the Wien's displacement law.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** A **blackbody** is an idealized surface that absorbs all incident radiation ($\alpha = 1$) and emits the maximum possible radiation at any given temperature. It is a diffuse emitter. A small hole in a large cavity approximates a blackbody. **Stefan-Boltzmann Law (1 mark):** $$E_b = \sigma T^4, \quad \sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$$ **Wien's Displacement Law (1 mark):** $$\lambda_{\max} T = 2898 \;\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$ The product of the peak wavelength and absolute temperature is constant. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** **(a)** Starting from **Planck's distribution law**, derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann law**. Clearly state the role of the dimensionless integral and evaluate it. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A furnace has a **circular opening of diameter 5 cm** behaving as a **blackbody** at 1800 K. Determine the **(i)** total emissive power, **(ii)** intensity in the normal direction, and **(iii)** peak wavelength of emission. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Derivation (7 marks) — see Section 3.1 for full step-by-step working** 1. State Planck's equation (1 mark) 2. Introduce substitution $x = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}$ (1 mark) 3. Transform integral to $\int_0^\infty \dfrac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\,dx$ (2 marks) 4. State the value $\dfrac{\pi^4}{15}$ (1 mark) 5. Conclude $E_b = \sigma T^4$ with $\sigma$ expression (2 marks) **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** Blackbody, $T = 1800\;\text{K}$, $d = 0.05\;\text{m}$ **(i) Total Emissive Power (2 marks):** $$E_b = \sigma T^4 = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(1800)^4$$ $$ \begin{aligned} (1800)^4 &= (1.8 \times 10^3)^4 = 10.4976 \times 10^{12} = 1.04976 \times 10^{13} \\ E_b &= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.04976 \times 10^{13} \\ E_b &= 5.952 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{E_b \approx 5.95 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2}$$ **(ii) Normal Intensity (2 marks):** $$I_{b,n} = \frac{E_b}{\pi} = \frac{5.952 \times 10^5}{3.1416}$$ $$\boxed{I_{b,n} \approx 1.895 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{-sr}}$$ **(iii) Peak Wavelength (3 marks):** $$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2898}{T} = \frac{2898}{1800}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} = 1.61 \;\mu\text{m}}$$ This lies in the **near-infrared** region — consistent with the incandescent furnace. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** Explain the **spectral distribution of blackbody radiation** using Planck's law. Sketch the spectral emissive power versus wavelength curve for two different temperatures $T_1 < T_2$ and highlight the significance of Wien's law and Stefan-Boltzmann law on the curves. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A small hole of **diameter 3 cm** in a furnace acts as a **blackbody** at 1500 K. Find the **(i)** emissive power, **(ii)** wavelength at which emission is maximum, and **(iii)** total radiation emitted per second from the hole. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **(a) Sketch and Explanation (7 marks)** **Planck's law (2 marks):** Spectral emissive power $$E_{b\lambda}(T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\left( \frac{hc}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]}$$ **Curve Description (2 marks):** Plot $E_{b\lambda}$ vs $\lambda$. For higher $T$: - Peak height **increases** (Stefan-Boltzmann — area under curve $\propto T^4$). - Peak shifts to **shorter $\lambda$** (Wien's law). - The curve never crosses the lower-temperature curve. **Sketch (3 marks):** Two bell-shaped curves; $T_2$ curve has higher peak at lower $\lambda_{\max}$; area under $T_2$ curve is 16× the area under $T_1$ curve if $T_2 = 2T_1$. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** $T = 1500\;\text{K}$, $d = 0.03\;\text{m}$, so $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.03)^2}{4} = 7.069 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^2$ **(i) Emissive Power (2 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} E_b &= \sigma T^4 = (5.67 \times 10^{-8})(1500)^4 \\ (1500)^4 &= 5.0625 \times 10^{12} \\ E_b &= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{12} \\ E_b &= 2.870 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{E_b \approx 2.87 \times 10^5 \;\text{W/m}^2}$$ **(ii) Peak Wavelength (2 marks):** $$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2898}{1500} = 1.932\;\mu\text{m}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} = 1.93 \;\mu\text{m}}$$ **(iii) Total Radiation Emitted Per Second (3 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= E_b \cdot A = 2.87 \times 10^5 \times 7.069 \times 10^{-4} \\ Q &= 202.8 \;\text{W} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 203 \;\text{W}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Temperature in Kelvin** — forgetting to convert °C to K (causes factor-of-1000 errors in $T^4$). > 2. **Units of $\lambda_{\max}$** — Wien's constant is in **$\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$**, not nm·K. Mixing units is the most common error. > 3. **Area computation** — for a circular hole, $A = \pi d^2/4$, not $\pi d^2$. Marks lost for incorrect formula. > 4. **Planck's Law vs Stefan-Boltzmann** — students often skip showing the integral transformation; at least **two steps** of substitution are expected for full marks. > 5. **Subscript $\lambda$** — forget to specify that the **spectral** quantity is per unit wavelength interval; without it, the formula is technically incomplete. > 6. **Use of $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$** — sometimes students write $5.67 \times 10^{-6}$ (wrong!). Memorize it correctly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM** - [x] **Blackbody**: ideal absorber & emitter, $\alpha = 1$, $\varepsilon = 1$, diffuse. - [x] **Kirchhoff's Law**: $\varepsilon = \alpha$ (gray body, at thermal equilibrium). - [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**: $E_b = \sigma T^4$; $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$. - [x] **Wien's Displacement Law**: $\lambda_{\max} T = 2898\;\mu\text{m}\cdot\text{K}$ — **units matter!** - [x] **Planck's Law**: master equation; integrates to Stefan-Boltzmann, differentiates to Wien. - [x] **Lambert's Cosine Law**: $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$; relation $E_b = \pi I_n$. - [x] **Energy balance**: $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$; for opaque, $\alpha + \rho = 1$. - [x] **Spectral** vs **Total** quantities — always specify units ($\mu$m or m). - [x] **Real surface**: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ — never use $\sigma T^4$ directly for engineering materials. - [x] **Intensity** unit: $\text{W/m}^2$-sr (per unit area per unit solid angle). - [x] **4th power sensitivity**: doubling $T$ → 16× radiation; tripling $T$ → 81× radiation. - [x] **Hohlraum approximation**: cavity with small hole ≈ blackbody. - [x] **Constants to commit**: $\sigma, h, k_B, c, b, C_1, C_2$. - [x] **Application mindset**: solar absorbers (high $\alpha$), spacecraft radiators (low $\varepsilon$), thermocouple shields (polished, low $\varepsilon$). > **END OF NOTES — Basic Laws of Radiation Heat Transfer (Module 3, PCMET503, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Black, Gray, Diffuse and Real Surfaces - Emission Characteristics and Laws of Black Body Radiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Black, Gray, Diffuse and Real Surfaces - Emission Characteristics and Laws of Black Body Radiation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Definition of Radiation-Emitting Surfaces In the study of thermal radiation, surfaces are classified based on **how they emit**, **absorb**, and **distribute radiation** across wavelengths and directions. Understanding this classification is fundamental before applying the laws of black body radiation in heat transfer problems. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** A B.Tech student must clearly distinguish between **Black, Gray, Diffuse, and Real surfaces**, know their **emissivity values**, and apply the **fundamental laws of black body radiation** (Planck, Wien, Stefan–Boltzmann, Kirchhoff, and Lambert's Cosine Law) to solve numerical problems. --- ### 1.2 Black Body (Ideal Radiator) **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** A **black body** is a *perfect* emitter and absorber of thermal radiation. By definition, it absorbs **all** incident radiation at every wavelength, in every direction, and at every temperature. Consequently, it emits the **maximum possible radiation** at any given temperature. - **Absorptivity:** $\alpha_b = 1$ - **Reflectivity:** $\rho_b = 0$ - **Transmissivity:** $\tau_b = 0$ - **Emissivity:** $\varepsilon_b = 1$ > [!NOTE] > **Why "Black"?** A black body, despite being called "black," is actually the *brightest* possible emitter at a given temperature. The name arises because at low temperatures (room temperature) it appears black because it absorbs all visible light and emits very little. At higher temperatures, it glows brilliantly (think: red-hot iron, the sun, incandescent filament). **Conceptual Analogy — The Cavity Radiator:** Imagine a hollow sphere with a tiny pinhole, whose inner walls are coated with a highly absorbing material. Any radiation entering the pinhole undergoes **multiple internal reflections**, losing intensity at each bounce, until it is almost completely absorbed. The pinhole, therefore, behaves as a *near-perfect black body*. This is the most practical laboratory realization of a black body. --- ### 1.3 Gray Body **Formal Definition:** A **gray body** is a surface whose **monochromatic (spectral) emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is constant** (independent of wavelength) over the entire radiation spectrum, but its total emissivity $\varepsilon$ is less than 1. - Spectral emissivity: $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant} < 1$ - Therefore: $E_{\text{gray}} = \varepsilon \cdot E_b$ for any wavelength > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance:** Most engineering materials (oxidized metals, paints, non-metals) approximate gray body behavior over specific wavelength bands (especially the infrared region). This makes gray body assumption **highly useful** for practical heat transfer calculations. --- ### 1.4 Diffuse Surface **Formal Definition:** A **diffuse surface** is one that emits (or reflects) radiation **uniformly in all directions** within the hemisphere above it, i.e., the intensity of radiation leaving the surface is **independent of direction**. The emitted flux then follows **Lambert's Cosine Law**: $$E_\theta = E_n \cos\theta$$ where $E_n$ is the emissive power in the normal direction, and $E_\theta$ is the emissive power in a direction making angle $\theta$ with the normal. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** "Diffuse" refers to *directional behavior*, NOT spectral behavior. A surface can be black AND diffuse, or gray AND diffuse. Most rough, oxidized, or coated surfaces behave as diffuse emitters. --- ### 1.5 Real Surface **Formal Definition:** A **real surface** is *neither* a perfect black body, *nor* a perfect gray body, *nor* perfectly diffuse. Its monochromatic emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ varies with wavelength, and its directional distribution does not strictly follow Lambert's cosine law. - $\varepsilon_\lambda$ depends on $\lambda$ - $\varepsilon_\lambda$ depends on $\theta$ (angle of emission) Most polished metals and many engineering surfaces fall into this category. --- ### 1.6 Quick Comparison Table | Property | Black Body | Gray Body | Diffuse Surface | Real Surface | |----------|:----------:|:---------:|:---------------:|:------------:| | Absorptivity ($\alpha$) | 1 | $<1$ | N/A | $<1$, $\lambda$-dependent | | Emissivity ($\varepsilon$) | 1 | $<1$, constant | N/A | $<1$, $\lambda$-dependent | | $\varepsilon_\lambda$ vs $\lambda$ | = 1 always | constant | N/A | varies with $\lambda$ | | Directional behavior | Diffuse (ideal) | May be diffuse | Uniform in all $\theta$ | Non-uniform | | Physical existence | Ideal | Approximate (most non-metals) | Approximate (rough surfaces) | Most actual materials | --- ### 1.7 Radiation Emission Characteristics — Key Terms | Term | Symbol | Definition | Units | |------|:------:|-----------|:-----:| | **Emissive Power** | $E$ | Total radiation energy emitted per unit area per unit time | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | **Spectral (Monochromatic) Emissive Power** | $E_\lambda$ | Rate of energy emission per unit area per unit wavelength | $\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \mu\text{m}$ | | **Irradiation** | $G$ | Total radiation energy incident per unit area per unit time | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | **Emissivity** | $\varepsilon$ | Ratio of emissive power of a surface to that of a black body at same $T$ | dimensionless | | **Spectral Emissivity** | $\varepsilon_\lambda$ | $\varepsilon_\lambda = E_\lambda / E_{b\lambda}$ | dimensionless | | **Intensity of Radiation** | $I$ | Energy emitted per unit time per unit area per unit solid angle | $\text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{sr}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse *emissivity* (a property of the surface) with *emissive power* (which depends on temperature). Emissivity is the ratio; emissive power is the absolute quantity. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Logical Framework of Thermal Radiation The classification of surfaces and the laws governing radiation emission follow a **hierarchical logic**: 1. **Emission exists** at every temperature above absolute zero (0 K). 2. For an *ideal* reference case, we define a **black body** — the perfect emitter/absorber. 3. All *real* surfaces are compared to this black body via the property **emissivity ($\varepsilon$)**. 4. Radiation is characterized **spectrally** (over wavelengths) and **directionally** (over solid angles). 5. The behavior of real materials in terms of these two dimensions gives rise to the four surface classifications. --- ### 2.2 Why "Why" and "How" of Each Classification **Why classify surfaces?** - Engineering calculations require the emissive power $E$ of real materials. - Since $E$ depends on wavelength, temperature, and direction in a complex way, simplifying assumptions (gray, diffuse) are needed. - The classification system provides a structured way to apply these simplifications. **How are surfaces classified?** - **Spectral criterion:** How does $\varepsilon_\lambda$ vary with $\lambda$? - $\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$ (independent of $\lambda$) → **Black body** - $\varepsilon_\lambda = \text{constant} < 1$ → **Gray body** - $\varepsilon_\lambda = f(\lambda)$ → **Real (selective) surface** - **Directional criterion:** How does intensity vary with $\theta$? - $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ → **Diffuse surface** - Otherwise → **Specular / Directional surface** --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Law / Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes | |:-:|--------------|----------|-------------------|-------| | 1 | **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | $E_b$: W/m², $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴, $T$: K | Total emissive power of a black body | | 2 | Spectral form of S–B | $E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda$ | — | Integration over all wavelengths | | 3 | **Planck's Distribution Law** | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right)}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $\lambda$: m | Spectral distribution of black body radiation | | 4 | **Wien's Displacement Law** | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | $\lambda_{\max}$: m, $T$: K | Wavelength of peak emission | | 5 | Peak spectral emissive power | $E_{b\lambda_{\max}} \propto T^5$ | — | Increases sharply with temperature | | 6 | **Kirchhoff's Law** | $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ | dimensionless | For a surface in thermal equilibrium | | 7 | **Lambert's Cosine Law** | $E_\theta = E_n \cos\theta$ | $E_n$: W/m², $\theta$: angle from normal | Directional distribution for diffuse surface | | 8 | Intensity from emissive power | $I = \dfrac{E}{\pi}$ | W/m²·sr | For a diffuse surface | | 9 | Emissivity (definition) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{E}{E_b}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of real to black body emissive power | | 10 | Gray body relation | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | W/m² | Used when $\varepsilon_\lambda = \text{const}$ | | 11 | Solar/spectral division of $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{\int_0^\infty \alpha_\lambda G_\lambda\, d\lambda}{\int_0^\infty G_\lambda\, d\lambda}$ | dimensionless | Important for solar absorbers | > [!NOTE] > **Units Warning (KTU pitfall):** Always use **Kelvin (K)** for temperature in Stefan–Boltzmann and Planck's laws. Celsius (°C) is **not allowed** in the formulas — convert to K by adding 273.15. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Surface Type Used | Why | |-------------|------------------|-----| | Solar water heater absorber plate | Selective (real) surface | High $\alpha$ for solar $\lambda$, low $\varepsilon$ for IR (minimizes re-radiation loss) | | Furnace inner lining | Approximated as black body | Multiple internal reflections make cavity behave as black body | | Tungsten filament in bulb | Gray body approximation | $\varepsilon \approx 0.3$–$0.5$ over visible range | | Thermographic imaging | Black body calibration source | Known reference for accurate temperature measurement | | Satellite thermal control | Selective coatings (real surface) | Tunes $\alpha$ and $\varepsilon$ independently for thermal balance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Modern spacecraft thermal control uses **selective surfaces** (e.g., $\alpha / \varepsilon \approx 0.2 / 0.85$ for white paints) to maintain temperature balance in space. This is a direct application of the gray-vs-real surface distinction. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Stefan–Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law **Step 1: Start with Planck's spectral distribution** The spectral emissive power of a black body at wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$ is given by: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right)}$$ **Step 2: To find total emissive power, integrate over all wavelengths** $$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right)} d\lambda$$ **Step 3: Use substitution to simplify** Let $x = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda k T}$, so $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{x k T}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{hc}{x^2 k T} dx$. Substituting: $$E_b = \int_0^\infty \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\left(\dfrac{hc}{x k T}\right)^5 \left(e^x - 1\right)} \cdot \frac{hc}{x^2 k T} dx$$ **Step 4: Simplify the powers** The denominator term $\left(\dfrac{hc}{x k T}\right)^5$ simplifies to $\dfrac{(hc)^5}{x^5 (kT)^5}$. So the integrand becomes: $$E_b = \int_0^\infty 2\pi h c^2 \cdot \frac{x^5 (kT)^5}{(hc)^5} \cdot \frac{1}{(e^x - 1)} \cdot \frac{hc}{x^2 k T} dx$$ **Step 5: Combine and collect terms** Collecting all constants: $$E_b = 2\pi h c^2 \cdot \frac{(kT)^5}{(hc)^5} \cdot \frac{hc}{kT} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$ Simplifying the constants: $$E_b = \frac{2\pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$ **Step 6: Evaluate the integral** The integral is a known result: $$\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ **Step 7: Substitute and define the Stefan–Boltzmann constant** $$E_b = \frac{2\pi k^4}{h^3 c^2} \cdot \frac{\pi^4}{15} \cdot T^4 = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} T^4$$ Define: $$\sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{E_b = \sigma T^4}$$ This is the **Stefan–Boltzmann Law**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Wien's Displacement Law **Step 1: To find the wavelength of maximum emission, differentiate Planck's law with respect to $\lambda$ and set to zero** $$\frac{dE_{b\lambda}}{d\lambda} = 0$$ **Step 2: Take the derivative** Let $C_1 = 2\pi h c^2$ (constant). Then: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)}$$ where $C_2 = \dfrac{hc}{k} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K (second radiation constant). **Step 3: Differentiate and simplify** Let $f(\lambda) = \lambda^5 (e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1)$. The minimum of $f$ gives maximum of $E_{b\lambda}$. $$\frac{df}{d\lambda} = 5\lambda^4 (e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1) + \lambda^5 \left(-\frac{C_2}{\lambda^2 T}\right) e^{C_2/\lambda T} = 0$$ **Step 4: Divide by $\lambda^4$ and rearrange** $$5(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1) - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T} e^{C_2/\lambda T} = 0$$ **Step 5: Let $y = C_2 / (\lambda_{\max} T)$** $$5(e^y - 1) - y e^y = 0 \implies 5e^y - 5 - y e^y = 0 \implies e^y (5 - y) = 5$$ $$e^y = \frac{5}{5 - y}$$ **Step 6: Solve iteratively** This is a transcendental equation. Iterative solution gives: $$y \approx 4.9651$$ **Step 7: Solve for $\lambda_{\max} T$** $$\lambda_{\max} T = \frac{C_2}{y} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{4.9651} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{K}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}\cdot\text{K}}$$ This is **Wien's Displacement Law**. --- ### 3.3 Proof of Kirchhoff's Law **Step 1: Consider a small body 1 enclosed in a large isothermal cavity at temperature $T$** - Cavity walls: behave as a black body at $T$ (large cavity ensures this). - Body 1: arbitrary real surface, also at $T$ (thermal equilibrium). **Step 2: Apply radiation balance at thermal equilibrium** Let: - $G$ = irradiation on body 1 (W/m²) — incident from cavity walls. - $\alpha_1$ = absorptivity of body 1. - $E_1$ = emissive power of body 1. - $A_1$ = surface area of body 1. **Step 3: Radiative energy balance** Energy absorbed by body 1 = Energy emitted by body 1 (at equilibrium): $$\alpha_1 G A_1 = E_1 A_1$$ $$\frac{E_1}{\alpha_1} = G$$ **Step 4: If body 1 were a black body, $\alpha_1 = 1$, so $E_b = G$** Therefore: $$\frac{E_1}{\alpha_1} = E_b$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{\alpha_1 = \frac{E_1}{E_b} = \varepsilon_1 \implies \varepsilon = \alpha \quad \text{(at thermal equilibrium)}}$$ This is **Kirchhoff's Law** — emissivity equals absorptivity, both evaluated at the *same wavelength* (spectral form) and *same temperature*. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Each Law | Problem Type | Use This Law | Given → Find | |-------------|--------------|--------------| | Total heat transfer between surfaces | Stefan–Boltzmann | $T$ → $E_b$ | | Peak wavelength of emission | Wien's Displacement | $T$ → $\lambda_{\max}$ | | Spectral distribution of radiation | Planck's Law | $\lambda, T$ → $E_{b\lambda}$ | | Relation between $\varepsilon$ and $\alpha$ | Kirchhoff's Law | $\alpha$ ↔ $\varepsilon$ | | Directional variation of emitted flux | Lambert's Cosine Law | $E_n$ → $E_\theta$ | | Emissive power of real surface | Gray body relation $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | $T, \varepsilon$ → $E$ | --- ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A tungsten filament has $\varepsilon = 0.30$ and operates at 2500 K. Find: (i) its total emissive power, (ii) wavelength of maximum emission, and (iii) peak spectral emissive power using Planck's law. **Solution:** **(i) Total emissive power (Stefan–Boltzmann for gray body):** $$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 = 0.30 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (2500)^4$$ $$E = 0.30 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.906 \times 10^{13}$$ $$E = 0.30 \times 2.215 \times 10^6 = 6.645 \times 10^5\ \text{W/m}^2$$ **(ii) Wavelength of maximum emission (Wien's Law):** $$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{2500} = 1.159 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} = 1.159\ \mu\text{m}$$ **(iii) Peak spectral emissive power (Planck's law at $\lambda_{\max}$):** $$E_{b\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda_{\max}^5 (e^{C_2/\lambda_{\max} T} - 1)}$$ Since $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K, we have $C_2 / (\lambda_{\max} T) = y = 4.9651$, so $e^y \approx e^{4.9651} = 143.32$. $$E_{b\lambda_{\max}} = \frac{2\pi (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)^2}{(1.159 \times 10^{-6})^5 \times (143.32 - 1)}$$ $$= \frac{2\pi \times 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 9 \times 10^{16}}{(1.159)^5 \times 10^{-30} \times 142.32}$$ $$= \frac{3.748 \times 10^{-15}}{2.088 \times 10^{-30} \times 142.32} = \frac{3.748 \times 10^{-15}}{2.972 \times 10^{-28}}$$ $$E_{b\lambda_{\max}} \approx 1.261 \times 10^{13}\ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \text{m} = 1.261 \times 10^7\ \text{W/m}^2 \cdot \mu\text{m}$$ For tungsten (gray body, $\varepsilon = 0.3$): $$E_{\lambda_{\max}} = \varepsilon \cdot E_{b\lambda_{\max}} = 0.3 \times 1.261 \times 10^7 = 3.78 \times 10^6\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\mu\text{m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Tip:** The relationship $E_{b\lambda_{\max}} \propto T^5$ is a quick check. At $T = 2500$ K, $E_{b\lambda_{\max}} \approx 1.26 \times 10^7$ W/m²·μm is a standard reference value often quoted in KTU problems. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Surface Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Thermal Radiation Source
at Temperature T above 0 K]:::root A --> B{Spectral Behavior
emissivity vs wavelength} A --> C{Directional Behavior
intensity vs angle} B --> B1[Black Body
epsilon lambda = 1
Maximum emitter]:::black B --> B2[Gray Body
epsilon lambda = constant less than 1
Spectral uniform]:::gray B --> B3[Real Selective Surface
epsilon lambda varies with lambda]:::real C --> C1[Diffuse Surface
I theta = I n cos theta
Lambertian]:::diffuse C --> C2[Specular / Directional
Concentrated emission]:::spec B1 --> D1[Ideal Reference Case
Used in Stefan-Boltzmann Law]:::out B2 --> D2[Approximation for
engineering materials]:::out B3 --> D3[Accurate but complex
requires spectral data]:::out C1 --> D4[Lambert Cosine Law applies]:::out C2 --> D5[Directional equations required]:::out classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,color:#fff,stroke:#000 classDef black fill:#000,color:#fff,stroke:#444 classDef gray fill:#808080,color:#fff,stroke:#222 classDef real fill:#ffcc99,color:#000,stroke:#a85 classDef diffuse fill:#cce6ff,color:#003,stroke:#369 classDef spec fill:#ffd6d6,color:#600,stroke:#a33 classDef out fill:#e6ffe6,color:#030,stroke:#393 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Logical Flow of Black Body Radiation Laws ```mermaid flowchart LR L1[Start
Black Body at T K]:::start L2[Planck Distribution
E b lambda vs lambda]:::core L3{Integrate
over all lambda}:::decide L4[Stefan-Boltzmann Law
E b = sigma T to power 4]:::law L5{Find peak
dE b lambda by d lambda = 0}:::decide L6[Wien Displacement Law
lambda max T = 2.898 times 10 to power -3]:::law L7{At equilibrium
with cavity}:::decide L8[Kirchhoff Law
epsilon equals alpha]:::law L9{Isotropic
emission}:::decide L10[Lambert Cosine Law
E theta = E n cos theta]:::law L11[Gray Body Extension
E = epsilon sigma T to power 4]:::law L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 -->|Yes| L4 L3 -->|No| L5 L5 -->|Peak found| L6 L1 --> L7 L7 -->|Equilibrium| L8 L1 --> L9 L9 -->|Yes| L10 L4 --> L11 classDef start fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#000 classDef core fill:#fff5cc,color:#330,stroke:#aa0 classDef decide fill:#cce6ff,color:#003,stroke:#369 classDef law fill:#d4f4dd,color:#030,stroke:#393 ``` ### 4.3 Spectral Distribution Block Diagram (Peak Shift with Temperature) ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[Low Temperature T1 less than 1000 K]:::lo T2[Medium Temperature T2 equals 2500 K]:::md T3[High Temperature T3 greater than 5000 K]:::hi T1 --> P1[Peak at long wavelength
lambda max around 3 to 5 micrometer
IR region]:::loout T2 --> P2[Peak at shorter wavelength
lambda max around 1.16 micrometer
Near IR visible]:::mdout T3 --> P3[Peak at very short wavelength
lambda max around 0.5 to 0.6 micrometer
Visible yellow white]:::hiout P1 --> N1[Total emissive power
E b1 = sigma T1 to power 4
low magnitude]:::note P2 --> N2[Total emissive power
E b2 = sigma T2 to power 4
moderate magnitude]:::note P3 --> N3[Total emissive power
E b3 = sigma T3 to power 4
very high magnitude]:::note classDef lo fill:#3366cc,color:#fff,stroke:#003 classDef md fill:#ff9933,color:#000,stroke:#a50 classDef hi fill:#cc0033,color:#fff,stroke:#600 classDef loout fill:#6699ff,color:#fff,stroke:#003 classDef mdout fill:#ffcc66,color:#000,stroke:#a50 classDef hiout fill:#ff6666,color:#fff,stroke:#600 classDef note fill:#ffffcc,color:#330,stroke:#aa0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of Block Diagram:** As temperature increases: > - Peak wavelength **decreases** (Wien's Law). > - Peak spectral emissive power increases as $T^5$. > - Total emissive power increases as $T^4$. > - The curve becomes **broader and shifts left**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** #### **Q1. Define a black body. State any two of its characteristic properties.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **black body** is an ideal surface that absorbs all incident radiation at all wavelengths, in all directions, and at all temperatures. It is the *perfect* emitter and absorber of thermal radiation. **Two characteristic properties (1.5 marks each):** 1. **Absorptivity is unity:** $\alpha = 1$ for all wavelengths $\lambda$ and all angles of incidence. 2. **Emissivity is unity:** $\varepsilon = 1$ at every temperature. Hence, by Kirchhoff's law, it emits the maximum possible radiation for a given temperature. 3. *(Optional, for 3rd point if needed)* Its emissive power is given by the Stefan–Boltzmann law: $E_b = \sigma T^4$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse "black" with "cold." A black body at 5000 K emits enormous radiation. Marks will be deducted if the student implies black body is a "non-emitting" surface. --- #### **Q2. State and explain Kirchhoff's Law of thermal radiation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement:** *Kirchhoff's Law* states that, for a surface in thermal equilibrium with its surroundings, the **spectral emissivity** of the surface equals its **spectral absorptivity** at the same wavelength and temperature: $$\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$$ **Explanation (1.5 marks):** A good emitter at a particular wavelength is also a good absorber at that wavelength. This follows from a thermodynamic energy balance — in equilibrium, the energy emitted by a body must equal the energy absorbed. **Significance (1 mark):** This law allows us to determine a surface's emissivity (which is hard to measure directly) by simply measuring its absorptivity, and vice versa. It also implies that *no surface can have emissivity greater than 1* (since $\alpha \le 1$). --- ### 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, KTU Model Paper Style]** #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **A. (a) Derive the Stefan–Boltzmann Law of black body radiation from Planck's distribution law. State clearly the assumptions and the final form of the constant $\sigma$.** (7 marks) **A. (b) A furnace has a small opening of diameter 5 cm behaving as a black body, with an internal temperature of 1200 K. Calculate: (i) the total emissive power, (ii) the wavelength of maximum emission, and (iii) the total radiation energy emitted per minute from the opening.** (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for A(a) — 7 marks** **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. Radiation is emitted in an evacuated, isothermal enclosure. 2. The surface is in thermodynamic equilibrium with its surroundings. 3. Statistical mechanics and quantum theory apply (Planck's postulate of discrete energy levels). **Starting point (1 mark):** Planck's spectral distribution: $$E_{b\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right)}$$ **Integration to obtain $E_b$ (3 marks):** $$E_b = \int_0^\infty E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda = \int_0^\infty \frac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 \left(e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1\right)} d\lambda$$ Substituting $x = hc/(\lambda kT)$: $$E_b = \frac{2\pi k^4 T^4}{h^3 c^2} \int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx$$ **Evaluate the integral (1 mark):** $$\int_0^\infty \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1} dx = \frac{\pi^4}{15}$$ **Final form (1 mark):** $$E_b = \sigma T^4,\quad \text{where } \sigma = \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 h^3 c^2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}^4$$ --- #### **Model Solution for A(b) — 7 marks** **Given:** $d = 5$ cm = 0.05 m, $T = 1200$ K, black body opening. **Area of opening (0.5 mark):** $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^2$$ **(i) Total emissive power (2 marks):** $$E_b = \sigma T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1200)^4$$ $$(1200)^4 = 2.0736 \times 10^{12}$$ $$E_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.0736 \times 10^{12} = 1.176 \times 10^5\ \text{W/m}^2$$ **(ii) Wavelength of maximum emission (2 marks):** $$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1200} = 2.415 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} = 2.415\ \mu\text{m}$$ **(iii) Total radiation energy emitted per minute (2.5 marks):** Energy emitted per second (total radiative power): $$Q = E_b \times A = 1.176 \times 10^5 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} = 230.9\ \text{W}$$ Energy per minute: $$Q_{\text{min}} = 230.9 \times 60 = 13{,}854\ \text{J} \approx 13.85\ \text{kJ/min}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **B. (a) Explain the difference between a gray body and a real (selective) surface. State the relation between emissive power of a real surface and that of a black body, and define monochromatic emissivity.** (7 marks) **B. (b) A solar collector plate has a selective surface with $\alpha_s = 0.92$ for solar radiation and $\varepsilon = 0.15$ for IR radiation. If the plate operates at 400 K and the sun's intensity at the surface is $G_s = 900$ W/m², calculate the net heat absorbed per m² by the plate. Take surrounding temperature $T_{\text{sur}} = 300$ K.** (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for B(a) — 7 marks** **Gray body (2 marks):** - A surface whose **monochromatic emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda$ is constant** (independent of wavelength) over the entire spectrum. - $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon = \text{constant} < 1$. - Relation: $E = \varepsilon \cdot E_b = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. - Most engineering non-metals approximate gray body behavior in the IR region. **Real (selective) surface (2 marks):** - A surface whose $\varepsilon_\lambda$ (and therefore $\alpha_\lambda$) varies significantly with wavelength. - Cannot be described by a single emissivity value; requires spectral data. - E.g., $\alpha_s = 0.92$ (solar) vs $\varepsilon = 0.15$ (IR) — typical of selective coatings used in solar collectors. **Relation between emissive powers (2 marks):** $$E(\text{real}) = \int_0^\infty \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot E_{b\lambda}\, d\lambda$$ **Monochromatic emissivity definition (1 mark):** $$\varepsilon_\lambda = \frac{E_\lambda}{E_{b\lambda}}$$ The ratio of spectral emissive power of the real surface to that of a black body at the same $T$ and $\lambda$. --- #### **Model Solution for B(b) — 7 marks** **Given:** $\alpha_s = 0.92$, $\varepsilon = 0.15$, $T_s = 400$ K, $T_{\text{sur}} = 300$ K, $G_s = 900$ W/m². **Energy absorbed from sun (1.5 marks):** $$Q_{\text{abs}} = \alpha_s \cdot G_s = 0.92 \times 900 = 828\ \text{W/m}^2$$ **Energy re-radiated by plate (using Stefan–Boltzmann, treating surroundings as black body) (2.5 marks):** $$Q_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$$ $$T_s^4 = (400)^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10}$$ $$T_{\text{sur}}^4 = (300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^9$$ $$T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10} - 0.81 \times 10^{10} = 1.75 \times 10^{10}$$ $$Q_{\text{rad}} = 0.15 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.75 \times 10^{10} = 148.8\ \text{W/m}^2$$ **Net heat absorbed (3 marks):** $$Q_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{abs}} - Q_{\text{rad}} = 828 - 148.8 = 679.2\ \text{W/m}^2$$ > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting $T_{\text{sur}}$:** Students often compute only $Q_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \sigma T_s^4$ and forget to subtract the radiation coming *in* from the surroundings ($\sigma T_{\text{sur}}^4$). This loses **2 marks** in typical KTU valuation. > 2. **Mixing up $\alpha_s$ and $\varepsilon$:** Solar absorptivity $\alpha_s$ applies to the *solar spectrum*; $\varepsilon$ applies to *thermal IR* emission. They are **different** for a selective surface — do not interchange them. > 3. **Unit conversion:** $T$ must be in **Kelvin**, not Celsius. Forgetting +273 leads to a completely wrong answer and full mark deduction. > 4. **Using $\sigma$ value incorrectly:** Use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴ consistently. Writing $5.67 \times 10^{-6}$ is a common slip. > 5. **Forgetting to multiply by area** in problems where total energy is asked (not flux). Read the question carefully. --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam!):** - ✅ **Black body** is the *perfect* emitter and absorber: $\alpha = 1$, $\varepsilon = 1$, $\rho = 0$, $\tau = 0$. - ✅ **Gray body:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \text{constant} < 1$ → use $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$. - ✅ **Diffuse surface:** $I_\theta = I_n \cos\theta$ (Lambert's Cosine Law). Independent of $\lambda$. - ✅ **Real/selective surface:** $\varepsilon_\lambda$ varies with $\lambda$ — requires spectral integration. - ✅ **Stefan–Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma T^4$, where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴. - ✅ **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K. - ✅ **Planck's Law:** $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda k T} - 1)}$. - ✅ **Kirchhoff's Law:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \alpha_\lambda$ (at thermal equilibrium). - ✅ **Lambert's Cosine Law:** $E_\theta = E_n \cos\theta$; $I = E/\pi$ for diffuse surface. - ✅ **Peak spectral emissive power** scales as $T^5$; **total emissive power** as $T^4$. - ✅ **Constants to memorize:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²·K⁴, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $C_2 = 0.014388$ m·K. - ✅ **Always convert °C to K** before using any radiation formula. - ✅ **Gray vs Real distinction** is the key to solar collector design — selective surfaces exploit the *difference* between solar and IR behavior. - ✅ A black body cavity is a *practical* realization — furnace openings, small holes in hollow spheres, etc. > **One-line memory aid:** *"Black absorbs all, Gray absorbs less but evenly, Selective absorbs selectively, Diffuse emits everywhere evenly, Real is the messy truth."*

topics.title: Solid Angle and Radiation Intensity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solid Angle and Radiation Intensity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔹 1.1 Solid Angle — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Angle (ω or Ω)** is defined as the ratio of the area projected on a unit sphere, centered at the point from which the surface is being viewed, to the square of the radius of that sphere.

For an elemental surface area $dA_1$ located at a distance $r$ from a point $P$, viewed at an angle $\theta$ between the surface normal and the line of sight:

$$d\omega = \frac{dA_1 \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

- **SI Unit:** **Steradian (sr)**
- **Dimensional formula:** $[\omega] = M^0 L^0 T^0$ (dimensionless ratio)

| Configuration | Solid Angle |
|---------------|-------------|
| Small (point) element | $d\omega$ |
| Hemisphere | $2\pi$ sr |
| Full Sphere | $4\pi$ sr |
| A complete plane (viewed from one side) | $2\pi$ sr |

---

## 🔹 1.2 Radiation Intensity — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiation Intensity ($I$)** is defined as the rate of energy radiation leaving a surface **per unit solid angle** ($d\omega$) and **per unit area projected normal to the direction** of radiation.

$$I = \frac{dQ}{dA \cos\theta \, d\omega} = \frac{q_{\theta,r}}{\cos\theta}$$

- **SI Unit:** **W/(m²·sr)**
- **Physical Meaning:** How "bright" or directional the radiation appears to an observer looking at the surface from angle $\theta$.

---

## 🔹 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Flashlight vs. The Lantern**

Imagine standing in a dark room:

- **A flashlight** sends light in a *narrow, intense beam*. Its **radiation intensity is HIGH**, but only along a small solid angle (≈0.01 sr). The light is highly directional.
- **A bare lantern** sends light in *all directions equally*. Its **radiation intensity is MODERATE**, but distributed over a wide solid angle (2π sr into the upper hemisphere).

**Solid angle (ω)** is like the *cone of vision* — wider cone = more directions captured.
**Radiation intensity (I)** is like the *brightness along a specific line of sight*.

If you place a small detector at a fixed distance and rotate it around the emitting surface, it records different intensities for different angles unless the surface is a **diffuse (Lambertian) emitter**, where $I$ is *independent of $\theta$*.

> [!TIP]
> A perfect **diffuse surface** emits with constant intensity in *all directions* — just like a chalkboard looks equally bright from every viewing angle. This is the foundation for view-factor analysis in KTU Module 3.

---

## 🔹 1.4 Key Physical Constants Used

- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ **W/(m²·K⁴)**
- **Geometric constant:** Solid angle of full sphere = **$4\pi$ sr ≈ 12.566 sr**
- **Hemisphere projection:** $\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin\theta \cos\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \pi$ (key for diffuse intensity)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔹 2.1 Solid Angle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why do we need solid angles in radiation heat transfer?**

Because radiation is a **directional phenomenon**. Unlike conduction (normal to surface) or convection (along bulk flow), thermal radiation energy travels in *all directions* from a surface point. To quantify "how much energy goes in which direction," we need a **directional measure** — that measure is the **solid angle**.

### Logic Steps:

1. **Starting Point:** A point source $P$ emits energy in all directions.
2. **Construction:** Draw a unit sphere (radius = 1) centered at $P$.
3. **Observation:** An elemental surface $dA_1$ at distance $r$ subtends an area on this sphere.
4. **Key Realization:** The area on the sphere scales as $r^2$, but the area of $dA_1$ scales as its own size. The **ratio** is independent of $r$.
5. **Definition Formed:** This ratio = solid angle $d\omega$.
6. **Spherical Coordinates:** On the sphere, position is given by $(\theta, \phi)$, and the projected elemental area is $\sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$.
7. **Integration Yields:** $\int_{\text{sphere}} d\omega = 4\pi$ sr (consistent with geometry of full sphere).

---

## 🔹 2.2 Radiation Intensity — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why define radiation intensity instead of using emissive power directly?**

Emissive power $E$ (or $q_r$) gives **total** energy leaving a surface in *all directions*. But for *view-factor* calculations, we need to know **how that energy is distributed with direction**. Radiation intensity $I$ captures this directional dependence.

### Logic Steps:

1. **Start with emissive power in direction $\theta$:** $E_\theta = \dfrac{dQ}{dA \, d\omega}$ [units: W/(m²·sr)]
2. **Recognize the cosine projection:** The *projected* area perpendicular to direction $\theta$ is $dA \cos\theta$, NOT $dA$ itself.
3. **Define Radiation Intensity:** Normalize $E_\theta$ by $\cos\theta$:
$$I = \frac{E_\theta}{\cos\theta} = \frac{dQ}{dA \cos\theta \, d\omega}$$
4. **Diffuse Surface Assumption:** A surface is **diffuse** if $I$ = constant (independent of $\theta, \phi$).
5. **Recover Total Emissive Power:** Integrate over hemisphere:
$$E = \int_{\text{hemisphere}} I \cos\theta \, d\omega$$
6. **Result for Diffuse Surface:**
$$E = I \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = I\pi \implies I = \frac{E}{\pi}$$

---

## 🔹 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|-------------|
| **Furnace Design** | Compute radiative heat flux on charge material using intensity distributions |
| **Solar Thermal** | Estimate solar intensity on tilted collectors (uses solid angle of sun ≈ $6.8 \times 10^{-5}$ sr) |
| **Lighting Engineering** | Calculate illuminance from lamps using candela (luminous intensity) |
| **Satellite Thermal Control** | View-factor analysis between satellite surfaces uses intensity integration |
| **Fire Safety** | Radiant heat flux from flames to nearby objects uses intensity models |

---

## 🔹 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $d\omega = \dfrac{dA \cos\theta}{r^2}$ | Solid angle subtended by elemental area | sr |
| 2 | $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$ | Solid angle in spherical coordinates | sr |
| 3 | $\omega_{\text{hemisphere}} = 2\pi$ | Solid angle of a hemisphere | sr |
| 4 | $\omega_{\text{sphere}} = 4\pi$ | Solid angle of a full sphere | sr |
| 5 | $I = \dfrac{dQ}{dA \cos\theta \, d\omega}$ | Radiation intensity (definition) | W/(m²·sr) |
| 6 | $I = \dfrac{E_\theta}{\cos\theta}$ | Intensity in terms of directional emissive power | W/(m²·sr) |
| 7 | $E = \pi I$ | Total emissive power for **diffuse** surface | W/m² |
| 8 | $I = \dfrac{E}{\pi}$ | Intensity for **diffuse** surface | W/(m²·sr) |
| 9 | $E_b = \sigma T^4$ | Emissive power of blackbody | W/m² |
| 10 | $I_b = \dfrac{\sigma T^4}{\pi}$ | Intensity of blackbody (diffuse) | W/(m²·sr) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula 7 is the most heavily tested identity in KTU** — it bridges **directional intensity** with the **total hemispherical emissive power**. Memorize it as $E = \pi I$.

---

## 🔹 2.5 Diffuse vs. Non-Diffuse Surfaces — Conceptual Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Diffuse Surface (Lambertian):**
> - $I$ = constant, independent of direction
> - Brightness is uniform from every viewing angle
> - Example: Chalkboard, rough metal, snow

> **Specular (Mirror-like) Surface:**
> - $I$ varies sharply with direction
> - Energy concentrated in reflection angle
> - Example: Polished silver mirror

> **Directional Surface:**
> - Custom $I(\theta, \phi)$ profile
> - Example: Headlight reflector, laser diode
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔹 3.1 Derivation 1 — Solid Angle Element in Spherical Coordinates

**Objective:** Express $d\omega$ in terms of angular coordinates $\theta, \phi$.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
Consider a sphere of radius $r$ centered at point $P$. A small patch on this sphere is bounded by:
- A change $d\theta$ in polar angle (from north pole)
- A change $d\phi$ in azimuthal angle

**Step 2 — Compute arc lengths.**
The two edges of the patch have lengths:
- Meridional direction: $r \, d\theta$
- Latitudinal direction: $r \sin\theta \, d\phi$

**Step 3 — Compute patch area.**
The elemental area on the sphere is the product of these two orthogonal arc lengths:

$$dA_{\text{sphere}} = (r \, d\theta)(r \sin\theta \, d\phi) = r^2 \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

**Step 4 — Apply the solid angle definition.**
By definition, $d\omega = \dfrac{dA_{\text{sphere}}}{r^2}$:

$$\boxed{d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi}$$

**Step 5 — Verify by integrating over a hemisphere.**

$$\omega_{\text{hemisphere}} = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

Let us compute the inner integral first:

$$\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin\theta \, d\theta = [-\cos\theta]_0^{\pi/2} = -\cos(\pi/2) + \cos(0) = 0 + 1 = 1$$

Then the outer integral:

$$\int_0^{2\pi} 1 \, d\phi = 2\pi$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{\text{hemisphere}} = 2\pi \text{ sr}}$$

---

## 🔹 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relation Between Radiation Intensity and Emissive Power for a Diffuse Surface

**Objective:** Show that $E = \pi I$ for a diffuse emitter.

**Step 1 — Express energy leaving $dA$ into solid angle $d\omega$.**
By definition of directional emissive power $E_\theta$:

$$dQ = E_\theta \, dA \, d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $E_\theta = I \cos\theta$ (from intensity definition).**

$$dQ = I \cos\theta \, dA \, d\omega$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over the entire hemisphere (above $dA$) to get total energy.**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = dA \int_{\text{hemisphere}} I \cos\theta \, d\omega$$

**Step 4 — Apply diffuse assumption: $I$ = constant, pull it out.**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = I \, dA \int_{\text{hemisphere}} \cos\theta \, d\omega$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$.**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = I \, dA \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the double integral.**

Use $u = \sin\theta$, $du = \cos\theta \, d\theta$:
