$$\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \int_0^1 u \, du = \left[\frac{u^2}{2}\right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2}$$

Then:

$$\int_0^{2\pi} \frac{1}{2} \, d\phi = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 2\pi = \pi$$

**Step 7 — Total emissive power = energy per unit area.**

$$E = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{dA} = I \cdot \pi$$

$$\boxed{E = \pi I \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad I = \frac{E}{\pi}}$$

---

## 🔹 3.3 Derivation 3 — Intensity of a Blackbody

**Objective:** Show that a blackbody's intensity is $I_b = \sigma T^4 / \pi$.

**Step 1 — Start with Stefan-Boltzmann law.**
A blackbody emits total hemispherical emissive power:

$$E_b = \sigma T^4$$

**Step 2 — Apply the diffuse-emitter result $I_b = E_b / \pi$** (since a blackbody is the *perfect* diffuse emitter — Kirchhoff's law).

$$\boxed{I_b = \frac{\sigma T^4}{\pi}}$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation.**
Even though $E_b$ depends *only* on temperature (via $T^4$), the intensity $I_b$ is **independent of direction**. This is the defining characteristic of a blackbody: it obeys **Lambert's cosine law**.

---

## 🔹 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Directional Dependence Summary

| Surface Type | $I(\theta)$ Behavior | $E_\theta(\theta) = I \cos\theta$ | Total $E$ |
|--------------|----------------------|------------------------------------|-----------|
| **Diffuse (Lambertian)** | Constant ($I = I_0$) | $I_0 \cos\theta$ | $\pi I_0$ |
| **Specular** | Delta at reflection angle | Spike in one direction | $\int I \cos\theta \, d\omega$ |
| **Blackbody** | $I_b = \sigma T^4/\pi$ | $(\sigma T^4/\pi)\cos\theta$ | $\sigma T^4$ |
| **Real Graybody** | $I = \varepsilon \sigma T^4/\pi$ | $\varepsilon (\sigma T^4/\pi)\cos\theta$ | $\varepsilon \sigma T^4$ |

---

## 🔹 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A small surface emits with intensity $I = 500$ W/(m²·sr) uniformly in all directions in the upper hemisphere. Compute its total emissive power.

**Solution:**
By the diffuse-surface relation:

$$E = \pi I = \pi \times 500 = 1570.8 \text{ W/m}^2$$

**Physical check:** If $E_b = 1570.8 = \sigma T^4$, then:

$$T^4 = \frac{1570.8}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.77 \times 10^{10} \text{ K}^4 \implies T \approx 724 \text{ K} \approx 451°\text{C}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📒 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Radiation Intensity. State its SI unit and physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Radiation Intensity ($I$):** The rate of energy radiated from a surface per unit solid angle and per unit area projected normal to the direction of radiation.
> $$I = \frac{dQ}{dA \cos\theta \, d\omega}$$
> - **SI Unit:** **W/(m²·sr)**
> - **Significance:** It quantifies the *directional distribution* of radiation. For a diffuse surface, $I$ is constant; for non-diffuse surfaces, $I$ varies with direction. This is the foundation for view-factor analysis.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define solid angle. What is the solid angle subtended at the centre of a hemisphere?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Solid Angle ($\omega$):** The ratio of the area projected on a unit sphere (centered at the vertex) to the square of the radius.
> $$d\omega = \frac{dA \cos\theta}{r^2} \quad ; \quad \text{Unit: Steradian (sr)}$$
> **Solid angle of a hemisphere = $2\pi$ sr**

---

## 🔹 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, PCMET503]`

**(a)** Derive the relation $E = \pi I$ for a diffuse (Lambertian) surface, starting from the definition of radiation intensity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A small grey surface of area $A = 0.02$ m² has emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.8$ and is at temperature $T = 1000$ K. Calculate:
   (i) Its total emissive power
   (ii) Its radiation intensity (assuming diffuse behavior)
   (iii) The radiant energy emitted per unit solid angle in the normal direction ($\theta = 0$) **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Full Derivation:**

Starting from the definition of radiation intensity:

$$I = \frac{dQ}{dA \cos\theta \, d\omega}$$

The energy leaving area $dA$ into solid angle $d\omega$ at angle $\theta$:

$$dQ = I \cos\theta \, dA \, d\omega$$

Integrating over the entire upper hemisphere (which represents all directions into which radiation can leave $dA$):

$$Q_{\text{total}} = dA \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2} I \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

For a **diffuse surface**, $I$ = constant. Therefore:

$$Q_{\text{total}} = I \, dA \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

Evaluating the inner integral using $u = \sin\theta$:

$$\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta = \int_0^1 u \, du = \frac{1}{2}$$

Evaluating the outer integral:

$$\int_0^{2\pi} \frac{1}{2} \, d\phi = \pi$$

Therefore total emissive power:

$$E = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{dA} = \boxed{\pi I}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total Emissive Power:**

For a grey surface:

$$E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 = 0.8 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000)^4$$

Computing step-by-step:

$$T^4 = (1000)^4 = 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$$

$$\sigma T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{12} = 5.67 \times 10^{4} = 56700 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$E = 0.8 \times 56700 = \boxed{45360 \text{ W/m}^2}$$

**(ii) Radiation Intensity:**

Using $E = \pi I$:

$$I = \frac{E}{\pi} = \frac{45360}{3.14159} = \boxed{14438.8 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{sr)}}$$

**(iii) Energy per unit solid angle in normal direction ($\theta = 0$):**

$$I = \frac{dQ}{dA \cos\theta \, d\omega} \implies \frac{dQ}{d\omega} = I \, dA \cos\theta$$

At $\theta = 0$, $\cos\theta = 1$:

$$\frac{dQ}{d\omega} = I \cdot dA \cdot 1 = 14438.8 \times 0.02 = \boxed{288.78 \text{ W/sr}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of solid angle with a neat sketch. Show that the solid angle of a hemisphere is $2\pi$ steradians. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A black surface at 1500 K has an elemental area of $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m². Determine:
   (i) The total radiation emitted per second from this element
   (ii) The intensity of radiation
   (iii) The directional emissive power at $\theta = 60°$ from the normal **(7 Marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Solid Angle Concept + Hemisphere Proof:**

The solid angle $d\omega$ subtended by elemental area $dA$ at point $P$ is the area intercepted by the cone on a unit sphere centered at $P$, divided by $r^2$:

$$d\omega = \frac{dA \cos\theta}{r^2}$$

In spherical coordinates, with the vertex at the origin:

$$d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

To find the solid angle of the entire upper hemisphere ($\theta: 0 \to \pi/2$, $\phi: 0 \to 2\pi$):

$$\omega = \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^{\pi/2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$$

Inner integral:

$$\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin\theta \, d\theta = [-\cos\theta]_0^{\pi/2} = 1$$

Outer integral:

$$\int_0^{2\pi} 1 \, d\phi = 2\pi$$

$$\boxed{\omega_{\text{hemisphere}} = 2\pi \text{ sr}}$$

**Geometric Sketch (described):**
A point $P$ at the center; a hemisphere of radius $r$ drawn above; a small patch $dA$ on the hemisphere, viewed at angle $\theta$ from the vertical axis; the cone subtending this patch has half-angle $\theta$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total radiation per second:**

$$Q = E_b \cdot A = \sigma T^4 \cdot A$$

$$T^4 = (1500)^4 = 5.0625 \times 10^{12} \text{ K}^4$$

$$\sigma T^4 = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0625 \times 10^{12} = 287044 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$Q = 287044 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = \boxed{143.52 \text{ W}}$$

**(ii) Intensity of radiation:**

$$I_b = \frac{\sigma T^4}{\pi} = \frac{287044}{3.14159} = \boxed{91367.5 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{sr)}}$$

**(iii) Directional emissive power at $\theta = 60°$:**

$$E_\theta = I_b \cos\theta = 91367.5 \times \cos(60°) = 91367.5 \times 0.5 = \boxed{45683.75 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{sr)}}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Cost: 2–4 Marks Each)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the cosine factor:** Students often write $d\omega = dA/r^2$ instead of $dA \cos\theta / r^2$. Always include $\cos\theta$ when the surface is *not* perpendicular to the viewing direction.
>
> 2. **Wrong solid angle for hemisphere:** Some students incorrectly write $2\pi r^2$ instead of $2\pi$ (forgetting to divide by $r^2$). The unit is *steradians*, not m².
>
> 3. **Using $I$ instead of $E$ for total radiation:** The intensity $I$ gives *directional* information. To get *total* energy, you MUST integrate over the hemisphere using $E = \pi I$ for a diffuse surface.
>
> 4. **Missing $d\omega$ in energy calculation:** When computing $dQ$, always include the $d\omega$ term:
> $$dQ = I \cos\theta \, dA \, d\omega$$
> Omitting $d\omega$ loses 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Mixing up emissivity:** For a greybody, $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, but $I = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 / \pi$. The factor $\pi$ always appears with intensity, NOT with emissive power.

---

## 🔹 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Solid angle** = projected area on unit sphere ÷ $r^2$. Unit = **steradian (sr)**.
- ✅ Elemental solid angle in spherical coords: $d\omega = \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi$.
- ✅ **Hemisphere → $2\pi$ sr**, **Full sphere → $4\pi$ sr**.
- ✅ **Radiation intensity** = energy per unit *projected* area per unit solid angle.
- ✅ Formula: $I = \dfrac{dQ}{dA \cos\theta \, d\omega}$. Unit = **W/(m²·sr)**.
- ✅ **Diffuse surface** = $I$ = constant (independent of $\theta, \phi$).
- ✅ **Master relation:** $\boxed{E = \pi I}$ — this is the most-tested identity.
- ✅ **Blackbody intensity:** $I_b = \dfrac{\sigma T^4}{\pi}$ (constant, diffuse).
- ✅ **Directional emissive power:** $E_\theta = I \cos\theta$ (Lambert's cosine law).
- ✅ **Greybody:** $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, $I = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 / \pi$.
- ✅ Integration $\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos\theta \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi = \pi$ — memorize this for derivations.
- ✅ **Cosine projection is mandatory** in $d\omega$ and $I$ definitions — never skip it.
- ✅ Diffuse emitters look **equally bright** from all viewing angles (Lambert's law).
- ✅ Solid angle of the sun as seen from earth ≈ $6.8 \times 10^{-5}$ sr (useful for solar problems).
- ✅ When a problem says "radiation in normal direction," assume $\theta = 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Heat Exchange Between Two Infinite and Finite Back Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Heat Exchange Between Two Infinite and Finite Back Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Radiation Heat Exchange — Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radiation Heat Exchange** is the net rate of thermal energy transferred between two (or more) surfaces by electromagnetic waves, in the absence of any intervening medium, governed by the surface temperatures, emissivities, and the **geometrical arrangement** (view factors) of the surfaces.

The **net radiative heat transfer** between two diffuse, gray, opaque surfaces is, in its most general form:

$$
q_{1 \to 2} = A_1 F_{12} \, \sigma \left( T_1^4 - T_2^4 \right) \quad \text{(black surfaces)}
$$

For **gray** surfaces, an additional resistance term appears, leading to the **standard radiation network analogy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spotlight on a Stage"

Imagine two actors (Surface 1 and Surface 2) standing on a dark stage, each carrying a flashlight that emits light (radiation) in *all directions*.

- The fraction of light leaving Actor 1 that *hits* Actor 2 is called the **View Factor** $F_{12}$ (also called **Shape Factor** or **Configuration Factor** $F_{1 \to 2}$).
- If Actor 1 stands very far away and faces away, $F_{12} \to 0$.
- If Actor 1 is completely enclosed by Actor 2, $F_{12} = 1$ (all the light must hit something on the enclosure, and by symmetry it must hit Actor 2).
- The actors are not perfect mirrors — some light is **absorbed** and some is **re-emitted**, depending on their surface quality (emissivity $\varepsilon$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key constant:** The Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is fundamental to all radiation calculations.

## 1.3 The View Factor $F_{ij}$ — Rigorous Definition

The **view factor** (or geometric shape factor) from surface $i$ to surface $j$ is defined as:

$$
F_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Fraction of total radiation leaving surface } i \text{ that strikes surface } j}{\text{Total radiation leaving surface } i}
$$

Mathematically, for diffuse surfaces:

$$
F_{ij} = \frac{1}{A_i} \int_{A_i} \int_{A_j} \dfrac{\cos \theta_i \, \cos \theta_j}{\pi r^2} \, dA_j \, dA_i
$$

where $r$ is the distance between the two elemental areas and $\theta_i$, $\theta_j$ are the angles the line joining the elements makes with their respective normals.

## 1.4 Two Classes of Geometries We Will Study

| Class | Examples | Defining Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Infinite / Large** | Two infinite parallel plates | Surfaces extend to infinity; $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$ |
| **Finite Back Surfaces** | Concentric cylinders, concentric spheres, furnace cavities with re-radiating walls | Surfaces form a closed or partially closed enclosure; $F_{12} \ne 1$ in general |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 View Factor Algebra — The Three Cardinal Rules

Before deriving any heat-transfer formula, the **three fundamental view-factor theorems** must be internalised:

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 1 — Summation Rule (Closure):**
> For an *enclosure* where surface $i$ sees $N$ other surfaces,
> $$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1$$
> The sum of all view factors from a given surface equals unity (all radiation leaving must strike some surface in a closed enclosure).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 2 — Reciprocity Relation:**
> $$A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$$
> This is the geometric symmetry of the radiation exchange; it is *independent* of surface temperature and emissivity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule 3 — Superposition Rule:**
> The view factor from surface $i$ to a *composite* surface $j$ (made of $n$ sub-surfaces) is the area-weighted sum:
> $$A_j F_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{jk} F_{i \to jk}$$
> Useful for splitting complex surfaces into solvable primitives.

## 2.2 Radiosity and Irradiation

For a **gray, diffuse, opaque** surface, the **Radiosity** $J$ (total radiation leaving per unit area) is:

$$
J = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 + \rho \, G
$$

where:
- $\varepsilon$ = emissivity
- $\rho = 1 - \alpha = 1 - \varepsilon$ (for gray, opaque) = reflectivity
- $G$ = irradiation (incoming radiation per unit area)

The net heat transfer from a gray surface is:

$$
q = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon} \, A \sigma T^4 \;-\; A J
$$

## 2.3 Electrical-Network Analogy (KTU Favourite)

> [!TIP]
> For every radiation problem, draw the **resistance network**. Heat-flow = current, temperature (in $T^4$ form) = voltage, and surface resistances are:
> $$R_i = \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A_i} \quad \text{(surface resistance)}, \qquad R_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{A_i F_{ij}} \quad \text{(space resistance)}$$

This makes complex 3-surface problems trivial to solve.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Configuration | Net Heat Transfer Formula | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two **infinite parallel black** plates | $q = A \,\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ | $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$, no edge effects |
| 2 | Two **infinite parallel gray** plates | $q = \dfrac{A \,\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$ | Both surfaces are diffuse-gray |
| 3 | Concentric **infinite cylinders / spheres** (inner hot, outer cold) | $q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}$ | $A_1 < A_2$; $F_{12} = 1$ |
| 4 | Two parallel plates with **re-radiating surface** $R$ between them | $q_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_R} - 1\right)^{-1} + \left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_R} - 1\right)^{-1}}$ | $F_{1R} = F_{2R} = 1$; $R$ has zero net heat flux |
| 5 | Concentric cylinders with **re-radiating intermediate surface** | $q_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_R A_R}\left(\frac{1-\varepsilon_2 A_2/A_R}{\dots}\right)}$ | See derivation in Section 3 |
| 6 | View-factor reciprocity | $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$ | Always valid |
| 7 | Surface resistance | $R_i = \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A_i}$ | Used in network analysis |
| 8 | Space resistance | $R_{ij} = \dfrac{1}{A_i F_{ij}}$ | Independent of $\varepsilon$ and $T$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Absolute value notation:** When using $\vert$ inside formulas, write $\lvert T_1^4 - T_2^4 \rvert$ to avoid breaking the table. Direction of heat flow is always from higher to lower $T^4$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Furnace design** (reheating furnaces, glass-melting tanks) → parallel-plate and re-radiating-surface analysis.
- **Heat-loss estimation** from steam/utility pipes → concentric cylinder model.
- **Cryogenic storage vessels** (LNG, vacuum-insulated dewars) → concentric sphere model.
- **Solar collector absorber plates** with selective coatings → parallel-plate with re-radiation.
- **Nuclear reactor fuel-rod bundles** → concentric geometry.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case 1 — Two Infinite Parallel Gray Plates (Both Diffuse)

Consider two large parallel plates of area $A$, separated by a small gap (so $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$). Plate 1 is at $T_1$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_1$, Plate 2 at $T_2$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_2$.

**Step 1 — Write radiosities for both plates.**

For a gray, opaque, diffuse surface in an enclosure, the radiosity (leaving radiation) is the sum of reflected and emitted parts.

$$
J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1) \, G_1
$$

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) \, G_2
$$

**Step 2 — Write irradiation on each plate.**

Since $F_{12} = 1$ and the only surface each plate "sees" is the other, the irradiation equals the radiosity of the *opposite* plate.

$$
G_1 = J_2, \qquad G_2 = J_1
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $G$ into the radiosity equations.**

$$
J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1) \, J_2
$$

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) \, J_1
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the two equations simultaneously for $J_1 - J_2$.**

From the first equation:

$$
J_1 - (1-\varepsilon_1) J_2 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4
$$

From the second equation rearranged:

$$
(1-\varepsilon_2) J_1 - J_2 = -\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4
$$

Writing as a matrix and solving (or by elimination):

$$
J_1 - J_2 = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Relate $J_1 - J_2$ to the net heat flux.**

The net heat leaving Surface 1 (which is the heat transferred to Surface 2) is:

$$
q_{1\to 2} = A (J_1 - G_1) = A (J_1 - J_2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\, q_{1\to 2} = \dfrac{A \, \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1} \,}
$$

**Step 6 — Sanity check.**

- If $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 1$ (black), denominator becomes $1 + 1 - 1 = 1$, reducing to $A\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$. ✓
- If $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon$, denominator becomes $\frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1 = \frac{2-\varepsilon}{\varepsilon}$, and the formula collapses to:

$$
q = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, A \, \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{2 - \varepsilon}
$$

This is the classical Holman/Çengel result for two equal-emissivity infinite parallel plates.

---

## 3.2 Case 2 — Two Concentric Cylinders (or Spheres) — Finite Back Surfaces

Let Surface 1 be the **inner** cylinder of area $A_1$, radius $r_1$, emissivity $\varepsilon_1$. Surface 2 is the **outer** cylinder of area $A_2$, radius $r_2$, emissivity $\varepsilon_2$. Surface 1 is completely enclosed by Surface 2, so $F_{12} = 1$.

By reciprocity: $A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$, so $F_{21} = A_1/A_2$.

**Step 1 — Irradiations.**

Surface 1 sees only Surface 2:

$$
G_1 = F_{12} J_2 = J_2
$$

Surface 2 sees itself (with $F_{22} = 1 - A_1/A_2$) and Surface 1 (with $F_{21} = A_1/A_2$):

$$
G_2 = F_{21} J_1 + F_{22} J_2 = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} J_1 + \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right) J_2
$$

**Step 2 — Radiosity equations.**

$$
J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1) J_2
$$

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) \left[ \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} J_1 + \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right) J_2 \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Net heat transfer = $A_1 (J_1 - G_1) = A_1 (J_1 - J_2)$.**

After algebraic manipulation of the second equation to eliminate $J_2$ on the RHS, and using the first to express $J_1$ in terms of $T_1^4$ and $J_2$, we obtain:

$$
\boxed{\, q_{1\to 2} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}} \,}
$$

**Step 4 — Special case check.**

If $A_1 = A_2$ (parallel plates enclosed, which reduces to Case 1), then $q = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4) / [1/(\varepsilon_1 A) + (1-\varepsilon_2)/(\varepsilon_2 A)]$. Multiply numerator and denominator by $A$:

$$
q = \dfrac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2}}
$$

Since $\frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$, this collapses exactly to the Case 1 result. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> The formula is **identical** for concentric spheres — only the area definitions change ($A = 4\pi r^2$).

---

## 3.3 Case 3 — Re-Radiating Surface $R$ Between Two Parallel Plates

This is a classic KTU 14-mark derivation. Surface $R$ is an *adiabatic, re-radiating* surface (e.g., a radiation shield or a thin foil). It absorbs and re-emits radiation but has **zero net heat flux** through it.

**Configuration:** Plates 1, 2, $R$ all infinite and parallel. $F_{1R} = F_{2R} = 1$. Plate $R$ is between 1 and 2.

**Step 1 — Re-radiation condition.**

The net heat flux into $R$ is zero:

$$
q_{1 \to R} = q_{R \to 2}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer between two parallel plates (1 ↔ R) using Case 1 result.**

$$
q_{1 \to R} = \dfrac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_R^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_R} - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Similarly for $R$ ↔ 2.**

$$
q_{R \to 2} = \dfrac{A \sigma (T_R^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_R} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions (since $q_{1R} = q_{R2} = q_{net}$).**

$$
q_{net} = \dfrac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_R^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_R} - 1} = \dfrac{A \sigma (T_R^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_R} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Eliminate $T_R^4$ (the re-radiating surface temperature is unknown).**

Let $D_1 = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_R} - 1$ and $D_2 = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_R} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$.

From the equality: $T_1^4 - T_R^4 = D_1 \cdot q_{net}/(A\sigma)$ and $T_R^4 - T_2^4 = D_2 \cdot q_{net}/(A\sigma)$. Adding:

$$
T_1^4 - T_2^4 = \dfrac{q_{net}}{A\sigma}(D_1 + D_2)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\, q_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{A \, \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_R} - 1\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_R} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)} \,}
$$

**Step 6 — Equivalent compact form using parallel-resistance analogy.**

The denominator is the sum of two series "thermal resistances" of the form (Case 1), i.e., the re-radiating surface $R$ effectively places the two plate-to-plate paths **in series**.

$$
q_{1 \to 2} = \left[ \left( \dfrac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_R^4)}{\dots} \right)^{-1} + \left( \dfrac{A \sigma (T_R^4 - T_2^4)}{\dots} \right)^{-1} \right]^{-1}
$$

For **identical emissivities** $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_R = \varepsilon$:

$$
D_1 = D_2 = \frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1
$$

$$
q_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{2 \left( \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1 \right)} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{2(2 - \varepsilon)}
$$

Compare with **no shield** (Case 1 with equal $\varepsilon$):

$$
q_{no\,shield} = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{2 - \varepsilon}
$$

The ratio is:

$$
\dfrac{q_{1 \to 2,\,with\,shield}}{q_{no\,shield}} = \dfrac{1}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway:** A single re-radiating radiation shield *halves* the radiative heat transfer (when emissivities are equal). This is the principle behind multi-layer insulation in cryogenics and reflective foils in fire-fighter suits.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Radiation Network Topology

The following functional block diagram captures the *logical flow* of energy in the three cases studied. (Due to the difficulty of rendering physically accurate geometry in plain Mermaid, the *network analog* is used — which is the standard KTU teaching tool.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Surface 1 at T1, eps1] -- Space resistance R12 = 1 over A1 F12 --> B[Node J1 radiosity]
    A -- Surface resistance Rs1 = 1 minus eps1 over eps1 A1 --> C[Emitted Eb1 = sigma T1^4]
    B -- Net heat q1 --> D[Surface 2 at T2, eps2]
    D -- Surface resistance Rs2 --> C2[Emitted Eb2 = sigma T2^4]
    D -- Space resistance R21 = 1 over A2 F21 --> B2[Node J2 radiosity]
    R[Re radiating Surface R, epsR, TR] -. inserts between A and D .-> A
    R -. zero net flux constraint .-> B
```

## 4.2 Schematic Mapping — Geometry to Network

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CASE_A[Case 1 Infinite Parallel Plates]
        direction LR
        P1[Plate 1 eps1, T1] -- F12 equals 1 --> P2[Plate 2 eps2, T2]
    end
    subgraph CASE_B[Case 2 Concentric Cylinders]
        direction LR
        C1[Inner cylinder eps1, T1, A1] -- F12 equals 1 --> C2[Outer cylinder eps2, T2, A2]
        C1 -. enclosed by .-> C2
    end
    subgraph CASE_C[Case 3 Re radiating Shield]
        direction LR
        S1[Plate 1 eps1, T1] -- F1R equals 1 --> SR[Shield R epsR, TR, adiabatic]
        SR -- FR2 equals 1 --> S2[Plate 2 eps2, T2]
    end
```

## 4.3 Resistance-Network Functional Diagram (Most Important for KTU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1N[Eb1 = sigma T1^4] --- R1s[Rs1 = 1 minus eps1 over eps1 A1]
    R1s --- J1N[J1 node]
    J1N --- R12sp[Space R12 = 1 over A1 F12]
    R12sp --- J2N[J2 node]
    J2N --- R2s[Rs2 = 1 minus eps2 over eps2 A2]
    R2s --- T2N[Eb2 = sigma T2^4]
    T1N2[Eb1] --- R1Rs[Rs1]
    R1Rs --- J1N2[J1]
    J1N2 --- R1Rsp[R1R]
    R1Rsp --- JRn[JR shield node]
    JRn --- RR2sp[RR2]
    RR2sp --- J2N2[J2]
    J2N2 --- R2Rs[Rs2]
    R2Rs --- T2N2[Eb2]
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each **block** is an electrical-resistance equivalent.
- Heat flows from the *higher $T^4$* node to the *lower* one.
- For concentric cylinders, simply set $A_1 F_{12} = A_1$ and the space resistance drops out of the picture.
- For the re-radiating shield, the current through both branches of the network must be equal — this enforces the adiabatic constraint automatically.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Hint:** Whenever the question says "two surfaces form an enclosure" or "a thin foil is placed between two surfaces", *always* draw this resistance network first, then solve with simple series-parallel combinations.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the term *view factor* and state the **reciprocity theorem**. Mention its significance.  `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **View Factor** $F_{ij}$ is defined as the fraction of the total radiation leaving surface $i$ that strikes surface $j$, assuming all radiation from $i$ is intercepted by the enclosure.
>
> **Reciprocity Theorem:** For any two surfaces $i$ and $j$ forming (or part of) an enclosure,
> $$A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$$
>
> **Significance:** It allows calculation of an unknown view factor (e.g., $F_{21}$ for a small surface inside a large one) from a known one (e.g., $F_{12} = 1$). It is purely a *geometric* relation, independent of temperature and emissivity.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark statement, 1 mark significance)*

---

### Q2. What is a **re-radiating surface**? Give one practical example where the concept is used.  `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **re-radiating surface** (also called adiabatic surface) is a thin, opaque surface placed between two radiating surfaces. It *absorbs* all incident radiation, *re-emits* it on both sides, and has **zero net heat flux** through it.
>
> **Example 1:** A thin polished metal *radiation shield* placed between the inner wall and outer wall of a cryogenic dewar flask to cut radiative heat loss by half (per shield).
>
> **Example 2:** A refractory *intermediate wall* in a multi-zone furnace that sees radiation from both a hotter zone and a colder zone but does not allow net heat conduction through it.
>
> The key equation governing it is $q_{1\to R} = q_{R \to 2}$ (zero net accumulation).

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark re-emission property, 1 mark example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**(a) Derive an expression for the net radiative heat exchange between two infinite parallel gray plates of equal area $A$, emissivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, and temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$.** *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the radiosity of each plate, where radiosity = emitted + reflected.

$$
J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1) G_1
$$

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_2) G_2
$$

**Step 2:** Since the plates are infinite and parallel, $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$. Hence the irradiation on each plate equals the radiosity of the other.

$$
G_1 = J_2, \qquad G_2 = J_1
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $G$ back into the radiosity equations.

$$
J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2
$$

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1
$$

**Step 4:** Solve simultaneously. Subtract the second from the first multiplied out:

$$
J_1 - J_2 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 - \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2 - (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1
$$

Rearranging:

$$
J_1 \left[1 - (1 - \varepsilon_2)\right] - J_2 \left[1 - (1 - \varepsilon_1)\right] = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 - \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2 J_1 - \varepsilon_1 J_2 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 - \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4
$$

Multiply through by $1/(\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2)$:

$$
\dfrac{J_1}{\varepsilon_1} - \dfrac{J_2}{\varepsilon_2} = \dfrac{\sigma T_1^4}{\varepsilon_2} - \dfrac{\sigma T_2^4}{\varepsilon_1}
$$

**Step 5:** We seek $J_1 - J_2$. Combine the two original equations to isolate it. A cleaner method: from Step 3, eliminate $J_2$ between the two equations.

From the first equation, $J_2 = (J_1 - \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4)/(1 - \varepsilon_1)$. Substitute into the second:

$$
\dfrac{J_1 - \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4}{1 - \varepsilon_1} = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) J_1
$$

Multiply by $(1 - \varepsilon_1)$:

$$
J_1 - \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 = (1-\varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1)(1-\varepsilon_2) J_1
$$

Collect $J_1$ terms:

$$
J_1 \left[1 - (1-\varepsilon_1)(1-\varepsilon_2)\right] = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4
$$

Note that $1 - (1-\varepsilon_1)(1-\varepsilon_2) = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2$. By symmetry for $J_2$:

$$
J_2 \left[\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2\right] = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2)\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4
$$

Subtracting:

$$
J_1 - J_2 = \dfrac{\sigma T_1^4 \left[\varepsilon_1 - (1-\varepsilon_2)\varepsilon_1\right] - \sigma T_2^4 \left[\varepsilon_2 - (1-\varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2\right]}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}
$$

After simplifying the numerators (using $\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_1(1-(1-\varepsilon_2))$ and similar):

$$
J_1 - J_2 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 \left(\sigma T_1^4 - \sigma T_2^4\right)}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}
$$

**Step 6:** Net heat transfer from Plate 1 to Plate 2 = $A(J_1 - J_2)$:

$$
\boxed{\, q_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{A \, \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1} \,}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks setup, 3 marks algebra, 2 marks final formula with limits and physical check)*

---

**(b) Two large parallel plates have emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.8$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.4$, temperatures $T_1 = 800$ K and $T_2 = 400$ K. Find (i) the net heat-transfer rate per unit area, and (ii) the temperature of an intermediate re-radiating shield $R$ (with $\varepsilon_R = 0.05$) placed between them.** *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Without shield:**

$$
q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}
$$

Compute $T_1^4 = 800^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} \ \text{K}^4$.
Compute $T_2^4 = 400^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10} \ \text{K}^4$.

$$
T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} - 2.56 \times 10^{10} = 3.84 \times 10^{11} \ \text{K}^4
$$

$$
\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.8} + \frac{1}{0.4} - 1 = 1.25 + 2.5 - 1 = 2.75
$$

$$
q = \dfrac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.84 \times 10^{11}}{2.75} = \dfrac{21772.8}{2.75} = 7917.4 \ \text{W/m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\, q \approx 7.92 \ \text{kW/m}^2 \,}
$$

**(ii) With re-radiating shield $R$:**

Net heat through the system (using the formula derived in Section 3.3):

$$
q_{new} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_R} - 1\right) + \left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_R} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}
$$

Denominator:

$$
\left(\frac{1}{0.8} + \frac{1}{0.05} - 1\right) + \left(\frac{1}{0.05} + \frac{1}{0.4} - 1\right) = (1.25 + 20 - 1) + (20 + 2.5 - 1) = 20.25 + 21.5 = 41.75
$$

$$
q_{new} = \dfrac{21772.8}{41.75} \approx 521.5 \ \text{W/m}^2
$$

The shield cuts the heat transfer by a factor of about $15$.

**Shield temperature $T_R$:** From $q_{1 \to R} = q_{new}$:

$$
T_1^4 - T_R^4 = \dfrac{q_{new}}{\sigma} \left( \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_R} - 1 \right) = \dfrac{521.5}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \times 20.25
$$

$$
T_1^4 - T_R^4 = 9.198 \times 10^{9} \times 20.25 = 1.8626 \times 10^{11} \ \text{K}^4
$$

$$
T_R^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} - 1.8626 \times 10^{11} = 2.2334 \times 10^{11} \ \text{K}^4
$$

$$
T_R = (2.2334 \times 10^{11})^{1/4} = 687.4 \ \text{K}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, q_{new} \approx 521.5 \ \text{W/m}^2, \quad T_R \approx 687.4 \ \text{K} \,}
$$

*(7 marks: 2 marks formula, 2 marks calculation part (i), 3 marks calculation part (ii) including $T_R$ derivation)*

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks

**(a) Derive the expression for net radiative heat transfer between two concentric cylinders of areas $A_1$ (inner) and $A_2$ (outer), with emissivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$ and temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$. State clearly the view-factor relations used.** *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Enclosure topology. The inner cylinder 1 is *fully enclosed* by the outer cylinder 2. Therefore $F_{12} = 1$ (all radiation leaving Surface 1 strikes Surface 2).

**Step 2:** Apply the reciprocity theorem:

$$
A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21} \Rightarrow F_{21} = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}
$$

**Step 3:** Closure on Surface 2: $F_{21} + F_{22} = 1$, so

$$
F_{22} = 1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}
$$

**Step 4:** Irradiations.

Surface 1 sees only Surface 2:

$$
G_1 = F_{12} J_2 = J_2
$$

Surface 2 sees itself and Surface 1:

$$
G_2 = F_{21} J_1 + F_{22} J_2 = \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} J_1 + \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right) J_2
$$

**Step 5:** Radiosity equations.

$$
J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1) J_2
$$

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) \left[ \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} J_1 + \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right) J_2 \right]
$$

**Step 6:** Expand the second equation:

$$
J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} J_1 + (1-\varepsilon_2) \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right) J_2
$$

Collect $J_2$ terms on the left:

$$
J_2 \left[ 1 - (1-\varepsilon_2)\left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right) \right] = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) \dfrac{A_1}{A_2} J_1
$$

**Step 7:** From the first radiosity equation, $J_2 = (J_1 - \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4)/(1 - \varepsilon_1)$. Substitute into the simplified second equation and collect $J_1$ terms. After algebraic manipulation (straightforward but lengthy — examiners usually allow 1 mark for the *form* of the result), we arrive at:

$$
J_1 - J_2 = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}
$$

**Step 8:** The net heat transfer is the heat leaving Surface 1 (which equals heat gained by Surface 2 in steady state):

$$
q = A_1 (J_1 - G_1) = A_1 (J_1 - J_2)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{\, q_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}} \,}
$$

**Step 9 — Sanity checks:**
- If $A_1 = A_2$ (parallel-plate limit), denominator becomes $(1/\varepsilon_1 + 1/\varepsilon_2 - 1)/A$ and we recover the Case 1 result. ✓
- If $\varepsilon_2 = 1$ (outer surface black), the second term in the denominator vanishes, giving $q = \varepsilon_1 A_1 \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ — physical: outer black surface absorbs everything that hits it, so the inner surface behaves as if radiating to a blackbody at $T_2$. ✓
- The formula is identical in form for concentric spheres, with $A_1 = 4\pi r_1^2$ and $A_2 = 4\pi r_2^2$.

*(7 marks: 2 marks view-factor setup, 2 marks radiosity equations, 2 marks algebra, 1 mark final boxed result with sanity checks)*

---

**(b) A long, thin-walled cylindrical heating element of diameter 5 mm and emissivity 0.7 is enclosed concentrically inside a circular duct of diameter 50 mm and emissivity 0.85. The heater surface is at 1000 K and the duct wall is at 400 K. Calculate the net heat transfer per unit length of the heater. Also find the heat transfer if the duct-wall emissivity drops to 0.3 (heater emissivity unchanged).** *(7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Heater (Surface 1): $d_1 = 5$ mm $= 0.005$ m, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.7$, $T_1 = 1000$ K.
- Duct (Surface 2): $d_2 = 50$ mm $= 0.050$ m, $\varepsilon_2 = 0.85$ (first case), $T_2 = 400$ K.
- Per unit length $L = 1$ m: $A_1 = \pi d_1 L = \pi \times 0.005 = 0.01571 \ \text{m}^2$; $A_2 = \pi d_2 = 0.1571 \ \text{m}^2$.

**Formula:**

$$
q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}}
$$

**Compute $T^4$ values:**

$$
T_1^4 = 10^{12} \ \text{K}^4, \quad T_2^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10} \ \text{K}^4
$$

$$
T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10} = 9.744 \times 10^{11} \ \text{K}^4
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4) = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.744 \times 10^{11} = 5.5248 \times 10^{4} \ \text{W}
$$

**Denominator — Case (i): $\varepsilon_2 = 0.85$:**

$$
\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} = \dfrac{1}{0.7 \times 0.01571} = \dfrac{1}{0.010997} = 90.93 \ \text{m}^{-2}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.85 \times 0.1571} = \dfrac{0.15}{0.1335} = 1.1235 \ \text{m}^{-2}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 90.93 + 1.1235 = 92.05 \ \text{m}^{-2}
$$

$$
q_{(i)} = \dfrac{5.5248 \times 10^4}{92.05} = 600.2 \ \text{W per m length}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, q_{(i)} \approx 600 \ \text{W/m} \,}
$$

**Denominator — Case (ii): $\varepsilon_2 = 0.3$:**

$$
\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.3 \times 0.1571} = \dfrac{0.7}{0.04713} = 14.85 \ \text{m}^{-2}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 90.93 + 14.85 = 105.78 \ \text{m}^{-2}
$$

$$
q_{(ii)} = \dfrac{5.5248 \times 10^4}{105.78} = 522.3 \ \text{W/m}
$$

$$
\boxed{\, q_{(ii)} \approx 522 \ \text{W/m} \,}
$$

**Observation:** Even though the duct-wall emissivity dropped from 0.85 to 0.3 (a 65 % reduction), the heat transfer per metre only fell by about 13 %. This is because the dominant resistance in a concentric cylinder with a *small* inner surface is the inner-surface resistance $1/(\varepsilon_1 A_1)$, not the outer one. This is a frequently tested KTU insight!

*(7 marks: 2 marks data extraction and formula selection, 2 marks part (i) computation, 2 marks part (ii) computation, 1 mark physical insight and commentary)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to use $T^4$ in Kelvin** — A common 2-mark deduction. $T$ must be absolute (K), not °C.
> 2. **Sign of $\Delta T^4$** — If computed negative, the student often reports a negative *heat loss*. The correct phrasing is: "heat is transferred *from* the surface with higher $T^4$ *to* the surface with lower $T^4$."
> 3. **For concentric cylinders, students often use $\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}$** — this is **WRONG**. The correct second term is $\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}$. This single error costs 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Re-radiating surface problem** — students forget that $T_R$ is *unknown* and must be *eliminated*, not calculated directly. Examiners expect the final formula *without* $T_R$ in it.
> 5. **Units of $\sigma$** — use $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \text{K}^4$, *not* $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. This is silently checked by the examiner.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **View factor $F_{ij}$** = fraction of radiation leaving $i$ that strikes $j$. Depends only on **geometry**.
- [ ] **Three view-factor rules:** (i) Summation $\sum F_{ij} = 1$, (ii) Reciprocity $A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$, (iii) Superposition.
- [ ] **Infinite parallel plates (black):** $q = A\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$.
- [ ] **Infinite parallel plates (gray):** $q = A\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4) \big/ \left( \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 \right)$.
- [ ] **Concentric cylinders / spheres (gray):** $q = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4) \big/ \left( \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2} \right)$.
- [ ] For concentric geometries, $F_{12} = 1$ (inner sees only outer); $F_{21} = A_1/A_2$ by reciprocity.
- [ ] **Re-radiating (adiabatic) surface $R$** has zero net heat flux; the heat-transfer formula places two Case-1 paths in **series**.
- [ ] A single re-radiating shield of equal emissivity **halves** the heat transfer; $n$ shields reduce it by factor $(n+1)$.
- [ ] **Resistance network analogy:** $R_s = (1-\varepsilon)/(\varepsilon A)$ (surface), $R_{sp} = 1/(A_i F_{ij})$ (space).
- [ ] **Always use absolute temperature** in Kelvin inside $T^4$.
- [ ] **Emissivity of a black body** $\varepsilon = 1$. For real surfaces $0 < \varepsilon < 1$.
- [ ] **Sanity check** your formula in the limits: $\varepsilon \to 1$ should recover the black-body result.
- [ ] The inner surface (small $A_1$) dominates the resistance in concentric problems — this is why *improving* the outer surface emissivity has limited impact.
- [ ] **Stefan–Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \ \text{W/m}^2 \text{K}^4$ — remember!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shape Factors - Radiation Heat Exchange Between Two Infinite Parallel Diffuse Gray Surfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shape Factors - Radiation Heat Exchange Between Two Infinite Parallel Diffuse Gray Surfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Shape Factor (View Factor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shape Factor (F₁₂):** The fraction of the total radiation leaving surface 1 (in all directions) that is intercepted by surface 2. It is also called the **Geometric View Factor**, **Configuration Factor**, or **Angle Factor**.

Mathematically, for two diffuse surfaces:

$$F_{1 \to 2} = \frac{1}{A_1} \int_{A_1} \int_{A_2} \frac{\cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2}{\pi r^2} \, dA_2 \, dA_1$$

where:
- $\theta_1, \theta_2$ are the angles that the connecting line $r$ makes with the normals to surfaces 1 and 2.
- $r$ is the distance between the two differential elements $dA_1$ and $dA_2$.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are standing in a room with a single light bulb on the ceiling. The **view factor** is simply the fraction of light (and therefore heat) that escapes from that bulb directly through a specific window, instead of bouncing off the walls first. If the window is huge and right in front of the bulb, almost **100 %** of the light passes through it (F = 1). If the window is small or at a sharp angle, only a tiny fraction passes through (F ≈ 0). It is a purely **geometric** property, independent of surface temperature or material.

### 1.3 Definition: Infinite Parallel Diffuse Gray Surfaces

Two surfaces are said to be **infinite, parallel, and diffuse gray** when:
- They are **parallel plates** of area $A$ separated by a small gap compared to their lateral dimensions.
- They are **diffuse** — they emit and reflect radiation uniformly in all directions (Lambert's Law holds).
- They are **gray** — emissivity $\varepsilon$ and absorptivity $\alpha$ are constant across all wavelengths ($\varepsilon = \alpha$).
- Each surface sees **only** the other surface (no edge losses).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For such a configuration, the view factors simplify beautifully: **F₁₂ = F₂₁ = 1**, because every ray leaving surface 1 must strike surface 2 (and vice-versa).

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Stefan–Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$
- **Emissivity of a black body:** $\varepsilon_b = 1$
- **Emissivity of a gray surface:** $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$
- **Kirchhoff's Law (for gray surfaces):** $\alpha = \varepsilon$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Fundamental Rules of Shape Factors

**Step 1 — Summation Rule (Enclosure Property):**
For any surface $i$ in an enclosure of $N$ surfaces, the sum of all view factors from $i$ must equal unity.

$$\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$$

**Why?** Every ray leaving surface $i$ must land on *some* surface in the closed enclosure.

**Step 2 — Reciprocity Rule (Symmetry of Radiation):**
The total radiation exchanged between two surfaces is the same whether measured from surface 1 or from surface 2.

$$A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$$

**Why?** This is a direct consequence of energy conservation between the two differential areas.

### 2.2 Special Case: Infinite Parallel Plates

For two large, parallel plates of equal area $A$ facing each other:
- All radiation leaving Plate 1 hits Plate 2 → $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$
- All radiation leaving Plate 2 hits Plate 1 → $F_{2 \to 1} = 1$
- Reciprocity: $A \cdot 1 = A \cdot 1$ ✓ (automatically satisfied)

### 2.3 Net Radiation Heat Exchange Derivation Logic

**Step A — Energy leaving a gray surface (Radiosity):**
A gray surface emits its own thermal radiation AND reflects the radiation incident on it.

$$J = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 + \rho G$$

where $\rho$ is the reflectivity, and $G$ is the irradiation.

**Step B — Applying to our two surfaces:**

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + \rho_1 G_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1) J_2$$

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) J_1$$

**Step C — Net heat transfer from 1 to 2:**

$$q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{J_1 - J_2}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A} + \dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A}} \quad \text{(network form)}$$

**Step D — Simplification for infinite parallel surfaces ($F_{12}=F_{21}=1$, equal area $A$):**

$$q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sum F_{i \to j} = 1$ | Summation rule for surface $i$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | $A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$ | Reciprocity relation | m² |
| 3 | $F_{1 \to 2} = 1$ | View factor for parallel infinite plates (1 → 2) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $F_{2 \to 1} = 1$ | View factor for parallel infinite plates (2 → 1) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $J = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 + (1-\varepsilon) G$ | Radiosity of a gray surface | W/m² |
| 6 | $q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$ | Net heat flux (parallel gray plates) | W/m² |
| 7 | $Q = A \cdot q$ | Total heat transfer | W |
| 8 | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | W/m²K⁴ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Note:** If either surface is a **black body** ($\varepsilon = 1$), the formula reduces to $q = \sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$, the classic Stefan–Boltzmann result.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Solar Collectors:** Flat-plate collectors face a glass cover; the back-loss is modeled with this exact formula.
- **Building Insulation:** Radiation between two layers of an insulating window (Low-E coatings work by reducing $\varepsilon$).
- **Furnace Linings:** Heat transfer between the inner radiating wall and the charge.
- **Cryogenic Dewars:** Multi-layer radiation shields between warm and cold surfaces.
- **Electronic Cooling:** Radiation between parallel PCB layers in a vacuum environment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Net Heat Exchange Between Two Infinite Parallel Gray Plates

**Given:** Two infinite parallel plates of area $A$, with emissivities $\varepsilon_1$ and $\varepsilon_2$, absolute temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$, and $T_1 > T_2$.

**Goal:** Derive the closed-form expression for net heat transfer $Q_{1 \to 2}$.

---

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary condition (infinite parallel):**
Because each surface sees only the other, the irradiation on Plate 1 is exactly the radiosity leaving Plate 2.

$$G_1 = J_2$$

**Step 2 — Similarly, the irradiation on Plate 2 is the radiosity leaving Plate 1:**

$$G_2 = J_1$$

**Step 3 — Write the radiosity equations for the two gray surfaces:**

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1) G_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1) J_2$$

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) G_2 = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) J_1$$

**Step 4 — Solve the first equation for $J_1 - J_2$:**

$$J_1 - (1-\varepsilon_1) J_2 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4$$

**Step 5 — Solve the second equation for $J_2$ in terms of $J_1$:**

$$J_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) J_1}{1} = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) J_1$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $J_2$ into the Step 4 equation:**

$$J_1 - (1-\varepsilon_1)\left[ \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2) J_1 \right] = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4$$

**Step 7 — Expand and collect terms involving $J_1$:**

$$J_1 - (1-\varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 - (1-\varepsilon_1)(1-\varepsilon_2) J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4$$

**Step 8 — Group the $J_1$ terms on the left-hand side:**

$$J_1 \left[ 1 - (1-\varepsilon_1)(1-\varepsilon_2) \right] = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + (1-\varepsilon_1)\varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4$$

**Step 9 — Simplify the bracket using the identity $(1-a)(1-b) = 1 - a - b + ab$:**

$$1 - (1-\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2) = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2$$

**Step 10 — Similarly, derive $J_2$ in symmetric form (or simply note by symmetry):**

$$J_2 \left[ \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 \right] = \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + (1-\varepsilon_2)\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4$$

**Step 11 — Subtract $J_2$ from $J_1$:**

$$J_1 - J_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 - \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 + \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_2^4 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 \sigma T_1^4}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

**Step 12 — Factor $\sigma$ in the numerator and combine $T_1^4$ and $T_2^4$ terms:**

$$J_1 - J_2 = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4) \left[ \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 \right] \cdot \text{...}}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

**Step 13 — After careful cancellation (symmetric numerator), we get:**

$$J_1 - J_2 = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the conversion:** We used $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 \left( \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 \right)$ to arrive at the compact denominator.

**Step 14 — Net heat flux (per unit area) is exactly the net radiosity leaving Plate 1:**

$$q_{1 \to 2} = J_1 - J_2 = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 15 — Total heat transfer:**

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = A \cdot \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Emissivity on Heat Transfer

Let $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon$ (both plates identical):

| $\varepsilon$ | Heat Flux Reduction Factor $\left( \dfrac{1}{2/\varepsilon - 1} \right)$ | Interpretation |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1.00 (black) | 1.000 | Maximum heat transfer (Stefan–Boltzmann) |
| 0.50 | 0.333 | 67 % reduction — useful for low-emissivity shields |
| 0.20 | 0.111 | 89 % reduction — Low-E window glass |
| 0.05 | 0.0256 | 97 % reduction — aluminized mylar in cryogenics |
| 0.02 | 0.0102 | 99 % reduction — spacecraft multilayer insulation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Radiation Network Analogy (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The infinite parallel plate problem is best represented using the **Radiation Network Method** pioneered by Hottel. The heat flows like current in an electrical network.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Emitter Surface 1: Eb1 = sigma T1^4] --> B[Surface Resistance 1 to 1 minus epsilon 1 / epsilon 1 A]
    B --> C[Node J1: Radiosity of Surface 1]
    C --> D[Space Resistance 1 divided by A times 1 minus F12 over F12]
    D --> E[Node J2: Radiosity of Surface 2]
    E --> F[Surface Resistance 1 minus epsilon 2 / epsilon 2 A]
    F --> G[Emitter Surface 2: Eb2 = sigma T2^4]
    C --> H[Net Heat Transfer Q1 to 2 equals J1 minus J2 times A]
    E --> H
    H --> I[Equivalent Single Equation: Q equals A sigma T1 to power 4 minus T2 to power 4 divided by 1 by epsilon 1 plus 1 by epsilon 2 minus 1]
```

### 4.2 Physical Schematic of the Configuration

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Plate 1: Temperature T1, Emissivity epsilon 1] -->|Radiation Q12| P2[Plate 2: Temperature T2, Emissivity epsilon 2]
    P1 -.->|View Factor F12 equals 1| P2
    P2 -.->|View Factor F21 equals 1| P1
    P1 -->|Emitted Eb1| R1[Radiosity J1]
    R1 -->|Net Flow| R2[Radiosity J2]
    P2 -->|Emitted Eb2| R2
```

### 4.3 Concept Map: Path from Shape Factor to Heat Exchange Formula

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Geometric Setup - Two Infinite Parallel Plates] --> S2[Step 2: Identify F12 equals 1 and F21 equals 1]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply Summation Rule Sum Fij equals 1]
    S2 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Reciprocity A1 F12 equals A2 F21]
    S3 --> S5[Step 5: Write Radiosity Equations for Both Surfaces]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Algebraic Elimination of G1 and G2]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Final Net Heat Exchange Formula]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Engineering Application - Solar Collectors, Low-E Windows, Cryogenic Dewars]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The **radiation network** treats each surface as having a "surface resistance" $\frac{1-\varepsilon}{\varepsilon A}$ and the space between them as a "space resistance" $\frac{1}{A F_{1\to 2}}$. The current $Q$ flows from the hot emitter $E_{b1}$ to the cold emitter $E_{b2}$ through this network. This analogy is the foundation of the **electrical network method** of solving multi-surface radiation problems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **Q1. Define the term 'shape factor' (or view factor) in radiation heat transfer. State the two fundamental laws it obeys.**
> *(3 Marks – CO3, Bloom Level: Remember)*

**Model Answer:**
The **shape factor** $F_{1 \to 2}$ is defined as the fraction of the total radiation energy leaving surface 1 that strikes surface 2 directly. It is purely a geometric quantity, independent of surface temperature or material properties.

The two fundamental laws are:
1. **Summation Rule:** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$ — all radiation leaving surface $i$ must reach some surface in the enclosure.
2. **Reciprocity Rule:** $A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$ — radiation exchange is symmetric.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **Q2. Two large parallel plates, each of area 1 m², are maintained at 800 K and 300 K, with emissivities 0.8 and 0.3 respectively. Calculate the net heat exchange if both surfaces are diffuse and gray.**
> *(3 Marks – CO3, Bloom Level: Apply)*

**Model Answer:**

$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

Substitute the values:
- $T_1^4 = 800^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}$ K⁴
- $T_2^4 = 300^4 = 8.1 \times 10^{9}$ K⁴
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴
- Denominator: $\frac{1}{0.8} + \frac{1}{0.3} - 1 = 1.25 + 3.33 - 1 = 3.583$

$$q = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (4.096 \times 10^{11} - 8.1 \times 10^{9})}{3.583}$$

$$q = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.015 \times 10^{11}}{3.583} = \frac{22765}{3.583} \approx 6352 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$Q = 1 \times 6352 \approx 6352 \text{ W} \approx 6.35 \text{ kW}$$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A (a)** Two large parallel plates of area 2 m² are at temperatures 1000 K and 400 K with emissivities 0.6 and 0.4. Determine the net heat transfer rate and the temperature of the radiation shield (a thin gray plate of emissivity 0.05) placed midway between them.
*(7 Marks – CO3, Apply + Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_1 = 1000$ K, $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$; $T_3 = 400$ K, $\varepsilon_3 = 0.4$; shield at center: $\varepsilon_2 = 0.05$; $A = 2$ m².

**Net heat transfer without shield:**

$$Q_{13} = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_3^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1}$$

$$= \frac{2 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000^4 - 400^4)}{1.667 + 2.5 - 1}$$

$$= \frac{2 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10})}{3.167}$$

$$= \frac{2 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.744 \times 10^{11}}{3.167} \approx 34{,}876 \text{ W}$$

**With shield, steady state gives $Q_{12} = Q_{23}$:**

$$\frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1} = \frac{A \sigma (T_2^4 - T_3^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1}$$

Denominator₁ = $\frac{1}{0.6} + \frac{1}{0.05} - 1 = 1.667 + 20 - 1 = 20.667$
Denominator₂ = $\frac{1}{0.05} + \frac{1}{0.4} - 1 = 20 + 2.5 - 1 = 21.5$

So:

$$\frac{T_1^4 - T_2^4}{20.667} = \frac{T_2^4 - T_3^4}{21.5}$$

$$21.5 (10^{12} - T_2^4) = 20.667 (T_2^4 - 2.56 \times 10^{10})$$

$$2.15 \times 10^{13} - 21.5 T_2^4 = 20.667 T_2^4 - 5.29 \times 10^{11}$$

$$2.15 \times 10^{13} + 5.29 \times 10^{11} = 42.167 T_2^4$$

$$2.203 \times 10^{13} = 42.167 T_2^4$$

$$T_2^4 = 5.226 \times 10^{11} \Rightarrow T_2 \approx 851 \text{ K}$$

**Net heat transfer with shield:**

$$Q = \frac{2 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (10^{12} - 2.56 \times 10^{10})}{20.667 + 21.5} \approx \frac{2.203 \times 10^{13} \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8}}{42.167}$$

$$Q \approx \frac{1.249 \times 10^{6}}{42.167} \approx 29{,}620 \text{ W} \approx 29.6 \text{ kW}$$

**Conclusion:** The low-emissivity shield (0.05) reduces heat transfer from 34.9 kW to 29.6 kW — a ~15 % reduction.

---

**Q.A (b)** Using the radiation network method, derive the formula for net heat exchange between two infinite parallel diffuse gray surfaces. State clearly the significance of view factor in this context.
*(7 Marks – CO3, Understand + Apply)*

**Model Solution:**
The derivation follows the systematic elimination of irradiation terms $G_1$ and $G_2$ as shown in **Section 3.1** above. The key steps are:

1. Apply the enclosure condition for parallel plates: $G_1 = J_2$ and $G_2 = J_1$.
2. Write radiosity equations for both surfaces.
3. Use $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1$ as the combined resistance.
4. Obtain: $Q = \dfrac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$.

**Significance of view factor:** For two infinite parallel plates, $F_{12} = F_{21} = 1$ because every ray leaving one surface must strike the other. This greatly simplifies the network — the "space resistance" $\frac{1}{A F_{12}} = \frac{1}{A}$ becomes trivial, leaving only the two "surface resistances" $\frac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A}$ and $\frac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A}$ in series. The view factor is therefore a **geometric bridge** between the physical configuration and the thermal resistance network.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Internal Choice*

**Q.B (a)** Explain the **Summation Rule** and **Reciprocity Rule** of shape factors. For an enclosure of three gray surfaces with areas 1, 2, 3 m² and shape factors $F_{12} = 0.2$, $F_{13} = 0.4$, find $F_{11}$, $F_{23}$ and $F_{33}$ using the available relations.
*(7 Marks – CO3, Apply + Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**Summation Rule:** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$
**Reciprocity Rule:** $A_i F_{i \to j} = A_j F_{j \to i}$

**Given:** $A_1 = 1$, $A_2 = 2$, $A_3 = 3$; $F_{12} = 0.2$, $F_{13} = 0.4$.

**Step 1 — Find $F_{11}$ using summation rule for surface 1:**

$$F_{11} + F_{12} + F_{13} = 1$$
$$F_{11} = 1 - 0.2 - 0.4 = 0.4$$

**Step 2 — Find $F_{21}$ using reciprocity:**

$$A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21} \Rightarrow 1 \times 0.2 = 2 \times F_{21} \Rightarrow F_{21} = 0.1$$

**Step 3 — Find $F_{23}$ using summation for surface 2:**

$$F_{21} + F_{22} + F_{23} = 1 \Rightarrow 0.1 + F_{22} + F_{23} = 1$$

We need one more relation. Use reciprocity: $A_1 F_{13} = A_3 F_{31} \Rightarrow 1 \times 0.4 = 3 \times F_{31} \Rightarrow F_{31} = 0.1333$

**Step 4 — Find $F_{33}$ using summation for surface 3:**

$$F_{31} + F_{32} + F_{33} = 1 \Rightarrow 0.1333 + F_{32} + F_{33} = 1$$

Reciprocity gives: $A_2 F_{23} = A_3 F_{32} \Rightarrow 2 F_{23} = 3 F_{32}$.

From Step 3: $F_{22} = 0.9 - F_{23}$.

For a convex or flat surface $F_{22} = 0$, hence $F_{23} = 0.9$.
Then $F_{32} = \frac{2 \times 0.9}{3} = 0.6$.

**Step 5 — Now $F_{33}$:**

$$F_{33} = 1 - 0.1333 - 0.6 = 0.2667$$

**Final Answers:** $F_{11} = 0.4$, $F_{23} = 0.9$, $F_{33} \approx 0.267$.

---

**Q.B (b)** A furnace wall at 1200 K ($\varepsilon_1 = 0.85$) radiates onto a work surface at 600 K ($\varepsilon_2 = 0.7$). The intervening medium is non-absorbing. The wall and work-piece each have an area of 4 m². Calculate the net radiant heat transfer. If a polished aluminum radiation shield ($\varepsilon_s = 0.05$) of the same area is placed between them, what is the percentage reduction in heat transfer?
*(7 Marks – CO3, Apply + Analyze)*

**Model Solution:**

**Without shield:**

$$Q_1 = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

$$= \frac{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1200^4 - 600^4)}{1.176 + 1.428 - 1}$$

$$= \frac{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (2.074 \times 10^{12} - 1.296 \times 10^{11})}{1.604}$$

$$= \frac{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.944 \times 10^{12}}{1.604} \approx \frac{4.41 \times 10^{5}}{1.604} \approx 2.75 \times 10^{5} \text{ W} = 275 \text{ kW}$$

**With one shield:** Effective resistance doubles, so $Q_2 \approx Q_1 / 2 = 137.5$ kW.

More precisely:

$$Q_2 = \frac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{2 \left( \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} - 1 \right) + \left( \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} - 1 \right)}$$

$$= \frac{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.944 \times 10^{12}}{2(1.176 + 20 - 1) + (1.428 + 20 - 1)}$$

$$= \frac{4.41 \times 10^{5}}{2(20.176) + 20.428} = \frac{4.41 \times 10^{5}}{60.78} \approx 7255 \text{ W} \approx 7.26 \text{ kW}$$

**Percentage reduction:**

$$\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{275 - 7.26}{275} \times 100 \approx 97.4 \text{ \%}$$

The polished aluminum shield reduces heat transfer by ~97 %, demonstrating the power of low-emissivity surfaces in industrial insulation.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use emissivity in the denominator:** Many students write $Q = A\sigma(T_1^4 - T_2^4)$ for gray surfaces, ignoring the $\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1}+\frac{1}{\varepsilon_2}-1$ term. This is the **#1 mistake** — losing 3 to 4 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch on $T$:** Temperatures MUST be in **Kelvin**, not °C, because the Stefan–Boltzmann law involves $T^4$. Converting 800 °C to 1073 K is mandatory.
> 3. **Confusing F₁₂ with F₂₁ for unequal areas:** Students sometimes write $F_{12} = F_{21}$ directly, ignoring that this holds only when $A_1 = A_2$. The correct statement is **reciprocity** ($A_1 F_{12} = A_2 F_{21}$).
> 4. **Incorrect sign of net Q:** Convention is $Q_{1 \to 2}$ from hot to cold. If $T_1 < T_2$, the answer is negative, indicating flow from 2 to 1.
> 5. **Shield problems:** Students often assume the shield has the **same emissivity** as the plates. Always read carefully — shields are designed to be **low-emissivity**.
> 6. **Skipping the Summation Rule step:** When asked to find an unknown view factor in a 3-surface enclosure, the most efficient path is **Summation + Reciprocity** combined. Showing both steps clearly fetches full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shape Factors & Parallel Plate Radiation**

- **Definition:** Shape factor $F_{1 \to 2}$ = fraction of radiation leaving surface 1 that strikes surface 2. Purely geometric.
- **Summation Rule:** $\sum_{j=1}^{N} F_{i \to j} = 1$ — energy conservation in an enclosure.
- **Reciprocity Rule:** $A_1 F_{1 \to 2} = A_2 F_{2 \to 1}$ — symmetry of radiation exchange.
- **Infinite parallel plates:** $F_{1 \to 2} = F_{2 \to 1} = 1$ because each surface sees only the other.
- **Radiosity (J):** Emitted + reflected energy leaving a surface; $J = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 + (1-\varepsilon) G$.
- **Irradiation (G):** Total radiation incident on a surface from all directions.
- **Gray surface:** $\varepsilon_\lambda = \varepsilon$ (constant across wavelengths); by Kirchhoff's law, $\alpha = \varepsilon$.
- **Black body:** $\varepsilon = 1$, $J = \sigma T^4$.
- **Net heat exchange (parallel gray plates):** $Q = \dfrac{A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$.
- **Black-body limit:** When $\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = 1$, the formula reduces to $Q = A \sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)$.
- **Radiation shield:** A thin low-$\varepsilon$ plate placed between surfaces; with $N$ identical shields, $Q$ is reduced by a factor of $(N+1)$.
- **Constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴. Always use **Kelvin** for $T$.
- **Engineering uses:** Solar collectors, Low-E windows, cryogenic dewars, furnace linings, spacecraft MLI.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Network Analogy for Radiation Heat Exchange
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical Network Analogy for Radiation Heat Exchange

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Network Analogy for Radiation Heat Exchange** is an analytical method in which radiation heat transfer between gray, diffuse, opaque surfaces forming an enclosure is represented as an equivalent **resistive thermal circuit** (analogous to an electrical network). In this analogy, **emissive powers (E_b)** of surfaces act as node potentials, **radiative resistances** act as circuit elements, and the **net heat transfer rate (Q)** acts as the current flowing through the network. Complex multi-surface radiation problems are solved using the same rules applied to DC electrical circuits: **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** and **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)**, along with the **current-divider** and **series-parallel reduction** rules.

The blackbody emissive power of a surface is given by the **Stefan–Boltzmann law**:

$$E_b = \sigma T^{4}$$

where the fundamental physical constant is **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** (Stefan–Boltzmann constant).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — Water flow in pipes:**
> Imagine a water tank (hot surface at high temperature) connected through pipes to a drain (cold surface at low temperature). Water (heat) flows from high level to low level. The pipes (resistances) restrict the flow. Now make this picture: connect multiple such tanks with multiple pipes forming a network. The water flow rate (Q) depends on the height difference (driving potential = E_b₁ − E_b₂) divided by the total resistance of the pipes.
>
> In radiation, the "height" of the tank is replaced by the **fourth power of absolute temperature (T⁴)**, and the "pipe resistance" is the **radiative resistance** (which depends on surface emissivity and geometry/view factor). The analogy is mathematically exact for gray, diffuse, opaque enclosures.

## 1.3 Parameter Mapping

The mapping between electrical and radiation quantities is summarized below:

| Electrical Quantity | Radiation Equivalent | Symbol |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Voltage / EMF | Blackbody emissive power | E_b = σT⁴ |
| Current | Net heat transfer rate | Q (W) |
| Resistance | Radiative resistance | R (m⁻²) |
| Node | Surface | i = 1, 2, 3, ... |
| Closed loop | Radiation network loop | — |
| KCL at node | Energy balance at surface | ΣQ_in = ΣQ_out |
| KVL around loop | Net potential drop = 0 | ΣE_b = 0 |
| Ground (0 V) | Reference at 0 K (E_b = 0) | Vacuum sink |

## 1.4 Why This Method Is Used in KTU Problems

- **Geometric complexity**: Direct integration of radiosity/view factor equations for 3+ surfaces is tedious.
- **Re-use of circuit theory**: The student can apply familiar series, parallel, Δ–Y transformations, and nodal analysis.
- **Physical insight**: Visualizing the radiative network makes the *direction* and *magnitude* of energy flow between surfaces immediately apparent.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Concept of Radiosity (J) — The Node Potential

For a gray, opaque, diffuse surface, the total radiation **leaving** the surface per unit area is called **Radiosity (J)**:

$$J = \varepsilon E_b + \rho G = \varepsilon E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) G$$

where:
- **G** = irradiation (incoming radiation per unit area from other surfaces)
- **ρ = 1 − α = 1 − ε** (for opaque, gray, diffuse surfaces; α = absorptivity)
- **E_b = σT⁴** = blackbody emissive power

> [!NOTE]
> **Radiosity is the radiation network equivalent of a node voltage**. Heat leaves surface *i* at a rate A_i·J_i, and arrives at rate A_i·G_i. The net heat loss from the surface is **Q_i = A_i (J_i − G_i)**.

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Radiative Resistances

### 2.2.1 Surface Resistance (R_s)

This represents the resistance offered by the **surface itself** due to its non-ideal (non-black) nature.

$$R_{s,i} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i \, A_i}$$

It is connected between the *fixed blackbody node* **E_b,i** and the *radiosity node* **J_i**.

### 2.2.2 Space (Geometric) Resistance (R_sp)

This represents the resistance due to the **geometric configuration** (view factor) between two surfaces, independent of their emissivities.

$$R_{sp,ij} = \frac{1}{A_i F_{ij}} = \frac{1}{A_j F_{ji}}$$

using the **reciprocity relation** $A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}$.

### 2.2.3 Combined / Total Resistance for Two Surfaces

For an enclosure formed by two surfaces, the three resistances are in **series** between the E_b nodes:

$$R_{total} = R_{s,1} + R_{sp,12} + R_{s,2} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1} + \frac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} + \frac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}$$

The net heat transfer becomes (Ohm's-law analogy):

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{E_{b,1} - E_{b,2}}{R_{total}} = \frac{\sigma \left(T_1^{4} - T_2^{4}\right)}{R_{total}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | E_b = σT⁴ | Blackbody emissive power | W/m² |
| 2 | J = εE_b + (1 − ε)G | Radiosity of gray diffuse surface | W/m² |
| 3 | Q_i = A_i (J_i − G_i) | Net heat leaving surface i | W |
| 4 | Q = (J_i − J_j) / (1/(A_i F_{ij})) | Heat flow between radiosity nodes | W |
| 5 | R_{s,i} = (1 − ε_i)/(ε_i A_i) | Surface resistance | m⁻² |
| 6 | R_{sp,ij} = 1/(A_i F_{ij}) | Space resistance | m⁻² |
| 7 | A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji} | Reciprocity of view factors | m² |
| 8 | Σ_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1 | Summation rule | — |
| 9 | Q_12 = σ(T_1⁴ − T_2⁴) / [1/ε_1 + 1/ε_2 − 1] | Two infinite parallel plates (A_1 = A_2) | W/m² |
| 10 | Q_12 = (E_{b,1} − E_{b,2}) / R_total | General two-surface enclosure | W |
| 11 | Q_i = A_i (E_{b,i} − J_i) / R_{s,i} | Heat through surface resistance i | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In any table where absolute value bars or "divided by" notation are needed, always use the LaTeX macros **\vert** or **\mid** (for example, $\vert T_1^{4} - T_2^{4} \vert$) to keep markdown tables from breaking.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Furnace and boiler design**: Calculating heat flux between hot gas zones and water/steam tubes forming multi-surface enclosures.
- **Spacecraft thermal control**: Modeling heat exchange between radiator panels, the Sun, and deep space.
- **Building energy analysis**: Radiant heat flow between interior walls, windows, and the floor in conditioned spaces.
- **High-temperature process equipment**: Re-heating furnaces, glass melting tanks, and nuclear reactor shields.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Net Heat Transfer Between Two Infinite Parallel Plates (A_1 = A_2, F_12 = 1)

### Step 1: Geometry & Setup

Two large, parallel, diffuse, gray, opaque plates of the **same area A** are placed facing each other. Their temperatures are T_1 and T_2, and emissivities are ε_1 and ε_2. Since the plates are infinite, **F_12 = F_21 = 1** (every ray leaving one plate strikes the other).

### Step 2: Radiosity Equations

For surface 1: irradiation G_1 = J_2 (because F_21 = 1)

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

For surface 2: irradiation G_2 = J_1 (because F_12 = 1)

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1 \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

### Step 3: Solve the Two Linear Equations

From equation (ii), isolate J_1:

$$J_1 = \frac{J_2 - \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2}}{1 - \varepsilon_2}$$

Substitute into equation (i):

$$\frac{J_2 - \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2}}{1 - \varepsilon_2} = \varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2$$

Multiply both sides by (1 − ε_2):

$$J_2 - \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} = (1 - \varepsilon_2)\varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + (1 - \varepsilon_2)(1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2$$

Collect the J_2 terms on the left:

$$J_2 \left[1 - (1 - \varepsilon_1)(1 - \varepsilon_2)\right] = \varepsilon_1(1 - \varepsilon_2) E_{b,1} + \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2}$$

Expand the bracket on the left:

$$1 - (1 - \varepsilon_1)(1 - \varepsilon_2) = 1 - \left(1 - \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2\right) = \varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2$$

Hence:

$$J_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_1(1 - \varepsilon_2) E_{b,1} + \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2}}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

By symmetry (or by re-solving):

$$J_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + \varepsilon_2(1 - \varepsilon_1) E_{b,2}}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

### Step 4: Compute Net Heat Transfer

Net heat leaving surface 1 (and arriving at surface 2):

$$Q_1 = A (J_1 - J_2)$$

Subtract the two radiosities:

$$J_1 - J_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + \varepsilon_2(1 - \varepsilon_1) E_{b,2} - \varepsilon_1(1 - \varepsilon_2) E_{b,1} - \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2}}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

Simplify the numerator term by term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= \varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} - \varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 E_{b,1} - \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} \\
&= \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 E_{b,1} - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} \\
&= \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 \left(E_{b,1} - E_{b,2}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$Q_1 = \frac{A \, \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 \left(E_{b,1} - E_{b,2}\right)}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

### Step 5: Express in Network Form

Divide numerator and denominator by ε_1 ε_2:

$$Q_1 = \frac{A \left(E_{b,1} - E_{b,2}\right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1} = \frac{A \left(E_{b,1} - E_{b,2}\right)}{R_{s,1} + R_{sp,12} + R_{s,2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two infinite plates have **F_12 = 1**, so the space resistance is 1/A. The denominator's three terms are exactly the **three series resistances** in the electrical analogy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — General Two-Surface Enclosure (Different Areas, F_12 < 1)

### Step 1: Energy Balance at Surface 1

The irradiation on surface 1 is the fraction of J_2 intercepted by surface 1:

$$G_1 = J_2 F_{21} = J_2 \frac{A_2 F_{12}}{A_1}$$

The radiosity of surface 1:

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2 \frac{A_2 F_{12}}{A_1} \quad \text{...(I)}$$

### Step 2: Energy Balance at Surface 2

$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1 \frac{A_1 F_{12}}{A_2} \quad \text{...(II)}$$

### Step 3: Net Heat Transfer

The net heat leaving surface 1 to surface 2 is the radiation that leaves 1 and reaches 2 (F_12) minus radiation from 2 to 1 (F_21), which together yield:

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = A_1 F_{12} (J_1 - J_2)$$

Algebraic elimination of J_1 and J_2 (the full algebra is lengthy but identical in pattern to the previous case) gives:

$$Q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{E_{b,1} - E_{b,2}}{R_{s,1} + R_{sp,12} + R_{s,2}}$$

where:

$$R_{s,1} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A_1}, \qquad R_{sp,12} = \frac{1}{A_1 F_{12}}, \qquad R_{s,2} = \frac{1 - \varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A_2}$$

### Step 4: Limiting Cases (Sanity Check)

| Limit | Formula Reduces To | Physical Meaning |
|:---|:---|:---|
| ε_1 = ε_2 = 1 (black) | Q = A_1 F_12 σ(T_1⁴ − T_2⁴) | Standard blackbody exchange |
