| A_1 = A_2 = A, F_12 = 1 | Q/A = σ(T_1⁴ − T_2⁴) / (1/ε_1 + 1/ε_2 − 1) | Two infinite parallel plates |
| ε_1 ≪ 1, ε_2 = 1 | Q ≈ A_1 ε_1 σ(T_1⁴ − T_2⁴) | Surface 1 controls heat flow |

## 3.3 Three-Surface Enclosure — Network Reduction Strategy

For N surfaces, the analogy extends naturally. For three surfaces 1, 2, 3 with view factors F_12, F_13, F_23, the network has:

- **Three E_b nodes** (at fixed potentials σT_i⁴)
- **Three radiosity nodes** J_1, J_2, J_3
- **Three surface resistances** R_{s,1}, R_{s,2}, R_{s,3}
- **Three space resistances** R_{sp,12}, R_{sp,13}, R_{sp,23}

The current at each radiosity node (Kirchhoff's current law applied to surface i) yields:

$$\frac{E_{b,i} - J_i}{R_{s,i}} = \sum_{j \neq i} \frac{J_i - J_j}{R_{sp,ij}}$$

This is a **system of three linear equations** in three unknowns (J_1, J_2, J_3) that can be solved by:
1. Matrix inversion (preferred for KTU), or
2. Successive node-voltage analysis (analogous to nodal analysis in EE).

Once J_i is known, the heat transfer rate at any surface is:

$$Q_i = \frac{E_{b,i} - J_i}{R_{s,i}} = A_i \frac{\varepsilon_i}{1 - \varepsilon_i} \left(E_{b,i} - J_i\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **sign convention** is: Q_i > 0 means heat *leaves* surface i, Q_i < 0 means heat *enters* surface i. For energy conservation in a closed enclosure with no external source/sink: **Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 0** (when all surfaces are inside the enclosure and the enclosure is adiabatic externally).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mapping Between Electrical and Radiation Networks

The following block-level flow diagram captures the conceptual mapping between a generic DC electrical network and its radiation heat exchange analogue.

```mermaid
graph LR
  subgraph A["ELECTRICAL NETWORK"]
    A1[Voltage Source E1]
    A2[Resistor R1]
    A3[Node Voltage V1]
    A4[Resistor R12]
    A5[Node Voltage V2]
    A6[Resistor R2]
    A7[Voltage Source E2]
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7
  end

  subgraph B["RADIATION NETWORK"]
    B1[Emissive Power E_b1 = sigma T1 to power 4]
    B2[Surface Resistance R_s1 = 1 minus eps1 over eps1 A1]
    B3[Radiosity J1]
    B4[Space Resistance R_sp12 = 1 over A1 F12]
    B5[Radiosity J2]
    B6[Surface Resistance R_s2 = 1 minus eps2 over eps2 A2]
    B7[Emissive Power E_b2 = sigma T2 to power 4]
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> B7
  end

  A1 -.analogy.-> B1
  A2 -.analogy.-> B2
  A3 -.analogy.-> B3
  A4 -.analogy.-> B4
  A5 -.analogy.-> B5
  A6 -.analogy.-> B6
  A7 -.analogy.-> B7
```

## 4.2 Two-Surface Radiation Network (Series)

For an enclosure of two surfaces, the three radiative resistances are in **series** between the two emissive power nodes (E_b,1 and E_b,2).

```mermaid
graph LR
  node1[EB1: Blackbody Node Surface 1]
  node2[RS1: Surface Resistance of Surface 1]
  node3[J1: Radiosity Node of Surface 1]
  node4[RSP12: Space Resistance Between 1 and 2]
  node5[J2: Radiosity Node of Surface 2]
  node6[RS2: Surface Resistance of Surface 2]
  node7[EB2: Blackbody Node Surface 2]

  node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5 --> node6 --> node7

  node2 -. "R_s1 = 1 minus eps1 over eps1 A1" .-> node2
  node4 -. "R_sp12 = 1 over A1 F12" .-> node4
  node6 -. "R_s2 = 1 minus eps2 over eps2 A2" .-> node6
```

## 4.3 Three-Surface Radiation Network (Series–Parallel Hybrid)

For a three-surface enclosure, **J_1** has two parallel paths — one through J_2 and one through J_3 — to reach the other surfaces. The reduction follows the **current-divider rule** of network theory.

```mermaid
graph LR
  p1[EB1: Surface 1]
  p2[RS1: Surface 1 Resistance]
  p3[J1: Radiosity 1]
  p4[RSP12: Space 1 to 2]
  p5[RSP13: Space 1 to 3]
  p6[J2: Radiosity 2]
  p7[J3: Radiosity 3]
  p8[RS2: Surface 2 Resistance]
  p9[RS3: Surface 3 Resistance]
  p10[EB2: Surface 2]
  p11[EB3: Surface 3]

  p1 --> p2 --> p3
  p3 --> p4 --> p6
  p3 --> p5 --> p7
  p6 --> p8 --> p10
  p7 --> p9 --> p11
```

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Solution Methodology

```mermaid
graph TD
  stepA[Define Geometry and Surface Properties]
  stepB[Compute View Factors F_ij using Reciprocity and Summation]
  stepC[Calculate E_b_i = sigma T_i to power 4 for all surfaces]
  stepD[Build Equivalent Electrical Network with E_b and J nodes]
  stepE[Compute Radiative Resistances R_s and R_sp]
  stepF[Apply KCL at each Radiosity Node]
  stepG[Solve Linear System for J_1 J_2 ... J_N]
  stepH[Compute Q_i from E_b_i and J_i]
  stepI[Verify Energy Balance Sum Q_i = 0]

  stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH --> stepI
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams**: In every block diagram, the **driving potential** (E_b) sits on the outer nodes, the **resistive elements** are in the middle, and the **radiosity nodes (J)** are the unknown internal node voltages that must be solved using nodal analysis — exactly as one would solve a multi-node electrical circuit.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, 3 marks]**
**State the electrical analogy for radiation heat exchange. List the three types of radiative resistances.**

**Model Answer:**
In the electrical analogy, the net heat transfer rate **Q** between surfaces is treated as **current**, the blackbody emissive power **E_b = σT⁴** acts as **voltage (driving potential)**, and the **radiosities (J)** of the surfaces act as **node potentials**. Radiative heat flow is governed by **Kirchhoff's current and voltage laws** applied to a resistive network.

The three types of radiative resistances are:

1. **Surface resistance**: $R_{s,i} = \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A_i}$ — represents non-blackness of a surface.
2. **Space (geometric) resistance**: $R_{sp,ij} = \dfrac{1}{A_i F_{ij}}$ — represents the geometric view factor between two surfaces.
3. **Combined resistance** (sum of the above two in series) — appears in the general two-surface exchange formula.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, 3 marks]**
**What is radiosity? How is it used as a node potential in the radiation network?**

**Model Answer:**
**Radiosity (J)** is the total rate of radiation leaving a surface per unit area, including both the **emitted component** (εE_b) and the **reflected component** ((1 − ε)G), where G is the irradiation on the surface.

$$J = \varepsilon E_b + (1 - \varepsilon) G$$

In the radiation network, each surface has a radiosity node **J_i** connected to its blackbody emissive power node **E_{b,i}** through the surface resistance **R_{s,i}**. The value of J_i is found by applying KCL at that node. Once J_i is known, the net heat transfer from the surface is:

$$Q_i = \frac{E_{b,i} - J_i}{R_{s,i}}$$

J_i is therefore the radiation analogue of an **internal node voltage** in an electrical network.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Style with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 marks)**

#### (a) **[7 marks]**
Two large, parallel, gray plates of area 1 m² each are separated by a large distance. Their temperatures are T_1 = 800 K and T_2 = 500 K, with emissivities ε_1 = 0.7 and ε_2 = 0.5. Using the **electrical network analogy**, calculate the net rate of heat transfer between the plates.

#### (b) **[7 marks]**
Explain, with a suitable diagram, the **three-resistance network** representation for two surfaces forming an enclosure. Show how the expression for net heat transfer is obtained from this network.

---

### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) **[7 marks]**
**Derive** the expression for the net radiation heat transfer between two large parallel plates of different emissivities, starting from the radiosity equations.

#### (b) **[7 marks]**
Two long concentric cylinders form a cylindrical enclosure. The inner cylinder has radius r_1 = 5 cm, temperature T_1 = 1000 K and emissivity ε_1 = 0.7. The outer cylinder has radius r_2 = 10 cm, temperature T_2 = 600 K and emissivity ε_2 = 0.5. Calculate the **net heat transfer per unit length** of the cylinder using the network method. Take σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A (a)

**Step 1 — Identify the configuration**

Since the plates are parallel and large, F_12 = 1 and A_1 = A_2 = A = 1 m².

**Step 2 — Compute blackbody emissive powers**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{b,1} &= \sigma T_1^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (800)^{4} \\
&= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 4.096 \times 10^{11} \\
&= 23{,}224.32 \text{ W/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{b,2} &= \sigma T_2^{4} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (500)^{4} \\
&= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.25 \times 10^{10} \\
&= 3{,}543.75 \text{ W/m}^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute radiative resistances**

$$R_{s,1} = \frac{1 - 0.7}{0.7 \times 1} = \frac{0.3}{0.7} = 0.4286 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$R_{sp,12} = \frac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} = \frac{1}{1 \times 1} = 1.0000 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$R_{s,2} = \frac{1 - 0.5}{0.5 \times 1} = \frac{0.5}{0.5} = 1.0000 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Total resistance and net heat transfer**

$$R_{total} = 0.4286 + 1.0000 + 1.0000 = 2.4286 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$Q = \frac{E_{b,1} - E_{b,2}}{R_{total}} = \frac{23{,}224.32 - 3{,}543.75}{2.4286} = \frac{19{,}680.57}{2.4286}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 8{,}103.8 \text{ W} \quad (\text{since } A = 1 \text{ m}^{2})}$$

---

### Solution to Question A (b) — Three-Resistance Network

The schematic is given in Section 4.2 above. Each surface has a **blackbody emissive power node** (E_{b,i}) connected via its **surface resistance (R_{s,i})** to a **radiosity node (J_i)**. The two J-nodes are then connected via the **space resistance R_{sp,12}**.

By Ohm's-law analogy:

$$Q = \frac{E_{b,1} - E_{b,2}}{R_{s,1} + R_{sp,12} + R_{s,2}}$$

The three resistances are in **series** because the same heat flux Q passes through each one: from E_{b,1} to J_1 (across R_{s,1}), from J_1 to J_2 (across R_{sp,12}), and from J_2 to E_{b,2} (across R_{s,2}). This is the **fundamental result** of the network analogy for two-surface enclosures.

---

### Solution to Question B (a)

The full derivation is given in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The starting radiosity equations are:

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 E_{b,1} + (1 - \varepsilon_1) J_2$$
$$J_2 = \varepsilon_2 E_{b,2} + (1 - \varepsilon_2) J_1$$

Solving simultaneously yields:

$$J_1 - J_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2 (E_{b,1} - E_{b,2})}{\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 - \varepsilon_1 \varepsilon_2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{Q}{A} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^{4} - T_2^{4})}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}}$$

---

### Solution to Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Geometry and areas**

For unit length (L = 1 m):

$$A_1 = 2\pi r_1 L = 2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1 = 0.3142 \text{ m}^{2}$$
$$A_2 = 2\pi r_2 L = 2\pi \times 0.10 \times 1 = 0.6283 \text{ m}^{2}$$

For concentric cylinders, the inner cylinder sees the outer one completely, so **F_12 = 1**. By reciprocity: A_1 F_12 = A_2 F_21 → F_21 = A_1/A_2 = 0.5.

**Step 2 — Emissive powers**

$$E_{b,1} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1000)^{4} = 56{,}700 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$
$$E_{b,2} = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (600)^{4} = 7{,}348.32 \text{ W/m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Radiative resistances**

$$R_{s,1} = \frac{1 - 0.7}{0.7 \times 0.3142} = \frac{0.3}{0.2199} = 1.3641 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$R_{sp,12} = \frac{1}{A_1 F_{12}} = \frac{1}{0.3142 \times 1} = 3.1831 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$R_{s,2} = \frac{1 - 0.5}{0.5 \times 0.6283} = \frac{0.5}{0.3142} = 1.5915 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Total resistance and heat transfer**

$$R_{total} = 1.3641 + 3.1831 + 1.5915 = 6.1387 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$Q = \frac{56{,}700 - 7{,}348.32}{6.1387} = \frac{49{,}351.68}{6.1387}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 8{,}038.5 \text{ W per metre length}}$$

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing radiosity (J) with emissive power (E_b)** — J is at a node, E_b is the *fixed blackbody potential* of the source. They are equal **only if ε = 1**.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute F_ij from reciprocity** when A_1 ≠ A_2. Many students wrongly use F_12 = 1 even for different-area surfaces. Use A_1 F_12 = A_2 F_21 to get the right view factor.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — the resistance formulas give units of **m⁻²**, *not* m² or dimensionless. KTU examiners deduct marks for inconsistent units in the final answer.
> 4. **Sign convention confusion** — In KTU answer scripts, always state explicitly whether Q is **leaving** or **entering** the surface. Energy balance **ΣQ_i = 0** is a great self-check.
> 5. **Missing the factor A in the space resistance** — A common error is writing R_sp = 1/F_12. The correct form is R_sp = 1/(A_i F_ij).
> 6. **Using T (°C) instead of T (K)** in σT⁴ — this is the *single most common mistake* in KTU radiation problems. Always convert to **Kelvin** first.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for this topic:**
> - **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**: σ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** (memorize).
> - **Emissive power**: E_b = σT⁴, **always in Kelvin**.
> - **Radiosity**: J = εE_b + (1 − ε)G = node potential in the radiation network.
> - **Three radiative resistances**:
>   - Surface: R_{s,i} = (1 − ε_i)/(ε_i A_i)
>   - Space: R_{sp,ij} = 1/(A_i F_{ij}) = 1/(A_j F_{ji})
>   - Total (two surfaces): R_{s,1} + R_{sp,12} + R_{s,2} (series)
> - **Network current (Ohm's law)**: Q = (E_{b,1} − E_{b,2})/R_total.
> - **View factor rules** (must use them correctly):
>   - Reciprocity: A_i F_{ij} = A_j F_{ji}
>   - Summation: Σ_{j=1}^{N} F_{ij} = 1
> - **Limiting cases to remember**:
>   - Two infinite parallel plates → denominator 1/ε_1 + 1/ε_2 − 1.
>   - Concentric cylinders (inner sees outer) → F_12 = 1, but A_1 F_12 ≠ A_2 F_21.
>   - Black surfaces (ε = 1) → R_s = 0, only space resistance matters.
> - **Energy balance check** (closure): ΣQ_i = 0.
> - **Solve method** for 3+ surfaces: write KCL at each J-node, solve the linear system, then compute Q_i from E_{b,i} and J_i.
> - **Always state sign convention** and **always use SI units + Kelvin**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radiation Shields
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Shields

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **radiation shield** is a thin, opaque, low-emissivity (highly reflective) plate placed between two radiating surfaces to reduce the net rate of radiation heat transfer between them without altering the surface temperatures of the primary emitter and receiver. The shield intercepts the radiant energy, partially absorbs and partially re-emits it, and by thermodynamic energy balance reduces the net heat flux exchanged.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMET503 / Module 3):** Radiation shields are thin parallel plates interposed between two large parallel surfaces to reduce radiation heat transfer. The analysis assumes the shields are opaque, thin (negligible thickness), and oriented parallel to the bounding surfaces so that view factors remain unity.

The **net radiation heat transfer** between two infinite parallel plates without any shield is given by the classic Stefan–Boltzmann relation modified by emissivity:

$$q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma \left(T_1^4 - T_2^4\right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

where $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ is the **Stefan–Boltzmann constant**, and $\varepsilon$ is the **emissivity** (dimensionless, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sitting near a campfire. The direct radiant heat is intense. Now imagine placing a thin, polished aluminum sheet (a shiny plate) between yourself and the fire. The aluminum does not let most of the heat pass through; it reflects the radiant energy back toward the fire while emitting very little of its own. Your skin receives a drastically reduced amount of thermal radiation.

In this analogy:
- **You** = the cold receiving surface ($T_2$).
- **The fire** = the hot emitting surface ($T_1$).
- **The aluminum sheet** = the **radiation shield**.

The shield does not eliminate radiation; instead, it forces the energy to be **re-radiated, absorbed, and re-emitted multiple times** between itself and the neighboring surfaces. Each time, a fraction is rejected, and a fraction is sent forward — the net result being a significantly reduced flux reaching the cold side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Insight:** A radiation shield does **not** need to be a perfect reflector ($\varepsilon = 0$); even a shield with moderate emissivity (e.g., $\varepsilon = 0.05$ for polished aluminum) can reduce heat transfer by an order of magnitude when placed between two highly emitting surfaces.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8}}$ | $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |
| Emissivity of polished aluminum | $\varepsilon$ | 0.03 – 0.10 | dimensionless |
| Emissivity of polished copper | $\varepsilon$ | 0.03 – 0.05 | dimensionless |
| Emissivity of oxidized steel | $\varepsilon$ | 0.60 – 0.80 | dimensionless |
| Emissivity of blackbody | $\varepsilon$ | **1.00** | dimensionless |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Why a Shield Works

The radiation shield works because the net heat flux between any two large parallel diffuse-gray surfaces is governed by a **surface resistance** that depends on emissivity. Placing a shield introduces an **additional resistance in series** in the radiation network.

The logic unfolds as follows:
- **Step 1 — Radiation emission:** The hot surface $T_1$ emits total radiation $\sigma T_1^4$.
- **Step 2 — First interception:** The shield absorbs a fraction $\varepsilon_3$ and reflects a fraction $\rho_3 = 1 - \alpha_3 \approx 1 - \varepsilon_3$ (for opaque diffuse-gray surfaces, $\alpha = \varepsilon$).
- **Step 3 — Shield re-emission:** The shield, once at its steady-state temperature $T_3$, re-emits $\varepsilon_3 \sigma T_3^4$ on both faces.
- **Step 4 — Series resistance:** The two bounding surfaces (emitter and receiver) "see" an extra series radiative resistance equal to $1/(\varepsilon_3 \sigma)$ from the shield.
- **Step 5 — Net result:** Heat flux drops by a factor approximately equal to $(N+1)$ if all emissivities are equal.

## 2.2 The Radiation Network Analogy

The electrical-resistance analogy treats radiative heat transfer as a current flowing through a series of resistances driven by a "voltage" equal to $\sigma T^4$.

| Electrical Quantity | Radiative Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Voltage $V$ | Emissive power $\sigma T^4$ |
| Current $I$ | Heat flux $q$ |
| Resistance $R$ | $\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon}{\varepsilon A \sigma}$ (surface) or $\dfrac{1}{A F_{ij} \sigma}$ (space) |
| Series combination | Radiation shields (additional resistance) |
| Parallel combination | Surfaces facing multiple other surfaces |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Scenario | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two infinite parallel plates (no shield) | $q = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$ | Standard result |
| 2 | With **one** shield (emissivity $\varepsilon_3$) | $q_{1 \to 2} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_3} - 2}$ | $T_3$ cancels in steady state |
| 3 | With **N** identical shields ($\varepsilon_s$) | $q_{1 \to (N+2)} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_{N+2}^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{N+2}} + \dfrac{2N}{\varepsilon_s} - (N+1)}$ | General form |
| 4 | All emissivities equal ($\varepsilon$) | $q_{\text{with N shields}} = \dfrac{q_{\text{no shield}}}{N + 1}$ | **Most-cited KTU result** |
| 5 | Shield temperature (equal $\varepsilon$, one shield) | $T_3^4 = \dfrac{T_1^4 + T_2^4}{2}$ | Shield temp arithmetic mean of fourth powers |
| 6 | Temperature of $k^{\text{th}}$ shield (equal $\varepsilon$, $N$ shields) | $T_{k+1}^4 = \dfrac{T_1^4 + T_{N+2}^4}{2}$ for equal emissivities — actually, $T_{k+1}^4 = \dfrac{T_1^4 + T_{N+2}^4}{2}$ for $N=1$ | General: $T_{k+1}^4 = T_1^4 - \dfrac{2k}{N+1}(T_1^4 - T_{N+2}^4)$ for equal $\varepsilon$ |
| 7 | Radiation resistance per surface | $R_i = \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_i}{\varepsilon_i A \sigma}$ | Used in network analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Constraint on Formulas:** All $\varepsilon$ values are dimensionless and bounded $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$. View factor $F_{ij} = 1$ for infinite parallel plates. The shields are assumed **opaque, thin, and equidistant** so that geometric view factors remain unity between every adjacent pair.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

Radiation shields are used pervasively in industry:
- **Cryogenic storage tanks** — multi-layer insulation (MLI) with 30–60 alternating aluminized Mylar sheets and low-conductivity spacers reduces radiative boil-off of liquid nitrogen or helium.
- **Furnace and boiler walls** — reflective stainless steel baffles reduce back-face heat loss.
- **Spacecraft thermal control** — multi-foil insulation wraps protect instruments from solar radiation.
- **Nuclear reactor shields** — though these are largely for ionizing radiation, similar logic applies for thermal radiation.
- **Building insulation** — reflective foil layers in walls and roofs reduce summer heat gain.
- **Firefighter suits and astronaut suits** — reflective outer layers block external radiation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heat Transfer Without a Shield (Baseline)

Consider two infinite parallel plates, surface 1 (hot) at $T_1$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_1$, and surface 2 (cold) at $T_2$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_2$. View factor $F_{12} = 1$.

**Step 1:** Surface 1 emits radiosity (emitted + reflected radiation) leaving surface 1.

$$J_1 = \varepsilon_1 \sigma T_1^4 + \rho_1 \, G_1$$

where $G_1$ is the irradiation on surface 1, and $\rho_1 = 1 - \varepsilon_1$ (opaque gray).

**Step 2:** For two infinite parallel plates, all radiation leaving one reaches the other: $G_1 = J_2$ and $G_2 = J_1$.

**Step 3:** Net heat flux from surface 1 to surface 2:

$$q_{1 \to 2} = J_1 - J_2$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and solve the linear system. After algebra (rearranging the radiosity equations for both surfaces):

$$q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma \left( T_1^4 - T_2^4 \right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

This is the famous **radiative heat transfer equation for two infinite parallel plates**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Heat Transfer With One Radiation Shield

Now introduce a thin opaque shield (surface 3) at temperature $T_3$ with emissivity $\varepsilon_3$ between surfaces 1 and 2. Assume all view factors equal 1, surfaces are infinite and parallel.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the shield (steady state):** No net heat is stored in the thin shield, so the flux from surface 1 to the shield equals the flux from the shield to surface 2.

$$q_{1 \to 3} = q_{3 \to 2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the two-plate formula twice.** Between 1 and 3:

$$q_{1 \to 3} = \frac{\sigma \left( T_1^4 - T_3^4 \right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1}$$

Between 3 and 2:

$$q_{3 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma \left( T_3^4 - T_2^4 \right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 3 — Set them equal and eliminate $T_3$.** Equating the two fluxes:

$$\frac{T_1^4 - T_3^4}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1} = \frac{T_3^4 - T_2^4}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $q = q_{1 \to 2}$ by summing the two temperature drops divided by the sum of resistances.** Recognizing this as two thermal resistances in series, the total resistance is the sum of denominators:

$$R_{\text{total}} = \left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1\right) + \left(\frac{1}{\varepsilon_3} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right) = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{2}{\varepsilon_3} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 2$$

Therefore:

$$q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma \left( T_1^4 - T_2^4 \right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_3} - 2}$$

**Step 5 — Special case where all emissivities are equal** ($\varepsilon_1 = \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_3 = \varepsilon$):

$$q_{\text{1 shield}} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{2 \left( \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1 \right)} = \frac{q_{\text{no shield}}}{2}$$

So **one shield halves the heat transfer** if all surfaces have equal emissivity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Generalization to N Identical Shields

By induction, with $N$ shields of emissivity $\varepsilon_s$ and bounding surfaces of emissivity $\varepsilon_1, \varepsilon_{N+2}$:

$$q_{1 \to (N+2)} = \frac{\sigma \left( T_1^4 - T_{N+2}^4 \right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{N+2}} + \dfrac{2N}{\varepsilon_s} - (N+1)}$$

**Special case** — all emissivities equal to $\varepsilon$:

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_{N+2}} + \frac{2N}{\varepsilon_s} - (N+1) = \frac{2}{\varepsilon} + \frac{2N}{\varepsilon} - (N+1) = \frac{N+1}{\varepsilon} \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \left(\frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right) \text{(simplifies to)} \, (N+1)\left(\frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)$$

Hence:

$$q_{N \text{ shields}} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(N+1)\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)} = \frac{q_{\text{no shield}}}{N+1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this KTU result:** *N identical radiation shields reduce the net heat transfer by a factor of $(N+1)$, provided all surface emissivities are equal.*

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Shield Temperature for the Equal-Emissivity Case

For $N$ identical shields between surfaces 1 and 2, the temperature distribution along the stack follows a **linear interpolation of $T^4$**:

$$T_{k+1}^4 = T_1^4 - \frac{2k}{N+1}\left( T_1^4 - T_{N+2}^4 \right)$$

**Derivation:** The heat flux is constant through each gap:

$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_{k+1}^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{k+1}} - 1} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_{N+2}^4)}{(N+1)\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)}$$

With equal emissivities and the identity above, this gives:

$$\frac{T_1^4 - T_{k+1}^4}{\frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1} = \frac{T_1^4 - T_{N+2}^4}{(N+1)\left(\frac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)}$$

Cancelling the common factor $\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)$:

$$T_1^4 - T_{k+1}^4 = \frac{T_1^4 - T_{N+2}^4}{N+1} \cdot 2k \cdot \frac{1}{1} \quad \text{(rearranged)}$$

which after rearrangement yields the stated interpolation formula.

**For $N = 1$ (single shield, equal $\varepsilon$):**

$$T_3^4 = T_1^4 - \frac{2 \cdot 1}{2}(T_1^4 - T_2^4) = T_1^4 - (T_1^4 - T_2^4) = \frac{T_1^4 + T_2^4}{2}$$

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem:** Two large parallel plates are at $T_1 = 800\,\text{K}$ and $T_2 = 400\,\text{K}$. Their emissivities are $\varepsilon_1 = 0.6$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.8$. Calculate (a) the net heat transfer rate without a shield, and (b) the net heat transfer rate with one polished aluminum shield ($\varepsilon_3 = 0.05$).

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — No shield:**

$$q_{\text{no shield}} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

Compute $T_1^4 = 800^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11}\,\text{K}^4$.
Compute $T_2^4 = 400^4 = 2.56 \times 10^{10}\,\text{K}^4$.

$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 4.096 \times 10^{11} - 2.56 \times 10^{10} = 3.840 \times 10^{11}\,\text{K}^4$$

$$\frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1 = \frac{1}{0.6} + \frac{1}{0.8} - 1 = 1.6667 + 1.25 - 1 = 1.9167$$

$$q_{\text{no shield}} = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.840 \times 10^{11}}{1.9167} = \frac{21{,}773}{1.9167} = 11{,}358\,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Part (b) — With one shield ($\varepsilon_3 = 0.05$):**

$$\text{Denominator} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \frac{2}{\varepsilon_3} - 2 = 1.6667 + 1.25 + 40 - 2 = 40.9167$$

$$q_{\text{1 shield}} = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.840 \times 10^{11}}{40.9167} = \frac{21{,}773}{40.9167} = 532\,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Reduction factor:** $q_{\text{no shield}} / q_{\text{1 shield}} = 11{,}358 / 532 \approx 21.4$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single thin polished-aluminum shield (with $\varepsilon_3 = 0.05$) reduces radiation heat transfer by a factor of about 21 — even though the bounding surfaces are quite emissive. This dramatic effect is precisely why low-emissivity shields are ubiquitous in thermal-insulation engineering.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Radiation Shield System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["HOT PLATE T1 eps1"] -->|emits sigma T1^4| nodeB["GAP 1 VIEW FACTOR 1"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["SHIELD T3 eps3"]
    nodeC -->|absorbs fraction eps3 reflects 1-eps3| nodeD["GAP 2 VIEW FACTOR 1"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["COLD PLATE T2 eps2"]
    nodeE -->|absorbs eps2 sigma T2^4| nodeF["NET HEAT FLUX q12"]
    nodeC -->|re-emits eps3 sigma T3^4 both faces| nodeB
    nodeC -->|re-emits eps3 sigma T3^4 both faces| nodeD
    nodeG["RADIATION NETWORK"] --> nodeH["R1 1 minus eps1 over eps1 A sigma"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["R3 1 minus eps3 over eps3 A sigma"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["R2 1 minus eps2 over eps2 A sigma"]
    nodeJ --> nodeF
```

## 4.2 Temperature Distribution Profile (N-Shield Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["T1 hot plate"] -->|"q = const"| node2["T_k+1 shield k"]
    node2 -->|"q = const"| node3["T more shield"]
    node3 -->|"q = const"| node4["T_N+2 cold plate"]
    node2 -.->|"T^4 linear interpolation"| node5["T^4 distribution linear"]
    node3 -.->|"T^4 linear interpolation"| node5
    node5 --> node6["4th power drops linearly across the stack"]
```

## 4.3 Series-Resistance Network Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["sigma T1^4 emissive power source"] --> nodeR1["R1 1 minus eps1 over eps1 A sigma"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeR3["R3 shield 1 minus eps3 over eps3 A sigma"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeR2["R2 1 minus eps2 over eps2 A sigma"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeB["sigma T2^4 sink"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Single loop series circuit q = V minus V sink divided by sum of R"]
```

## 4.4 Shield Effect Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Two parallel plates with hot T1 and cold T2"] --> q1["Compute q no shield"]
    q1 --> decision{"Need to reduce q?"}
    decision -->|No| endA["No shield required"]
    decision -->|Yes| shieldChoice["Choose shield material"]
    shieldChoice -->|"Aluminum eps = 0.05"| s1["Single shield"]
    shieldChoice -->|"Polished copper eps = 0.03"| s2["More efficient single shield"]
    shieldChoice -->|"Multiple MLI layers"| s3["N shields factor of N plus 1 reduction"]
    s1 --> check1["Verify temperature limits of shield material"]
    s2 --> check1
    s3 --> check1
    check1 --> endB["Final design with q new = q no shield divided by N plus 1"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]
**Define a radiation shield. Why is a low-emissivity material preferred for the shield?**

**Model Answer:**
A **radiation shield** is a thin opaque plate placed between two large radiating surfaces to reduce the net radiative heat transfer without altering the bounding surface temperatures.

A low-emissivity material is preferred because the **radiation resistance** introduced by the shield is $R_s = (1 - \varepsilon_s) / (\varepsilon_s A \sigma)$. As $\varepsilon_s \to 0$, the resistance grows very large, so the **net heat transfer is significantly reduced**. For example, polished aluminum ($\varepsilon_s \approx 0.05$) introduces roughly 20 times the resistance of a black surface ($\varepsilon = 1$).

> **Marking scheme:** 2 marks for definition, 1 mark for emissivity justification.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]
**For two large parallel plates with equal emissivities, derive the ratio of net heat transfer with $N$ radiation shields to that without any shield.**

**Model Answer:**

Without a shield:
$$q_0 = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1}$$

With $N$ shields (equal $\varepsilon$):
$$q_N = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{(N+1)\left(\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1\right)}$$

Taking the ratio:
$$\frac{q_N}{q_0} = \frac{1}{N+1}$$

> **Marking scheme:** 1 mark for stating $q_0$, 1 mark for stating $q_N$, 1 mark for the final ratio.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for net radiation heat transfer between two large parallel plates with **one radiation shield** in between. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two large parallel plates are at temperatures $T_1 = 1000\,\text{K}$ and $T_2 = 500\,\text{K}$, with emissivities $\varepsilon_1 = 0.7$ and $\varepsilon_2 = 0.5$. A single radiation shield of polished aluminum ($\varepsilon_3 = 0.05$) is placed between them. Calculate the percentage reduction in heat transfer. Also find the shield temperature. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Assumptions:**
1. Surfaces are infinite and parallel; view factors $F_{12} = F_{13} = F_{32} = 1$.
2. Surfaces are opaque, diffuse, and gray ($\alpha = \varepsilon$).
3. Shield is thin, so it has negligible thermal capacity and no temperature gradient across its thickness.
4. Steady state, no convection or conduction between the gaps.

**Derivation:**

Energy balance on the shield (steady state, no storage):

$$q_{1 \to 3} = q_{3 \to 2} = q$$

Apply the two-plate formula between 1–3 and 3–2:

$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_3^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1} = \frac{\sigma (T_3^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$

Adding the two numerator terms and the two denominator terms (resistances in series):

$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1\right) + \left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_3} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1\right)}$$

$$\boxed{q_{1 \to 2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_3} - 2}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Heat transfer without shield:**

$$T_1^4 = (1000)^4 = 1.0 \times 10^{12}\,\text{K}^4$$
$$T_2^4 = (500)^4 = 6.25 \times 10^{10}\,\text{K}^4$$
$$T_1^4 - T_2^4 = 9.375 \times 10^{11}\,\text{K}^4$$

$$q_0 = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.375 \times 10^{11}}{\dfrac{1}{0.7} + \dfrac{1}{0.5} - 1} = \frac{53{,}156}{1.4286 + 2 - 1} = \frac{53{,}156}{2.4286} = 21{,}887\,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer with one shield ($\varepsilon_3 = 0.05$):**

$$\text{Denominator} = \frac{1}{0.7} + \frac{1}{0.5} + \frac{2}{0.05} - 2 = 1.4286 + 2 + 40 - 2 = 41.4286$$

$$q_1 = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 9.375 \times 10^{11}}{41.4286} = \frac{53{,}156}{41.4286} = 1283\,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Percentage reduction:**

$$\% \text{ reduction} = \frac{21{,}887 - 1283}{21{,}887} \times 100\% = 94.14\%$$

**Step 4 — Shield temperature:**

For unequal emissivities, we solve for $T_3^4$ using $q_{1 \to 3} = q$:

$$T_1^4 - T_3^4 = q_1 \left( \frac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_3} - 1 \right) = 1283 \times (1.4286 + 20 - 1)$$

$$T_1^4 - T_3^4 = 1283 \times 20.4286 = 26{,}210$$

$$T_3^4 = 1.0 \times 10^{12} - 2.621 \times 10^{4} \cdot 10^0 = 9.9997 \times 10^{11}$$

$$T_3 \approx 999.9\,\text{K} \approx 1000\,\text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The shield sits very close to the hot plate because $\varepsilon_1 = 0.7$ is moderate and $\varepsilon_3 = 0.05$ is very small — most radiation from the hot plate is reflected back, so the shield barely absorbs energy and stays near $T_1$.

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **electrical network analogy** for radiation heat transfer. Show that a single shield between two surfaces acts as an additional series resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cryogenic storage vessel uses 40 layers of super-insulation (MLI) between its inner ($T_1 = 80\,\text{K}$) and outer ($T_2 = 300\,\text{K}$) walls. Each layer has emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.05$. The bounding walls also have $\varepsilon = 0.05$. Determine: (i) the heat transfer with and without the MLI per unit area, and (ii) the temperature of the 10th, 20th, and 30th layer from the cold side. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Network Analogy Rules:**
- Each radiosity $J_i$ acts like a **node voltage**.
- Each surface resistance $R_i = (1 - \varepsilon_i)/(\varepsilon_i A \sigma)$ acts like a series resistor.
- Space (geometric) resistance between two surfaces $i$ and $j$ is $R_{ij} = 1/(A F_{ij} \sigma)$.
- A **current** (heat flux) flows from high $J$ node to low $J$ node.

**Without a shield:** The two surfaces form a series loop:

$$q = \frac{\sigma T_1^4 - \sigma T_2^4}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1-\varepsilon_1}{\varepsilon_1 A \sigma} + \dfrac{1-\varepsilon_2}{\varepsilon_2 A \sigma}} = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1} \cdot A$$

**With one shield:** The shield node $J_3$ lies between the two surface nodes, so the resistors are in **series**:

$$q = \frac{\sigma T_1^4 - \sigma T_2^4}{R_1 + R_3 + R_2}$$

The shield therefore adds $R_3 = (1-\varepsilon_3)/(\varepsilon_3 A \sigma)$ to the denominator, increasing total resistance and reducing $q$. The result is identical to the formula derived earlier.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Given data:** $N = 40$ shields, all emissivities $\varepsilon = 0.05$, $T_1 = 80\,\text{K}$ (cold inner wall), $T_2 = 300\,\text{K}$ (warm outer wall).

**Step 2 — Heat transfer without MLI:**

$$q_0 = \frac{\sigma (T_2^4 - T_1^4)}{\dfrac{2}{\varepsilon} - 1}$$

$$T_2^4 = (300)^4 = 8.1 \times 10^9\,\text{K}^4$$
$$T_1^4 = (80)^4 = 4.096 \times 10^7\,\text{K}^4$$
$$T_2^4 - T_1^4 \approx 8.059 \times 10^9\,\text{K}^4$$

$$\frac{2}{0.05} - 1 = 40 - 1 = 39$$

$$q_0 = \frac{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.059 \times 10^9}{39} = \frac{457.0}{39} = 11.72\,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Heat transfer with 40 shields** (equal $\varepsilon$):

$$q_{40} = \frac{q_0}{N + 1} = \frac{11.72}{41} = 0.286\,\text{W/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Temperature distribution.** Indexing from the cold side (surface 1 at $T_1$), the $k^{\text{th}}$ shield temperature (counting shield 1 as the one closest to the cold wall) is given by:

$$T_{k+1}^4 = T_1^4 + \frac{2k}{N+1}\left( T_2^4 - T_1^4 \right)$$

For $k = 10, 20, 30$ (counting from cold side):

- **10th layer from cold side** ($k = 10$):
$$T_{11}^4 = 4.096 \times 10^7 + \frac{20}{41}(8.059 \times 10^9) = 4.096 \times 10^7 + 3.931 \times 10^9 = 3.972 \times 10^9$$
$$T_{11} = (3.972 \times 10^9)^{1/4} = 251.0\,\text{K}$$

- **20th layer from cold side** ($k = 20$):
$$T_{21}^4 = 4.096 \times 10^7 + \frac{40}{41}(8.059 \times 10^9) = 4.096 \times 10^7 + 7.862 \times 10^9 = 7.903 \times 10^9$$
$$T_{21} = (7.903 \times 10^9)^{1/4} = 298.1\,\text{K}$$

- **30th layer from cold side** ($k = 30$):
$$T_{31}^4 = 4.096 \times 10^7 + \frac{60}{41}(8.059 \times 10^9) = 4.096 \times 10^7 + 1.179 \times 10^{10} = 1.183 \times 10^{10}$$
$$T_{31} = (1.183 \times 10^{10})^{1/4} = 330.4\,\text{K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** The 40th shield should be at $T_{41}^4 = T_2^4 = 8.1 \times 10^9$, i.e., $T_{41} = 300\,\text{K}$ — exactly the outer wall temperature, confirming the formula. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in front of $1/\varepsilon_s$** when using the shield formula. The denominator must contain $2/\varepsilon_3$ (because the shield has **two** faces participating in the exchange), not $1/\varepsilon_3$.
> 2. **Mixing up $T^4$ and $T$ in temperature interpolation.** The shield temperature is found by interpolating $T^4$ **linearly**, not $T$.
> 3. **Applying the $(N+1)$ shortcut when emissivities differ.** The shortcut $\, q_{\text{with N shields}} = q_{\text{no shield}}/(N+1)\,$ is **only valid when all surface emissivities are equal**. Use the full formula otherwise.
> 4. **Forgetting the assumption of view factor = 1.** Radiation shields analysis (as taught in KTU Module 3) assumes large parallel plates. For other geometries, the network must include geometric (space) resistances $1/(A F_{ij} \sigma)$.
> 5. **Unit errors.** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ and $T$ in **Kelvin**. Mixing Celsius will cause a 273-offset catastrophe.
> 6. **Sign errors** when the cold side is at higher temperature. The flux direction is from the high-$T^4$ side to the low-$T^4$ side; the formula is symmetric in $|T_1^4 - T_2^4|$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A radiation shield is a thin opaque plate placed between two radiating surfaces to **reduce net radiation heat transfer** by introducing additional radiative resistance.
- **Core formula (no shield):**
$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} - 1}$$
- **Core formula (one shield):**
$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_2} + \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon_3} - 2}$$
- **Core formula (N shields):**
$$q = \frac{\sigma (T_1^4 - T_2^4)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_1} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{N+2}} + \dfrac{2N}{\varepsilon_s} - (N+1)}$$
- **Memorize:** For **equal emissivities**, the heat transfer is divided by $(N+1)$:
$$q_N = \frac{q_0}{N+1}$$
- **Shield temperature formula (equal $\varepsilon$):**
$$T_{k+1}^4 = T_1^4 + \frac{2k}{N+1}\left( T_2^4 - T_1^4 \right)$$
- **Lower emissivity of shield = greater heat-transfer reduction.** Polished aluminum ($\varepsilon \approx 0.05$) is a typical engineering choice.
- **Steady-state assumption** is mandatory — the shield has no thermal storage, so flux in = flux out.
- **Electrical analogy:** $J = \sigma T^4$ is the "voltage"; $R = (1 - \varepsilon)/(\varepsilon A \sigma)$ is the "resistance"; flux is the "current." Shields are **series resistors** in this network.
- **Real-world usage:** Cryogenic MLI (multi-layer insulation), spacecraft thermal blankets, furnace baffles, building foil insulation, fire-protective garments.
- **Common pitfall:** The factor of **2** in front of $1/\varepsilon_s$ — it accounts for both faces of the shield.
- **Unit discipline:** $T$ in K, $\sigma$ in $\text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, $q$ in $\text{W/m}^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conduction Shape Factor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conduction Shape Factor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conduction Shape Factor (S)** is a purely **geometric parameter** that relates the steady-state, one-dimensional or two-dimensional conductive heat transfer rate between two isothermal surfaces to the temperature difference between them and the thermal conductivity of the medium.

The general governing relation is:

$$Q = S \cdot k \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- **Q** = Steady-state heat transfer rate (**W**)
- **S** = Conduction shape factor (**m** for 1D problems, dimensionless for 2D plane geometries)
- **k** = Thermal conductivity of the conducting medium (**W/m·K**)
- **ΔT** = Temperature difference between the two isothermal surfaces (**K** or °C**)

The shape factor is **independent of the actual temperatures** involved and depends **only on the geometry** of the system and the spatial arrangement of the isothermal boundaries.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> Think of a shape factor as the **"geometric amplifier"** for heat flow — just like an electrical conductor's conductance (G = 1/R) determines how easily current flows, the shape factor S determines how easily heat can flow between two surfaces of given shape and position.

**Real-World Analogy – The Funnel Metaphor:**
Imagine pouring water (heat) from one container into another. A wide, short funnel lets the water pour through quickly (high shape factor). A narrow, long, twisted funnel restricts the flow (low shape factor). The funnel's *shape* — not the water's *amount* or *temperature* — controls the rate. Similarly, the *shape* of the conducting geometry controls how much heat can flow for a given temperature difference.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The shape factor is a powerful design tool — once you know S and k, the heat transfer rate is immediately known for *any* applied temperature difference, making it ideal for quick engineering estimates.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Range/Value |
|-----------|--------|------|-------------|
| Thermal Conductivity | k | W/m·K | 0.02 (insulators) – 400+ (metals) |
| Shape Factor | S | m or – | Geometry-dependent |
| Heat Transfer Rate | Q | W | 0 to ∞ |
| Temperature Difference | ΔT | K or °C | 0 to ∞ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The unit of the shape factor is **metres (m)** for axisymmetric/1D problems and **dimensionless** for two-dimensional plane geometries (per unit length into the page).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Logical Foundation — Why Shape Factor Exists

The general **Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction** in differential form is:

$$q'' = -k \nabla T$$

Integrating this over a control volume bounded by two isothermal surfaces (T₁ and T₂) gives:

$$Q = k \int_S (- \nabla T \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$

For simple geometries, this integral reduces to a closed-form expression of the form:

$$Q = k \cdot \Delta T \cdot [\text{function of geometry only}]$$

The bracketed function is the **shape factor S**. Thus, S encapsulates the **integrated geometric resistance** of the conductive path.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

- **Step 1:** Identify the two isothermal surfaces (T₁ and T₂) bounding the conducting medium.
- **Step 2:** Apply Fourier's Law and integrate along the heat flow path (using Gauss divergence theorem or direct integration).
- **Step 3:** Group all geometric terms together; the proportionality constant between Q, k, and ΔT is the shape factor S.
- **Step 4:** Express S purely in terms of lengths, areas, radii, angles — **never in terms of temperature**.
- **Step 5:** Use the resulting expression for Q in design and analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Matters:** Shape factor is essential when geometry is complex (corners, eccentric arrangements, buried objects) and direct integration becomes intractable. Pre-computed S values from charts/tables make such problems trivial.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Shape Factor Expressions for KTU University Exams**

| Geometry | Shape Factor (S) | Remarks |
|----------|------------------|---------|
| Two large parallel planes (area A, spacing L) | $S = A / L$ | 1D plane wall |
| Long concentric cylinders (length L) | $S = 2\pi L / \ln(r_2 / r_1)$ | $r_2 > r_1$ |
| Concentric spheres | $S = 4\pi r_1 r_2 / (r_2 - r_1)$ | Equivalent to $4\pi / (1/r_1 - 1/r_2)$ |
| Isothermal cylinder buried in semi-infinite medium (depth D, radius r, D >> r) | $S = 2\pi L / \ln(2D / r)$ | Single buried pipe |
| Vertical wall to ground (wall footing, width W) | $S = 0.54 \, W$ | Per unit length |
| Edge of wall (3D corner) | $S = 0.54 \, W$ | Per unit length, 90° corner |
| Interior corner of three walls (3-way junction) | $S = 0.15 \, W$ | Per unit length |
| Isothermal cylinder of length L parallel to a plane (height z, L >> z) | $S = 2\pi L / \ln(2z / r)$ | z >> r |
| Two equal parallel cylinders (separation D, radius r) | $S = 2\pi L / \cosh^{-1}(D / 2r)$ | D > 2r |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Table-Writing Rule Applied:** All absolute value notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ form, and unit cells use the word "metres" to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Building Insulation Design:** Heat loss through wall corners, edges, and footings.
- **Underground Cable Sizing:** Heat dissipation from buried electrical cables.
- **Pipeline Networks:** Heat transfer between parallel steam pipes in trenches.
- **Nuclear Reactor Shielding:** Heat flow through irregular geometric components.
- **Heat Exchanger Manifolds:** Conduction between headers and tube bundles.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Two Large Parallel Planes (1D Wall)

**Starting Equation:** Fourier's Law for 1D steady conduction:

$$Q = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**
- At x = 0: T = T₁
- At x = L: T = T₂

**Integration Step:**

$$\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = -\frac{Q}{kA} \int_0^L dx$$

$$T_2 - T_1 = -\frac{Q \, L}{kA}$$

**Solving for Q:**

$$Q = \frac{kA}{L}(T_1 - T_2) = k \cdot \Delta T \cdot \frac{A}{L}$$

**Identifying the Shape Factor:**

$$\boxed{S = \frac{A}{L}}$$

> This is the simplest case — the shape factor equals the cross-sectional area divided by the path length.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Long Concentric Cylinders (Cylindrical Wall)

**Setup:** Inner cylinder radius r₁ at T₁, outer radius r₂ at T₂, length L (with L >> r₂ so end effects are negligible).

**Fourier's Law in Radial Form:**

$$Q = -k (2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Separation and Integration:**

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -2\pi k L \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$Q \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Solving for Q:**

$$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}$$

**Identifying the Shape Factor:**

$$\boxed{S = \frac{2\pi L}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}}$$

> This is the **most-tested shape factor in KTU exams** — memorize it cold.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Concentric Spheres (Spherical Wall)

**Setup:** Inner sphere radius r₁ at T₁, outer radius r₂ at T₂.

**Fourier's Law in Radial Form for Spheres:**

$$Q = -k (4\pi r^2) \frac{dT}{dr}$$

**Separation and Integration:**

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r^2} = -4\pi k \int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT$$

$$Q \left[-\frac{1}{r}\right]_{r_1}^{r_2} = -4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$

$$Q \left(\frac{1}{r_1} - \frac{1}{r_2}\right) = 4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)$$

**Solving for Q:**

$$Q = \frac{4\pi k (T_1 - T_2)}{(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)} = \frac{4\pi k \, r_1 r_2 (T_1 - T_2)}{(r_2 - r_1)}$$

**Identifying the Shape Factor:**

$$\boxed{S = \frac{4\pi r_1 r_2}{r_2 - r_1}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Isothermal Cylinder Buried in Semi-Infinite Medium

**Setup:** A long cylinder of radius r at temperature T_s is buried at depth D (measured from centre to ground surface) in a semi-infinite medium of thermal conductivity k. The surface is at temperature T₀.

**Key Physical Insight (Method of Images):**
Using the method of images, an identical *cooling* cylinder is placed symmetrically at distance D above the surface, creating an effective two-cylinder problem.

**For Two Cylinders Separated by 2D (equal radii r):**

The shape factor for two equal parallel cylinders of radius r separated by distance 2D is:

$$S_{\text{pair}} = \frac{2\pi L}{\cosh^{-1}(D / r)}$$

For the buried case, the *single* cylinder's heat flow to the surface is half of the pair's heat flow (by symmetry, since the isothermal plane bisects the two cylinders):

$$S_{\text{single}} = \frac{S_{\text{pair}}}{2} = \frac{\pi L}{\cosh^{-1}(D / r)}$$

**Approximation for D >> r:** Using $\cosh^{-1}(D/r) \approx \ln(2D/r)$ for large D/r:

$$\boxed{S \approx \frac{2\pi L}{\ln(2D / r)}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Shape Factors

| Derivation | Key Integral Solved | Final S | Limitation |
|------------|---------------------|---------|------------|
| 1D Plane Wall | $\int dx$ | $A/L$ | Edge effects neglected |
| Cylindrical Wall | $\int dr/r$ | $2\pi L / \ln(r_2/r_1)$ | L >> r₂ |
| Spherical Wall | $\int dr/r^2$ | $4\pi r_1 r_2 / (r_2 - r_1)$ | Concentric |
| Buried Cylinder | Method of images | $2\pi L / \ln(2D/r)$ | D >> r |
| Eccentric Cylinders | Conformal mapping | $2\pi L / \cosh^{-1}\!\left[\frac{D^2 - r_1^2 - r_2^2}{2 r_1 r_2}\right]$ | Numerical in general |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hint:** For KTU problems with eccentric cylinders, the formula in the bottom row appears verbatim in the KTU Heat Transfer Data Book. Always cross-check with the data book in the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shape Factor Methodology

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete analytical procedure for solving a heat conduction problem using shape factors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Geometry and Boundaries] --> B{Geometry Type?}
    B --> C1[1D Plane Wall]
    B --> C2[Cylindrical/Concentric Cylinders]
    B --> C3[Spherical/Concentric Spheres]
    B --> C4[Complex 2D/3D]
    C4 --> D[Use Method of Images or Numerical Solution]
    D --> E[Compute Shape Factor S]
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    E --> F[Identify Thermal Conductivity k of Medium]
    F --> G[Determine Temperature Difference Delta T]
    G --> H[Apply Governing Equation Q equals S times k times Delta T]
    H --> I[Compute Heat Transfer Rate Q]
    I --> J[End: Validate Against Energy Balance]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Shape Factor Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SF[Conduction Shape Factor S] --> G1[One Dimensional]
    SF --> G2[Two Dimensional]
    SF --> G3[Three Dimensional]
    G1 --> P1[Parallel Planes]
    G1 --> P2[Concentric Cylinders]
    G1 --> P3[Concentric Spheres]
    G2 --> Q1[Buried Cylinder]
    G2 --> Q2[Wall Corners and Edges]
    G2 --> Q3[Parallel Cylinders]
    G3 --> R1[Three Way Wall Junctions]
    R1 --> R2[Underground Cable Spheres]
    R1 --> R3[Complex Manifold Geometries]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for KTU Numerical Problem Solving

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[KTU Exam Problem on Conduction] --> Step1[Identify Two Isothermal Surfaces]
    Step1 --> Step2{Type of Geometry}
    Step2 --> CaseA[Two Parallel Plates]
    Step2 --> CaseB[Concentric Cylinders]
    Step2 --> CaseC[Concentric Spheres]
    Step2 --> CaseD[Buried Pipe in Soil]
    Step2 --> CaseE[Wall Footing or Corner]
    CaseA --> ApplyA[Use S equals A over L]
    CaseB --> ApplyB[Use S equals 2 pi L over ln r2 over r1]
    CaseC --> ApplyC[Use S equals 4 pi r1 r2 over r2 minus r1]
    CaseD --> ApplyD[Use S equals 2 pi L over ln 2D over r]
    CaseE --> ApplyE[Use Tabulated Multiplier times W]
    ApplyA --> Final[Compute Q equals S k Delta T]
    ApplyB --> Final
    ApplyC --> Final
    ApplyD --> Final
    ApplyE --> Final
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "conduction shape factor" and state its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**
The conduction shape factor S is a purely geometric parameter that relates the steady-state heat transfer rate between two isothermal surfaces to the temperature difference and the thermal conductivity of the medium, through the relation **Q = S·k·ΔT**.

**Physical Significance:**
- It is **independent of temperature** and depends only on geometry.
- It quantifies the integrated geometric resistance of the conduction path.
- A higher shape factor means heat can flow more easily for a given ΔT.
- It allows direct calculation of Q without solving the differential heat equation afresh for each new temperature condition.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Derive the shape factor for a long concentric cylindrical geometry.**

**Model Answer:**

For long concentric cylinders (length L, inner radius r₁, outer radius r₂):

$$Q = -k(2\pi r L)\frac{dT}{dr}$$

Integrating from r₁ to r₂ with T(r₁) = T₁ and T(r₂) = T₂:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}$$

Comparing with **Q = S·k·ΔT**:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{2\pi L}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}}$$

The unit is **metres**, and the result is valid when L >> r₂ (end effects negligible).

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**A steam pipe of outer diameter 80 mm carrying steam at 200 °C is buried at a depth of 1.2 m in soil of thermal conductivity 1.5 W/m·K. The surface of the soil is at 25 °C. Estimate the heat loss per metre length of the pipe.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Outer radius: r = 0.040 m
- Depth of burial (centre to surface): D = 1.2 m
- Pipe surface temperature: T_s = 200 °C
- Soil surface temperature: T_0 = 25 °C
- Thermal conductivity: k = 1.5 W/m·K
- Length: L = 1 m

**Step 1: Verify the Geometric Assumption**
D / r = 1.2 / 0.040 = 30
Since D >> r (ratio > 10), the approximation is valid.

**Step 2: Apply the Buried Cylinder Shape Factor**

$$S = \frac{2\pi L}{\ln(2D / r)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$S = \frac{2\pi (1)}{\ln(2 \times 1.2 / 0.040)} = \frac{2\pi}{\ln(60)}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate Logarithm**

$$\ln(60) = 4.0943$$

**Step 5: Compute S**

$$S = \frac{6.2832}{4.0943} = 1.5344 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Compute Heat Loss**

$$Q = S \cdot k \cdot \Delta T = 1.5344 \times 1.5 \times (200 - 25)$$

$$Q = 1.5344 \times 1.5 \times 175 = 402.78 \text{ W per metre}$$

**Final Answer:** **Q ≈ 403 W per metre length of pipe.**

---

#### (a) Sub-part (7 marks) — Alternative Geometry: **Concentric Spherical Tank**

**A spherical steam storage tank has inner diameter 2 m and outer diameter 2.2 m. The inner surface is at 180 °C and the outer surface is at 40 °C. The wall thermal conductivity is 45 W/m·K. Find the heat loss per hour.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- r₁ = 1.0 m, r₂ = 1.1 m
- T₁ = 180 °C, T₂ = 40 °C
- k = 45 W/m·K

**Step 1: Apply Spherical Shape Factor**

$$S = \frac{4\pi r_1 r_2}{r_2 - r_1}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$S = \frac{4\pi (1.0)(1.1)}{1.1 - 1.0} = \frac{4\pi (1.1)}{0.1} = 44\pi = 138.23 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Compute Heat Loss Rate**

$$Q = S \cdot k \cdot \Delta T = 138.23 \times 45 \times (180 - 40) = 138.23 \times 45 \times 140$$

$$Q = 870{,}849 \text{ W} \approx 870.85 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Convert to Heat Loss per Hour**

$$Q_{hour} = 870.85 \text{ kJ/s} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} = 3{,}135{,}060 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 3135 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Final Answer:** **Heat loss ≈ 871 kW or 3135 MJ per hour.**

---

#### (b) Sub-part (7 marks) — **Wall-Footing Conduction Loss**

**A concrete wall of thickness 0.4 m extends from a floor slab. Estimate the heat transfer rate per metre length of the wall through the floor-footing junction if the wall surface is at 30 °C, the floor is at 15 °C, and the concrete k = 0.9 W/m·K.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Geometry — Wall-Footing Edge**
This is a 2D corner conduction problem. The shape factor is:

$$S = 0.54 \, W \text{ per unit length}$$

where W is the wall thickness.

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$S = 0.54 \times 0.4 = 0.216 \text{ m per metre length}$$

**Step 3: Compute Heat Transfer Rate**

$$Q = S \cdot k \cdot \Delta T = 0.216 \times 0.9 \times (30 - 15) = 0.216 \times 0.9 \times 15$$

$$Q = 2.916 \text{ W per metre length}$$

**Final Answer:** **Q ≈ 2.92 W per metre length of the wall.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A long cylindrical heating element of 20 mm diameter is placed horizontally at a height of 150 mm above a large concrete floor. The element surface is at 90 °C and the floor surface is at 25 °C. The intervening medium (air) has k = 0.025 W/m·K. Calculate the heat transfer rate per metre length using the shape factor method.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Cylinder radius: r = 0.010 m
- Height above floor: z = 0.150 m
- T_cylinder = 90 °C
- T_floor = 25 °C
- k = 0.025 W/m·K
- L = 1 m

**Step 1: Verify Geometry Assumption — z >> r**
z / r = 0.150 / 0.010 = 15
The condition z >> r is well satisfied.

**Step 2: Apply the Cylinder-Above-Plane Shape Factor**

$$S = \frac{2\pi L}{\ln(2z / r)}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Values**

$$S = \frac{2\pi (1)}{\ln(2 \times 0.150 / 0.010)} = \frac{2\pi}{\ln(30)}$$

**Step 4: Evaluate Logarithm**

$$\ln(30) = 3.4012$$

**Step 5: Compute S**

$$S = \frac{6.2832}{3.4012} = 1.8473 \text{ m}$$

**Step 6: Compute Heat Transfer Rate**

$$Q = S \cdot k \cdot \Delta T = 1.8473 \times 0.025 \times (90 - 25)$$

$$Q = 1.8473 \times 0.025 \times 65 = 3.002 \text{ W per metre}$$

**Final Answer:** **Q ≈ 3.0 W per metre length.**

---

#### (a) Sub-part (7 marks) — **Two Parallel Plates with Edge Loss**

**A furnace wall consists of two parallel plates, each of area 0.5 m², separated by 0.05 m of insulating material (k = 0.04 W/m·K). The hot plate is at 350 °C and the cold plate is at 50 °C. Compute the heat transfer rate.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Apply Parallel Plate Shape Factor**

$$S = \frac{A}{L} = \frac{0.5}{0.05} = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute Q**

$$Q = S \cdot k \cdot \Delta T = 10 \times 0.04 \times (350 - 50) = 10 \times 0.04 \times 300$$

$$Q = 120 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answer:** **Q = 120 W.**

---

#### (b) Sub-part (7 marks) — **Three-Wall Interior Corner Junction**

**Three identical walls of thickness 0.3 m meet at an interior corner. The temperature difference between the inner air and outer air is 25 °C. The wall material has k = 0.7 W/m·K. Determine the heat transfer rate per metre of wall length through the corner junction.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Geometry — Interior 3-Wall Corner**
For an interior corner where three walls meet (a 3-way junction in 2D), the shape factor is:

$$S = 0.15 \, W \text{ per unit length}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$S = 0.15 \times 0.3 = 0.045 \text{ m per metre length}$$

**Step 3: Compute Heat Transfer Rate**

$$Q = S \cdot k \cdot \Delta T = 0.045 \times 0.7 \times 25 = 0.7875 \text{ W per metre length}$$

**Final Answer:** **Q ≈ 0.79 W per metre length of wall.**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap (–1 mark):** Students often compute S in metres but forget to check whether the problem is per unit length or for a specified total length L. Always verify L explicitly.
>
> 2. **Approximation Validity Trap (–2 marks):** The buried-cylinder formula $S = 2\pi L / \ln(2D/r)$ is **only valid** when D >> r. If the ratio is small (D/r < 5), you must use the more general $\cosh^{-1}$ form. Examiners *specifically* check this.
>
> 3. **Logarithm Argument Trap (–1 to –2 marks):** In the buried-cylinder formula, the argument is $2D/r$ — **not** $D/r$. Confusing this leads to roughly 30% error in the final answer.
>
> 4. **Shape Factor Unit Trap (–1 mark):** For 2D plane geometries (walls, corners), the shape factor has no units. For axisymmetric or 1D problems with a length, the unit is metres. Mixing these up loses marks.
>
> 5. **Tabulated Values Trap (–1 mark):** For wall corners, footings, and buried pipes, KTU expects you to use the **exact numerical coefficient** from the data book (0.54, 0.15, etc.). Do not invent a coefficient.
>
> 6. **Missing ΔT Sign Convention (–1 mark):** Always present Q as a positive quantity with ΔT = T_hot − T_cold. Reporting negative Q without comment loses a mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Governing Equation:** $\boxed{Q = S \cdot k \cdot \Delta T}$ — the **master formula** for all shape factor problems.
- **Shape Factor is Geometry-Only:** It contains **no temperature dependence**; only lengths, areas, radii, and angles.
- **Two-D vs Three-D Distinction:** 
  - 2D plane problems (walls, corners) → dimensionless S or S per unit length.
  - 1D/axisymmetric problems (cylinders, spheres) → S in metres.
- **Most-Tested Formula:** $S = 2\pi L / \ln(r_2/r_1)$ — for concentric cylinders. **Memorize and derive cold.**
- **Spherical Formula:** $S = 4\pi r_1 r_2 / (r_2 - r_1)$ — derived by integrating $1/r^2$ over a sphere.
- **Buried Cylinder:** $S = 2\pi L / \ln(2D/r)$ — **valid only when D/r > 10**; uses the method of images.
- **2D Wall Corners (Standard Coefficients):**
  - Wall edge / footing: **0.54 W** per unit length.
  - Interior 3-way junction: **0.15 W** per unit length.
- **Parallel Cylinder Formula (General):** $S = 2\pi L / \cosh^{-1}(D/2r)$ for two equal cylinders.
- **Engineering Applications:** Building insulation, buried cables, furnace walls, nuclear shielding, pipeline trenches.
- **KTU Data Book:** For complex geometries, the official KTU Heat and Mass Transfer Data Book contains pre-computed shape factor values — **always cross-check** in the exam.
- **Symmetry Trick:** In problems with multiple identical heat sources/sinks, you can use superposition of shape factors (e.g., two buried pipes = sum of individual shape factors if far apart).
- **Typical KTU Numerical Setup:** Given diameter/depth/temperatures/k → compute S → compute Q = SkΔT. Three-step solution, three key formulas.
- **Sign Convention:** Q > 0 always; report ΔT explicitly as T_hot − T_cold.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Formula Card (Save for Exam Day):**
> 
> | # | Geometry | Shape Factor S |
> |---|----------|---------------|
> | 1 | Plane Wall | $A/L$ |
> | 2 | Concentric Cylinders | $2\pi L / \ln(r_2/r_1)$ |
> | 3 | Concentric Spheres | $4\pi r_1 r_2 / (r_2 - r_1)$ |
> | 4 | Buried Pipe (D >> r) | $2\pi L / \ln(2D/r)$ |
> | 5 | Cylinder above Plane (z >> r) | $2\pi L / \ln(2z/r)$ |
> | 6 | Wall Footing / Edge | $0.54 W$ |
> | 7 | 3-Wall Interior Junction | $0.15 W$ |

---

topics.title: Mass Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mass Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass Transfer** is the net movement of a chemical species from a region of higher concentration (chemical potential) to a region of lower concentration through a medium (gas, liquid, or solid) under the influence of a **concentration gradient**, **pressure gradient**, **temperature gradient**, or **external force field**.

In engineering terms, mass transfer deals with the transport of one or more components of a mixture across a flowing or quiescent medium. The fundamental driving force is the **chemical potential gradient** ($\nabla \mu$), but for most practical engineering applications, this is approximated by the **concentration gradient** ($\nabla C$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCMET503 - Module 4):**
> The KTU 2024 scheme for this module focuses on:
> - Modes/analogy of mass transfer
> - Fick's First and Second Laws of Diffusion
> - Steady-state and unsteady-state diffusion (plane wall, cylinder, sphere)
> - Equimolar counter diffusion and Unimolecular (Stagnant Film) diffusion
> - Convective mass transfer coefficients
> - Analogies between heat, momentum, and mass transfer (Reynolds, Prandtl, Chilton-Colburn)

## 1.2 The Two Principal Modes of Mass Transfer

Mass transfer occurs in nature via **two primary mechanisms** analogous to heat transfer:

| Mode | Heat Transfer Analogy | Driving Force | Examples |
|------|----------------------|---------------|----------|
| **Molecular Diffusion** | Conduction | Concentration gradient ($\partial C/\partial x$) | Perfume spreading in still air, salt dissolving in water |
| **Convective Mass Transfer** | Convection | Combined diffusion + bulk fluid motion | Evaporation of water from a flowing air stream, scrubbing of gas |

**Molecular Diffusion** → movement at the **microscopic molecular level** (no bulk motion).
**Convective Mass Transfer** → movement assisted by the **macroscopic bulk motion** of the fluid.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a sugar cube dropped into a cup of still tea (no stirring).**
> - Initially, sugar concentration is **very high** at the cube and **zero** far away.
> - Sugar molecules jiggle randomly and gradually migrate outward.
> - This microscopic random walk is **molecular diffusion** — analogous to **heat conduction**.
> - Now, if you **stir the tea with a spoon**, sugar spreads almost instantly due to bulk fluid motion. This is **convective mass transfer** — analogous to **forced convection heat transfer**.

**Another analogy:** Consider an incense stick (agarbatti) lit in a closed room.
- The first few seconds: you smell fragrance only near the stick (diffusion dominates).
- After ventilation/fan is turned on: fragrance spreads rapidly across the room (convection dominates).

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

The following fundamental quantities form the language of mass transfer:

- **Concentration ($C$)**: Mass per unit volume of mixture $[\text{kg/m}^3]$ or moles per unit volume $[\text{kmol/m}^3]$.
- **Mass Fraction ($\omega_A$)**: Mass of species A / Total mass of mixture. Dimensionless.
- **Mole Fraction ($x_A, y_A$)**: Moles of species A / Total moles. Dimensionless.
- **Mass Flux ($N_A$)**: Mass of A transferred per unit area per unit time $[\text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}]$.
- **Mass Diffusivity / Diffusion Coefficient ($D_{AB}$)**: A **property of the species pair** indicating how easily A moves through B $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$. Analogous to thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$) in heat conduction.
- **Molar Concentration ($C_A$)**: Moles of A per unit volume $[\text{kmol/m}^3]$.
- **Total Pressure ($P$)** and **Total Concentration ($C$)** related by ideal gas law: $C = P / RT$ $[\text{kmol/m}^3]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values of $D_{AB}$ at 1 atm, 25°C (298 K):**
> - Air – Water vapor: $D_{AB} \approx 0.26 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
> - Air – CO₂: $D_{AB} \approx 0.16 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
> - Water – NaCl (in liquid): $D_{AB} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (note: **6 orders of magnitude smaller** than gases!)

This stark contrast ($D_{gas} \gg D_{liquid}$) is why **gaseous mass transfer is far more rapid** than liquid mass transfer — a critical engineering insight.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1-D Steady State)

**Statement (KTU Exact Wording):**
> "The molar flux of a species A by molecular diffusion under steady-state, one-dimensional conditions is proportional to the negative of the concentration gradient of that species."

Mathematically,

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

Where:
- $N_A$ = Molar flux of species A relative to **stationary coordinates** $[\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}]$
- $D_{AB}$ = Binary diffusion (mass diffusivity) coefficient of A in B $[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$
- $dC_A/dx$ = Concentration gradient of A in the $x$-direction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The negative sign** indicates that mass flows from **high to low concentration** (i.e., opposite to the gradient direction) — analogous to Fourier's Law in conduction.

### Why and How — Physical Reasoning

1. Molecules of a gas (or solute) are in continuous **random thermal motion** (Brownian motion).
2. When a concentration gradient exists, statistically **more molecules move from the high-concentration side to the low-concentration side** than the reverse.
3. This net statistical imbalance manifests as a measurable **macroscopic flux**.
4. The proportionality constant $D_{AB}$ encodes information about molecular size, velocity, and intermolecular forces.

## 2.2 Fick's Second Law of Diffusion (Unsteady State)

When concentration varies with **both position and time**, Fick's First Law is combined with a **species mass balance** over a differential control volume to yield:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$$

In three dimensions (general form):

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \left( \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial z^2} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Analogy for KTU:** Compare this with the **transient heat conduction equation (Fourier's Second Law):**
> $\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$ — the structure is **identical**, with $D_{AB}$ replacing thermal diffusivity $\alpha$ and $C_A$ replacing $T$.

## 2.3 Steady-State Diffusion Through a Plane Wall

For one-dimensional steady diffusion from $x = 0$ (concentration $C_{A1}$) to $x = L$ (concentration $C_{A2}$):

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}$$

## 2.4 Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)

When two species A and B diffuse in **opposite directions** in equal molar amounts (e.g., distillation of a binary liquid), there is **no net bulk flow**. The flux of A is:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L} = -N_B$$

## 2.5 Unimolecular Diffusion Through a Stagnant Gas Film

When species A diffuses through **stagnant B** (e.g., evaporation of water into still air, absorption of a gas into a liquid), the flux is modified because of the bulk flow generated by A's addition. Using the **Stefan Equation**:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot L} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{B2}}{P - P_{B1}}\right)$$

Or in terms of partial pressures,

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot L} \cdot \frac{(P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{(P_{BM})}$$

Where $P_{BM}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of B**:

$$P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The KTU examiner **deducts marks** if students use the simple linear formula $N_A = D_{AB}(C_{A1}-C_{A2})/L$ for stagnant-film conditions. Always use **Stefan equation** (or log-mean form) when B is stagnant.

## 2.6 Convective Mass Transfer

When a fluid flows past a surface, mass transfer across the fluid boundary layer is described by an empirical equation analogous to Newton's Law of Cooling:

$$N_A = h_m (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

Where:
- $h_m$ = **Convective mass transfer coefficient** $[\text{m/s}]$
- $C_{A,s}$ = Concentration of A at the surface
- $C_{A,\infty}$ = Concentration of A in the bulk fluid

## 2.7 Analogies Between Heat, Momentum, and Mass Transfer

This is a **high-weightage KTU topic**. Three famous analogies:

### a) Reynolds Analogy
For **Pr ≈ Sc ≈ 1** (analogous to Pr = 1 in heat transfer):

$$\frac{h}{\rho V c_p} = \frac{f}{8} = \frac{h_m}{V} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

Or simply:

$$St_m = \frac{f}{8}, \qquad St = \frac{f}{8}$$

where $St_m = h_m / V$ is the mass-transfer Stanton number, $St = h/(\rho V c_p)$ is the heat-transfer Stanton number, and $f$ is the Darcy friction factor.

### b) Prandtl Analogy
For boundary layer flows with Pr > 0.6:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{8}$$

Similarly for mass transfer:

$$St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{f}{8}$$

### c) Chilton–Colburn Analogy (Most Accurate, Most Tested in KTU)
For both heat and mass transfer across a wide range of Pr and Sc:

$$j_H = j_D = \frac{f}{8}$$

where

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{h}{\rho V c_p} Pr^{2/3}$$

$$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{h_m}{V} Sc^{2/3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Chilton–Colburn analogy is the most frequently asked analogy in KTU university exams**. Memorize: $j_H = j_D$ for laminar/turbulent flow over flat plates and inside tubes.

## 2.8 Non-Dimensional Numbers in Mass Transfer

| Number | Definition | Heat Transfer Analog | Physical Meaning |
|--------|------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Sherwood Number, $Sh$** | $Sh = h_m L / D_{AB}$ | Nusselt $Nu$ | Ratio of convective to diffusive mass transfer |
| **Schmidt Number, $Sc$** | $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ | Prandtl $Pr$ | Ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity |
| **Stanton Number (mass), $St_m$** | $St_m = h_m / V$ | Stanton $St$ | Ratio of mass transfer at wall to bulk transport |
| **Peclet Number (mass), $Pe_m$** | $Pe_m = Re \cdot Sc = V L / D_{AB}$ | Peclet $Pe$ | Ratio of bulk to diffusive transport |

---
