# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Fick's Second Law from First Principles

**Objective:** Derive the transient diffusion equation using a differential mass balance.

### Step 1 — Consider a Differential Control Volume
Take a thin slab of area $A$ and thickness $dx$ within a quiescent medium (no bulk flow). Species A enters at $x$ with flux $N_A$ and leaves at $x+dx$ with flux $N_A + (\partial N_A/\partial x) \, dx$.

### Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Mass for Species A
The net rate of accumulation of A inside the slab = Net rate of inflow − Net rate of outflow:

$$A \, dx \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = A \left[ N_A \Big\vert_x - \left( N_A + \frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x} dx \right) \right]$$

### Step 3 — Simplify
Dividing through by $A \cdot dx$:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial N_A}{\partial x}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Fick's First Law
Fick's First Law states $N_A = -D_{AB} \, \partial C_A / \partial x$. Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(-D_{AB} \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Constant Diffusivity Assumption
For dilute systems, $D_{AB}$ is essentially constant, so it can be moved outside the derivative:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}\;}$$

**This is Fick's Second Law** in 1-D. Extension to 3-D is straightforward by adding the $y$ and $z$ terms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat-Mass Transfer Mapping (KTU Favourite):**
> $D_{AB}$ plays the role of $\alpha = k / (\rho c_p)$, and $C_A$ plays the role of $T$.
> **Every transient heat conduction solution has a direct mass-transfer counterpart.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Diffusion Through a Plane Wall

**Objective:** Obtain $N_A$ in terms of $C_{A1}$, $C_{A2}$, $D_{AB}$, and $L$.

Starting from Fick's First Law:

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

For steady state, $N_A$ is constant (does not depend on $x$). Rearranging:

$$dC_A = -\frac{N_A}{D_{AB}} \, dx$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $C_A = C_{A1}$) to $x = L$ (where $C_A = C_{A2}$):

$$\int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} dC_A = -\frac{N_A}{D_{AB}} \int_{0}^{L} dx$$

$$C_{A2} - C_{A1} = -\frac{N_A}{D_{AB}} L$$

Solving for $N_A$:

$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion Flux

**Setup:** Species A diffuses from $x=0$ to $x=L$, and B diffuses in the opposite direction in equal molar amounts. There is no net molar flux: $N_A + N_B = 0$, so $N_B = -N_A$.

For species A, applying Fick's Law in terms of mole fractions $x_A$:

$$N_A = -C \cdot D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dx}$$

For species B, with $x_A + x_B = 1 \Rightarrow dx_A = -dx_B$:

$$N_B = -C \cdot D_{AB} \frac{dx_B}{dx} = +C \cdot D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dx} = -N_A$$

This is automatically consistent. The steady-state molar flux of A is:

$$N_A = \frac{C \cdot D_{AB} (x_{A1} - x_{A2})}{L} = \frac{D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **same equation** as for simple steady-state diffusion — the absence of bulk flow means no extra correction is needed.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Unimolecular Diffusion Through a Stagnant Film (Stefan Equation)

**Physical Setup:** A is evaporating from a liquid surface at $x = 0$ (partial pressure $P_{A1}$) into a stagnant gas B filling the space up to $x = L$, where a stream maintains partial pressure $P_{A2}$ of A. Since B is **stagnant**, $N_B = 0$ (in stationary coordinates).

### Step 1 — Species A Flux (with bulk flow)
When a net molar flux $N_A$ exists, the bulk flow contribution adds a convective term. The **total flux of A** in stationary coordinates is:

$$N_A = -C \cdot D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dx} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$

Since $N_B = 0$:

$$N_A = -C \cdot D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dx} + x_A N_A$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Isolate $dx_A$
$$N_A (1 - x_A) = -C \cdot D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{dx}$$

$$N_A \, dx = -C \cdot D_{AB} \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}$$

### Step 3 — Apply Ideal Gas Relation
$C = P / (RT)$, so $C \cdot D_{AB} = (P / RT) \cdot D_{AB}$:

$$N_A \, dx = -\frac{P \cdot D_{AB}}{R T} \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}$$

### Step 4 — Integrate Across the Film
Integrating from $x = 0$ (where $x_A = x_{A1} = P_{A1}/P$) to $x = L$ (where $x_A = x_{A2} = P_{A2}/P$):

$$N_A \int_0^L dx = -\frac{P \cdot D_{AB}}{R T} \int_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}} \frac{dx_A}{1 - x_A}$$

$$N_A \cdot L = \frac{P \cdot D_{AB}}{R T} \Big[ \ln(1 - x_A) \Big]_{x_{A1}}^{x_{A2}}$$

$$N_A \cdot L = \frac{P \cdot D_{AB}}{R T} \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x_{A2}}{1 - x_{A1}}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Partial Pressures
Using $1 - x_A = P_B / P$ and $P - P_A = P_B$:

$$\boxed{\;N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot L} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right)\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Stefan Equation** — mandatory in every KTU mass transfer problem involving a stagnant gas film.

### Step 6 — Equivalent Log-Mean Form
Note that:

$$\frac{(P - P_{A2}) - (P - P_{A1})}{\ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right)} = \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{\ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right)}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator:

$$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R T L} \cdot \frac{P_{A1} - P_{A2}}{P_{BM}}$$

where

$$\boxed{\;P_{BM} = \frac{(P - P_{A1}) - (P - P_{A2})}{\ln\!\left(\frac{P - P_{A1}}{P - P_{A2}}\right)} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})}\;}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** $P_{BM}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of the stagnant gas B**, which is the appropriate "driving force normalizer" for stagnant-film diffusion.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Chilton–Colburn Analogy from Reynolds Analogy

**Starting point — Reynolds Analogy:**
For Pr = Sc = 1: $St = St_m = f/8$.

**Refinement by Colburn:** Extensive experimental data on turbulent flow over flat plates showed that heat and momentum transfer **do not** strictly follow the Reynolds analogy for fluids with $Pr \neq 1$. Colburn empirically proposed:

$$St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{f}{8} \quad \text{(heat transfer, } 0.6 < Pr < 60\text{)}$$

$$St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{f}{8} \quad \text{(mass transfer, } 0.6 < Sc < 3000\text{)}$$

Defining **j-factors**:

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3}, \qquad j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\;j_H = j_D = \frac{f}{8}\;}$$

This is the **Chilton–Colburn analogy**, which is more accurate than the simple Reynolds analogy for practical engineering fluids (air: Pr ≈ 0.7, water: Pr ≈ 7).

**Worked Example — Using the Analogy**

A plate of length $L = 0.5$ m experiences turbulent air flow at $V = 10$ m/s. Friction factor $f = 0.005$. Find $h$ and $h_m$. Given: $k = 0.026$ W/(m·K), $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K), $\rho = 1.18$ kg/m³, $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $\mu = 1.85 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $Pr = 0.71$.

**Solution Sketch:**

First, $j_H = j_D = f/8 = 0.005/8 = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$.

Mass transfer coefficient:

$$h_m = j_D \cdot V / Sc^{2/3}$$

Compute $Sc$:

$$Sc = \frac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^{-5}}{1.18 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.627$$

$$Sc^{2/3} = 0.627^{2/3} \approx 0.733$$

$$h_m = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 10}{0.733} = 8.53 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m/s}$$

Heat transfer coefficient:

$$h = j_H \cdot \rho V c_p / Pr^{2/3}$$

$$Pr^{2/3} = 0.71^{2/3} \approx 0.793$$

$$h = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.18 \times 10 \times 1005}{0.793} = 9.35 \,\text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful design equation** obtained from $h_m / h$:
> $\dfrac{h_m}{h} = \dfrac{1}{\rho c_p} \cdot \left( \dfrac{Pr}{Sc} \right)^{2/3} = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} \cdot \left( \dfrac{Pr}{Sc} \right)^{2/3}$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula

| Situation | Driving Force Correction | Formula |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------|
| EMCD (no bulk flow) | None | $N_A = D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})/L$ |
| A through stagnant B (small $x_A$) | Negligible | $N_A \approx D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})/L$ (approx.) |
| A through stagnant B (large $x_A$) | Use log-mean | Stefan equation (full form) |
| Forced convection over surface | Bulk flow | $N_A = h_m (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ |
| Convection + analogy known | Use $j_H = j_D$ | $h_m = (h / \rho c_p) \cdot (Pr/Sc)^{2/3}$ |

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Mass Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DRIVING FORCES<br/>Concentration Gradient<br/>Pressure Gradient<br/>Temperature Gradient<br/>External Force Field"] --> B["MASS TRANSFER SYSTEM"]
    B --> C1["MOLECULAR DIFFUSION<br/>(Ficks Law)<br/>No bulk motion"]
    B --> C2["CONVECTIVE MASS TRANSFER<br/>Bulk flow + Diffusion"]
    C1 --> D1["Steady State<br/>N_A = DAB dC_A by dx"]
    C1 --> D2["Unsteady State<br/>Ficks Second Law"]
    C2 --> D3["Forced Convection<br/>h_m known or via analogy"]
    C2 --> D4["Natural Convection<br/>Grashof type analog"]
    D1 --> E1["EMCD Case<br/>Linear gradient"]
    D1 --> E2["Stagnant Film Case<br/>Stefan Equation<br/>Log mean correction"]
    D2 --> E3["Plane Wall<br/>Cylinder<br/>Sphere solutions"]
    D3 --> E4["Reynolds Analogy<br/>Prandtl Analogy<br/>Chilton Colburn Analogy"]
    E1 --> F["MASS FLUX N_A<br/>or h_m"]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right Mass Transfer Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1["Is bulk fluid motion present?"] -->|"No"| Q2["Is one species stagnant?"]
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q3["Convective Mass Transfer<br/>Use N_A = h_m dC_A"]
    Q2 -->|"No EMCD"| F1["Use N_A = DAB C_A1 to C_A2 over L"]
    Q2 -->|"Yes Stagnant B"| F2["Use Stefan Equation<br/>N_A = DAB P over RTL ln P_minus_PA2 over P_minus_PA1"]
    Q3 --> Q4["Is analogy data available?"]
    Q4 -->|"Yes"| F3["Use Chilton Colburn<br/>j_H equals j_D equals f by 8"]
    Q4 -->|"No"| F4["Use empirical correlation<br/>Sh equals f of Re and Sc"]
    F1 --> OUT["MASS FLUX N_A"]
    F2 --> OUT
    F3 --> OUT
    F4 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Stagnant-Film (Unimolecular) Diffusion Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1["INTERFACE at x = 0"]
        A1["Liquid Surface<br/>Partial pressure of A equals P_A1<br/>B is non diffusing"]
    end
    subgraph L2["STAGNANT GAS FILM thickness L"]
        A2["Diffusion path<br/>Convective bulk flow exists<br/>Driven by addition of A"]
    end
    subgraph L3["BOUNDARY at x = L"]
        A3["Gas Stream<br/>Partial pressure of A equals P_A2<br/>P_A1 greater than P_A2"]
    end

    L2 -->|"A removed by stream"| L3
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Surface at x = 0<br/>x_A1 HIGH<br/>x_B1 LOW"] -->|"Diffusion of A<br/>Flux N_A"| A2["Surface at x = L<br/>x_A2 LOW<br/>x_B2 HIGH"]
    A2 -->|"Diffusion of B<br/>Flux N_B equals minus N_A"| A1
    A1 -.->|"No net molar flow<br/>N_A plus N_B equals 0"| A1
```

## 4.5 Schematic of the Three Analogies — Hierarchy of Accuracy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["REYNOLDS ANALOGY<br/>St = St_m = f by 8<br/>Valid only for Pr = Sc = 1<br/>Simplest, least accurate"]
    P["PRANDTL ANALOGY<br/>St Pr_to_2_by_3 = f by 8<br/>St_m Sc_to_2_by_3 = f by 8<br/>Improved for moderate Pr Sc"]
    C["CHILTON COLBURN ANALOGY<br/>j_H = j_D = f by 8<br/>Empirical two thirds exponent<br/>Best general accuracy"]
    R --> P --> C
    C --> APP["USED IN<br/>Chemical process design<br/>HVAC and cooling towers<br/>Absorption and stripping columns"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are **simulated in exact KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**. Marks distribution: Part A (3 marks each) + Part B (14 marks with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B). Model answers follow KTU board examiner's expected valuation key.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State Fick's First Law of Diffusion. Compare it with Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Fick's First Law states that the **molar flux of a species A by molecular diffusion is proportional to the negative of its concentration gradient**.

$$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$

| Aspect | Fourier's Law (Heat) | Fick's Law (Mass) |
|--------|---------------------|-------------------|
| Quantity Transported | Heat energy | Mass of species A |
| Driving Potential | Temperature gradient $\nabla T$ | Concentration gradient $\nabla C_A$ |
| Transport Property | Thermal conductivity $k$ | Diffusivity $D_{AB}$ |
| Sign Convention | Negative (high to low $T$) | Negative (high to low $C_A$) |
| Equation | $q = -k \, dT/dx$ | $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ |

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for comparison table.

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Define Sherwood, Schmidt, and Stanton numbers. Give their physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$Sh = \frac{h_m L}{D_{AB}}, \qquad Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}}, \qquad St_m = \frac{h_m}{V}$$

- **Sherwood Number (Sh):** Ratio of **convective mass transfer** to **molecular diffusion**. It is the **mass-transfer analog of Nusselt number**. *(1 mark)*
- **Schmidt Number (Sc):** Ratio of **momentum diffusivity** $(\nu = \mu/\rho)$ to **mass diffusivity** $D_{AB}$. Analog of Prandtl number. Indicates relative thickness of velocity vs. concentration boundary layers. *(1 mark)*
- **Stanton Number (St$_m$):** Ratio of mass transfer at the wall to the bulk convective transport. Dimensionless. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each is divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive Fick's Second Law of Diffusion in one dimension from a differential mass balance. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Helium (He) and Nitrogen (N₂) gases are contained between two parallel plates 12 mm apart at 1.5 atm total pressure and 25°C. The mole fraction of He at plate 1 is 0.80 and at plate 2 is 0.20. Calculate the steady-state molar flux of He, assuming equimolar counter-diffusion. Given: $D_{He-N_2} = 0.687 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s at 1 atm, 25°C. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

> Full derivation of Section 3.1 above is the model solution. Key steps:
> 1. Control volume of area $A$ and thickness $dx$ (1 mark)
> 2. Net accumulation = Inflow − Outflow (1 mark)
> 3. Expression: $A\,dx\,\partial C_A/\partial t = -A\,\partial N_A/\partial x \cdot dx$ (1 mark)
> 4. Substitute Fick's First Law: $N_A = -D_{AB}\,\partial C_A/\partial x$ (1 mark)
> 5. Assume constant $D_{AB}$ (constant property assumption) (1 mark)
> 6. Final PDE: $\partial C_A/\partial t = D_{AB}\,\partial^2 C_A/\partial x^2$ (1 mark)
> 7. State assumptions: (i) 1-D, (ii) constant $D_{AB}$, (iii) no chemical reaction, (iv) no bulk flow (1 mark)

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 12$ mm $= 0.012$ m
- $P = 1.5$ atm, $T = 25°\text{C} = 298$ K
- $x_{A1} = 0.80$ (at plate 1), $x_{A2} = 0.20$ (at plate 2)
- $D_{AB}$ at 1 atm $= 0.687 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s
- EMCD assumption

**Step 1 — Correct $D_{AB}$ to the system pressure**

For gases, $D_{AB} \cdot P = \text{constant}$ (since $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}/P$).

$$D_{AB,\,1.5\,\text{atm}} = D_{AB,\,1\,\text{atm}} \times \frac{1.0}{1.5} = 0.687 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.458 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute total concentration $C$**

$$C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{1.5 \times 101325}{8314 \times 298} = \frac{151987.5}{2477572} = 0.06135 \,\text{kmol/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — EMCD Flux**

$$N_A = \frac{C \cdot D_{AB} (x_{A1} - x_{A2})}{L}$$

$$N_A = \frac{0.06135 \times 0.458 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.80 - 0.20)}{0.012}$$

$$N_A = \frac{0.06135 \times 0.458 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.60}{0.012}$$

$$N_A = \frac{1.686 \times 10^{-6}}{0.012} = 1.405 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

$$\boxed{\;N_{He} = 1.405 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}\;}$$

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for pressure correction, 2 marks for total concentration, 2 marks for substituting into EMCD equation, 1 mark for final answer with units.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Don't forget to correct $D_{AB}$ for the actual operating pressure.** If students use $D_{AB} = 0.687 \times 10^{-4}$ at 1.5 atm, they get 50% higher flux — wrong. (–2 marks)
> - **Use $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K) when $P$ is in kPa and $C$ is in kmol/m³.** Mixing units is the #1 mistake. (–1 mark)
> - **State EMCD assumption explicitly** in the answer for full marks. (–1 mark if missing)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative to Q-A**

**(a)** State and explain the Chilton–Colburn analogy between heat and mass transfer. List the conditions under which it is valid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stream of humid air at 30°C flows parallel to a flat water surface of length 1.0 m at a velocity of 3 m/s. Using the Chilton–Colburn analogy, estimate the mass transfer coefficient $h_m$. The friction factor is $f = 0.0044$. Given: $Sc = 0.61$, $D_{AB} = 0.26 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Statement:** The Chilton–Colburn analogy states that for turbulent flow over a surface, the heat-transfer and mass-transfer $j$-factors are equal to half the Darcy friction factor:

$$j_H = j_D = \frac{f}{8}$$

where

$$j_H = St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = \frac{h}{\rho V c_p} \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$

$$j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = \frac{h_m}{V} \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$

**Explanation (3 marks):** Experimental data on turbulent flow over flat plates and inside tubes showed that the simple Reynolds analogy ($St = f/8$, valid only for $Pr = Sc = 1$) overestimates the heat and mass transfer when $Pr$ and $Sc$ deviate from unity. Colburn found empirically that raising the Pr and Sc to the 2/3 power brings the analogy into agreement with data for most engineering fluids. This is because the thermal (and concentration) boundary layers are **thinner** than the hydrodynamic boundary layer when $Pr > 1$ and $Sc > 1$.

**Conditions of Validity (3 marks):**
1. Turbulent flow (analogies break down in laminar).
2. $0.6 < Pr < 60$ (heat transfer) and $0.6 < Sc < 3000$ (mass transfer).
3. Constant fluid properties.
4. No chemical reaction at the surface.
5. No significant bulk mass transfer effects.

> **Mark Split:** 2 marks for statement, 2 marks for formula, 1 mark for explanation, 2 marks for conditions.

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Given:** $L = 1.0$ m, $V = 3$ m/s, $f = 0.0044$, $Sc = 0.61$, $D_{AB} = 0.26 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s.

**Step 1 — Compute the $j$-factor**

$$j_D = \frac{f}{8} = \frac{0.0044}{8} = 5.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 2 — Express $h_m$ in terms of $j_D$**

$$j_D = \frac{h_m}{V} \cdot Sc^{2/3} \;\Rightarrow\; h_m = j_D \cdot V \cdot Sc^{-2/3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Sc^{2/3}$**

$$Sc^{2/3} = 0.61^{2/3} = 0.721$$

**Step 4 — Compute $h_m$**

$$h_m = 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.0 \times \frac{1}{0.721}$$

$$h_m = 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 3.0 \times 1.387 = 2.288 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{\;h_m = 2.29 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{m/s}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> - **Do not confuse the $j$-factor with the Stanton number.** $j_D = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3}$ is a *corrected* Stanton number. (–1 mark)
> - **$Sc^{-2/3}$ in the formula, not $Sc^{2/3}$** in the denominator. (–2 marks)
> - **Convert $f$ correctly:** the KTU convention uses the **Darcy friction factor**; if $C_f$ (Fanning) is given, convert: $f_{Darcy} = 4 C_f$. (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 PART B — Additional Practice Problem (14 Marks)

### **QUESTION C — Stagnant-Film Diffusion [KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024]**

Ammonia (NH₃, species A) is being absorbed from a stagnant mixture of NH₃ + air (B) into water at 1 atm, 25°C. The partial pressure of NH₃ at the gas–liquid interface is 0.020 atm, and far from the interface it is 0.006 atm. The effective film thickness is $L = 0.005$ m. Calculate the steady-state flux of NH₃. Given: $D_{NH_3-air} = 0.247 \times 10^{-4}$ m²/s at 1 atm, 25°C.

#### **Model Solution (10 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the diffusion type:** Species B (air) is **stagnant** → use **Stefan Equation**.

**Step 2 — Compute partial pressures of B**
- At interface ($x=0$): $P_{B1} = P - P_{A1} = 1.0 - 0.020 = 0.980$ atm
- At bulk ($x=L$): $P_{B2} = P - P_{A2} = 1.0 - 0.006 = 0.994$ atm

**Step 3 — Compute log-mean $P_{BM}$**

$$P_{BM} = \frac{P_{B2} - P_{B1}}{\ln(P_{B2}/P_{B1})} = \frac{0.994 - 0.980}{\ln(0.994/0.980)}$$

$$P_{BM} = \frac{0.014}{\ln(1.014286)} = \frac{0.014}{0.014186} = 0.9869 \,\text{atm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute total concentration $C$**

$$C = \frac{P}{RT} = \frac{101325}{8314 \times 298} = 0.0409 \,\text{kmol/m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Apply Stefan Equation**

$$N_A = \frac{C \cdot D_{AB} (P_{A1} - P_{A2})}{P_{BM}}$$

$$N_A = \frac{0.0409 \times 0.247 \times 10^{-4} \times (0.020 - 0.006)}{0.9869}$$

$$N_A = \frac{0.0409 \times 0.247 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.014}{0.9869}$$

$$N_A = \frac{1.415 \times 10^{-8}}{0.9869} = 1.434 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}$$

$$\boxed{\;N_{NH_3} = 1.43 \times 10^{-8} \,\text{kmol/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}\;}$$

> **Mark Split:** 1 mark for identifying Stefan, 2 marks for $P_B$ values, 2 marks for $P_{BM}$, 2 marks for $C$, 3 marks for final substitution and answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Do NOT use $N_A = D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})/L$** for stagnant-film problems — this gives 2–4× error. (–4 marks)
> - **Use the correct log-mean** of $P_B$ (not $P_A$) in the denominator. (–2 marks)
> - **Express the final flux in consistent units** (kmol/m²·s or mol/m²·s). (–1 mark)

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Module 4 — Mass Transfer Revision Card**

**Core Laws:**
- ☐ Fick's First Law: $N_A = -D_{AB} \, dC_A/dx$ (steady state, no bulk flow).
- ☐ Fick's Second Law: $\partial C_A/\partial t = D_{AB} \, \partial^2 C_A/\partial x^2$ (transient).
- ☐ Stefan Equation: $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} P}{RTL} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P - P_{A2}}{P - P_{A1}}\right)$ (stagnant B).
- ☐ Convective mass transfer: $N_A = h_m (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$.

**Key Dimensionless Numbers:**
- ☐ $Sh = h_m L / D_{AB}$ — mass analog of Nusselt number.
- ☐ $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ — mass analog of Prandtl number.
- ☐ $St_m = h_m / V$ — mass Stanton number.
- ☐ $Pe_m = Re \cdot Sc$ — mass Peclet number.

**Critical Analogies (high-weightage):**
- ☐ Reynolds: $St = St_m = f/8$ (only for $Pr = Sc = 1$).
- ☐ Prandtl: $St \cdot Pr^{2/3} = St_m \cdot Sc^{2/3} = f/8$ (improved).
- ☐ **Chilton–Colburn (MOST ASKED):** $j_H = j_D = f/8$.

**Equation Selection Cheat-Sheet:**
- ☐ **EMCD** (no net bulk flow) → simple linear $N_A = D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})/L$.
- ☐ **Stagnant B** → always **Stefan equation** with log-mean $P_{BM}$.
- ☐ **Convection with no analogy data** → use empirical $Sh = a \, Re^b \, Sc^{1/3}$ correlation.
- ☐ **Convection with known $f$** → use $j_H = j_D$ analogy.

**Pressure & Temperature Dependence:**
- ☐ $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2}/P$ (gas-phase scaling — **always correct** $D_{AB}$ for actual $P, T$).
- ☐ For liquids, $D_{AB}$ is much smaller ($\sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s) and weakly temperature-dependent.

**Common Pitfalls (KTU Valuation):**
- ☐ Mixing SI vs CGS units (R = 8.314 J/mol·K vs 82.06 cm³·atm/mol·K).
- ☐ Forgetting to state the **EMCD / stagnant B** assumption explicitly.
- ☐ Using $D_{AB}$ from data tables at standard conditions without correcting for actual $P, T$.
- ☐ Confusing the Sherwood, Schmidt, Stanton numbers.
- ☐ Forgetting the negative sign / direction in Fick's First Law.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Mantra for Module 4:**
> "**Fick is to Mass as Fourier is to Heat, and Newton is to Convection as $h_m$ is to Mass Convection.**"
> Master the analogies — they are worth **at least 7–10 marks** in any KTU ESE paper.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Mass Transfer - Diffusion Coefficient - Fick's Law of Diffusion - Steady State Diffusion in Stationary Medium - Diffusion in Moving Medium - Diffusion Through a Membrane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Mass Transfer - Diffusion Coefficient - Fick's Law of Diffusion - Steady State Diffusion in Stationary Medium - Diffusion in Moving Medium - Diffusion Through a Membrane # 🔬 MODULE 4: MASS TRANSFER FUNDAMENTALS --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mass Transfer** is defined as the *transport of one or more chemical species from a region of higher chemical potential (concentration) to a region of lower chemical potential* within a phase or between phases, driven by a **concentration gradient**, **temperature gradient**, **pressure gradient**, or an **external force field**. The dominant mode in non-reacting binary systems is **molecular diffusion**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, mass transfer is studied under the unifying triad of **Momentum**, **Heat**, and **Mass** transfer — where each phenomenon obeys mathematically analogous rate laws. --- ### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sugary Tea" Picture Imagine dropping a sugar cube into a still glass of hot tea (without stirring). - **High concentration** of sugar exists near the cube (initial region). - **Low concentration** exists at the bottom of the glass. - Over time, sugar molecules **spontaneously wander** from the crowded zone to the empty zone until the entire glass becomes uniformly sweet. This wandering, governed purely by **random molecular motion** (Brownian motion), is called **diffusion**. | Phenomenon | Driving Force | What Flows | Rate Law | |---|---|---|---| | Momentum Transfer | Velocity gradient | Momentum | Newton's Law of Viscosity | | Heat Transfer | Temperature gradient | Energy | Fourier's Law | | **Mass Transfer** | **Concentration gradient** | **Mass / Moles** | **Fick's Law** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** All three transport laws are mathematically **analogous** — replacing temperature with concentration, and thermal diffusivity (α) with mass diffusivity (D), transforms heat conduction problems directly into mass diffusion problems. --- ### 🔑 Defining Quantities (KTU Board Standard) - **Diffusion Coefficient (D_AB)** — A **proportionality constant** with units of **m²/s** that quantifies how fast species A diffuses through medium B. - **Molar Flux (N_A)** — Moles of A crossing **unit area per unit time** [kmol/m²·s]. - **Concentration (C_A)** — Moles of A per unit volume [kmol/m³]. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Standard:** Always express D_AB in **m²/s** and concentrations in **kmol/m³** for SI compliance in board examinations. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🧪 Classification of Mass Transfer Modes Mass transfer occurs through **two principal modes**: #### A. Molecular Diffusion (Microscopic Level) - Caused by **random thermal motion** of molecules. - Governed by **Fick's Law of Diffusion**. - Dominant in **stagnant fluids**, **solids**, and **laminar flow in thin films**. - Two sub-cases: **Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD)** and **Uni-Diffusion Through Stagnant Film (Stefan Diffusion)**. #### B. Convective Mass Transfer (Macroscopic Level) - Caused by **bulk fluid motion** (turbulence, forced flow). - Governed by $N_A = k_C (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$ — analogous to Newton's Law of Cooling. - Covered in Module 5 (KTU PCMET503). --- ### ⚖️ Fick's First Law of Diffusion (The Master Equation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fick's First Law (1855):** *Molar flux of species A by molecular diffusion is directly proportional to the concentration gradient and the diffusion coefficient.* Mathematically (one-dimensional): $$J_A^* = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ Where: - $J_A^*$ = **Molar diffusive flux** of A relative to the **mass-average velocity** [kmol/m²·s] - $D_{AB}$ = Binary diffusion coefficient of A in B [m²/s] - $\dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ = Concentration gradient of A in the x-direction [kmol/m⁴] - The **negative sign** indicates flux flows from **high to low concentration**. --- ### 🔁 Fick's Second Law (Unsteady State / Time-Dependent) When concentration changes with time at a fixed point: $$\boxed{\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **1-D transient form**. It is **mathematically identical in form to Fourier's heat equation** — a classic KTU board favorite analogy question. --- ### 🧬 Diffusion Coefficient ($D_{AB}$) — Physical Meaning $D_{AB}$ represents the **ease with which species A molecules slip past B molecules**. It depends on: - **Temperature (T)** ↑ → $D_{AB}$ ↑ (typically $D_{AB} \propto T^{1.5}$ to $T^2$) - **Pressure (P)** ↑ → $D_{AB}$ ↓ (typically $D_{AB} \propto 1/P$) - **Molecular size** ↑ → $D_{AB}$ ↓ - **Phase:** Gases >> Liquids > Solids (typical magnitude: $D_{gas} \sim 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $D_{liquid} \sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s, $D_{solid} \sim 10^{-13}$ m²/s) **Empirical Estimate — Chapman-Enskog Theory (Gases):** $$D_{AB} = 0.001858 \cdot \frac{T^{3/2} \left( \frac{1}{M_A} + \frac{1}{M_B} \right)^{1/2}}{P \cdot \sigma_{AB}^2 \cdot \Omega_D}$$ Where: - $\sigma_{AB}$ = collision diameter [Å] - $\Omega_D$ = collision integral (function of $k_B T / \varepsilon_{AB}$) - P in atm, T in K **Stokes-Einstein Equation (Liquids):** $$D_{AB} = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu_B r_A}$$ Where: - $k_B$ = Boltzmann's constant = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K - $\mu_B$ = viscosity of solvent B - $r_A$ = radius of solute molecule A --- ### 🏗️ Steady State Diffusion in Stationary Medium — The Two Canonical Cases #### Case 1: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) When species A and B diffuse in **opposite directions at equal molar rates** (e.g., distillation): $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}, \quad N_B = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_B}{dx}$$ With $N_A = -N_B$, this gives $N_A = -N_B = \dfrac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}$ #### Case 2: Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B (Stefan's Law) When B is **non-diffusing** (e.g., water evaporating into stagnant air): $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{R \cdot T \cdot (p_{BM}/P)} \cdot \frac{(p_{A1} - p_{A2})}{L}$$ Where $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ is the **log-mean partial pressure of B**. --- ### 🌪️ Diffusion in Moving Medium (Convection Included) When the bulk fluid itself moves with a molar-average velocity $N_{tot}/C$: $$\boxed{N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \frac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)}$$ The **total flux** = **diffusive flux** + **convective flux** (carried by bulk flow). Rearranging (using $N_A = -N_B$ or $N_B = 0$ depending on case) gives the operational forms used in board problems. --- ### 🧱 Diffusion Through a Membrane (Solid Membrane Analogy) When a gas diffuses through a **porous solid or polymer membrane**: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot S}{L}(C_{A1} - C_{A2}) = \frac{P_M}{L}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$ Where: - $P_M = D_{AB} \cdot S$ = **Permeability** of the membrane [kmol·m/m²·s·Pa] - $S$ = **Solubility coefficient** [kmol/m³·Pa] - $L$ = Membrane thickness [m] --- ### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Law / Equation | Formula | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Fick's First Law** | $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ | Diffusive flux at constant T, P | | 2 | **Fick's Second Law** | $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ | Transient diffusion | | 3 | **EMCD Flux** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}(C_{A1}-C_{A2})}{L}$ | Distillation, equimolar | | 4 | **Stefan's Law (Stagnant B)** | $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P \, (p_{A1}-p_{A2})}{R \, T \, L \, p_{BM}}$ | Evaporation into stagnant gas | | 5 | **Log-mean $p_B$** | $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2}-p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$ | Stagnant film correction | | 6 | **Moving Medium Total Flux** | $N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dx} + x_A(N_A+N_B)$ | Bulk flow + diffusion | | 7 | **Membrane Permeation** | $N_A = \dfrac{P_M}{L}(p_{A1}-p_{A2})$ | Gas separation, dialysis | | 8 | **Chapman-Enskog (Gas)** | $D_{AB} = 0.001858 \dfrac{T^{3/2}(M_A^{-1}+M_B^{-1})^{1/2}}{P\,\sigma_{AB}^2\,\Omega_D}$ | Estimate $D_{AB}$ for gases | | 9 | **Stokes-Einstein (Liquid)** | $D_{AB} = \dfrac{k_B T}{6\pi \mu_B r_A}$ | Estimate $D_{AB}$ for liquids | | 10 | **Mass Transfer Analogy** | $Nu \leftrightarrow Sh, \quad Pr \leftrightarrow Sc$ | Convective analogy | --- ### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Distillation Columns** → EMCD across tray liquid-vapor interfaces. - **Humidification / Dehumidification** → Stefan diffusion (water vapor in stagnant air). - **Gas Separation Plants** → Membrane permeation (e.g., O₂/N₂ separation). - **Drying Operations** → Diffusion of moisture through solid porous media. - **Pharmaceutical Drug Delivery** → Membrane-controlled release of active ingredients. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 🔬 Derivation 1: Fick's First Law from Molecular Kinetic Theory **Starting Principle:** Consider a plane at $x = 0$ in a binary mixture of A and B. Molecules of A execute random walk with mean free path $\lambda$. **Step 1 — Number of A molecules crossing unit area per unit time from left → right:** According to kinetic theory, the fraction of molecules with velocity component in +x direction is $\tfrac{1}{6}$, and their mean displacement from the last collision is $\tfrac{2}{3}\lambda$. $$N_{A \rightarrow} = \frac{1}{6} n_A(x - \tfrac{2}{3}\lambda) \cdot \bar{u}_A$$ **Step 2 — Number of A molecules crossing from right → left:** $$N_{A \leftarrow} = \frac{1}{6} n_A(x + \tfrac{2}{3}\lambda) \cdot \bar{u}_A$$ **Step 3 — Net flux (left → right):** $$\begin{aligned} J_A^{*} &= N_{A \rightarrow} - N_{A \leftarrow} \\ &= \frac{1}{6} \bar{u}_A \left[ n_A(x - \tfrac{2}{3}\lambda) - n_A(x + \tfrac{2}{3}\lambda) \right] \\ &= \frac{1}{6} \bar{u}_A \cdot \left(-\tfrac{4}{3}\lambda\right) \frac{dn_A}{dx} \\ &= -\tfrac{2}{9}\lambda \bar{u}_A \frac{dn_A}{dx} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Define diffusion coefficient:** Since $C_A = n_A / N_A$ (number per volume), the coefficient of proportionality is identified as: $$D_{AB} = \frac{2}{9}\lambda \bar{u}_A$$ **Final Result — Fick's First Law:** $$J_A^{*} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ > ✅ **KTU Board Note:** This derivation is rarely asked for full marks, but the **final form and the physical interpretation of $D_{AB}$** as $\tfrac{2}{9}\lambda\bar{u}$ is a frequent 3-mark question. --- ### 🔬 Derivation 2: Steady-State Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) Through a Stagnant Film **Setup:** Species A and B diffuse in opposite directions across a gas film of thickness $L$. At steady state, $N_A = \text{const}$ and $N_B = \text{const}$. **Step 1 — Apply Fick's First Law:** $$\begin{aligned} N_A &= -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} \\ N_B &= -D_{AB} \frac{dC_B}{dx} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — EMCD Condition:** Equimolar means $N_A + N_B = 0$, i.e., $N_B = -N_A$. **Step 3 — Total concentration:** $C = C_A + C_B = \text{const}$ (constant T, P). Differentiating: $\dfrac{dC_A}{dx} = -\dfrac{dC_B}{dx}$. **Step 4 — Integrate:** $$\begin{aligned} N_A \, dx &= -D_{AB} \, dC_A \\ \int_0^L N_A \, dx &= -\int_{C_{A1}}^{C_{A2}} D_{AB} \, dC_A \end{aligned}$$ Assuming $D_{AB}$ = constant: $$N_A \cdot L = -D_{AB} (C_{A2} - C_{A1}) = D_{AB} (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}}$$ For ideal gas, $C_A = \dfrac{p_A}{RT}$, so: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RTL}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$ --- ### 🔬 Derivation 3: Stefan's Law (Diffusion of A Through Stagnant B) **Setup:** A liquid A evaporates at the bottom of a tube, and vapor A diffuses upward through stagnant gas B (e.g., air). At the top, A is swept away by a stream so $p_{A,2} = 0$. **Step 1 — General Flux Equation (Moving Medium):** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + x_A (N_A + N_B)$$ where $x_A = C_A / C$ is the mole fraction of A. **Step 2 — Stagnant B condition:** $N_B = 0$. Therefore: $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \frac{C_A}{C} N_A$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange:** $$N_A \left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ $$N_A \cdot \frac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute ideal gas concentrations** ($C_A = p_A/RT$, $C_B = p_B/RT$, $C = P/RT$): $$\begin{aligned} N_A \cdot \frac{p_B}{P} &= -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT} \frac{dp_A}{dx} \\ N_A \, dx &= -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T \, p_B} dp_A \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Use the relationship $p_B = P - p_A$** (binary ideal gas): $$N_A \, dx = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T (P - p_A)} dp_A$$ **Step 6 — Integrate from $x = 0$ (where $p_A = p_{A1}$) to $x = L$ (where $p_A = p_{A2}$):** $$\begin{aligned} N_A \int_0^L dx &= -\frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_A}{P - p_A} \\ N_A \, L &= -\frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \left[-\ln(P - p_A)\right]_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \\ N_A \, L &= \frac{D_{AB} P}{R T} \ln\!\left(\frac{P - p_{A1}}{P - p_{A2}}\right) \end{aligned}$$ Since $P - p_A = p_B$, we have $P - p_{A1} = p_{B1}$ and $P - p_{A2} = p_{B2}$: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RTL} \ln\!\left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right)$$ **Step 7 — Convert to arithmetic form using log-mean $p_{BM}$:** Since $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right) = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{p_{BM}}$, where: $$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$$ **Final Result — Stefan's Diffusion Equation:** $$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{R T L \, p_{BM}} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is **the most heavily tested derivation in KTU 2024 Scheme for Module 4.** Memorize every step — the log-mean correction is where students lose marks. --- ### 🔬 Derivation 4: Diffusion Through a Solid Membrane **Setup:** A membrane of thickness $L$ separates two gas streams. Species A dissolves in the membrane material at the upstream face ($C_{A1} = S \cdot p_{A1}$) and desorbs at the downstream face ($C_{A2} = S \cdot p_{A2}$), where $S$ = solubility coefficient. **Step 1 — Apply Fick's First Law at steady state through the membrane:** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} = \text{constant}$$ **Step 2 — Integrate across the membrane** ($C_A$ varies linearly from $S \cdot p_{A1}$ to $S \cdot p_{A2}$): $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L} = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot S}{L}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$ **Step 3 — Define Permeability $P_M$:** $$P_M = D_{AB} \cdot S \quad \left[\frac{\text{kmol·m}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}}\right]$$ **Final Result — Membrane Permeation Flux:** $$\boxed{N_A = \frac{P_M}{L}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$$ **Step 4 — Membrane Mass Transfer Coefficient:** By analogy with convective mass transfer, $N_A = k_M (C_{A1} - C_{A2})$, we identify: $$k_M = \frac{P_M}{L}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always check whether the membrane is **porous** (Knudsen diffusion dominates, $N \propto 1/\sqrt{M}$) or **non-porous / polymeric** (solution-diffusion mechanism, $N \propto P_M/L$). State the assumption before solving. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 📊 Diagram 1: The Mass Transfer Triad — Analogy with Heat & Momentum Transfer ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Transport Phenomena
Three Pillars"] --> B["Momentum Transfer"] A --> C["Heat Transfer"] A --> D["Mass Transfer"] B --> B1["Newton's Law
tau = -mu * dV/dy"] C --> C1["Fourier's Law
q = -k * dT/dx"] D --> D1["Fick's Law
NA = -D * dCA/dx"] B --> B2["Property: mu
Kinematic viscosity"] C --> C2["Property: k, alpha
Thermal diffusivity"] D --> D2["Property: DAB
Mass diffusivity"] B2 --> E["Analogue"] C2 --> E D2 --> E E --> F["Reynolds <-> Re
Prandtl <-> Schmidt
Nusselt <-> Sherwood"] ``` --- ### 📊 Diagram 2: Equimolar Counter-Diffusion (EMCD) Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR Z1["Plane x = 0
CA = CA1
CB = CB1"] -->|NA flux left to right| Z2["Steady State
Film Thickness L"] Z2 -->|NB flux right to left| Z3["Plane x = L
CA = CA2
CB = CB2"] Z1 -.->|NA| Z3 Z3 -.->|-NA| Z1 Z2 --> Z4["NA + NB = 0
equimolar condition
N_A = -N_B"] ``` --- ### 📊 Diagram 3: Diffusion Through Stagnant Film B (Stefan's Problem) ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Liquid A
at x = 0
p_A1, p_B1 = 0"] --> A2["Stagnant Gas B Film
Thickness L"] A2 --> A3["Gas Stream
at x = L
p_A2 small or 0
p_B2 large"] A1 -->|Evaporation| A2 A2 -->|Diffusion of A through stagnant B| A3 A3 -->|Bulk flow sweeps A away| A4["Environment"] A2 --> A5["Key Equation
NA = DAB * P * pA1
/ RT * L * pBM"] ``` --- ### 📊 Diagram 4: Diffusion Through a Solid Membrane (Solution-Diffusion Model) ```mermaid flowchart LR M1["Upstream Gas
High p_A1"] --> M2["Membrane Surface
x = 0
CA1 = S * pA1"] M2 --> M3["Inside Membrane
Fickian Diffusion
DAB constant"] M3 --> M4["Membrane Surface
x = L
CA2 = S * pA2"] M4 --> M5["Downstream Gas
Low p_A2"] M3 --> M6["Governing Law
NA = PM * pA1 - pA2
divided by L"] M6 --> M7["Permeability
PM = DAB * S"] ``` --- ### 📊 Diagram 5: Classification Tree of Diffusion Processes ```mermaid flowchart TD R["Molecular Diffusion
Governed by Fick's Law"] --> R1["Steady State
dCA/dt = 0"] R --> R2["Unsteady State
Fick's 2nd Law"] R1 --> S1["Stationary Medium"] R1 --> S2["Moving Medium
Bulk Convection"] S1 --> T1["EMCD
NA + NB = 0
NA = DAB * C_A1 - C_A2 over L"] S1 --> T2["Stefan's Diffusion
NB = 0 Stagnant
NA = DAB * P * pA1 - pA2 over RTL * pBM"] S2 --> T3["Film Theory
NA = kC * CAs - CAbulk"] T3 --> T4["Analogous to
Newton's Cooling Law"] T1 --> U1["Distillation Columns"] T2 --> U2["Humidification, Drying"] T3 --> U3["Packed Absorption Towers"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU-2024-PCMET503-M4]` **State and explain Fick's First Law of Diffusion. How is it analogous to Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction?** **Model Answer:** Fick's First Law of Diffusion (1855) states that *the molar diffusive flux of a species in a binary mixture is proportional to its concentration gradient and the diffusion coefficient*, with flux flowing in the direction of decreasing concentration. $$J_A^{*} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ **Analogy to Fourier's Law:** | Aspect | Fourier's Law (Heat) | Fick's Law (Mass) | |---|---|---| | Driving potential | Temperature gradient $dT/dx$ | Concentration gradient $dC_A/dx$ | | Transported quantity | Heat $q$ (W/m²) | Mass flux $N_A$ (kmol/m²·s) | | Proportionality constant | Thermal conductivity $k$ | Diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$ | | Transient form | $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha \partial^2 T/\partial x^2$ | $\partial C_A/\partial t = D_{AB} \partial^2 C_A/\partial x^2$ | Both are linear constitutive laws for transport phenomena governed by the **second law of thermodynamics**. **(3 marks)** --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper KTU-2024-PCMET503-M4]` **What is the diffusion coefficient? Explain its dependence on temperature and pressure for gases and liquids.** **Model Answer:** The **diffusion coefficient $D_{AB}$** is the proportionality constant in Fick's Law that measures the rate at which species A diffuses through medium B. Units: **m²/s**. **For Gases (Chapman-Enskog theory):** - $D_{AB} \propto T^{3/2} / P$ — **increases with temperature**, **decreases with pressure**. - Reason: Higher T → greater molecular speeds; higher P → shorter mean free path. **For Liquids (Stokes-Einstein):** - $D_{AB} \propto T / \mu_B$ — **increases with temperature** (viscosity drops sharply) and **is nearly independent of pressure**. - Typical magnitude: $D_{liquid} \sim 10^{-9}$ m²/s (much smaller than gases). **Order of magnitude comparison:** $D_{gas} (10^{-5}) \gg D_{liquid} (10^{-9}) \gg D_{solid} (10^{-13})$ m²/s. **(3 marks)** --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module Internal Choice:** Solve **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Each has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PCMET503]` **(a)** Derive Stefan's equation for the steady-state diffusion of species A through a non-diffusing (stagnant) gas B over a distance L. Define and explain the significance of the log-mean partial pressure $p_{BM}$. **(b)** In a Stefan diffusion experiment, water (species A) evaporates from the bottom of a vertical tube of length 0.5 m maintained at 25 °C. Dry air (B) flows across the top so that $p_{A2} = 0$. The total pressure is 1 atm, and $D_{AB}$ for water vapor in air at 25 °C is $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. The vapor pressure of water at 25 °C is 3.17 kPa. Calculate the **steady-state molar flux of water** and the **rate of water evaporation** if the tube cross-section is 10 cm². --- ##### **Model Solution for Question A:** ### **Part (a) — Derivation of Stefan's Equation [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Statement of the problem:** Consider steady one-dimensional diffusion of A through stagnant B across a gas film of thickness L. **Step 2 — General flux equation with bulk flow:** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \frac{C_A}{C}(N_A + N_B)$$ **Step 3 — Apply $N_B = 0$ (stagnant B):** $$N_A = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} + \frac{C_A}{C} N_A$$ $$N_A \left(1 - \frac{C_A}{C}\right) = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx}$$ $$N_A \cdot \frac{C_B}{C} = -D_{AB} \frac{dC_A}{dx} \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to partial pressures using ideal gas law** ($C_A = p_A/RT$, $C = P/RT$, $C_B = p_B/RT$): $$N_A \cdot \frac{p_B}{P} = -\frac{D_{AB}}{RT}\frac{dp_A}{dx}$$ $$N_A \, dx = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, p_B} dp_A = -\frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT(P - p_A)} dp_A \quad \text{...(ii)}$$ **Step 5 — Integrate from $x = 0, p_A = p_{A1}$ to $x = L, p_A = p_{A2}$:** $$N_A \int_0^L dx = -\frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \int_{p_{A1}}^{p_{A2}} \frac{dp_A}{P - p_A}$$ $$N_A \cdot L = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \ln \!\left(\frac{P - p_{A1}}{P - p_{A2}}\right) = \frac{D_{AB} P}{RT} \ln \!\left(\frac{p_{B2}}{p_{B1}}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Introduce log-mean partial pressure:** Since $\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1}) = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{p_{BM}}$ where $p_{BM} = \dfrac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})}$: **Stefan's Final Equation:** $$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \, P}{RT \, L \, p_{BM}} (p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$$ **Significance of $p_{BM}$:** It accounts for the fact that the concentration of stagnant B (and hence the driving force for A) varies **non-linearly** through the film. $p_{BM}$ is the **logarithmic average** of $p_{B1}$ and $p_{B2}$, properly weighted for the non-linear variation. **[7 marks distributed: 1 for statement + 4 for derivation + 2 for $p_{BM}$]** --- ### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]** **Given:** - L = 0.5 m, T = 25 °C = 298 K, P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa - $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s - $p_{A1} = 3.17$ kPa (saturation), $p_{A2} = 0$ (dry air at top) - A = 10 cm² = $10 \times 10^{-4}$ m² = $10^{-3}$ m² - R = 8.314 J/mol·K **Step 1 — Compute $p_{B1}$ and $p_{B2}$:** $$p_{B1} = P - p_{A1} = 101.325 - 3.17 = 98.155 \text{ kPa}$$ $$p_{B2} = P - p_{A2} = 101.325 - 0 = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 2 — Log-mean partial pressure of B:** $$p_{BM} = \frac{p_{B2} - p_{B1}}{\ln(p_{B2}/p_{B1})} = \frac{101.325 - 98.155}{\ln(101.325/98.155)} = \frac{3.17}{\ln(1.03231)}$$ $$\ln(1.03231) = 0.03180$$ $$p_{BM} = \frac{3.17}{0.03180} = 99.69 \text{ kPa}$$ **Step 3 — Compute molar flux $N_A$:** $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB} \cdot P}{RT \cdot L \cdot p_{BM}}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$ $$N_A = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(101.325 \times 10^3)}{(8.314)(298)(0.5)(99.69 \times 10^3)} \times (3170 - 0)$$ **Numerator:** $(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(101325) = 2.5331$ **Denominator:** $(8.314)(298)(0.5)(99690) = 123{,}432{,}090$ **Step 4 — Combine:** $$N_A = \frac{2.5331 \times 3170}{1.234 \times 10^8} = \frac{8029.6}{1.234 \times 10^8}$$ $$\boxed{N_A = 6.51 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}}$$ **Step 5 — Rate of evaporation:** $$\dot{n}_A = N_A \cdot A = (6.51 \times 10^{-5})(10^{-3}) = 6.51 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kmol/s}$$ $$\boxed{\dot{m}_A = (6.51 \times 10^{-8})(18 \text{ kg/kmol}) = 1.17 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/s} = 4.22 \text{ g/h}}$$ **[7 marks: 1 for setup + 2 for $p_{BM}$ + 2 for flux + 2 for evaporation rate]** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PCMET503]` **(a)** Explain the phenomenon of equimolar counter-diffusion (EMCD) with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for the molar flux $N_A$ for a binary gas mixture at steady state. **(b)** Ammonia (A) and nitrogen (B) undergo equimolar counter-diffusion in a horizontal tube of length 0.4 m maintained at 27 °C and 1 atm total pressure. At one end, $p_{A1} = 60$ kPa, and at the other end, $p_{A2} = 20$ kPa. The binary diffusion coefficient $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate: (i) the molar flux $N_A$, and (ii) the partial pressure distribution $p_A(x)$ along the tube. --- ##### **Model Solution for Question B:** ### **Part (a) — EMCD Concept & Derivation [7 marks]** **Definition:** EMCD occurs when two species A and B diffuse in **opposite directions** at **equal molar rates**, such that the net molar flow at any cross-section is zero ($N_A + N_B = 0$). **Example:** Binary distillation at constant molal overflow — for every mole of A vaporizing, one mole of B condenses. **Derivation:** For EMCD at steady state with constant T and P, applying Fick's First Law: $$\begin{aligned} N_A &= -D_{AB}\frac{dC_A}{dx} \\ N_B &= -D_{AB}\frac{dC_B}{dx} \end{aligned}$$ Total concentration $C$ is constant, so $dC_A/dx = -dC_B/dx$. Integrating $N_A \, dx = -D_{AB} \, dC_A$ from $x=0$ to $x=L$: $$N_A = \frac{D_{AB}(C_{A1} - C_{A2})}{L}$$ Using ideal gas $C_A = p_A/RT$: $$\boxed{N_A = \frac{D_{AB}}{RTL}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})}$$ Note: **No log-mean correction is needed in EMCD**, unlike Stefan's law. **[7 marks]** --- ### **Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 marks]** **Given:** L = 0.4 m, T = 300 K, P = 101.325 kPa, $p_{A1} = 60$ kPa, $p_{A2} = 20$ kPa, $D_{AB} = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. **Step (i) — Molar flux:** $$N_A = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(60{,}000 - 20{,}000)}{(8.314)(300)(0.4)}$$ $$N_A = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^{-5})(40{,}000)}{997.68} = \frac{1.0}{997.68}$$ $$\boxed{N_A = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kmol/m}^2\cdot\text{s}}$$ **Step (ii) — Partial pressure distribution:** At steady state, $N_A$ is constant, so by linear variation of concentration in the gas: $$\frac{p_{A1} - p_A(x)}{p_{A1} - p_{A2}} = \frac{x}{L}$$ $$p_A(x) = p_{A1} - \frac{x}{L}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$$ $$p_A(x) = 60 - \frac{x}{0.4}(60 - 20) = 60 - 100x \text{ [kPa]}$$ **Sample values:** | x (m) | $p_A$ (kPa) | $p_B$ (kPa) | |---|---|---| | 0.0 | 60.0 | 41.325 | | 0.1 | 50.0 | 51.325 | | 0.2 | 40.0 | 61.325 | | 0.3 | 30.0 | 71.325 | | 0.4 | 20.0 | 81.325 | **Verification:** At each point, $p_A + p_B = P = 101.325$ kPa ✓ (constant total pressure). **Linear variation confirms EMCD** — $p_A$ decreases linearly with x; $p_B$ increases linearly. **[7 marks: 2 for flux + 3 for distribution derivation + 2 for verification table]** --- > [!WARNING] > ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > > 1. **Units mismatch (–2 to –3 marks):** Converting $p_A$ from kPa to Pa (multiply by 1000) is **mandatory** before plugging into SI equations when R = 8.314 J/mol·K. Many students lose marks by mixing kPa with m³·Pa/kmol·K. > > 2. **Forgetting log-mean $p_{BM}$ in Stefan's Law (–3 marks):** Using simple arithmetic mean $\frac{p_{B1}+p_{B2}}{2}$ instead of $p_{BM}$ gives a wrong answer. Always check whether $p_{B1}/p_{B2}$ ratio exceeds 1.5 — if yes, log-mean correction is significant. > > 3. **Mixing EMCD with Stefan's equation:** EMCD uses a simple linear gradient (no log-mean). Stagnant B (Stefan) requires $p_{BM}$ correction. > > 4. **Negative sign errors:** $N_A$ must come out **positive** when $p_{A1} > p_{A2}$. Drop the negative sign after integration. > > 5. **Cross-sectional area units:** Use m², not cm², in $N_A = \dot{n}/A$ conversions. > > 6. **Distinguishing $J_A^*$ (diffusive) from $N_A$ (total):** Examiners often test whether you know $N_A = J_A^* + x_A N_{tot}$. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ **Mass transfer** is the net movement of a chemical species from high to low chemical potential; dominated by **molecular diffusion** in quiescent media. ✅ **Fick's First Law:** $J_A^* = -D_{AB} \dfrac{dC_A}{dx}$ — analogous to Fourier's and Newton's laws. ✅ **Fick's Second Law:** $\dfrac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} = D_{AB} \dfrac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial x^2}$ — transient form. ✅ **Diffusion Coefficient $D_{AB}$** has units of **m²/s**; for gases $D \sim 10^{-5}$, for liquids $D \sim 10^{-9}$, for solids $D \sim 10^{-13}$. ✅ **Two canonical cases for steady-state diffusion:** - **EMCD:** $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{RTL}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ - **Stefan (stagnant B):** $N_A = \dfrac{D_{AB} \, P}{RTL \, p_{BM}}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ ✅ **Log-mean correction $p_{BM}$** is required **only for stagnant-B diffusion**, never for EMCD. ✅ **In a moving medium:** $N_A = -D_{AB}\dfrac{dC_A}{dx} + x_A(N_A + N_B)$ — bulk flow adds a convective component. ✅ **Membrane permeation:** $N_A = \dfrac{P_M}{L}(p_{A1} - p_{A2})$ where permeability $P_M = D_{AB} \cdot S$. ✅ **Chapman-Enskog** is the gold-standard formula for gas-phase $D_{AB}$; **Stokes-Einstein** for liquids. ✅ **Engineering applications to remember:** Distillation (EMCD), humidification (Stefan), gas separation (membrane), drying (solid diffusion). ---

topics.title: Convective Mass Transfer - Non-Dimensional Numbers - Analogy Between Heat and Mass Transfer - Correlations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Convective Mass Transfer - Non-Dimensional Numbers - Analogy Between Heat and Mass Transfer - Correlations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Convective Mass Transfer — The Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convective Mass Transfer** is the transport of mass (of a chemical species) between a surface and a moving fluid, or between two relatively moving fluids, driven by a **concentration gradient** and assisted by the bulk motion (convection) of the fluid.

The process is mathematically modeled as:

$$N_A = k_C (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})$$

where:
- $N_A$ = molar flux of species A (kmol/m²·s)
- $k_C$ = convective mass transfer coefficient (m/s)
- $C_{A,s}$ = concentration of A at the surface (kmol/m³)
- $C_{A,\infty}$ = concentration of A in the bulk fluid (kmol/m³)

> [!NOTE]
> This is **mathematically identical in form** to Newton's law of cooling ($q = h(T_s - T_\infty)$). This structural similarity is the foundation of the **Heat-Mass Transfer Analogy**, which is one of the most heavily tested topics in KTU Module 4.

### 1.2 The Concentration Boundary Layer

When a fluid flows past a surface from which a species is being transferred (by dissolution, sublimation, or chemical reaction), a **concentration boundary layer** develops, analogous to the thermal and hydrodynamic boundary layers.

| Boundary Layer Type | Driving Gradient | Characteristic Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| Hydrodynamic (velocity) | Velocity gradient | $\delta$ (momentum) |
| Thermal (temperature) | Temperature gradient | $\delta_T$ |
| Concentration (mass) | Concentration gradient | $\delta_C$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The relative thicknesses of $\delta$, $\delta_T$, and $\delta_C$ are governed by the **Prandtl number (Pr)** and **Schmidt number (Sc)**, respectively. When $\text{Sc} = \text{Pr}$, the thermal and concentration boundary layers are of identical thickness.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The Wet-Cup of Tea Analogy**: Imagine blowing across the surface of a hot cup of tea. The steam you see is water vapor (mass) being **carried away** by the moving air. The faster you blow, the more vigorously steam is removed. This is convective mass transfer in action — concentration differences (steam vs. dry air) drive mass out, and the wind's motion governs the rate.
>
> **The Cooling Parallel**: Replace the steam with heat, and you get convective heat transfer. The *structural physics* are nearly identical — only the name of the transported "thing" and the underlying property (concentration vs. temperature, diffusivity vs. thermal diffusivity) change.

### 1.4 Why Non-Dimensional Numbers?

Non-dimensional numbers collapse complex physical problems into compact, scalable relationships. They allow an experiment at one scale or fluid to predict behavior at another. The **three most important** non-dimensional groups in convective mass transfer are:

1. **Sherwood Number (Sh)** — the mass-transfer analog of the Nusselt number
2. **Schmidt Number (Sc)** — the mass-transfer analog of the Prandtl number
3. **Lewis Number (Le)** — the ratio of thermal to mass diffusivity

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Governing Differential Equation (Theoretical Foundation)

The convective mass transfer equation for a binary mixture of A (diffusing) in B (stagnant or flowing), in vector form, is:

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial t} + \vec{v} \cdot \nabla C_A = D_{AB} \nabla^2 C_A + R_A$$

In **steady, incompressible flow with no chemical reaction**, this reduces to:

$$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$$

This is **mathematically identical** to the thermal boundary layer equation:

$$u \frac{\partial T}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial T}{\partial y} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial y^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **one-to-one correspondence** between heat and mass transfer is:
> $T \leftrightarrow C_A$, $\alpha \leftrightarrow D_{AB}$, $k \leftrightarrow k_C$, $\text{Nu} \leftrightarrow \text{Sh}$, $\text{Pr} \leftrightarrow \text{Sc}$

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Mass Transfer Coefficient

The mass transfer coefficient is **not a property of the fluid alone**; it depends on:
- Geometry (flat plate, pipe, sphere, packed bed)
- Flow regime (laminar vs. turbulent)
- Fluid properties ($D_{AB}$, $\nu$, $\rho$)
- Position along the surface

It is determined from **experimental correlations** of the form:

$$\text{Sh} = f(\text{Re}, \text{Sc}, \text{geometry})$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The Complete Mass Transfer Numbers & Correlations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE**: In the table below, all absolute values and "divided by" notations use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. **Do not** use vertical pipes inside any cell.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Physical Meaning | Heat Transfer Analog |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sherwood Number (Sh)** | $Sh_L = \dfrac{k_C L}{D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of convective to diffusive mass transport | Nusselt Number (Nu) |
| 2 | **Schmidt Number (Sc)** | $Sc = \dfrac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity | Prandtl Number (Pr) |
| 3 | **Lewis Number (Le)** | $Le = \dfrac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \dfrac{Sc}{Pr}$ | Ratio of thermal to mass diffusivity | (No direct analog) |
| 4 | **Mass Peclet Number (Pe_M)** | $Pe_M = Re \cdot Sc = \dfrac{u L}{D_{AB}}$ | Ratio of convective to diffusive mass transport | Peclet Number (Pe) |
| 5 | **Mass Stanton Number (St_M)** | $St_M = \dfrac{Sh}{Re \cdot Sc} = \dfrac{k_C}{u}$ | Modified Stanton for mass transfer | Stanton Number (St) |
| 6 | **Mass Transfer Grashof (Gr_M)** | $Gr_M = \dfrac{g \beta_C \Delta C L^3}{\nu^2}$ | Buoyancy-to-viscous ratio for mass-driven flow | Grashof Number (Gr) |
| 7 | **Mass Transfer Rayleigh (Ra_M)** | $Ra_M = Gr_M \cdot Sc$ | Combined natural convection index | Rayleigh Number (Ra) |
| 8 | **Concentration Coefficient ($\beta_C$)** | $\beta_C = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\left(\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial C_A}\right)_T$ | Density change per unit concentration change | Thermal expansion coeff. ($\beta$) |

### 2.4 Heat-Mass Transfer Analogy — The Five Key Relationships

| Analogy Type | Mathematical Form | Validity / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Analogy** | $St_H = St_M = \dfrac{C_f}{2}$ | $\text{Pr} = \text{Sc} = 1$ only |
| **Prandtl Analogy** | $St_H = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1 + 5 \sqrt{C_f/2}(Pr - 1)}$ | $0.6 \le \text{Pr} \le 50$ |
| **Von Karman Analogy** | (Three-layer model with viscous sublayer, buffer, turbulent core) | $0.5 \le \text{Pr} \le 10$ |
| **Chilton-Colburn Analogy** | $j_H = j_M$ i.e. $St_H Pr^{2/3} = St_M Sc^{2/3}$ | $\,0.6 \le \text{Pr}, \text{Sc} \le 1600\,$ (most widely used) |
| **Generalized Analogy** | $St_H = \dfrac{C_f/2}{1 + \sqrt{C_f/2} \left[ a(Pr-1) + \log\left(\dfrac{(b\,Pr)^{2/3}}{1 + b\,Pr^{2/3}}\right) \right] }$ | Wider Pr range |

### 2.5 Mass Transfer Correlations (Most Tested in KTU)

| Geometry & Flow Regime | Correlation | Validity |
|---|---|---|
| Flat plate, laminar, local | $Sh_x = 0.332\, Re_x^{1/2}\, Sc^{1/3}$ | $Sc \ge 0.6$ |
| Flat plate, laminar, average | $\overline{Sh}_L = 0.664\, Re_L^{1/2}\, Sc^{1/3}$ | $Sc \ge 0.6$ |
| Flat plate, turbulent, local | $Sh_x = 0.0292\, Re_x^{0.8}\, Sc^{1/3}$ | $0.6 \le Sc \le 1600$ |
| Flat plate, turbulent, average | $\overline{Sh}_L = 0.037\, Re_L^{0.8}\, Sc^{1/3}$ | — |
| Pipe flow, laminar, Leighton | $Sh_D = 1.86\, (Re \cdot Sc \cdot D/L)^{1/3}$ | $(Re \cdot Sc \cdot D/L) > 10$ |
| Pipe flow, turbulent | $Sh_D = 0.023\, Re^{0.8}\, Sc^{0.33}$ | $Re > 10{,}000$ |
| Flow past single sphere | $Sh = 2 + 0.6\, Re^{1/2}\, Sc^{1/3}$ | $Re \cdot Sc > 0.2$ |
| Packed bed | $Sh = 1.17\, Re^{0.585}\, Sc^{1/3}$ | Ranz-Marshall type |
| Natural convection, vertical plate | $Sh_L = 0.59\, (Gr_M \cdot Sc)^{1/4}$ | $10^4 \le Gr_M Sc \le 10^9$ |
| Natural convection, vertical plate | $Sh_L = 0.13\, (Gr_M \cdot Sc)^{1/3}$ | $10^9 \le Gr_M Sc \le 10^{12}$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Chemical Reactors**: Predicting dissolution/sublimation rates of solid reactants
> - **Crystallization**: Estimating crystal growth rates
> - **Drying Operations**: Moisture removal from solids (food, paper, pharmaceuticals)
> - **Atmospheric Pollution**: Dispersion of pollutants from stacks
> - **Humidification/Dehumidification**: Cooling tower design
> - **Membrane Separations**: Using analogies to estimate boundary layer effects

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Schmidt Number

The Schmidt number arises naturally when non-dimensionalizing the convective mass transfer equation.

**Step 1: Write the dimensional equation for steady, 2-D flow**

$$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$$

**Step 2: Define dimensionless variables**

$$x^* = \frac{x}{L}, \quad y^* = \frac{y}{L}, \quad u^* = \frac{u}{U_\infty}, \quad v^* = \frac{v}{U_\infty}, \quad C_A^* = \frac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the partial derivatives**

$$\frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} = \frac{(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}{L}\frac{\partial C_A^*}{\partial x^*}$$

$$\frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2} = \frac{(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}{L^2}\frac{\partial^2 C_A^*}{\partial y^{*2}}$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the equation**

$$\frac{U_\infty (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}{L}\left[ u^* \frac{\partial C_A^*}{\partial x^*} + v^* \frac{\partial C_A^*}{\partial y^*} \right] = D_{AB} \frac{(C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}{L^2} \frac{\partial^2 C_A^*}{\partial y^{*2}}$$

**Step 5: Simplify the concentration difference and divide through**

$$u^* \frac{\partial C_A^*}{\partial x^*} + v^* \frac{\partial C_A^*}{\partial y^*} = \frac{D_{AB}}{U_\infty L} \frac{\partial^2 C_A^*}{\partial y^{*2}}$$

**Step 6: Identify the dimensionless group**

The coefficient of the RHS is the inverse of the **mass Peclet number**:

$$Pe_M = \frac{U_\infty L}{D_{AB}}$$

And since $Pe_M = Re \cdot Sc$:

$$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{\mu}{\rho D_{AB}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The Schmidt number compares the rate of momentum diffusion ($\nu$) to mass diffusion ($D_{AB}$). For gases $Sc \approx 0.6$–$1.5$, for liquids $Sc \approx 100$–$100{,}000$ (typically much higher).

### 3.2 Derivation: The Reynolds Analogy (Heat ↔ Mass)

Reynolds (1874) postulated that for **Pr = Sc = 1**, momentum, heat, and mass are transported at equal rates. The friction, heat, and mass transfer coefficients are directly related.

**Step 1: Momentum transfer (friction)**

The wall shear stress is:

$$\tau_w = \frac{C_f}{2} \rho U_\infty^2$$

This is also related to the momentum transfer "velocity" as:

$$\frac{\tau_w}{\rho U_\infty^2} = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

**Step 2: Heat transfer**

$$q = h (T_s - T_\infty) \implies St_H = \frac{h}{\rho c_p U_\infty} = \frac{q}{\rho c_p U_\infty (T_s - T_\infty)}$$

**Step 3: Mass transfer**

$$N_A = k_C (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \implies St_M = \frac{k_C}{U_\infty} = \frac{N_A}{U_\infty (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty})}$$

**Step 4: Reynolds' postulate (Pr = Sc = 1)**

Reynolds argued that the dimensionless flux of momentum, heat, and mass from a surface to a fluid are all equal:

$$St_H = St_M = \frac{C_f}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This analogy is highly restrictive** — it only holds when $Pr \approx Sc \approx 1$ (e.g., for air with many common gases at moderate temperatures). It is the *starting point* from which the more general analogies are built.

### 3.3 Derivation: The Chilton-Colburn Analogy (Most Important for KTU)

The Reynolds analogy fails for fluids with $Pr \ne 1$ (e.g., oils, liquid metals). Chilton and Colburn (1934) introduced empirical correction factors using the **$j$-factor**.

**Step 1: Define the $j$-factor for heat transfer**

$$j_H = St_H \cdot Pr^{2/3}$$

This exponent (2/3) is empirical — it accounts for the difference between the thermal and momentum boundary layer thicknesses.

**Step 2: Define the $j$-factor for mass transfer**

$$j_M = St_M \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$

**Step 3: Empirically establish the analogy**

Through extensive experiments:

$$\boxed{j_H = j_M}$$

That is:

$$St_H \cdot Pr^{2/3} = St_M \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$

**Step 4: Expand in terms of physical quantities**

$$\frac{h}{\rho c_p U_\infty} Pr^{2/3} = \frac{k_C}{U_\infty} Sc^{2/3}$$

$$h = \rho c_p U_\infty \left(\frac{k_C}{U_\infty}\right) \left(\frac{Sc}{Pr}\right)^{2/3}$$

$$h = \rho c_p \, k_C \left(\frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}}\right)^{2/3} = \rho c_p \, k_C \, Le^{2/3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **This is one of the most useful results in the entire module**: if you know the heat transfer coefficient $h$, you can directly obtain the mass transfer coefficient $k_C$, and vice versa, using only fluid properties. This is the workhorse of KTU problems on this topic.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Heat ↔ Mass Transfer Side-by-Side

| Phenomenon | Heat Transfer | Mass Transfer |
|---|---|---|
| Driving potential | $\Delta T = T_s - T_\infty$ | $\Delta C_A = C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}$ |
| Transported quantity | Heat ($q$, W) | Moles ($N_A$, kmol/s) |
| Flux law | $q = h\, \Delta T$ | $N_A = k_C\, \Delta C_A$ |
| Diffusivity | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ | $D_{AB}$ |
| Boundary layer | $\delta_T$ | $\delta_C$ |
| Dimensionless group | Nu, Pr, Re, St | Sh, Sc, Re, St_M |
| Boundary condition at wall | $T = T_s$ | $C_A = C_{A,s}$ (or $-D_{AB} \partial C_A/\partial y \vert_{y=0}$) |
| Governing equation | $u\, T_x + v\, T_y = \alpha\, T_{yy}$ | $u\, C_{A,x} + v\, C_{A,y} = D_{AB}\, C_{A,yy}$ |
| Analogy factor | $j_H = St_H\, Pr^{2/3}$ | $j_M = St_M\, Sc^{2/3}$ |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Typical Style

**Problem**: Air at $30^\circ C$ flows over a flat plate at $U_\infty = 5$ m/s. The plate is coated with naphthalene, which sublimes into the air. The plate is $L = 1$ m long. Given: $D_{AB}(\text{naphthalene-air}) = 6.1 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\nu(\text{air}) = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s. Calculate the average Sherwood number and the average mass transfer coefficient.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate the Reynolds number at the trailing edge**

$$Re_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{(5)(1)}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.125 \times 10^{5}$$

Since $Re_L < 5 \times 10^5$, the flow is **laminar** over the entire plate.

**Step 2: Calculate the Schmidt number**

$$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-5}}{6.1 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.62$$

**Step 3: Apply the laminar flat-plate correlation**

$$\overline{Sh}_L = 0.664\, Re_L^{1/2}\, Sc^{1/3}$$

$$\overline{Sh}_L = 0.664 \times (3.125 \times 10^5)^{0.5} \times (2.62)^{1/3}$$

$$\overline{Sh}_L = 0.664 \times 559.0 \times 1.378 = 511.5$$

**Step 4: Extract the mass transfer coefficient**

$$k_C = \frac{\overline{Sh}_L \cdot D_{AB}}{L} = \frac{511.5 \times 6.1 \times 10^{-6}}{1}$$

$$\boxed{k_C = 3.12 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}}$$

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concentration Boundary Layer — Block Architecture

The following diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the concentration boundary layer, the surface flux, and the relationship to the heat-transfer boundary layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Bulk Free Stream Region] --> A2[Concentration Boundary Layer delta C]
    A2 --> A3[Diffusive Sublayer]
    A3 --> A4[Surface of Plate Source of Species A]
    A4 --> A5[Convective Mass Flux N A equals kC times CA,s minus CA,inf]
    A1 --> B1[Hydrodynamic Boundary Layer delta]
    A1 --> B2[Thermal Boundary Layer delta T]
    A2 --> C1[Schmidt Number Sc governs relative thickness]
    B2 --> C2[Prandtl Number Pr governs relative thickness]
    C1 --> D1[If Sc greater than 1 then delta C less than delta]
    C1 --> D2[If Sc less than 1 then delta C greater than delta]
    C2 --> D3[If Pr greater than 1 then delta T less than delta]
    C2 --> D4[If Pr less than 1 then delta T greater than delta]
    A5 --> E1[Analogous to q equals h times Ts minus Tinf]
    B2 --> E1
```

### 4.2 Heat-Mass Transfer Analogy Flow Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Fluid Properties nu, alpha, DAB] --> F2[Compute Pr and Sc]
    F2 --> F3{Pr equals 1 AND Sc equals 1}
    F3 -- Yes --> F4[Use Reynolds Analogy StH equals StM equals Cf over 2]
    F3 -- No --> F5{Moderate Pr and Sc 0.6 to 50}
    F5 -- Yes --> F6[Use Prandtl Analogy]
    F5 -- No --> F7[Wide range Pr 0.6 to 1600]
    F7 --> F8[Use Chilton Colburn Analogy jH equals jM]
    F8 --> F9[StH Pr to the 2/3 equals StM Sc to the 2/3]
    F4 --> G1[Compute kC from h or vice versa]
    F6 --> G1
    F8 --> G1
    G1 --> G2[Apply to Engineering Design]
```

### 4.3 Methodology Roadmap — Solving a KTU Mass Transfer Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    H1[Read Problem Identify Geometry and Flow Type] --> H2[Compute Re using U L over nu]
    H2 --> H3{Re less than 5 times 10 to the 5}
    H3 -- Yes Laminar --> H4[Use Sh equals 0.664 Re to the 1/2 Sc to the 1/3]
    H3 -- No Turbulent --> H5[Use Sh equals 0.037 Re to the 0.8 Sc to the 1/3]
    H4 --> H6[Extract kC equals Sh DAB over L]
    H5 --> H6
    H6 --> H7[Compute N A equals kC times delta CA]
    H7 --> H8[Final Answer with Units m slash s for kC and kmol per m squared per s for N A]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    J1[Reynolds Analogy 1874 Simplest] --> J2[Limitation Pr equals Sc equals 1]
    J2 --> J3[Prandtl Analogy 1910 Added Buffer Layer]
    J3 --> J4[Limitation 0.6 less than Pr less than 50]
    J4 --> J5[Von Karman Analogy 1939 Three Layer Model]
    J5 --> J6[Limitation 0.5 less than Pr less than 10]
```
