## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Define Sherwood number and Schmidt number. What is their physical significance in convective mass transfer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Sherwood Number (Sh)**: It is the dimensionless number that represents the ratio of convective mass transfer to diffusive mass transfer, defined as $Sh_L = k_C L / D_{AB}$. It is the mass transfer analog of the Nusselt number (Nu).

> [!NOTE]
> **Schmidt Number (Sc)**: Defined as $Sc = \nu / D_{AB} = \mu / (\rho D_{AB})$, it is the ratio of momentum diffusivity to mass diffusivity. It is the mass transfer analog of the Prandtl number.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Significance**: Sh indicates the enhancement of mass transfer due to convection over pure diffusion. Sc indicates the relative ease of momentum and mass transport — for gases Sc ≈ 0.6–1.5 (comparable transport), for liquids Sc >> 1 (momentum diffuses much faster than mass, leading to a very thin concentration boundary layer).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **State the Chilton-Colburn analogy. How is it more useful than the Reynolds analogy?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Chilton-Colburn Analogy**: It states that the $j$-factors for heat and mass transfer are equal: $j_H = j_M$, i.e., $St_H \cdot Pr^{2/3} = St_M \cdot Sc^{2/3}$, where $j_H = St_H \cdot Pr^{2/3}$ and $j_M = St_M \cdot Sc^{2/3}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Advantage over Reynolds Analogy**: The Reynolds analogy is valid only when Pr = Sc = 1. The Chilton-Colburn analogy is valid over a much wider range ($0.6 \le Pr, Sc \le 1600$), making it applicable to gases, liquids, and oils. It is also more accurate because the $2/3$ exponent empirically corrects for the relative thickness difference between thermal/momentum and concentration/momentum boundary layers.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the dimensionless form of the convective mass transfer equation for steady, 2-D, incompressible flow past a flat plate. Clearly identify and define the Sherwood number, Schmidt number, and mass Peclet number. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A naphthalene plate of length 0.5 m is exposed to air at 1 atm and $25^\circ C$ flowing at $U_\infty = 3$ m/s. For naphthalene-air: $D_{AB} = 6.1 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, $\nu = 1.55 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $Sc = 2.57$. Assuming laminar flow, determine: (i) the average Sherwood number, (ii) the average mass transfer coefficient $k_C$, and (iii) the average molar flux $N_A$ if $C_{A,s} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6}$ kmol/m³ and $C_{A,\infty} = 0$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution A(a) — 7 marks:**

Starting from the dimensional equation:

$$u \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial C_A}{\partial y} = D_{AB} \frac{\partial^2 C_A}{\partial y^2}$$

Introduce the dimensionless variables:

$$x^* = \frac{x}{L}, \quad y^* = \frac{y}{L}, \quad u^* = \frac{u}{U_\infty}, \quad v^* = \frac{v}{U_\infty}, \quad C_A^* = \frac{C_A - C_{A,\infty}}{C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}}$$

Computing derivatives and substituting, after simplification we obtain:

$$u^* \frac{\partial C_A^*}{\partial x^*} + v^* \frac{\partial C_A^*}{\partial y^*} = \frac{1}{Pe_M}\frac{\partial^2 C_A^*}{\partial y^{*2}}$$

where the **mass Peclet number** is:

$$Pe_M = \frac{U_\infty L}{D_{AB}}$$

Defining $Sh_L = k_C L / D_{AB}$ and noting that $Pe_M = Re \cdot Sc$:

$$\boxed{Sh_L = Re_L \cdot Sc = \frac{U_\infty L}{D_{AB}}}$$

The three numbers are:

- $Sh_L = k_C L / D_{AB}$ — ratio of convective to diffusive mass flux
- $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$ — ratio of momentum to mass diffusivity
- $Pe_M = Re \cdot Sc$ — ratio of convective to diffusive transport

---

**Model Solution A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute the Reynolds number**

$$Re_L = \frac{U_\infty L}{\nu} = \frac{3 \times 0.5}{1.55 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.68 \times 10^4$$

Since $Re_L < 5 \times 10^5$, flow is **laminar**.

**Step 2: Apply the average Sherwood number correlation for laminar flow over a flat plate**

$$\overline{Sh}_L = 0.664 \cdot Re_L^{1/2} \cdot Sc^{1/3}$$

$$\overline{Sh}_L = 0.664 \times (9.68 \times 10^4)^{0.5} \times (2.57)^{1/3}$$

$$\overline{Sh}_L = 0.664 \times 311.1 \times 1.369 = 282.7$$

**Step 3: Extract the mass transfer coefficient**

$$k_C = \frac{\overline{Sh}_L \cdot D_{AB}}{L} = \frac{282.7 \times 6.1 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5}$$

$$k_C = 3.45 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute the molar flux**

$$N_A = k_C (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) = 3.45 \times 10^{-3} \times (4.5 \times 10^{-6} - 0)$$

$$N_A = 1.55 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kmol/m}^2 \text{·s}$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the concept of heat and mass transfer analogy. Derive the Chilton-Colburn analogy and discuss its range of validity. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Air at $50^\circ C$ flows over a wet flat plate of length 0.4 m at $U_\infty = 4$ m/s. Given: $\nu = 1.798 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $D_{AB}(\text{water vapor-air}) = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s, $k(\text{air}) = 0.0274$ W/m·K, $c_p = 1006$ J/kg·K. Using the Chilton-Colburn analogy, determine the mass transfer coefficient $k_C$ if the average heat transfer coefficient is $h = 28$ W/m²·K. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution B(a) — 7 marks:**

The heat-mass transfer analogy stems from the structural identity of their governing equations and boundary conditions. Momentum, heat, and mass are transported by **eddy diffusion** with analogous turbulent mechanisms.

**Derivation:**

For a turbulent boundary layer, the **heat flux** at the wall is:

$$q = h (T_s - T_\infty) \implies St_H = \frac{h}{\rho c_p U_\infty}$$

The **mass flux** is:

$$N_A = k_C (C_{A,s} - C_{A,\infty}) \implies St_M = \frac{k_C}{U_\infty}$$

Chilton and Colburn empirically correlated the friction, heat, and mass transfer data and proposed the $j$-factor concept:

$$j_H = St_H \cdot Pr^{2/3}, \quad j_M = St_M \cdot Sc^{2/3}$$

Their famous postulate:

$$\boxed{j_H = j_M \implies St_H \cdot Pr^{2/3} = St_M \cdot Sc^{2/3}}$$

**Range of Validity**: $0.6 \le Pr, Sc \le 1600$ — covers most engineering fluids (gases, water, oils). The exponent 2/3 corrects for the difference in the relative thickness of the thermal (or concentration) boundary layer with respect to the hydrodynamic layer.

---

**Model Solution B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute the Prandtl number**

$$Pr = \frac{\nu}{\alpha} = \frac{\nu \rho c_p}{k}$$

$$\alpha = \frac{k}{\rho c_p} = \frac{0.0274}{1.093 \times 1006} = 2.49 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

$$Pr = \frac{1.798 \times 10^{-5}}{2.49 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.722$$

**Step 2: Compute the Schmidt number**

$$Sc = \frac{\nu}{D_{AB}} = \frac{1.798 \times 10^{-5}}{3.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.599$$

**Step 3: Compute the Lewis number**

$$Le = \frac{\alpha}{D_{AB}} = \frac{Sc}{Pr} = \frac{0.599}{0.722} = 0.830$$

**Step 4: Apply the Chilton-Colburn analogy**

$$h = \rho c_p \, k_C \left(\frac{Sc}{Pr}\right)^{2/3} = \rho c_p \, k_C \, Le^{2/3}$$

$$k_C = \frac{h}{\rho c_p \cdot Le^{2/3}}$$

$$k_C = \frac{28}{1.093 \times 1006 \times (0.830)^{2/3}}$$

$$k_C = \frac{28}{1099.6 \times 0.880}$$

$$\boxed{k_C = 2.90 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m/s}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to check flow regime**: Many students blindly apply a turbulent correlation even when $Re < 5 \times 10^5$. Always verify $Re$ first.
>
> 2. **Confusing Sherwood with Peclet**: $Sh = k_C L / D_{AB}$ — it includes the mass transfer coefficient. $Pe_M = U_\infty L / D_{AB}$ — it includes velocity. They are not interchangeable.
>
> 3. **Wrong exponent on $Sc$**: For low Sc fluids (gas systems), $Sc^{1/3}$ is used. For very high Sc liquids, sometimes $Sc^{1/2}$ appears in some correlations. Read the correlation carefully.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Reynolds and Chilton-Colburn**: Reynolds gives $St_H = St_M = C_f/2$ (only for Pr = Sc = 1). Students often write $St_H = St_M$ for any fluid — **this is wrong** for Pr ≠ 1.
>
> 5. **Units mismatch in $N_A$**: $k_C$ has units m/s, $C_A$ has units kmol/m³, so $N_A$ has units kmol/m²·s. Keep conversions consistent.
>
> 6. **In the Chilton-Colburn analogy formula $h = \rho c_p k_C (Sc/Pr)^{2/3}$**, students often mistakenly use $D_{AB}/\alpha$ (the reciprocal). The correct ratio is $Sc/Pr$ because $Sc/Pr = D_{AB}/\alpha$ — wait, check this carefully: $Sc/Pr = (ν/D_{AB})/(ν/α) = \alpha/D_{AB} = 1/Le$. So the analog form is $h = \rho c_p k_C \cdot Le^{-2/3}$ as well, which equals $h = \rho c_p k_C (Pr/Sc)^{2/3}$. **Always derive from $St_H Pr^{2/3} = St_M Sc^{2/3}$**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 4**
>
> - Convective mass transfer is governed by $N_A = k_C \Delta C_A$, structurally identical to Newton's law of cooling.
> - **Three pillars**: Sh (analog of Nu), Sc (analog of Pr), and Le (ratio $\alpha/D_{AB}$).
> - **Schmidt Number**: $Sc = \nu / D_{AB}$. For gases ≈ 1, for liquids >> 1.
> - **Sherwood Number**: $Sh_L = k_C L / D_{AB}$. Always appears in correlations.
> - **Reynolds Analogy** ($St_H = St_M = C_f/2$) is valid **only for Pr = Sc = 1**.
> - **Chilton-Colburn Analogy** ($St_H Pr^{2/3} = St_M Sc^{2/3}$) is the workhorse — valid for $0.6 \le Pr, Sc \le 1600$.
> - **Flat plate laminar**: $\overline{Sh}_L = 0.664 Re_L^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$.
> - **Flat plate turbulent**: $\overline{Sh}_L = 0.037 Re_L^{0.8} Sc^{1/3}$.
> - **Pipe laminar (Leighton)**: $Sh_D = 1.86 (Re \cdot Sc \cdot D/L)^{1/3}$.
> - **Pipe turbulent**: $Sh_D = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Sc^{0.33}$.
> - **Sphere (Ranz-Marshall)**: $Sh = 2 + 0.6 Re^{1/2} Sc^{1/3}$.
> - **Lewis number** $Le = \alpha/D_{AB} = Sc/Pr$ — when Le = 1, the concentration and thermal boundary layers coincide.
> - The **$j$-factor** ($j_H$, $j_M$) is the bridge between friction, heat, and mass transfer.
> - In Chilton-Colburn, the analogy becomes: $h = \rho c_p k_C (Pr/Sc)^{2/3}$ or equivalently $h = \rho c_p k_C (1/Le)^{2/3}$.
> - **Always check the flow regime** by computing $Re_L$ first.
> - **Units must be SI throughout**: m, s, kg, kmol, W, J, K.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cooling of Electronic Equipments - Need - Various Methods - Influencing Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cooling of Electronic Equipments - Need - Various Methods - Influencing Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Cooling of Electronic Equipments** is a specialized branch of **thermal management engineering** that deals with the controlled removal of heat generated by active and passive electronic components (e.g., ICs, transistors, power modules, GPUs, CPUs) so that every component operates below its maximum permissible **junction temperature (T<sub>j</sub>)**, ensuring reliability, performance, and longevity of the device.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Heat and Mass Transfer syllabus, electronic cooling is treated as a **conjugate heat transfer problem** that combines **conduction** (through solid paths such as PCBs, heat sinks, and packages), **convection** (to ambient air, dielectric fluids, or coolants), and **radiation** (from hot surfaces to surroundings). The governing objective is to minimize the **overall thermal resistance (R<sub>th</sub>)** between the heat-generating junction and the ultimate heat sink (ambient).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The module emphasizes that electronic cooling is **NOT** a "passive" engineering choice — it is a **design-level constraint** that directly influences the choice of component packaging, PCB layout, fan selection, enclosure design, and the operating ambient envelope.

**Standard Metrics Used in Electronic Cooling:**

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|--------|--------|------|--------------|
| Junction Temperature | T<sub>j</sub> | °C or K | Hottest point inside the semiconductor die |
| Ambient Temperature | T<sub>a</sub> | °C or K | Temperature of the surrounding cooling medium |
| Thermal Resistance | R<sub>th</sub> or θ | °C/W or K/W | Resistance to heat flow |
| Power Dissipation | P or Q | W | Heat generated by the component |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | hours | Reliability index |
| Junction-to-Ambient Resistance | θ<sub>ja</sub> | °C/W | Critical parameter for PCB-mounted devices |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview / Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Body's Cooling System**
> 
> Think of an electronic circuit like a **human body running a marathon**. The components (muscles) generate heat while working. Without sweat (passive air convection) or drinking water (forced liquid cooling), the body would overheat and fail (thermal runaway). A **heat sink with fins** is like a **radiator grille on a car engine** — it increases surface area to dump heat faster. A **fan** is like **wind blowing across your face on a hot day** — it forces convection. And a **thermoelectric cooler (Peltier module)** is like an **air-conditioner patch** — it actively pumps heat from a colder side to a hotter side using electricity.

The **key insight** is that heat always flows from a higher temperature to a lower temperature, and the rate of this flow is governed by the **thermal resistance** of the path. The designer's job is to make this path as "wide" (low resistance) as possible, or to use **active means** (fans, pumps, refrigerators) to enhance the driving temperature difference.

## 1.3 Why Electronic Cooling is Essential — The "Need"

Modern electronic devices face a critical thermal challenge due to **Moore's Law-driven miniaturization** combined with **increasing switching frequencies** and **higher power densities**. The following table summarizes the core reasons why cooling is mandatory:

| Need | Engineering Consequence |
|------|--------------------------|
| Reliability | Every 10 °C rise in T<sub>j</sub> approximately **halves** the component life (Arrhenius relation) |
| Performance | Semiconductor switching speed drops; leakage current rises exponentially with T<sub>j</sub> |
| Safety | Thermal runaway can cause fire, smoke, or explosion in lithium-ion systems |
| Efficiency | Power devices (MOSFETs, IGBTs) lose efficiency when hot due to increased on-resistance |
| Miniaturization | More power in smaller packages = higher heat flux (W/m²) |
| Regulatory Compliance | Standards like **IPC-9592**, **UL 60950**, and **RoHS** impose thermal limits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Equation (must be memorized for KTU exams):**
> 
> $$T_j = T_a + (P \times R_{th,ja})$$
> 
> This single equation encapsulates the entire philosophy of electronic cooling. The designer controls **P** (by system architecture), **R<sub>th,ja</sub>** (by packaging, heat sink, airflow), while **T<sub>a</sub>** is set by the environment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Cooling Methods

Electronic cooling methods are broadly classified based on the **heat transfer mode** employed and whether they use **passive** or **active** driving forces.

### 2.1.1 By Heat Transfer Mode

**A. Conduction-Based Cooling (Solid Path)**
- Heat conducted from the die → through the package → through the PCB → to a heat sink or chassis.
- Governed by **Fourier's Law of Conduction**:
  $$q = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$
- Materials chosen: **copper (k ≈ 400 W/m·K)**, **aluminum (k ≈ 205 W/m·K)**, **thermal interface materials (TIMs)** with k ranging from **1 to 12 W/m·K**, **graphite**, **diamond (k ≈ 1000 W/m·K)**.

**B. Convection-Based Cooling (Fluid Path)**
- **Natural (Free) Convection:** Buoyancy-driven flow; h ≈ **2–25 W/m²·K** for air.
- **Forced Convection:** Fan or blower-driven; h ≈ **10–250 W/m²·K** for air.
- **Forced Liquid Cooling:** Pump-driven; h ≈ **500–20,000 W/m²·K**.

**C. Radiation Cooling**
- Governed by **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:
  $$q = \epsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$
- Where **ε** is emissivity, **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴** is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant.
- Becomes significant at high temperatures (T > 400 K) and in vacuum (space electronics).

**D. Phase-Change Cooling**
- Uses latent heat of vaporization of refrigerants.
- Examples: **Heat pipes**, **vapor chambers**, **two-phase loops**.

### 2.1.2 By Driving Force (Passive vs. Active)

| Category | Description | Examples |
|----------|-------------|----------|
| **Passive Cooling** | No external power; relies on natural processes | Heat sinks, heat spreaders, natural convection fins, heat pipes |
| **Active Cooling** | Uses external power to enhance heat removal | Forced air fans, blowers, liquid pumps, thermoelectric coolers, refrigeration cycles |
| **Hybrid** | Combines passive + active | Heat sink with fan (forced air), heat pipe with fan assist |

## 2.2 The Thermal Resistance Network (Electric Analogy)

This is **the single most important conceptual model** for KTU exams. Heat flow is analogous to current flow, temperature difference is analogous to voltage, and thermal resistance is analogous to electrical resistance.

**Electrical Analogy Mapping:**

| Electrical Domain | Thermal Domain |
|-------------------|----------------|
| Voltage (V) | Temperature Difference (ΔT) |
| Current (I, amperes) | Heat Flow Rate (Q, watts) |
| Resistance (R, ohms) | Thermal Resistance (R<sub>th</sub>, °C/W) |
| Capacitance (C) | Thermal Capacitance (C<sub>th</sub>, J/K) |

**The Master Thermal Resistance Equation:**

$$T_j - T_a = Q \times R_{th,ja} = Q \times (R_{th,jc} + R_{th,cs} + R_{th,sa})$$

Where:
- **R<sub>th,jc</sub>** = Junction-to-Case thermal resistance (package internal)
- **R<sub>th,cs</sub>** = Case-to-Sink thermal resistance (TIM + contact)
- **R<sub>th,sa</sub>** = Sink-to-Ambient thermal resistance (convection from fins)

## 2.3 Detailed Analysis of Major Cooling Methods

### 2.3.1 Heat Sinks (Most Common in KTU Context)

A heat sink is an extended surface (fins) attached to the component to enhance convective heat transfer. The performance metric is the **fin efficiency (η<sub>f</sub>)** and **overall surface efficiency (η<sub>o</sub>)**.

**Fin Efficiency Derivation (Adiabatic Tip Assumption):**

For a straight rectangular fin of length L, cross-section A<sub>c</sub>, perimeter P, and thermal conductivity k:

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$$

where 

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}}$$

The total heat dissipated is:

$$Q = \eta_o \cdot h \cdot A_t \cdot (T_b - T_\infty)$$

where A<sub>t</sub> is the total surface area (fins + base) and η<sub>o</sub> is the **overall surface efficiency**:

$$\eta_o = 1 - \frac{A_f}{A_t}(1 - \eta_f)$$

### 2.3.2 Forced Air Cooling

Uses fans/blowers to increase h. The **Nusselt Number (Nu)** correlations used in KTU problems are:

**For Flow Over a Flat Plate (Laminar):**
$$Nu_L = 0.664 \, Re_L^{1/2} \, Pr^{1/3}$$

**For Flow Over a Flat Plate (Turbulent):**
$$Nu_L = 0.037 \, Re_L^{4/5} \, Pr^{1/3}$$

**For Flow in a Parallel-Plate Channel (Between PCBs):**
$$Nu_D = 7.54 + \frac{0.03(x/D)Re_D \, Pr}{1 + 0.016[(x/D)Re_D \, Pr]^{2/3}}$$

### 2.3.3 Liquid Cooling

Used in high-power applications (data centers, EV battery packs, IGBT modules). Coolants include **water, glycol mixtures, dielectric fluids (fluorinert), mineral oil**.

**Dittus-Boelter Correlation (Turbulent Flow in Pipes, Re > 10,000, 0.7 < Pr < 160):**

$$Nu_D = 0.023 \, Re_D^{0.8} \, Pr^n$$

where **n = 0.4** for heating and **n = 0.3** for cooling of the fluid.

**Sieder-Tate Correlation (with viscosity correction):**

$$Nu_D = 0.027 \, Re_D^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu_b}{\mu_s}\right)^{0.14}$$

### 2.3.4 Heat Pipes

A heat pipe is a sealed device containing a working fluid that transports heat by **evaporation at the hot end** and **condensation at the cold end**. It has extremely high effective thermal conductivity (up to **100,000 W/m·K** in copper-water heat pipes).

**Heat Transport Capacity (Axial):**

$$Q_{max} = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg}$$

where ṁ is the mass flow rate of the working fluid and h<sub>fg</sub> is the latent heat of vaporization.

**Capillary Limit (most common operational limit):**

$$Q_{cap,max} = \frac{\rho_l \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \sigma_{lv} \cdot d_e \cdot A_w}{4\mu_l \cdot L_{eff}} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{r_c} - \frac{\rho_l \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta}{\sigma_{lv}}\right)^{-1}$$

### 2.3.5 Thermoelectric (Peltier) Coolers

Solid-state devices using the Peltier effect. They **pump** heat from the cold side to the hot side when DC current flows. Used in precision temperature control of laser diodes, CCD sensors.

**Heat Pumped (at Cold Side):**
$$Q_c = \alpha T_c I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T$$

**Coefficient of Performance (COP):**
$$COP = \frac{Q_c}{P_{input}} = \frac{\alpha T_c I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T}{\alpha (T_h - T_c) I + I^2 R}$$

where α is the Seebeck coefficient, R is the electrical resistance, and K is the thermal conductance of the module.

## 2.4 Influencing Factors in Electronic Cooling

The performance of any cooling system is dictated by a set of **interacting design and environmental parameters**. These are commonly tested in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions.

| Factor | Influence on Design |
|--------|---------------------|
| **Power Dissipation (Q)** | Direct proportionality: higher Q demands lower R<sub>th,ja</sub> |
| **Ambient Temperature (T<sub>a</sub>)** | Sets the baseline; higher T<sub>a</sub> reduces ΔT available |
| **Component Density** | Local hot spots; requires careful PCB layout and thermal vias |
| **Airflow Velocity (V)** | Increases h; for forced convection h ∝ V<sup>0.6 to 0.8</sup> |
| **Altitude / Pressure** | Lower air density at altitude reduces h (matters for avionics) |
| **Humidity** | Affects condensation, reliability, and surface emissivity |
| **Dust Contamination** | Clogs fins → degrades h over time |
| **Vibration and Shock** | Mechanical integrity of fans, pumps, and heat sink bonds |
| **Acoustic Constraints** | Limits fan RPM and size in consumer products |
| **Size and Weight Constraints** | Drives choice between heat sink vs. liquid cooling |
| **Cost** | Determines feasibility of exotic solutions (vapor chambers, TECs) |
| **Orientation** | Affects buoyancy-driven flow in natural convection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Favorite):**
> 
> For **natural convection** in air, use **h ≈ 5 to 15 W/m²·K**.
> For **forced convection** in air (typical electronic enclosures), use **h ≈ 25 to 250 W/m²·K**.
> For **forced convection** in water, use **h ≈ 500 to 20,000 W/m²·K**.
> 
> This 10× to 100× jump in h is why **liquid cooling** is preferred for heat fluxes > 1000 W/cm² (e.g., modern GPUs, data center CPUs).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Application / Context | Units |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_j = T_a + Q \cdot R_{th,ja}$ | Master equation for junction temperature | T in °C/K, Q in W, R in °C/W |
| 2 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ | Conduction thermal resistance (1-D slab) | L in m, k in W/m·K, A in m² |
| 3 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ | Convection thermal resistance | h in W/m²·K, A in m² |
| 4 | $Q = \eta_o h A_t (T_b - T_\infty)$ | Heat sink total dissipation | Q in W |
| 5 | $\eta_f = \dfrac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}$, $m=\sqrt{hP/kA_c}$ | Fin efficiency | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $Nu_L = 0.664 Re_L^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$ | Laminar flat plate | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $Nu_L = 0.037 Re_L^{4/5} Pr^{1/3}$ | Turbulent flat plate | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $Nu_D = 0.023 Re_D^{0.8} Pr^n$ | Dittus-Boelter (internal flow) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $q = \epsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | Radiation heat flux | q in W/m² |
| 10 | $Q = \dot{m} h_{fg}$ | Heat pipe axial transport | Q in W |
| 11 | $Q_c = \alpha T_c I - 0.5 I^2 R - K \Delta T$ | Thermoelectric cooler | Q<sub>c</sub> in W |
| 12 | $\Delta P = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho V^2}{2}$ | Fan / pump pressure drop (sizing) | Pa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The thermal resistance network is the **standard language** used by IC manufacturers (Texas Instruments, Intel, ON Semiconductor) in their datasheets. Every component datasheet lists **θ<sub>ja</sub>, θ<sub>jc</sub>, θ<sub>cs</sub>** curves. The KTU expects you to be able to **read these values** and **compute the safe operating power** for any given ambient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Thermal Resistance of a Composite Wall (PCB + Heat Sink Base)

**Problem Setup:** A power IC dissipates **Q = 25 W**. It is mounted on a PCB (k<sub>PCB</sub> = 0.3 W/m·K, thickness t = 1.6 mm) which is bonded to an aluminum heat sink (k<sub>Al</sub> = 205 W/m·K, thickness t = 5 mm, footprint A = 36 cm² = 36 × 10⁻⁴ m²). Find the **total conduction resistance** of this stack-up.

### Step 1: Identify the Heat Flow Path

Heat flows in **one dimension** (normal to the layers). The three resistances are in **series**.

### Step 2: Compute PCB Resistance

$$R_{th,PCB} = \frac{t_{PCB}}{k_{PCB} \cdot A}$$

Substituting values:

$$R_{th,PCB} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{0.3 \times 36 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$R_{th,PCB} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{1.08 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$R_{th,PCB} = 1.481 \, ^\circ C/W$$

> **Logic:** The PCB is a **poor conductor** (k is 700× lower than aluminum), so it dominates the conduction path. This is why high-power components use **thermal vias and direct heat sink attachment**.

### Step 3: Compute Aluminum Heat Sink Base Resistance

$$R_{th,Al} = \frac{t_{Al}}{k_{Al} \cdot A}$$

$$R_{th,Al} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{205 \times 36 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$R_{th,Al} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{0.738}$$

$$R_{th,Al} = 6.78 \times 10^{-3} \, ^\circ C/W$$

> **Logic:** Aluminum's high k makes its resistance **negligible** compared to the PCB. This justifies modeling the heat sink base as **isothermal** in subsequent convection calculations.

### Step 4: Sum the Series Resistances

$$R_{th,cond,total} = R_{th,PCB} + R_{th,Al}$$

$$R_{th,cond,total} = 1.481 + 0.00678$$

$$R_{th,cond,total} \approx 1.488 \, ^\circ C/W$$

### Step 5: Temperature Rise Due to Conduction Alone

$$\Delta T_{cond} = Q \times R_{th,cond,total} = 25 \times 1.488 = 37.2 \, ^\circ C$$

> **Interpretation:** If the heat sink base is at 50 °C, the junction is at **87.2 °C** — dangerously close to the typical 100 °C limit. The remaining cooling must be done by **convection from the fins**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Sink Sizing for Natural Convection

**Problem Setup:** The same IC (Q = 25 W) must be cooled to keep T<sub>j</sub> < 100 °C in an ambient of T<sub>a</sub> = 40 °C. The IC's internal R<sub>th,jc</sub> = 1.5 °C/W. Find the **required heat sink resistance R<sub>th,sa</sub>** assuming natural convection.

### Step 1: Apply the Master Junction Equation

$$T_j = T_a + Q \cdot (R_{th,jc} + R_{th,cs} + R_{th,sa})$$

### Step 2: Rearrange for R<sub>th,sa</sub>

$$R_{th,sa} = \frac{T_j - T_a}{Q} - R_{th,jc} - R_{th,cs}$$

### Step 3: Substitute Values

Assume R<sub>th,cs</sub> = 0.1 °C/W (typical for thermal grease + clip):

$$R_{th,sa} = \frac{100 - 40}{25} - 1.5 - 0.1$$

$$R_{th,sa} = \frac{60}{25} - 1.6$$

$$R_{th,sa} = 2.4 - 1.6 = 0.8 \, ^\circ C/W$$

### Step 4: Compute Required Heat Transfer Coefficient

For natural convection, h ≈ 10 W/m²·K. Therefore:

$$R_{th,sa} = \frac{1}{h \cdot A_t}$$

$$A_t = \frac{1}{h \cdot R_{th,sa}} = \frac{1}{10 \times 0.8} = 0.125 \, m^2 = 1250 \, cm^2$$

### Step 5: Design Fin Array

Using 10 vertical fins of dimensions 5 cm × 5 cm × 5 cm, with 0.5 cm spacing:

- Fin area per fin (both sides + tip): A<sub>f</sub> = 2 × (5 × 5) + (0.5 × 5) = 50 + 2.5 = 52.5 cm²
- Total fin area: 10 × 52.5 = 525 cm²
- Base area between fins: 9 gaps × 0.5 cm × 5 cm = 22.5 cm²
- Total surface area: A<sub>t</sub> = 525 + 22.5 = 547.5 cm²

This is **less than 1250 cm²**, so natural convection is **insufficient**. The designer must either:
- Increase fin count to 20+, or
- Switch to **forced convection** (h ≈ 50 W/m²·K → A<sub>t</sub> = 250 cm², achievable with 10 fins).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fin Efficiency for a Pin Fin Array

**Problem Setup:** Cylindrical pin fins, diameter D = 5 mm, length L = 25 mm, k = 200 W/m·K (aluminum), h = 30 W/m²·K (forced air).

### Step 1: Compute the Fin Parameter m

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}}$$

For a cylinder, P = πD, A<sub>c</sub> = πD²/4:

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{h \cdot \pi D}{k \cdot \pi D^2/4}} = \sqrt{\frac{4h}{kD}}$$

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 30}{200 \times 0.005}} = \sqrt{\frac{120}{1}} = \sqrt{120} \approx 10.95 \, m^{-1}$$

### Step 2: Compute mL

$$mL = 10.95 \times 0.025 = 0.2738$$

### Step 3: Compute Fin Efficiency

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} = \frac{\tanh(0.2738)}{0.2738} = \frac{0.2673}{0.2738} = 0.9763 \approx 97.6\%$$

> **Interpretation:** The fin is **highly efficient** (η<sub>f</sub> > 95%) because mL is small. This means **increasing L further** would not significantly improve performance — we are approaching the **diminishing returns** regime.

### Step 4: Overall Surface Efficiency (10 pins + base)

With 10 pins and base area A<sub>b</sub> = 20 cm², fin area per pin A<sub>f</sub> = πDL + πD²/4 = π(0.005)(0.025) + π(0.005)²/4 ≈ 3.93 × 10⁻⁴ + 1.96 × 10⁻⁵ ≈ 4.13 × 10⁻⁴ m²

Total fin area: A<sub>f,total</sub> = 10 × 4.13 × 10⁻⁴ = 4.13 × 10⁻³ m²

Total area: A<sub>t</sub> = A<sub>f,total</sub> + A<sub>b</sub> = 4.13 × 10⁻³ + 2.0 × 10⁻³ = 6.13 × 10⁻³ m²

$$\eta_o = 1 - \frac{A_f}{A_t}(1 - \eta_f) = 1 - \frac{4.13 \times 10^{-3}}{6.13 \times 10^{-3}}(1 - 0.9763)$$

$$\eta_o = 1 - 0.6737 \times 0.0237 = 1 - 0.0160 = 0.984$$

### Step 5: Total Heat Dissipated

$$Q = \eta_o \cdot h \cdot A_t \cdot (T_b - T_\infty)$$

$$Q = 0.984 \times 30 \times 6.13 \times 10^{-3} \times (T_b - T_\infty)$$

$$Q = 0.181 \times (T_b - T_\infty) \, W$$

If the base is at 70 °C and ambient is 40 °C:

$$Q = 0.181 \times 30 = 5.43 \, W \text{ per 10 pins}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Cooling Methods)

| Method | Typical h (W/m²·K) | Heat Flux Handled (W/cm²) | Pros | Cons | KTU Typical Use |
|--------|---------------------|----------------------------|------|------|-----------------|
| Natural Convection + Heat Sink | 5–15 | 0.1–0.5 | No power, silent, reliable | Bulky, limited | Low-power PCBs, linear regulators |
| Forced Air (Fan + Heat Sink) | 25–250 | 0.5–5 | Compact, cost-effective | Noise, dust, fan failure | PCs, laptops, telecom |
| Forced Liquid Cooling | 500–20,000 | 5–1000 | Highest heat flux, uniform | Pump, leak risk, cost | Data centers, EVs, IGBTs |
| Heat Pipe | Effective k up to 10⁵ W/m·K | 1–50 | Passive, no pump, very flat | Wick limits, orientation | Laptops, space electronics |
| Vapor Chamber | 2D equivalent of heat pipe | 5–100 | Isothermalizes 2D area | Cost, weight | High-end GPUs, AI chips |
| Thermoelectric (Peltier) | Active pumping | 0.1–10 | Precise T control, no moving parts | Low COP, waste heat | Laser diodes, IR sensors |
| Immersion Cooling | 1000–50,000 | 10–5000 | Extreme heat flux, silent | Fluid cost, sealing | Crypto mining, HPC |
| Jet Impingement | 10,000–100,000 | 100–10,000 | Highest single-phase h | Nozzle complexity | Turbine blades, leading edge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU Examinations:** When asked to **choose** a cooling method, use the following decision logic:
> 
> 1. Power < 5 W → Natural convection heat sink
> 2. Power 5–100 W → Forced air (fan + heat sink)
> 3. Power 100–1000 W → Liquid cooling (cold plate)
> 4. Power > 1000 W or very localized → Phase change / two-phase cooling
> 5. Precision T control needed → Thermoelectric cooler
> 6. Isothermal spreading required → Vapor chamber

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Maximum Heat Transport in a Heat Pipe

**Problem Setup:** A copper-water heat pipe, length L<sub>eff</sub> = 0.3 m, wick radius r<sub>c</sub> = 0.1 mm, vapor temperature T<sub>v</sub> = 100 °C.

### Step 1: List Properties at 100 °C
- ρ<sub>l</sub> = 958 kg/m³
- h<sub>fg</sub> = 2257 × 10³ J/kg
- μ<sub>l</sub> = 2.82 × 10⁻⁴ kg/m·s
- σ<sub>lv</sub> = 0.0589 N/m
- θ = 0° (perfect wetting)

### Step 2: Compute Capillary Pressure

$$\Delta P_{cap} = \frac{2 \sigma_{lv}}{r_c} = \frac{2 \times 0.0589}{0.1 \times 10^{-3}} = 1178 \, Pa$$

### Step 3: Gravity Head (Assume Horizontal, cos θ = 0)

Gravity term is zero, so:

$$Q_{cap,max} = \frac{\rho_l h_{fg} \sigma_{lv} d_e A_w}{4 \mu_l L_{eff}} \cdot \frac{1}{(2/r_c)}$$

Assuming d<sub>e</sub> = 0.2 mm and A<sub>w</sub> = 1 × 10⁻⁵ m²:

$$Q_{cap,max} = \frac{958 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times 0.0589 \times 0.2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \times 2.82 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3 \times 1178}$$

**Numerator:** 958 × 2257 × 10³ × 0.0589 × 0.2 × 10⁻³ × 10⁻⁵
= 958 × 2257 × 10³ × 1.178 × 10⁻¹⁰
≈ 254.7 × 10⁻⁴ = 0.02547

**Denominator:** 4 × 2.82 × 10⁻⁴ × 0.3 × 1178
= 4 × 2.82 × 10⁻⁴ × 353.4
= 0.3986

$$Q_{cap,max} = \frac{0.02547}{0.3986} \approx 0.064 \, W$$

> **Note:** A real heat pipe with optimized wick can transport 10–500 W. The small value here is due to the modest wick area assumed. In practice, **grooved heat pipes** give much higher Q<sub>max</sub>.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Critical Heat Flux in Boiling Cooling

For pool boiling on a flat surface (relevant to immersion cooling), the **Zuber's correlation** for critical heat flux is:

$$q_{CHF}'' = C \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \rho_v \left(\frac{\sigma g (\rho_l - \rho_v)}{\rho_v^2}\right)^{1/4}$$

For water at atmospheric pressure:
- h<sub>fg</sub> = 2257 × 10³ J/kg
- ρ<sub>v</sub> = 0.6 kg/m³
- ρ<sub>l</sub> = 958 kg/m³
- σ = 0.0589 N/m
- C = 0.149

$$q_{CHF}'' = 0.149 \times 2257 \times 10^3 \times 0.6 \left(\frac{0.0589 \times 9.81 \times 957.4}{0.36}\right)^{1/4}$$

$$q_{CHF}'' \approx 0.149 \times 1.354 \times 10^6 \times \left(\frac{553}{0.36}\right)^{1/4}$$

$$q_{CHF}'' \approx 2.017 \times 10^5 \times (1536)^{1/4} \approx 2.017 \times 10^5 \times 6.25 \approx 1.26 \times 10^6 \, W/m^2 = 126 \, W/cm^2$$

> **Logic:** Beyond this heat flux, a **vapor blanket** forms on the surface and heat transfer **drops dramatically** (post-CHF burnout). Designers must operate well below this value for safety.

The voltage drop across each resistor equals the **temperature drop** across that layer of the thermal path.

## 4.6 Heat Pipe Internal Structure (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Evaporator Section] -->|Vapor Flow| B[Adiabatic Section]
    B -->|Vapor Flow| C[Condenser Section]
    C -->|Liquid Return| D[Wick Structure]
    D -->|Capillary Pumping| A

    A1[Liquid in Wick] -.-> A
    C1[Condensed Liquid] -.-> C

    style A fill:#ff6b6b
    style C fill:#4d96ff
    style B fill:#f0e68c
    style D fill:#dda0dd
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Pipe Operational Phases (KTU often tests this):**
> 1. **Evaporation:** Liquid absorbs latent heat at hot end → vapor.
> 2. **Vapor Transport:** Vapor flows at low pressure to cold end.
> 3. **Condensation:** Vapor releases latent heat at cold end → liquid.
> 4. **Liquid Return:** Capillary action in wick pumps liquid back to hot end.
> 
> The process is **isothermal** (nearly constant T) along the length — heat is carried by **mass transport** of the working fluid, not by conduction through the wall.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Define thermal resistance in electronic cooling. Explain the electrical analogy used to analyze heat flow in electronic packages."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** Thermal resistance R<sub>th</sub> is defined as the ratio of temperature difference (ΔT) across a heat flow path to the steady-state heat transfer rate (Q) through that path. Its unit is **°C/W** or **K/W**.

**Equation:**
$$R_{th} = \frac{\Delta T}{Q} = \frac{T_j - T_a}{Q}$$

**Electrical Analogy (2 Marks):** The thermal-electrical analogy maps:
- Temperature difference (ΔT) ↔ Voltage (V)
- Heat flow rate (Q) ↔ Current (I)
- Thermal resistance (R<sub>th</sub>) ↔ Electrical resistance (R)
- Thermal capacitance (C<sub>th</sub>) ↔ Electrical capacitance (C)

This allows complex multi-layer electronic packages to be analyzed as **resistor networks**, where heat sources are modeled as current sources, and the temperature at any node is found using **Kirchhoff's laws**. The **junction-to-ambient resistance** is the sum of junction-to-case, case-to-sink, and sink-to-ambient resistances in series.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023]**
**"List the various methods used for cooling of electronic equipment. Explain any two methods in brief."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**List of Methods (1 Mark):**
1. Natural convection with heat sinks
2. Forced air cooling (fans/blowers)
3. Forced liquid cooling
4. Heat pipes and vapor chambers
5. Thermoelectric (Peltier) cooling
6. Immersion cooling
7. Jet impingement cooling
8. Phase-change cooling (boiling/evaporation)

**Explanation of Any Two (2 Marks):**

**Method 1: Forced Air Cooling**
A fan or blower forces air across finned heat sinks attached to components. This increases the convection coefficient **h from ~10 W/m²·K (natural) to ~100 W/m²·K (forced)**, dramatically improving heat dissipation. It is widely used in computers, telecom equipment, and industrial electronics.

**Method 2: Heat Pipe**
A heat pipe is a sealed tube with a wick structure containing a working fluid. Heat at the evaporator section vaporizes the fluid; the vapor flows to the condenser section where it releases latent heat and condenses. Capillary action in the wick returns the liquid to the evaporator. Heat pipes have **effective thermal conductivity up to 100,000 W/m·K**, making them ideal for spreading heat from localized sources to remote heat sinks (e.g., laptop CPU to side-mounted radiator).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer is the #1 reason for losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up Nusselt correlations** (laminar vs. turbulent, internal vs. external flow) costs full marks.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** (1-D, steady-state, constant properties) leads to deduction of 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the network diagram** in thermal resistance problems — always draw the analog circuit, even briefly.
> 5. **Misapplying fin efficiency formula** — use the correct version for **adiabatic tip** vs. **specified temperature** tip.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the need for cooling of electronic equipment. Discuss the various modes of heat transfer involved with suitable diagrams. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A power transistor dissipates 40 W. The thermal resistance from junction to case is 1.2 °C/W, and from case to heat sink is 0.4 °C/W. The heat sink is to be designed for natural convection in air with ambient temperature of 35 °C. The maximum allowable junction temperature is 120 °C. Find:
- (i) The maximum allowable heat sink-to-ambient thermal resistance.
- (ii) The required heat transfer coefficient if the heat sink surface area is 0.15 m².
- (iii) The required surface area if h = 12 W/m²·K. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

### Part (a) — Need for Cooling (7 Marks)

**Why cooling is needed (3 Marks):**
1. **Reliability:** Every 10 °C rise in junction temperature approximately halves the component life (Arrhenius relation: failure rate doubles per 10 °C rise).
2. **Performance:** Semiconductor switching speed decreases; leakage current increases exponentially with temperature.
3. **Efficiency:** Power devices such as MOSFETs and IGBTs show increased on-resistance (R<sub>DS(on)</sub>) at high temperatures, leading to higher conduction losses.
4. **Safety:** Thermal runaway can cause catastrophic failure (fire, explosion) — especially critical in Li-ion battery packs and high-energy capacitors.
5. **Miniaturization:** Higher power in smaller packages → high heat flux (W/cm²).
6. **Regulatory:** Standards like IPC-9592, MIL-STD-810 impose thermal limits.

**Modes of Heat Transfer (4 Marks):**

**1. Conduction:** Heat flows from the semiconductor die through solid paths (die attach, lead frame, package, PCB, heat sink base). Governed by **Fourier's Law**:
$$q_{cond} = -k \frac{dT}{dx}$$

**2. Convection:** Heat is transferred from the heat sink surfaces to the surrounding fluid (air, water, dielectric fluid). The rate is:
$$q_{conv} = h A (T_s - T_\infty)$$
- **Natural convection:** h ≈ 5–15 W/m²·K (driven by buoyancy).
- **Forced convection:** h ≈ 25–250 W/m²·K (driven by fan/pump).

**3. Radiation:** Heat is emitted from hot surfaces as electromagnetic waves. Governed by **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**:
$$q_{rad} = \epsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$$
Important at high temperatures (> 400 K) and in vacuum (space applications).

**Diagram (as a block schematic, refer to Section 4.2 for the standard block diagram):**
A die → package → TIM → heat sink base → fins → ambient, with conduction, convection, and radiation happening at each interface.

---

### Part (b) — Heat Sink Design (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Q = 40 W
- R<sub>th,jc</sub> = 1.2 °C/W
- R<sub>th,cs</sub> = 0.4 °C/W
- T<sub>a</sub> = 35 °C
- T<sub>j,max</sub> = 120 °C

**Step 1: Apply the Master Thermal Equation**

$$T_j = T_a + Q(R_{th,jc} + R_{th,cs} + R_{th,sa})$$

**Step 2: Solve for R<sub>th,sa</sub> (i)**

$$R_{th,sa} = \frac{T_j - T_a}{Q} - R_{th,jc} - R_{th,cs}$$

$$R_{th,sa} = \frac{120 - 35}{40} - 1.2 - 0.4$$

$$R_{th,sa} = \frac{85}{40} - 1.6 = 2.125 - 1.6 = 0.525 \, ^\circ C/W$$

**Answer (i): R<sub>th,sa</sub> = 0.525 °C/W** ✓

**Step 3: Compute h (ii)**

Using R<sub>th,sa</sub> = 1/(h·A) and A = 0.15 m²:

$$h = \frac{1}{R_{th,sa} \cdot A} = \frac{1}{0.525 \times 0.15} = \frac{1}{0.07875} \approx 12.70 \, W/m^2 \cdot K$$

**Answer (ii): h ≈ 12.7 W/m²·K** ✓

**Step 4: Required Surface Area (iii)**

With h = 12 W/m²·K:

$$A = \frac{1}{h \cdot R_{th,sa}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 0.525} = \frac{1}{6.30} \approx 0.159 \, m^2$$

**Answer (iii): A ≈ 0.159 m² = 1590 cm²** ✓

> **Interpretation:** This is a **large area** for natural convection, confirming that forced air would be preferable. Using a fan to achieve h = 50 W/m²·K would reduce the required area to ~380 cm².

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 – Module 4]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the working of (i) heat pipe and (ii) thermoelectric cooler used for electronic cooling. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A square aluminum heat sink of size 6 cm × 6 cm × 1 cm has 8 rectangular fins, each 6 cm tall, 0.4 cm thick, and 6 cm long. The base temperature is 70 °C and ambient air is at 30 °C. The heat transfer coefficient is 15 W/m²·K and k<sub>Al</sub> = 200 W/m·K. Assuming adiabatic fin tips, calculate:
- (i) Fin efficiency
- (ii) Overall surface efficiency
- (iii) Total heat dissipated by the heat sink. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

### Part (a) — Heat Pipe & TEC (7 Marks)

**(i) Heat Pipe (3.5 Marks):**

A heat pipe is a sealed, evacuated tube containing a **working fluid** (water, ammonia, acetone) and a **wick structure** (sintered powder, grooves, mesh). It has three sections:

1. **Evaporator section:** Absorbs heat from the source. The liquid wick saturates and evaporates.
2. **Adiabatic section:** Vapor flows from evaporator to condenser; minimal heat transfer.
3. **Condenser section:** Vapor condenses, releasing latent heat to the sink.

The condensed liquid returns to the evaporator via **capillary pumping** in the wick (or gravity, in thermosyphons). Effective thermal conductivity can reach **10⁵ W/m·K**, far exceeding any solid conductor.

**Limitations:** Capillary limit, entrainment limit, boiling limit, sonic limit.

**(ii) Thermoelectric Cooler (3.5 Marks):**

A thermoelectric cooler (TEC) is a solid-state device made of **n-type and p-type semiconductor pellets** (typically Bi₂Te₃) connected electrically in series and thermally in parallel. When DC current flows, the **Peltier effect** causes heat to be absorbed at the cold junction and rejected at the hot junction.

- **Heat pumped at cold side:** $Q_c = \alpha T_c I - 0.5 I^2 R - K \Delta T$
- **COP:** $COP = \dfrac{Q_c}{P_{input}} = \dfrac{\alpha T_c I - 0.5 I^2 R - K \Delta T}{\alpha (T_h - T_c) I + I^2 R}$

**Applications:** Laser diode cooling, IR detector stabilization, CPU overclocking enclosures, scientific instruments.

**Advantages:** No moving parts, precise T control, long life.
**Disadvantages:** Low COP (~0.5–1.0), high input power, must remove waste heat from hot side.

*(See Section 4.6 for a schematic block diagram of heat pipe operation.)*

---

### Part (b) — Heat Sink Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Base: 6 cm × 6 cm × 1 cm
- 8 fins, each: height H = 6 cm = 0.06 m, thickness t = 0.4 cm = 0.004 m, length L = 6 cm = 0.06 m
- T<sub>b</sub> = 70 °C, T<sub>∞</sub> = 30 °C
- h = 15 W/m²·K, k = 200 W/m·K
- Adiabatic fin tips

**Step 1: Fin Geometry**

Cross-section of one fin: A<sub>c</sub> = 0.004 × 0.06 = 2.4 × 10⁻⁴ m²
Perimeter of fin (not including tip): P = 2 × (0.004 + 0.06) = 0.128 m

**Step 2: Compute m and mL**

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}} = \sqrt{\frac{15 \times 0.128}{200 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-4}}}$$

$$m = \sqrt{\frac{1.92}{0.048}} = \sqrt{40} = 6.325 \, m^{-1}$$

$$mL = 6.325 \times 0.06 = 0.3795$$

**Step 3: Fin Efficiency (i)**

$$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL} = \frac{\tanh(0.3795)}{0.3795} = \frac{0.3615}{0.3795} \approx 0.9526$$

**Answer (i): η<sub>f</sub> ≈ 95.3%** ✓

**Step 4: Surface Areas**

Area of one fin (two large sides + tip):
A<sub>f,1</sub> = 2 × (0.004 × 0.06) + (0.004 × 0.06) = 2.88 × 10⁻⁴ + 2.4 × 10⁻⁴ = 5.28 × 10⁻⁴ m²
*(Note: "length" 0.06 m is the dimension into the page; height 0.06 m and thickness 0.004 m form the fin's cross-section.)*

Wait — re-evaluating: For a fin attached to a 6 cm × 6 cm base, the fins are arranged in parallel. Each fin is 0.06 m long (in the direction of airflow), 0.06 m high, and 0.004 m thick. So the heat-dissipating area per fin (two large faces + tip):
- Two sides: 2 × (0.06 × 0.06) = 7.2 × 10⁻³ m²
- Tip: 0.004 × 0.06 = 2.4 × 10⁻⁴ m²
- **A<sub>f,1</sub> = 7.44 × 10⁻³ m²**

Total fin area: A<sub>f,total</sub> = 8 × 7.44 × 10⁻³ = 5.95 × 10⁻² m²

Base area not covered by fins: 8 fins of thickness 0.004 m → covered length = 8 × 0.004 = 0.032 m. Gaps: 9 gaps of width (0.06 − 0.032)/9 = ... *(using simpler approach)*

Base area: A<sub>base</sub> = 0.06 × 0.06 = 3.6 × 10⁻³ m²
Uncovered base area (between fins): A<sub>b,uncovered</sub> = A<sub>base</sub> − (number of fins × fin footprint) = 3.6 × 10⁻³ − (8 × 0.004 × 0.06) = 3.6 × 10⁻³ − 1.92 × 10⁻³ = 1.68 × 10⁻³ m²

Total area: A<sub>t</sub> = A<sub>f,total</sub> + A<sub>b,uncovered</sub> = 5.95 × 10⁻² + 1.68 × 10⁻³ = 6.12 × 10⁻² m²

**Step 5: Overall Surface Efficiency (ii)**

$$\eta_o = 1 - \frac{A_f}{A_t}(1 - \eta_f) = 1 - \frac{5.95 \times 10^{-2}}{6.12 \times 10^{-2}}(1 - 0.9526)$$

$$\eta_o = 1 - 0.9722 \times 0.0474 = 1 - 0.0461 = 0.954$$

**Answer (ii): η<sub>o</sub> ≈ 95.4%** ✓

**Step 6: Total Heat Dissipated (iii)**

$$Q = \eta_o \cdot h \cdot A_t \cdot (T_b - T_\infty)$$

$$Q = 0.954 \times 15 \times 6.12 \times 10^{-2} \times (70 - 30)$$

$$Q = 0.954 \times 15 \times 0.0612 \times 40$$

$$Q = 0.954 \times 36.72 = 35.03 \, W$$

**Answer (iii): Q ≈ 35.0 W** ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cooling of Electronic Equipments**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- [ ] **Master Equation:** $T_j = T_a + Q \cdot R_{th,ja}$
- [ ] **Electrical Analogy:** ΔT ↔ V, Q ↔ I, R<sub>th</sub> ↔ R
- [ ] **Series Thermal Resistance:** R<sub>th,total</sub> = R<sub>th,jc</sub> + R<sub>th,cs</sub> + R<sub>th,sa</sub>
- [ ] **Three modes of heat transfer:** Conduction (Fourier), Convection (Newton's law of cooling), Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann)
- [ ] **Arrhenius rule:** Failure rate doubles per 10 °C rise in T<sub>j</sub>

### Key Formulas (Must Memorize)
- [ ] **Conduction resistance:** $R = L/(kA)$
- [ ] **Convection resistance:** $R = 1/(hA)$
- [ ] **Fin efficiency:** $\eta_f = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$, with $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$
- [ ] **Overall surface efficiency:** $\eta_o = 1 - (A_f/A_t)(1 - \eta_f)$
- [ ] **Heat sink dissipation:** $Q = \eta_o h A_t (T_b - T_\infty)$
- [ ] **Dittus-Boelter:** $Nu_D = 0.023 Re_D^{0.8} Pr^n$
- [ ] **Laminar flat plate:** $Nu_L = 0.664 Re_L^{1/2} Pr^{1/3}$
- [ ] **Turbulent flat plate:** $Nu_L = 0.037 Re_L^{4/5} Pr^{1/3}$
- [ ] **Radiation:** $q = \epsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)$, with σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴
- [ ] **Heat pipe:** $Q = \dot{m} h_{fg}$ (axial transport)
- [ ] **TEC cold-side:** $Q_c = \alpha T_c I - 0.5 I^2 R - K \Delta T$

### Cooling Methods (Must Know All)
- [ ] Natural convection heat sink
- [ ] Forced air cooling (fan + heat sink)
- [ ] Forced liquid cooling (cold plate)
- [ ] Heat pipe / vapor chamber
- [ ] Thermoelectric (Peltier) cooler
- [ ] Immersion cooling
- [ ] Jet impingement

### Influencing Factors (Must Know All 12)
- [ ] Power dissipation
- [ ] Ambient temperature
- [ ] Component density / layout
- [ ] Airflow velocity
- [ ] Altitude / pressure
- [ ] Humidity
- [ ] Dust contamination
- [ ] Vibration / shock
- [ ] Acoustic constraints
- [ ] Size and weight
- [ ] Cost
- [ ] Orientation (affects buoyancy)

### Typical h Values (Quick Reference)
- [ ] Natural convection air: **5–15 W/m²·K**
- [ ] Forced convection air: **25–250 W/m²·K**
- [ ] Natural convection water: **100–500 W/m²·K**
- [ ] Forced convection water: **500–20,000 W/m²·K**
- [ ] Boiling water: **5,000–100,000 W/m²·K**

### Common Pitfalls (Avoid These!)
- [ ] Forgetting to convert cm² to m² in area calculations
- [ ] Using the wrong Nusselt correlation (laminar vs. turbulent)
- [ ] Not accounting for fin efficiency when computing total heat dissipation
- [ ] Forgetting adiabatic vs. non-adiabatic tip assumption for fins
- [ ] Mixing up R<sub>th,jc</sub> and R<sub>th,ja</sub> (junction-to-case vs. junction-to-ambient)
- [ ] Using the wrong Pr exponent in Dittus-Boelter (0.4 for heating, 0.3 for cooling)

### KTU 2024 Scheme Specific (Syllabus-Aligned)
- [ ] Module 4 emphasizes **practical design** — expect numerical problems combining thermal resistance + fin design
- [ ] Look out for **NEP 2020** focus on **design thinking** — questions may ask for "selection justification"
- [ ] Bloom's level: Application (L3) and Analysis (L4) dominate this module
- [ ] **Course Outcomes (CO) mapping:** CO4 typically covers this topic — focus on **design calculations**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electric Battery Cooling - Various Methods - Phase Change Material, Fin Cooling, Air Cooling, Liquid Cooling (Description Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMET503 - HEAT AND MASS TRANSFER > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Electric Battery Cooling - Various Methods - Phase Change Material, Fin Cooling, Air Cooling, Liquid Cooling (Description Only) # Module 4: Electric Battery Thermal Management (BTMS) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Electric Battery Thermal Management? **Battery Thermal Management System (BTMS)** refers to the engineered system designed to regulate the operating temperature of electric vehicle (EV) batteries within their optimal performance window, typically between **20°C and 40°C**, with a strict upper safety limit of **~55°C to 60°C** for most lithium-ion cells. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMET503, Module 4):** > Battery cooling encompasses the various engineering methods employed to dissipate the heat generated due to irreversible electrochemical reactions (Joule heating + entropic heat) during charge/discharge cycles of EV battery packs, thereby ensuring cell longevity, safety, and performance consistency. The fundamental heat generation rate per unit volume within a battery cell can be conceptually expressed as: $$\dot{q}_{gen} = \dot{q}_{Joule} + \dot{q}_{reaction} + \dot{q}_{entropic}$$ Where the dominant contributor is **Joule heating** due to internal cell resistance (I²R losses). ### 1.2 Why is Battery Cooling Critical? — The Intuitive Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Sweating Marathon Runner"** > Imagine a marathon runner on a hot day. As the runner exerts energy, the body generates internal metabolic heat. If this heat is NOT removed (via sweat, convection to air, or cold water), the body overheats, leading to fatigue, cramps, organ failure, and ultimately collapse. > Similarly, an EV battery during fast charging or aggressive acceleration is the "runner." The electrochemical reactions generate heat. If this heat is **NOT actively managed**, the battery: > - Loses **capacity** (you drive less km per charge) > - Degrades faster (fewer charge cycles) > - Risks **thermal runaway** (fires/explosions) > - Delivers inconsistent power output Hence, the BTMS is the body's "sweating mechanism" — it must transfer heat OUT of the battery pack efficiently and reliably. ### 1.3 The Four Primary Cooling Methods (KTU Module 4 Focus) The KTU syllabus mandates a descriptive understanding of the following **four major cooling techniques**: | Sl. No | Cooling Method | Heat Transfer Mode | Medium | Typical Application | |--------|----------------|--------------------|--------|---------------------| | 1 | **Phase Change Material (PCM) Cooling** | Latent heat absorption | Solid-liquid PCM | Passive / Hybrid packs | | 2 | **Fin Cooling** | Extended surface convection | Air (natural/forced) | Compact EV modules | | 3 | **Air Cooling** | Forced/Natural convection | Ambient air | Entry-level EVs, HEVs | | 4 | **Liquid Cooling** | Forced convection (single/two-phase) | Water-glycol / dielectric oil | High-performance EVs (Tesla, BMW i-series) | > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 scheme tags this topic as **"Description Only"**, meaning questions are typically theoretical, comparative, or selection-justification based rather than computationally heavy. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Method 1: Phase Change Material (PCM) Cooling ### 2.1.1 Working Principle **Phase Change Materials** are substances that absorb large quantities of heat (latent heat of fusion, $L_f$) when transitioning from solid to liquid state, without undergoing a temperature rise during the phase transition itself. They act as **thermal capacitors** or **heat sponges**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The battery heat is absorbed isothermally (at constant temperature equal to the PCM's melting point, $T_m$) by the latent heat mechanism. This keeps the cell temperature pinned near $T_m$ throughout the melting process. ### 2.1.2 Common PCMs Used in EV Batteries - **Paraffin Waxes** (e.g., n-eicosane, $T_m$ = 36–38°C, $L_f$ ≈ 200–250 kJ/kg) - **Salt Hydrates** (e.g., Glauber's salt, $T_m$ ≈ 32°C) - **Fatty Acids** (e.g., Capric acid, $T_m$ ≈ 31°C) - **Composite PCMs** (PCM + graphite filler for enhanced thermal conductivity) ### 2.1.3 Advantages & Limitations | Advantages | Limitations | |------------|-------------| | Passive operation (no pump/blower) | Low thermal conductivity (~0.2 W/m·K) | | Compact, lightweight design | One-time melt; requires solidification reset | | Uniform temperature distribution | Risk of leakage in liquid state | | Silent, maintenance-free | Slow response to transient spikes | ### 2.1.4 Governing Heat Transfer Equations The total heat absorbed by the PCM during phase change: $$Q_{PCM} = m_{PCM} \cdot L_f + m_{PCM} \cdot c_{p,PCM} \cdot \Delta T$$ Where the first term is the **latent heat** (dominant) and the second is the **sensible heat** before/after phase transition. The transient heat conduction inside the PCM (1D slab assumption): $$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha_{PCM} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$ The **Stefan number** governing melting behavior: $$Ste = \frac{c_p (T_\infty - T_m)}{L_f}$$ --- ## 2.2 Method 2: Fin Cooling (Extended Surface Cooling) ### 2.2.1 Working Principle **Fins** are high-conductivity extensions (usually aluminum) attached to the battery cell surface to **augment the convective heat transfer area**. They increase the effective surface area available for heat exchange with the surrounding fluid (typically air). ### 2.2.2 Fin Types in BTMS - **Straight Fins** (rectangular profile) - **Pin Fins** (cylindrical, used in cylindrical 18650/21700 cells) - **Annular Fins** (around cylindrical cells) - **Plate Fins** (between prismatic cells) ### 2.2.3 Fin Efficiency & Effectiveness > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The two key performance metrics for fins are **Efficiency** ($\eta_f$) and **Effectiveness** ($\varepsilon_f$). For a fin of length $L$, perimeter $P$, and cross-section $A_c$: $$\eta_f = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}, \quad \text{where } m = \sqrt{\frac{hP}{kA_c}}$$ $$\varepsilon_f = \frac{Q_{fin}}{Q_{no\,fin}} = \eta_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c}$$ ### 2.2.4 Advantages & Limitations | Advantages | Limitations | |------------|-------------| | Passive, simple design | Performance limited by air's low $h$ (~5–50 W/m²K) | | Lightweight | Insufficient for high C-rate discharge | | Low cost | Adds weight and volume to pack | --- ## 2.3 Method 3: Air Cooling ### 2.3.1 Working Principle Air is the most accessible and economical coolant. Either **natural convection** (passive, relying on buoyancy) or **forced convection** (using a blower/fan) carries heat away from the battery surface. ### 2.3.2 Configurations - **Series Flow**: Air passes cells sequentially → temperature gradient builds up - **Parallel Flow**: Air distributed uniformly → more uniform cooling (preferred) - **Z-Flow / U-Flow**: Serpentine channels for compact design ### 2.3.3 Governing Equations The convective heat transfer from a battery cell to flowing air: $$Q = h_{air} \cdot A_s \cdot (T_{cell} - T_{air})$$ Using the Reynolds number to characterize flow regime inside channels: $$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot u \cdot D_h}{\mu}$$ Typical correlations for internal flow in battery cooling ducts: $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4} \quad \text{(turbulent, Dittus-Boelter)}$$ $$h = \frac{Nu \cdot k_{air}}{D_h}$$ Where $D_h$ is the hydraulic diameter of the cooling channel. ### 2.3.4 Advantages & Limitations | Advantages | Limitations | |------------|-------------| | Simplest, cheapest, lightest | Low heat capacity of air | | Easy maintenance | Non-uniform temperature distribution | | No leakage risk | Insufficient for > 3C continuous discharge | | Eco-friendly | Noise from blower | --- ## 2.4 Method 4: Liquid Cooling ### 2.4.1 Working Principle A liquid coolant (water-ethylene glycol mixture, dielectric oil, or refrigerant) circulates through channels/cold plates in **direct or indirect contact** with the battery cells, absorbing heat via forced convection with a much higher heat transfer coefficient than air. ### 2.4.2 Liquid Cooling Architectures - **Cold Plate Cooling**: Coolant flows through channels machined in a metal plate beneath cells - **Jacket Cooling**: Coolant channels wrapped around cylindrical cell arrays - **Immersion Cooling**: Cells fully submerged in dielectric fluid (most efficient, used in Tesla, Rimac) - **Two-Phase Cooling**: Coolant undergoes phase change (boiling) for ultra-high $h$ ### 2.4.3 Governing Equations For a cold plate with channel hydraulic diameter $D_h$: $$Nu = 0.023 \cdot Re^{0.8} \cdot Pr^{0.4}$$ Heat removed by coolant flow: $$Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,coolant} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ For two-phase (boiling) cooling, the **Boiling number** characterizes the regime: $$Bo = \frac{q''}{\rho_v \cdot h_{fg} \cdot u_v}$$ ### 2.4.4 Advantages & Limitations | Advantages | Limitations | |------------|-------------| | Highest heat transfer coefficient ($h$ = 500–10,000 W/m²K) | Complex pump + plumbing system | | Excellent temperature uniformity (±2°C) | Risk of coolant leakage (electrical hazard) | | Compact, high power density | Heavier than air cooling | | Supports fast charging (350 kW+) | Higher cost & maintenance | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Unit | |---|---------|---------|------| | 1 | $Q_{PCM} = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat absorbed by PCM | J | | 2 | $Ste = c_p(T_\infty - T_m)/L_f$ | Stefan number (melting criterion) | — | | 3 | $m = \sqrt{hP/(kA_c)}$ | Fin parameter | m⁻¹ | | 4 | $\eta_f = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$ | Fin efficiency | — | | 5 | $\varepsilon_f = \eta_f \cdot A_f/A_c$ | Fin effectiveness | — | | 6 | $Q = h A_s (T_s - T_\infty)$ | Newton's law of cooling | W | | 7 | $Re = \rho u D_h / \mu$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | — | | 8 | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter correlation | — | | 9 | $h = Nu \cdot k / D_h$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | W/m²K | | 10 | $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | Heat carried by coolant stream | W | | 11 | $Bo = q''/(\rho_v h_{fg} u_v)$ | Boiling number | — | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Comparison Matrix ## 3.1 Comparative Performance Analysis (The Most Likely KTU Question) Since this topic is **"Description Only"**, KTU typically asks for a **comparative description** or **selection justification**. The derivation below demonstrates how to *quantitatively justify* a cooling method selection. ### 3.1.1 Sample Comparative Problem (Worked Analytical Approach) **Problem Statement:** An EV battery pack generates $Q = 1500$ W of heat during a 2C discharge cycle. Compare the cooling capacity of (a) Air Cooling with $\dot{V} = 0.05$ m³/s, and (b) Liquid Cooling with $\dot{V} = 0.001$ m³/s of 50:50 water-glycol. Assume inlet temperature $T_{in} = 25°C$, exit temperature $T_{out,max} = 40°C$. **Step 1 — Air Cooling Capacity Calculation** Density and specific heat of air at ~30°C: $$\rho_{air} \approx 1.16 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad c_{p,air} \approx 1005 \text{ J/kg·K}$$ Mass flow rate of air: $$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot \dot{V}_{air} = 1.16 \times 0.05 = 0.058 \text{ kg/s}$$ Heat removal capacity: $$Q_{air} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ $$Q_{air} = 0.058 \times 1005 \times (40 - 25)$$ $$Q_{air} = 0.058 \times 1005 \times 15 \approx 874.4 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2 — Liquid Cooling Capacity Calculation** Density and specific heat of 50:50 water-glycol: $$\rho_{liq} \approx 1070 \text{ kg/m}^3, \quad c_{p,liq} \approx 3300 \text{ J/kg·K}$$ Mass flow rate of liquid: $$\dot{m}_{liq} = \rho_{liq} \cdot \dot{V}_{liq} = 1070 \times 0.001 = 1.07 \text{ kg/s}$$ Heat removal capacity: $$Q_{liq} = \dot{m}_{liq} \cdot c_{p,liq} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$ $$Q_{liq} = 1.07 \times 3300 \times (40 - 25)$$ $$Q_{liq} = 1.07 \times 3300 \times 15 \approx 52,965 \text{ W} \approx 53 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 — Comparative Conclusion** $$\frac{Q_{liq}}{Q_{air}} = \frac{52,965}{874.4} \approx 60.6$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Liquid cooling removes ~**60 times more heat** than air cooling using only 1/50th of the volumetric flow rate. This is precisely why **high-performance EVs (Tesla Model S, Lucid Air, Rimac Nevera)** universally adopt liquid cooling. ### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield) | Parameter | PCM Cooling | Fin Cooling | Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling | |-----------|-------------|-------------|-------------|----------------| | **Heat Transfer Mode** | Latent + Conduction | Extended Surface Convection | Convection (Natural/Forced) | Convection (Forced, 1φ/2φ) | | **Coolant** | Solid-Liquid PCM | Air | Air | Water-glycol / Oil / Refrigerant | | **Typical h (W/m²K)** | ~100–500 (effective) | ~10–50 | ~5–100 | ~500–10,000 | | **Active/Passive** | Passive | Passive | Active (typically) | Active | | **Temperature Uniformity** | Excellent | Moderate | Poor | Excellent | | **Weight/Pack Volume** | Low/Compact | Low/Moderate | Lowest/Compact | Moderate/Larger | | **Fast-Charging Capability** | Limited | Limited | Limited | Excellent | | **Complexity** | Low | Low | Low–Medium | High | | **Cost** | Medium | Low | Lowest | High | | **Leakage Risk** | Medium | None | None | High | | **Typical EV Users** | Niche/Hybrid | Compact EVs | Tata Tiago EV, Nissan Leaf | Tesla, BMW, Mercedes EQS | --- ## 3.3 Selection Logic (KTU Frequently Asked) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Which method is best for X application?"*, use the following decision logic: ``` STEP 1: Estimate battery heat generation Q Q = I²R_internal × (number of cells) STEP 2: Determine C-rate requirement High C-rate (>3C) → REJECT PCM, Air → USE Liquid Moderate C-rate (1–3C) → Air or PCM feasible Low C-rate (<1C) → PCM or fin feasible STEP 3: Apply packaging constraint Weight-sensitive (2W) → Air/PCM Volume-sensitive (compact) → Liquid cold plate STEP 4: Apply safety constraint High voltage → Dielectric fluid or indirect liquid Cost-sensitive → Air cooling ``` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a BTMS ```mermaid flowchart TB BAT[("Battery Pack
Heat Source Q_gen")]:::heat SENSE["Temperature Sensors
NTC / RTD"]:::sensor CTRL["BMS Controller
Microcontroller Unit"]:::ctrl ACT["Actuator Decision"]:::act PCM["PCM Module
Latent Heat Absorption"]:::method1 FIN["Fin Array
Extended Surface"]:::method2 AIR["Air Blower + Ducts
Forced Convection"]:::method3 LIQ["Pump + Cold Plate
Liquid Loop"]:::method4 RAD["Radiator / Heat Exchanger
Final Rejection"]:::reject AMB(["Ambient Air
Heat Sink"]):::ambient BAT -->|T_cell rises| SENSE SENSE -->|T_signal| CTRL CTRL -->|Compare with T_set| ACT ACT -->|Activate| PCM ACT -->|Activate| FIN ACT -->|Activate| AIR ACT -->|Activate| LIQ PCM --> RAD FIN --> RAD AIR --> RAD LIQ --> RAD RAD --> AMB classDef heat fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#fff classDef sensor fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#f59f00,color:#000 classDef ctrl fill:#4dabf7,stroke:#1971c2,color:#fff classDef act fill:#845ef7,stroke:#5f3dc4,color:#fff classDef method1 fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff classDef method2 fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff classDef method3 fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff classDef method4 fill:#51cf66,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#fff classDef reject fill:#ff922b,stroke:#d9480f,color:#fff classDef ambient fill:#ced4da,stroke:#495057,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Comparative Architecture of Cooling Methods ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["PCM Cooling"] A1["Battery Cell"] --> A2["Encapsulated PCM
Packing Around Cell"] A2 --> A3["Latent Heat Storage
Q = m·L_f"] end subgraph B["Fin Cooling"] B1["Battery Cell"] --> B2["Aluminum Fins
Extended Surface A_f"] B2 --> B3["Air Flow
h = 10-50 W/m²K"] end subgraph C["Air Cooling"] C1["Battery Cell Array"] --> C2["Cooling Ducts
Channels"] C2 --> C3["Blower / Fan
Forced Convection"] C3 --> C4["Exhaust to Ambient"] end subgraph D["Liquid Cooling"] D1["Battery Cell Array"] --> D2["Cold Plate
Machined Channels"] D2 --> D3["Pump + Coolant
50:50 Water-Glycol"] D3 --> D4["Chiller / Radiator
Secondary Loop"] end ``` ## 4.3 Heat Flow Path in Each Method ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PATH1["PCM Path"] P1a["Cell Core (T_cell)"] -->|Conduction| P1b["Cell Wall"] P1b -->|Conduction| P1c["PCM Layer"] P1c -->|Latent Heat| P1d["Phase Change
Solid → Liquid"] end subgraph PATH2["Fin Path"] P2a["Cell Surface"] -->|Conduction through Fin| P2b["Fin Tip"] P2b -->|Convection h_fin| P2c["Surrounding Air"] end subgraph PATH3["Air Path"] P3a["Cell Surface"] -->|Convection h_air| P3b["Channel Air"] P3b -->|Bulk Flow| P3c["Exhaust"] end subgraph PATH4["Liquid Path"] P4a["Cell Surface"] -->|Conduction through Cold Plate| P4b["Channel Wall"] P4b -->|Convection h_liq| P4c["Coolant Stream"] P4c -->|Enthalpy Rise| P4d["Pump → Chiller"] end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper] **Explain in brief the working principle of Phase Change Material (PCM) based battery cooling. Mention any two commonly used PCMs.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):** PCM cooling operates on the principle of **latent heat absorption** during the solid-to-liquid phase transition. When the battery cell temperature reaches the PCM's melting point ($T_m$), the PCM melts and absorbs a large amount of heat without a temperature rise (isothermal process), thus buffering the cell temperature spike. **Heat absorbed:** $$Q_{PCM} = m \cdot L_f \quad \text{(latent, dominant)} + m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{(sensible)}$$ **Two commonly used PCMs:** 1. **Paraffin waxes** (e.g., n-eicosane, $T_m \approx 36°C$, $L_f \approx 247$ kJ/kg) 2. **Salt hydrates** (e.g., Glauber's salt — Na₂SO₄·10H₂O, $T_m \approx 32°C$) **Advantage:** Passive, compact, silent, excellent temperature uniformity. **Limitation:** Low thermal conductivity; requires solidification (charging/cooldown phase) to reset. *(Award: 1 mark principle, 1 mark for the two PCMs, 1 mark for advantage/limitation.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary] **Compare forced air cooling and liquid cooling for electric vehicle battery packs on the basis of (i) heat transfer coefficient, (ii) temperature uniformity, and (iii) fast-charging capability.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Format):** | Parameter | Forced Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling | |-----------|---------------------|----------------| | **(i) Heat transfer coefficient (h)** | 5 – 100 W/m²K (low) | 500 – 10,000 W/m²K (high) | | **(ii) Temperature uniformity** | Poor (cells downstream see warmer air) | Excellent (±2°C across pack) | | **(iii) Fast-charging capability** | Limited (insufficient for >50 kW charge) | Excellent (supports 250–350 kW DC fast charging) | **Engineering Conclusion:** Liquid cooling is mandatory for high-performance EVs requiring fast charging and high discharge rates; air cooling suffices only for low-power urban EVs (e.g., Tata Tiago EV, entry-level commuter EVs). *(Award: 1 mark per correct comparative point.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT** write only advantages without limitations. KTU board examiners deduct 2–3 marks if the answer is one-sided. > - **Always include a comparison table** when comparing cooling methods — examiners look for it explicitly. > - **Cite at least one real EV model** using each technology (e.g., Tesla uses liquid, Nissan Leaf uses air). This earns the "application awareness" mark. > - **Mention both Stefan number and Reynolds number** where relevant — these signal concept depth. > - Avoid using vague terms like *"liquid is better"* — quantify with $h$ values or temperature uniformity numbers. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **(a)** Describe the working principle and types of **fin cooling** used in electric vehicle battery thermal management. Derive the expression for **fin efficiency** of a straight rectangular fin with insulated tip. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **air cooling** method for battery packs with neat sketches of series, parallel, and Z-type flow configurations. Discuss its advantages and limitations compared to liquid cooling. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **Working Principle (2 marks):** Fins are high-thermal-conductivity extensions (typically aluminum, $k \approx 205$ W/m·K) bonded to the cell surface. They **increase the effective convective surface area** ($A_f \gg A_c$), thereby enhancing heat dissipation to the surrounding air without changing the base temperature significantly. **Types of Fins used in EV batteries (2 marks):** | Type | Used With | Typical Geometry | |------|-----------|------------------| | Straight (rectangular) | Prismatic cells | Plate-like, length 20–50 mm | | Pin (cylindrical) | Cylindrical 18650/21700 cells | $d$ = 2–5 mm, $L$ = 15–30 mm | | Annular (radial) | Cylindrical cells | Ring around circumference | **Derivation of Fin Efficiency with Insulated Tip (3 marks):** Consider a straight rectangular fin of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A_c$, perimeter $P$, with insulated tip (no heat loss from tip). **Governing differential equation** (from 1D fin conduction with convection): $$\frac{d^2 \theta}{dx^2} - m^2 \theta = 0, \quad \text{where } \theta = T - T_\infty, \quad m^2 = \frac{hP}{kA_c}$$ **General solution:** $$\theta(x) = C_1 \cosh(mx) + C_2 \sinh(mx)$$ **Boundary conditions:** At $x = 0$: $\theta(0) = \theta_b = T_b - T_\infty$ → $C_1 = \theta_b$ At $x = L$ (insulated tip): $\dfrac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=L} = 0$ $$\frac{d\theta}{dx} = m \theta_b \sinh(mx) + m C_2 \cosh(mx)$$ At $x = L$: $m \theta_b \sinh(mL) + m C_2 \cosh(mL) = 0$ $$C_2 = -\theta_b \cdot \frac{\sinh(mL)}{\cosh(mL)} = -\theta_b \tanh(mL)$$ **Temperature distribution:** $$\boxed{\theta(x) = \theta_b \cdot \frac{\cosh[m(L-x)]}{\cosh(mL)}}$$ **Heat transfer at base:** $$Q_f = -kA_c \frac{d\theta}{dx}\bigg|_{x=0} = kA_c m \theta_b \tanh(mL)$$ **Maximum possible heat transfer** (if entire fin were at base temperature): $$Q_{max} = h A_f \theta_b = h (PL) \theta_b$$ **Fin Efficiency:** $$\boxed{\eta_f = \frac{Q_f}{Q_{max}} = \frac{\tanh(mL)}{mL}}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Working Principle of Air Cooling (2 marks):** Ambient air is forced (or naturally convected) over the battery cells via a blower. Heat is transferred from the cell surface to the air stream by forced convection. The warmed air is exhausted to the ambient. **Configurations (3 marks):** ``` Series Flow: [Cell1]→[Cell2]→[Cell3]→[Cell4] ↑ Inlet ↑ Outlet (T increases downstream — non-uniform cooling) Parallel Flow: Air splits into multiple parallel channels ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓ (uniform distribution — preferred) Z-Flow: [C1]→ ↓ ←[C2] ↓ [C3]→ (serpentine, compact) ``` **Advantages vs. Liquid Cooling (1 mark each pair):** | Feature | Air Cooling | Liquid Cooling | |---------|-------------|----------------| | Cost | Lower | Higher (pump, plumbing) | | Weight | Lighter | Heavier | | Maintenance | Simple | Complex | | Heat removal rate | Lower | **Far superior** | | Temperature uniformity | Poor | **Excellent** | | Fast charging support | No | **Yes** | **Conclusion:** Air cooling is suitable for low-power, cost-sensitive urban EVs. Liquid cooling is mandatory for high-performance and fast-charging applications. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] **(a)** Describe the **liquid cooling** method for EV battery packs, including cold plate and immersion configurations. Explain the role of Reynolds number and Dittus-Boelter correlation in designing the cooling channel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **Phase Change Material (PCM) cooling** method. Discuss how the Stefan number influences the melting behavior and justify why PCMs are preferred for short-duration high-heat spikes. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks) **Working Principle (2 marks):** A liquid coolant (typically 50:50 water-ethylene glycol, $c_p \approx 3300$ J/kg·K) is circulated via an electric pump through channels machined in a **cold plate** placed beneath or between battery cells. The high heat capacity and forced convection ($h$ up to 10,000 W/m²K) enable rapid and uniform heat extraction. **Architectures (2 marks):** - **Cold Plate Cooling:** Coolant flows through serpentine or parallel channels inside an aluminum cold plate in contact with the cell base. - **Immersion Cooling:** Cells are fully submerged in a dielectric fluid (e.g., 3M Novec, mineral oil). No electrical short-circuit risk; extremely uniform cooling. Used in **Tesla, Rimac Nevera, Lucid Air**. **Role of Reynolds Number (1.5 marks):** $$Re = \frac{\rho u D_h}{\mu}$$ Determines flow regime inside the cooling channel: - $Re < 2300$: Laminar flow (lower $h$) - $Re > 4000$: Turbulent flow (higher $h$, preferred for EV BTMS) **Role of Dittus-Boelter Correlation (1.5 marks):** For turbulent flow in smooth circular tubes ($Re > 10,000$, $0.7 < Pr < 160$): $$Nu_D = 0.023 \, Re_D^{0.8} \, Pr^n$$ where $n = 0.4$ for heating and $n = 0.3$ for cooling of the fluid. The convective heat transfer coefficient: $$h = \frac{Nu_D \cdot k_{coolant}}{D_h}$$ This is the **primary design equation** for sizing cold plate channels in EV BTMS. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks) **Working Principle (2 marks):** PCMs absorb heat primarily through **latent heat of fusion** ($L_f$) during melting, keeping cell temperature near $T_m$ for an extended duration without active intervention. They act as **thermal buffers** between the cell and the environment. **Mathematical Model (3 marks):** The transient 1D heat conduction (with phase change): $$\rho_{PCM} \cdot c_{p,PCM} \cdot \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k_{PCM} \cdot \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}$$ **Stefan Number (Ste):** $$Ste = \frac{c_p (T_\infty - T_m)}{L_f}$$ - $Ste \ll 1$: Sensible heat negligible → latent heat dominates → PCM behaves as near-perfect thermal capacitor. - $Ste \gg 1$: Sensible heat dominates → melting is delayed, less effective. **Justification for Short-Duration High-Heat Spikes (2 marks):** During rapid acceleration or fast charging, the battery experiences **short, intense heat pulses** (e.g., 5–10 kW for 30–60 seconds). PCM is ideal because: 1. **No active control needed** — passive absorption. 2. **Isothermal buffering** keeps cell at $T_m$. 3. **Solidification** during idle/low-load phases resets the PCM for the next cycle. 4. **Compact, lightweight** compared to a liquid loop with pump and radiator. **Limitation:** After complete melting, PCM loses its buffering capacity. Hence, PCMs are typically used in **hybrid systems** (e.g., PCM + air cooling) where the secondary loop solidifies the PCM during cruising. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams** - [ ] **BTMS Definition:** Engineered system to maintain EV battery cells in the **20–40°C** window. - [ ] **Heat generation sources:** Joule heating (dominant) + entropic + parasitic reactions. - [ ] **Upper safety limit:** ~55–60°C (beyond this → accelerated degradation + thermal runaway risk). - [ ] **Four KTU-mandated methods:** PCM, Fin, Air, Liquid. - [ ] **PCM Cooling:** Latent heat absorption ($L_f$); isothermal buffering; passive; paraffin/salt hydrates; limited by low $k$. - [ ] **Fin Cooling:** Extended surface; $\eta_f = \tanh(mL)/(mL)$; effectiveness $\varepsilon_f = \eta_f \cdot A_f/A_c$. - [ ] **Air Cooling:** Forced convection; $h \approx$ 5–100 W/m²K; cheapest; insufficient for fast charging. - [ ] **Liquid Cooling:** $h \approx$ 500–10,000 W/m²K; mandatory for high-performance EVs; cold plate or immersion. - [ ] **Dittus-Boelter (turbulent):** $Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{0.4}$ — use this for cold plate sizing. - [ ] **Stefan Number:** $Ste = c_p(T_\infty - T_m)/L_f$ — small $Ste$ means latent-dominant PCM behavior. - [ ] **Key comparator:** Liquid removes ~**60× more heat** than air for the same temperature rise (per the worked example). - [ ] **Always end with:** A comparison table + a named EV example (Tesla = liquid, Nissan Leaf = air). - [ ] **Examiner dislikes:** One-sided answers; missing units; no real-world EV reference; omitting limitations.
